…A wealthy merchant was watching the fruitless efforts of his workers to subdue a newly purchased unbroken stallion. Seeing that the would-be riders neither could, nor really tried, to master the enraged animal, he fell into a violent irritation.

Gulab Singh addressed him: "May I try, sir?"

The merchant looked doubtfully at the lean young man with hollow cheeks: "You look ready to die of hunger — how can you tame stallions…"

"Allow me to try, and see for yourself." Gulab Singh belonged to a noble line and, besides his excellent education, was a master of horsemanship. Speaking softly to the stallion, he began to lead it in a circle, calming it. Then, choosing the right moment, with a single leap he vaulted into the saddle, and the stallion launched into a frenzied gallop. After about ten kilometers, both were exhausted. The stallion shifted to a slow trot and soon submitted entirely to the rider. Approaching the square, Gulab Singh lost consciousness from fatigue, but did not fall from the lathered horse. The merchant ordered the young man carried to his house and allowed to rest. In the evening they got into conversation, and the merchant was impressed at how fluently his guest spoke Sanskrit, Persian, and English. Gulab Singh was immediately taken on as manager for timber export, with the additional duty of reading the Bhagavata Purana to the boss's elderly mother.

Several months passed. One evening, as usual, he was reading aloud. The elderly woman, seated in her chair, breathed evenly, and it was unclear whether she was listening or sleeping. This did not trouble him; he was absorbed in the sounds of the Sanskrit shlokas, which had on him simultaneously a trance-inducing and a sobering effect. Reaching the section where Shri Krishna instructs Uddhava [footnote 41], Gulab Singh experienced an upsurge of spiritual illumination and sank into a state of vairagya [footnote 42]. He was overcome by a strange indifference and detachment from all things worldly. The "ascetic disinterest" in everything that could provoke attachment or even dependence in most people filled him to the brim. Like the sting of a wasp, this sensation pierced his heart with the needle of longing: for another life, for other knowledge, for renunciation of the pain of being and of worldly pleasures. Soon he left Burma, setting off for the Kumbh Mela [footnote 43] at Allahabad [footnote 44]. In search of a Guru.

[footnote 41: Uddhava — devotee of Lord Krishna, the figure of the Uddhava Gita (the Bhagavata Purana), to whom Krishna explains bhakti and yoga. Uddhava is said to have been Krishna's childhood friend; in some texts he is mentioned as his cousin.]
[footnote 42: Vairagya — the practice of non-attachment to the external world, renunciation of pleasure and pain in order to attain liberation and to halt the wheel of births and deaths.]
[footnote 43: Kumbh Mela — a major Hindu pilgrimage festival. Celebrated once every twelve years in honor of every completed cycle of the planet Jupiter, or Brihaspati. Traditionally this takes place at four places of pilgrimage on the banks of rivers: at Allahabad (at the confluence of the Ganges, Yamuna, and Sarasvati), at Haridwar (on the banks of the Ganges), at Nashik (on the river Godavari), and finally at Ujjain (on the river Shipra). The festival is marked by ritual bathing, numerous fairs, educational and religious talks with saints, mass gatherings of monks and ascetics, and various entertainment programs. It is believed that bathing in the rivers washes away past mistakes and sins. Traditionally the founding of the Kumbh Mela is attributed to the 8th-century philosopher and saint Adi Shankara. However, no historical evidence of mass pilgrimages under the name "Kumbh Mela" prior to the 19th century has been discovered. In Sanskrit "Kumbha" literally means "pitcher, pot." The word "mela" means "to unite, to join, to meet, to move together," especially in the context of public festivals. Thus, Kumbh Mela — a "gathering, meeting, union" — takes place beside sacred waters.]
[footnote 44: Allahabad (or Ilahabad, now known as Prayagraj) — a metropolis in Uttar Pradesh, located near the Triveni Sangam, the "confluence of the three rivers" Ganges, Yamuna, and Sarasvati. It was known as Prayag, which means "place of sacrifice." Legends and scriptures say that the Creator Brahma performed the very first sacrifice (yajna) here. One of the world's oldest cities, mentioned in the ancient Vedas, Prayag was revered as a sacred city. The Mughal emperor Akbar visited the region in 1575 and was so impressed by the city's strategic position that he ordered a fort built there. The construction was completed by 1584 and named "the Abode of Allah," later, under Shah Jahan, officially renamed Allahabad. The city has great religious significance; the historical Prayag Kumbh Mela is held here every twelve years. In the Mahabharata, bathing at Prayag is mentioned as a means of repentance for past sins. For many years Prayag was the place where the ashes of national leaders, including Mahatma Gandhi, were scattered.]

At that time there lived in Allahabad a renowned sage, the spiritual mentor of the maharaja [footnote 45] of Patiala — Rajendra Singh. Gulab Singh attempted to attend his darshan, but it was nearly impossible to obtain an audience with the Guru — he was refused time after time. Gulab Singh's fate was changed by a telegram sent by the maharaja of Patiala to his mentor. The message was in English, and the sadhu bringing the telegram could not read it. Realizing that this was his chance, Gulab Singh volunteered to translate the message.

"Just do it!"

"I will read it to the Guru only in person."

He was brought before the sage, and Gulab Singh translated the telegram without faltering from English into impeccable Sanskrit — and asked for initiation. He received it. A few more months passed; he moved with the Guru to the ashram in Patiala and remained there for a long time. The Teacher was a silent and modest man, who wore only a loincloth and spent his days observing ants, children, birds, and playing puppies. Yet the Guru's wisdom was boundless, and everything he taught Gulab Singh remained with the disciple to his last breath.

[footnote 45: Maharaja — the title of "great king."]

Gulab Singh, possessing a lively mind and strong zeal, was after some time appointed mahant (head) of the ashram in Patiala. There were whispers that he might even become the Guru's successor. He now wore garments of fine cloth, food was served to him on silver dishes — but inner dissatisfaction and unease grew. He asked himself: "And what next? There is no trace of vairagya here, this is all imitation, a masquerade. What am I doing here?" He asked permission to leave for a time, and the Guru said: "Go. Whatever is to be will not be missed."

…On the train, he was recognized by a ticket-inspector, and Gulab Singh was detained at the station on a trivial pretext; the inspector contacted his family, who had been searching for him all this time. The arriving brothers almost forcibly took the prodigal son to his parents' house, where he was warmly received by his happy father and mother. But a wandering monk who had renounced the world held little interest for anyone, and within a month he was forcibly married.

Time passed, and Gulab Singh returned to the ashram to tell the Guru of the unexpected change in his life. The sage said: "Now this is your destiny. Fulfill your worldly duties, and then, when you wish to return to the ashram, come." The Teacher saw Gulab Singh's destiny and knew that karma is sometimes too strong to fight. To make a sadhu out of a wandering Brahmin with a Kshatriya soul was as hopeless an undertaking as trying to drink water out of a sieve.

For Gulab Singh began what is called normal life, and he tried honestly to adapt to it. The five villages bought by the great-grandfather brought in some income. But the cool wind of renunciation kept ruffling Gulab Singh's thoughts; vairagya still nested in his heart, and at some point he gave the land over to the farmers.

…An opportunity arose to enter a prestigious post, and Gulab Singh decided to enlist the support of the prince of Jodhpur, his former classmate and good friend. The prince presented him to his father, the maharaja of Jodhpur. His Highness said that the position in question was beneath the status of a jagirdar's son, but he could appoint him colonel of the cavalry. Gulab Singh was just over twenty, and the adrenaline of an adventurer pulsed wildly in his blood. He took part in the Afghan War of 1892, and a letter written in Queen Victoria's own hand stated that "…Colonel Gulab Singh is a hero of the Afghan War, possessing all outstanding military qualities…"

However, Colonel G. Singh was not consistent in his career advancement. After the war he left the service and returned to the village. And soon he again began to feel inner restlessness. At this same time he became acquainted with an underground movement whose participants struggled against British rule and were planning to rob a bank. Or, more precisely, a peaceful expropriation.

In Calcutta, Gulab Singh managed to imitate the signature of an important British official so deftly that on the forged promissory notes the conspirators received 200,000 rupees — fabulous money for that time. 170,000 rupees were handed over to the revolutionary leader Rasbehari Bose, who later fled to Japan; Gulab Singh returned to the village with the tidy sum of 30,000 rupees.

In Shivram-ki-Garhi (their ancestral estate on the bank of the Yamuna), it was impossible to conceal such wealth for long, and envious relatives reported him to the police. The promissory notes and most of the money were confiscated, and Gulab Singh was arrested and sent to Calcutta for a public trial.

Indian nationalists were accused of "waging hostilities against the government," and the grand trial of the revolutionaries lasted a whole year: from May 1908 to May 1909. In Gulab Singh's case the prosecution could not prove that the signatures belonged to him precisely — they had been forged so skillfully that no one could distinguish them from the original. The lack of convincing evidence led to the complete dismissal of the charges. The policeman who had arrested Gulab Singh was also present in the courtroom. Leaving the dock and passing by this officer, Gulab Singh said quietly to him: "I did it, and now I'm going free." The officer answered: "Lucky one…" And indeed, luck accompanied him in practically everything.

Awaiting trial in Calcutta, Gulab Singh became acquainted in prison with Shri Aurobindo. There the future renowned yogi and founder of integral yoga experienced his first mystical and spiritual experiences. In Aurobindo's case, too, no compelling evidence was found, and the future maharishi was released. Soon Aurobindo Ghosh left politics for the spiritual path and moved to Pondicherry, while Gulab Singh returned to his village — but maintained a correspondence with Shri Aurobindo for many long years.

The entire life of Gulab Singh was a chain of challenges to the most unusual circumstances. One day the maharaja of Jodhpur was preparing for a meeting with the Viceroy of India, where he was to appear in full ceremonial dress. They brought him his parade sword, but it was firmly stuck in the scabbard. The tall Rajasthani guards of the watch could not solve the problem, and Gulab Singh, who happened to be in the palace that day and observed the fruitless attempts, volunteered: "Your Highness, may I try?" The officers smiled discreetly: compared with the broad-shouldered, hefty guards, Colonel Singh was of rather slight build. Hanging the sword on a ceiling-hook with the scabbard upward, Gulab Singh put his full weight on the hilt, and the blade slipped out with the soft sound of awakened

steel. The maharaja applauded. Gulab Singh always believed in cleverness.

Slim, of medium height, full of energy, he was the owner of magnificent thick moustaches and an unquenchable enthusiasm. One day someone brought a new double-barreled gun, and with a single shot he downed a kite, remarking, "Excellent gun…" Colonel Singh was then about ninety-two years old, and he still loved to show off. In short, his style of life entirely corresponded to his surname, which meant "lion."

In the seventeenth century, one of his ancestors saved the maharaja of Bikaner from a tiger and was awarded the honorary title "Singh." Traditionally this was intended only for Kshatriyas, but their ancestor was apparently a special Brahmin, and the title passed to descendants, becoming a surname.

The tales of Gulab Singh breathed of the glory of epic battles, of the sharp aroma of adventures and mortal dangers; in them there was the bitterness of defeats and the sweetness of the nectar of victories. The grandfather lived a long life resembling a tropical storm or cyclone: gusty winds and downpours alternated with sunny days and total calm, and Gulab Singh enjoyed every minute.

…The grandson's full name reads: "Shailendra Bahadur Singh Sharma." Omitting the honorific "Singh" that his grandfather wore with pride, he signs his books simply "Shailendra Sharma." Over time, people began to address him by the name fully consonant with the path he had chosen: "Guruji," Teacher.

The Gangster, the Paan, and a Visit to Prison

In his last year of schooling, the surroundings of Gwalior and several other regions — Bhind and Morena — became famous throughout India thanks to the bandit formations of dacoits. The two most famous bandit leaders, Mohar Singh and Madho Singh, preferred to be called "baagis" — rebels. They did not rob the poor, but in the best traditions of Robin Hood took the wealthy as hostages and distributed the ransom among the needy rural inhabitants.

In the end, in 1972, Mohar Singh and his comrades surrendered to the famous socialist leader Jayaprakash Narayan under an agreement confirmed by Indira Gandhi herself. This agreement freed them from the death penalty. However, all legal obligations and formalities had to be observed in accordance with Indian law, and the bandits were temporarily housed in the Gwalior prison. The news made a sensation; the newspapers even published an interview with Mohar Singh.

"The dispute over my land allotment dragged on so long that in 1958 I rebelled against the system and was forced to take up arms to fight for justice. We were driven into a corner by the exhausting legal system, which deprived me and many other people of basic legal rights." After Mohar Singh killed a man, the price on his head reached 300,000 rupees. For that time, this was a quite large sum.

…Abandoning his homework, he read the interview and imagined a giant — a modern super-villain, a living hero of the comic books. The story captivated him incredibly; Mohar Singh was a living legend, and he had to see this dacoit! One of his uncles was a state prosecutor and often came from Morena to Gwalior prison to prepare the dacoits' case for court. When the uncle visited them on his next trip, he addressed him: "Take me with you to the prison, I want to see these famous bandits, who took hundreds of lives…" The uncle objected hesitantly: "You're too young to look at criminals." But he was unyielding, and in the end it was decided that in a couple of days a police jeep would be sent for him. Other family members were not at all inspired by this idea, and he set off to the prison alone.

On arrival it turned out that the uncle was very busy. Buried in tons of folders and papers together with other representatives of the prosecution, he was working over the bandits' cases. One of the junior officers undertook to conduct the tour and show the nephew the principal "characters."

In the prison yard, his eyes met an actual picnic: some bandits were doing push-ups and exercises; others were getting oil massages; food and sweets were being prepared in gigantic cauldrons right there, under the supervision of numerous servants, and the bored police were more like spectators than guards. The whole thing resembled a big festival simply taking place in a prison. The escorting officer pointed out the most famous bandits and even introduced him to some of them. On the people's faces lay the imprint of an utterly singular, calm self-assurance bordering on a readiness to take life without the slightest hesitation. Their eyes were empty and expressionless; the gaze moved slowly and appraisingly, like that of cattle-traders impassively evaluating the state of a herd. He felt like a lamb. To see, in one place, such a concentration of criminals who had taken so many human lives was extremely uncomfortable. The atmosphere of the prison yard no longer seemed festive; within him stirred the muted echo of fear. Standing at the gate, he awaited the police jeep that was to take him home, and was utterly unprepared for the surprise: Mohar Singh was being driven to the hospital for a medical examination in the same jeep. Because the dacoit had surrendered voluntarily, there was no special fuss over security; the prisoner was accompanied by four policemen and an inspector, and everyone looked rather relaxed. It was decided that Mohar Singh would first be taken to the hospital, and then they would drop the prosecutor's nephew at home…

In the car Mohar Singh sat directly opposite. Danger and self-assurance breathed from him. The bandit had a powerful wrestler's body, and his bandit's pride — enormous moustaches — was elaborately twisted into rings. Learning that the youth sitting opposite was the prosecutor's nephew, the dacoit addressed him cordially, calling him "lallu," "young man." He felt even more ill at ease.

The jeep drove up to the hospital. By the gates stood a kiosk selling paan [footnote 46], and the bandit commanded the police inspector: "Stop and buy paan for lallu." The car braked. Lallu, whom the bandit had decided to treat to paan, froze, not daring to object. All got out of the car: four hefty policemen, the strapping Mohar Singh, and the frightened lallu. They ordered the paan. Within a few minutes the news of the presence of Mohar Singh himself spread like wildfire, and townspeople began to gather around their small group. The dacoit, looking at the gawkers as a wolf looks at sheep, addressed him: "Lallu, tamasa te hoge? — do you want to see a show?" and turned to the paan seller: "Give me a glass of water." The seller obeyed, handing him the glass with trembling hands. Draining it in a few gulps, the dacoit made a threatening movement with the empty glass toward the crowd, bellowing: "Well then, you dorks?!" The crowd recoiled like a wave; several people fell to the ground in panic, and within a minute the street was absolutely empty. The dacoit laughed: "See, lallu, do you understand now how it's done?" The lallu had his breath taken away by the show.

[footnote 46: Paan (or pan) — areca nut, lime, and betel leaves; sometimes other substances are added for flavor and breath freshness. The areca itself may be replaced or mixed with tobacco, and the betel leaves are sometimes excluded. The mixture is not swallowed but spat out.]

The rest of the trip passed without incident. The bandit chief was escorted to the hospital, and he himself was driven home. The adventure was rather alarming and exciting, but on that day he saw something unique: the power of human will cutting through the will of others, like a knife passing through soft butter. A will that creates both a man and his reputation.

…In the archive of the district police department of Bhinda there is still a battered folder of documents. The yellowed pages of the dossier dryly state that Mohar Singh was "a ruthless, cruel, and hardened criminal, charged with 325 crimes, including 85 murders." Having served eight years in prison, the former bandit became the chairman of the BJP-supported [footnote 47] municipality of Nagar in his home town of Mahegaon, but he never came to love politics. In a newspaper interview he said openly: "I understood that there are far greater criminals — politicians." In 2020, the man who could scatter a crowd with an empty glass died at the age of ninety-two.

[footnote 47: BJP — Bharatiya Janata Party, "Indian People's Party" — one of the two principal political parties in India, in power since 2014 under the leadership of Prime Minister Narendra Modi.]

…Reading of Mohar Singh's death, he remembered the taste of the paan, the frightened crowd, and the triumphant laughter of the brigand. By karmic laws those who have taken human lives must be judged by the astral court, but for some reason it seemed to him that the spirit of Mohar Singh was so strong that he could withstand any verdict, even astral.

Past Lives. Mahadev

In Hinduism, the tradition of shaving a child's first hair is very important, almost obligatory. The mundan ceremony is one of the sixteen rituals of purification known as Shodasha Samskara. It is believed that the rite frees the child of all the negative of past life and contributes to mental and spiritual development, for the hair with which the child was born preserves karmic memories, so it is better to remove them quickly. If they are not cut, the child may fail to "accept" the "new" parents. Recurring memories in small children of strange places, names, or events are usually understood as memories of past life. At times these flashes of memory cause distress and must be "healed."

There is a belief that if one seats a child on a potter's wheel and turns it counterclockwise seven or eleven times, memories of past lives will be erased. And then all the experiences of previous incarnations, together with attachments, pain, desires, and mental imprints, will exert no slowing effect on the soul's development in the present life.

The Mundan ceremony took place when he was three or four months old, not too early and not too late. He showed no signs of connection with the past, but from the age of five he constantly repeated two words (or names): Bartoli and Martoli, and no one knew what they meant. Playing with the dogs, he gave them these names; the words were abracadabra, magical codes, mysterious mantras from the past (or the future). In Gujarat, Odisha, and Uttarakhand there were several small villages with such names, but no one was going to look for any clues or to investigate possible connections.

At about the same age he declared that he would write books when he grew up. What these future works would be about, he was hard-pressed to say. Probably comics, since to imagine that some other genre could interest him he could not. He also informed all who were interested that he would live in a huge house resembling a palace and surrounded by a wall, where nearby there would be three buildings and a pond with steps. And there would be people there, many people. Over the next three or four years, during family celebrations, he told the guests about future palaces… No, he did not claim he would become a king, since he already was the rightful owner of this place. To say exactly where this palace was located, and on what business visitors would come to him, he could not — but his confidence that this would happen was unshakable. Marveling at the scope of the child's imagination, the adults smiled, and of course no one took these stories seriously. But he was unyielding. The pieces of the mosaic of the future were so real that he considered

it beneath his dignity to prove the obvious.

Sometimes sadhus came to his uncle, a well-known Ayurvedic physician, and from time to time the uncle himself visited these ascetics — and would take him along. The sadhus often smoked chillums [footnote 48], filled not with tobacco or hashish but with some Ayurvedic herbs. One day, when he was eight or nine years old, a sadhu gave him a pipe to clean. The smooth ceramic pipe lay in his palm so naturally, as though he had held it before. A strange sensation came over him, as if something inside whispered: "This was part of your life, do you remember?"

And then there was the effect of fire. From an early age, every time he sat before a fire his mind would become empty, focused only on the dancing flame, and only the involuntary movements of his half-closed eyes echoed that dance. Such a trance could go on for hours. There was nothing unique in this, since every human being descends from gatherers and hunters, long subject to the hypnotic power of the most ancient of gods — Fire. And yet… The script of every destiny is written before the body's birth. Subconsciously and consciously he knew of the path that lay ahead, and of who he had been. But there were no words to express this. Moreover, sharing these feelings and thoughts with anyone would mean becoming vulnerable, over-exposed. By the age of ten he stopped mentioning palaces, visitors, and future books. All this was too personal.

[footnote 48: Chillum, or chilam — a smoking pipe, traditionally made of clay or soft stone. Widely used in India by ascetics and sadhus.]

In the summer of 1971 he turned fourteen, and the whole family went off to vacation in the Himalayas. The wife of his eldest brother was a princess of Jamnagar, and her family owned the Devaldhar estate not far from Bageshwar, to which they had been invited.

The Himalayan range, crowned by two majestic peaks — Trishul and Nanda Devi — was clearly visible for kilometers around, and the panorama took one's breath away. The upper terrace of the great house occupied almost three hundred acres of apple orchards; the idyllic, picturesque view was framed by distant snowy peaks. He usually climbed to the upper tier of the fruit barn and for hours enjoyed the stupendous views. In the mornings the Himalayas turned golden, and at twilight they were plunged into the reddish-gold liquid amber of sunset — a fantastic spectacle. The magical time spent at the estate flew by unnoticed: the family was preparing to return to Gwalior, and he already felt nostalgia for the place.

One day after lunch he went out onto the terrace. And, looking at the already familiar silhouettes of the peaks, he suddenly understood that he was looking at the Lord Shiva Himself. With one foot Mahadev [footnote 49] stood on the peak of Nanda Devi, with the other He trod the Trishul. Closing his eyes, he shook his head. When he opened them, the tangled locks of Shiva's hair spread across the sky like a flame. The hips of Mahadev were girt with a white tiger-skin, and the garland of skulls on His neck had shifted slightly from the movement of His right hand. Gripping a golden trident, Mahadev gazed straight at him; the deity's third eye was closed.

[footnote 49: Mahadev — one of the many names of Shiva, in Sanskrit "great god." Shiva is called Mahadev because all worship him: from the devas (gods) to the asuras (demons).]

The vision produced the strangest effect: he felt that some force was drawing him toward Shiva, that he was about to attain those heights on which the Lord of the Universe stood. And then the apparition vanished. Was this a play of the imagination? Hard to say. He was overcome by a feeling of disquiet, as though he had to act immediately. But nothing remained for him to do but to load with the rest of his family into the car and to return to worldly life.

Three months later the unusual vision repeated itself. That day, returning from school, he ate his lunch mechanically and lay down on the bed in his room, looking through a new comic. Leafing absent-mindedly through the pages, he suddenly saw the same vivid picture — Shiva rising above the Himalayas — and this vision lasted about a minute. Sitting up on the bed, he intended to set off for Bageshwar at once, convinced that Shiva was waiting for him. But it was impossible to explain all this to the adults, and the impulse had to be suppressed.

As subsequent events would show, he did not need to rush to the feet of Mahadev. Twenty-five years later, the Destroyer would come to him himself, and this time the hips of the Lord of the Universe would be wrapped in elephant-skin, the third eye would be half-open, and the sweat of the heated Deity would touch his own skin, leaving forever an invisible mark.

Blessings and Curses

His uncle, the well-known Ayurvedic physician, had many interesting acquaintances and visitors. One of them, Shradhanand Brahmacharya [footnote 50], was a disciple of a most famous guru from Tarapith, the mystical tantric smashan of Bengal. Besides being a realized tantric himself, Shradhanand was a masterful storyteller — and he had a great many tales.

Years later, after many travels, conversations, and meetings with various sadhus, gathering all that he had learned, he came to the conclusion that all religions are nothing but retold legends grown out of magic and spells. But at that time, being a ninth-grader, he listened to the adults' stories with wide-open eyes and a pounding heart.

[footnote 50: Brahmacharya — one who practices brahmacharya, the code of life according to the Vedic scriptures. May also be a surname or title in West Bengal, Assam, and Bangladesh. Brahmacharya is control of body and mind through ascetic exercises and disciplines. In Hindu, Jain, and Buddhist monastic traditions, brahmacharya implies, among other things, the renunciation of sex and marriage.]

One day Shradhanand told of a ritual devoted to Hanuman [footnote 51] that grants great wisdom and unusual abilities. The tale came straight from a trunk of dark fairy-stories. Preparation for this puja is rather complex, and the few who succeeded in performing the ritual were not ordinary people. Only experienced aghoris [footnote 52] and tantrics could perform it correctly, observing the safety measures. If something went wrong, the consequences were irreversible and deadly. This puja was not meant for commoners; only kings (and later major politicians) could set such a task. The gift received could endow a man with the extraordinary capacity to set in motion, or to reverse, a chain of large-scale events.

[footnote 51: Hanuman — one of the Hindu gods and a central figure of the Ramayana, the ardent devotee of Rama and his companion. One of the Seven Immortals and the son of the wind-god Vayu. He is widely known across South Asia, from Thailand and Cambodia to Indonesia. Possessing extraordinary strength and might, he is revered as]

One of the principal components of the ritual is a thick flatbread, called simply "sweet roti [footnote 53]," prepared from specially selected flour. A cloth was spread out, unsifted flour and ingredients poured onto it; the dough was kneaded in mid-air so it would not touch a hard surface (the earth). And then baked over an open fire… Various rites followed, culminating in a summoning of Hanuman. If everything was done correctly, He would appear and grant what was desired.

[footnote 51 cont.: the ideal devotee; he is often worshipped by those who observe celibacy.]
[footnote 52: Aghori (in Sanskrit "aghora" means "fearless") — a monastic order of Indian ascetics worshipping Shiva, most often in His wrathful form of Bhairava. This tantric, non-Puranic form of Shaivism arose between the 7th and 8th centuries CE. Aghoris often live at cremation grounds, smear their bodies with ash, use human bones as ornaments and ritual cups from skulls called kapalas. Participation in the last rites contradicts the postulates of orthodox Hinduism. Nevertheless, aghori gurus enjoy great respect and possess the power of healing, obtained through the performance of rites, strict discipline, and practices of renunciation. As followers of Shiva, the aghoris trace their origin to Baba Kinaram, a sage and ascetic who is believed to have lived 150 years, leaving the body in the second half of the 18th century.]
[footnote 53: Roti — an Indian flatbread, also called "chapati." Prepared from whole-grain flour and water.]

Shradhanand Brahmacharya claimed that he had once successfully performed the ritual, but never specified the purpose, nor what had been obtained as a result.

The main condition of a successful offering is the bhiksha given by representatives of three different castes. In Russian there is no term close to the concept of "bhiksha"; in approximate translation it is something given voluntarily. The bhiksha for this ritual (which had no name and was known as "THE puja of Hanuman") consisted of three portions of flour for the flatbread, taken from a eunuch, a sweeper, and a prostitute.

The first part of the flour could only be given by a hijra — a person whose nature was neither unequivocally male nor female. And it had to be given voluntarily. The blessing of hijras grants prosperity, so they are by tradition invited to various festivals. But such blessings must come from the heart, and therefore happen only very rarely.

The second portion of flour must be given by one who strictly followed the dharma [footnote 54] of his caste. It is held that sweeper-bhangis still live by the laws of dharma. They will never eat on someone else's veranda or on anyone else's territory; by tradition they take food in the street. But if a sweeper accepts an invitation to eat in the home or yard of a person to whom he feels affection, that person may count on prosperity and well-being.

The last part of the flour must be given by a woman who has never thought of a man (in the romantic, erotic sense). Therefore the flour is taken from the house of a prostitute, in whose life there is no "special man."

Years later, the Guru told him: "Always treat prostitutes, eunuchs, and sweepers with respect — all of them possess special powers." Good manners are useful in general, but with members of these three castes, as well as with yogis and sadhus, one is recommended to behave with particular respect. One never knows who these people really are; among them mighty beings may live, as history has confirmed many times.

A prostitute's heartfelt blessing can change a life. Take, for example, the story of Vivekananda. Narendranath Datta, known as Swami Vivekananda, was invited by Maharaja Ajit Singh Bahadur, ruler of the principality of Khetri in Rajasthan. Although versions of this story differ — some claim the incident took place at the court of the ruler of Jaipur — in any case, it is told that in the Pink City a festival of dance was organized, and several prostitutes had been invited there as singers and dancers. Learning of the presence of sex-workers, Vivekananda refused to come to the festival. It was said that he locked himself in his room. In any case, he expressed indignation and revulsion, and one of the priestesses of love noticed it.

[footnote 54: Dharma — "duty," ethical law, the laws of fundamental support of life, virtue, righteousness. One of the four components of the goals of life (according to Hinduism), dharma denotes behavior consonant with the "right path" that makes life and the Universe possible.]

She sang a bhajan, a poem by the great Surdas: "I am as the Lord has made me; it is not my fault that I sell myself for money…" Further it sang that the worldly will not touch one whose virtue is great. Allegorically, in the words of this song, the woman was asking why he — a sannyasi [footnote 55] — was afraid of her. Vivekananda was moved to tears. Falling to his knees, he touched the feet of the woman: "Ma, please forgive me and bless me!" She did so. After this small episode the life of Vivekananda changed greatly, and today he is venerated as a legendary personality.

The Guru added then: "The blessing of a prostitute differs greatly from the blessing of spiritual leaders, who today are more politicians than saints… Prostitutes are nobler and more honest."

Another story heard in the uncle's house was connected with

In the name of Trailanga Swami, the famous saint of Benares and a friend of Lahiri Mahasaya⁵⁶. Once, when a devastating flood struck Benares, the inhabitants feared that the fury of the Ganga would wash the eternal city from the face of the earth. The King of Benares personally went to Trailanga Swami⁵⁷, who was regarded as a living incarnation of Shiva. The saint said: “Find a woman who has never once thought of a man. She must utter these words: ‘I, who have never desired a man, beg you, O Mother Ganga, to calm your wrath!’” – and this was to stop the flood. The matter was not about purity of thought, but about absolute honesty.

The ruler of Benares announced a search for a woman willing to undertake this mission, but no volunteers came forward. Such an act could have consequences: at the slightest shadow of doubt, the incantation might fail, and the night-butterflies value their reputation. In despair, the ruler of Benares again turned to Trailanga Swami, who promised to assist in the search personally. Near the Chaukh bazaar, in the red-light district, lived a very old representative of the most ancient profession. Trailanga Swami told her of the king’s request, and the old woman wept: “I am only a randi, a prostitute… How can I take such responsibility upon myself…”. Trailanga Swami insisted: “Mother, I beg you, help the city!”. The old woman was carried to the river in a palanquin, and for some time she stood in silence, gazing at the turbulent waters the color of tea with milk. At that time she was over ninety years old; she was not as ancient as this river and this city, but she had lived long enough to know her fate.

Addressing the river, she slowly spoke the necessary words – and the water began to recede, as if a giant plug had been pulled somewhere on the riverbed. The old woman smoothed the folds of her sari, then sank to the ground. She was dead. The mission had been risky from the very beginning, yet the life of a prostitute concerned no one greatly. The incantation had worked, and the Shakti dwelling in a fragile female body had been able to calm the fury of the great river. In a body that had been bought so many times dwelt a soul possessing a special power. If a prostitute truly curses someone, lifting the curse is practically impossible; it will have a long-term effect. So too with a blessing. Only those who know the true face of humanity – the rejected, the despised, the persecuted – are capable of the most powerful emotions. The sincere feelings of the so-called “fallen” are like a lightning strike.

At some point, his uncle helped Shraddhanand Brahmachari rent a room in a neighborhood where mostly people of the lower castes lived. Shraddhanand Brahmachari found work in a pharmacy. In that area, he was a white crow and kept to the shadows, trying not to stand out. But one day he was visited by a friend, a sannyasi and musician. It is unknown what came into Shraddhanand’s mind that day, but he asked: “Swamiji, play the harmonium, and I will dance.” When the sannyasi began to dance… let us say, it attracted unwanted attention. People gathered around and stared at them as if they were strippers dancing around a pole.

The next day, when Shraddhanand went to the latrine (the common facilities in that house were literally shared), the neighbors began to mock him: “What are you, a eunuch? You dance like a girl!”. They even tried to tear off his clothes… Enraged, he began to recite an anushthan, a tantric curse involving the Maran mantra – an invocation of death. Within a week, the unfortunate jokers died the same terrible death: in the latrine, they would begin to bleed uncontrollably. All eight men died, one after another. Learning of this, his uncle understood the source and cause of the mass deaths and went to admonish Shraddhanand Brahmachari: “You are a sannyasi, you must forgive people. This is unworthy of you, please do not do this…”. Perhaps the pleas worked, for the deaths in the house ceased. But Shraddhanand Brahmachari himself fell gravely ill.

One day, a cousin who was bringing food to the sick man took him along. The completely naked sannyasi lay on the bed, unable even to cover himself with a sheet. Wherever the cloth touched his skin, Shraddhanand Brahmachari was pierced by a terrible pain, as if he were being stung by scorpions. It was unbearable to witness this agony, yet the doctors were powerless and could not alleviate the patient’s suffering. There were no blisters or bites on his body, but at the slightest touch the poor man screamed in pain. This continued for two or three months, until Shraddhanand Brahmachari died; towards the end, he could not even cry. Perhaps this was the price for those deaths.

…Having begun his spiritual journey, he gradually learned that true tantric rituals should never be used to cause harm. The red-hot sword of Tantra was to be kept in the icy sheath of a controlled mind, for these powers could provoke uncontrollable anger. Curses returned like a boomerang to those who sent them, bringing only misfortune. But greed and hatred usually make people reckless. Despite India being a land of dharma, it is full of those who use abhichara, black magic, as if they had never heard of the laws of karma. Of course, tantriks who perform rituals for money bear responsibility for the harm they intentionally cause. But their clients, those who order the rituals, take upon themselves an even greater responsibility by initiating such rites. Tantra was always intended for the wise. Using this knowledge for evil purposes guarantees the inevitable tasting of the ripe fruits of karma.

No special initiation was required to understand these laws; one only needed attention to the flow of life around him. Despite his young age, he grasped the art of discrimination quite quickly.

**Brother Hanuman**

He and Lalmani Sharma were college friends. Both were fifteen years old, and they could talk for hours, mostly about gods, myths, and history. Once, Lalmani mentioned that his father had seen Hanuman with his own eyes. This was so intriguing that he insisted on meeting Mr. Sharma, and one day he came to visit them, traveling a distance of fifteen kilometers.

Lalmani’s father, a retired officer, received him warmly. After an exchange of pleasantries, he politely inquired about the “incident with Hanuman,” and Mr. Sharma began his story. When he was serving in the state of Madhya Pradesh, in Sagar, his children were taken to school by horse-drawn cart. Every day, a tongawalla driver would pick them up and bring them back from school, and everyone called this man “Master.” It was known that he worshipped Hanuman, but he did not flaunt it; everyone simply knew. Lalmani’s father was a staunch atheist and a critic of all religions. Every time Master brought the children home, Officer Sharma would treat him to tea accompanied by an anti-religious lecture. For several hours, he would try to convince Master that his faith was mere ignorance: “Only a complete idiot worships a monkey; there is no logic in it…”.

Master listened patiently, sipping his tea, never attempting to argue with the speaker. This ritual continued for almost two years. When the time came for a transfer, and Lalmani’s father was preparing to leave the city, Master suddenly addressed him: “Sir, you constantly criticize the Deity I worship. Would you believe in His existence if you saw Him with your own eyes?”.

Mr. Sharma laughed: “That is impossible, because it is merely a game of your inflamed imagination.”

“Please, join us on the day of the full moon. Together with two other devotees, we will invoke Hanuman. If He decides to appear, you will see for yourself. But there is one condition – you must observe silence…”.

Considering the upcoming expedition a prank, Mr. Sharma accepted the invitation. On the appointed day, their small group set off for the mountains, taking about ten kilograms of sweets, flowers, and other ritual supplies. Stopping at an unremarkable spot, Mr. Sharma’s companions began to prepare the ritual site, while he sat nearby, absently gazing at the hills. The trip was beginning to seem pointless to him, although the panorama was wonderful, and a light breeze was refreshing and calming.

Cow dung was arranged in a square shape, upon which they laid a chowkpurna⁵⁸ with colored patterns, and placed sweets and flowers on top. When everything was ready, Master said: “Now sit quietly; I will call Dada.” That was what he called Hanuman – “elder brother.”

The ceremony had been going on for about half an hour when suddenly a flash of light blinded them: right in the center of the ritual altar stood Hanuman. A powerful, tall figure radiated a bright white neon light, but the features of the deity were indistinguishable due to the radiance. Clutching a mace, Hanuman addressed Master: “What do you want?”

“Dada, forgive me for disturbing you, but these devotees of yours have some problems. One of them has a son who has been mentally retarded since birth, and the other is completely childless…”

“From this moment, the boy will begin to improve. The other will have two children.” Hanuman’s voice came from all directions simultaneously, as if echoing off the walls of an invisible tin dome. It was impossible to determine the timbre of the voice, or whether it was male or female. Despite his astonishment, Mr. Sharma was still convinced that what was happening was a staged performance. He exclaimed: “I have a question! If such powerful gods as you exist, then why is there so much suffering in the world?”

Hanuman replied: “People reap the results of their own karmas; Gods never interfere in the karmic cycles of men. Your affairs do not interest us.” Evidently, some exceptions were made, for Hanuman himself had just bestowed his grace upon the devotees.

Master whispered to Mr. Sharma to be silent, and thanked Hanuman: “Dada, we are so grateful that you have appeared. Please continue to show us your grace and grant us your blessings, and appear again when I call you.”

A sound like a clap was heard, as if caused by a sharp gust of wind rushing over their heads, and along with this “supersonic” effect, all traces of the ritual offerings (the sweets, flowers, cow dung) vanished, as did the tall figure in the center of the improvised altar.

They returned to the city in silence. What he had seen did not convince Mr. Sharma, and the next day he returned to the scene with a magnifying glass, but found nothing of interest.

Some time later, he was transferred to another city. His eyesight began to deteriorate rapidly, and he had to wear glasses with thick lenses, but even with them he was forced to bring objects close to his eyes to see them. Soon he had to retire.

Perhaps that night in the mountains he was exposed to energies that were too strong, or the loss of sight was the result of breaking the prescribed silence. Or perhaps it was simply a hereditary predisposition, without any mysticism.

---
⁵⁶ Lahiri Mahasaya (Shyama Charan Lahiri, or Yogiraj Lahiri Mahasaya) – the first known disciple of Mahavatar Babaji, who spread the science of this ancient yoga among householders, and the Second Guru in the line of succession. Born 30.09.1828, left his body 26.09.1895.
⁵⁷ Trailanga Swami (also Tailang Swami, Telang Swami) – a yogi and mystic famed for spiritual abilities. His monastic name was Swami Ganapati Saraswati. He lived in Varanasi, presumably from 1607 to 1887. He is still considered a legendary figure in Bengal. There are numerous stories about his yogic abilities and longevity. It is believed that Trailanga Swami lived for 280 years and was an incarnation of Shiva. Sri Ramakrishna called him “the walking Shiva of Varanasi.”
⁵⁸ Chowkpurna – a folk art practiced in Punjab, Haryana, and some parts of Uttar Pradesh. The term chowkpurna refers to the decoration of the floor with various designs using flour and rice; motifs and symbolic geometric patterns include lines, dots, squares, circles, triangles. The same is done at the site of certain rituals.

After what had happened, Mr. Sharma felt no spiritual transformations and still did not believe in the existence of divine forces. Such thoughts would have been an admission of something unknown and could have destroyed the carefully constructed and finely tuned system of his beliefs and life coordinates.

And yet, after retiring, Mr. Sharma began to experience strange waves of nostalgia. Sometimes he felt the need to speak with the Master again. No, he was not going to discuss religion; his attitude toward it had not softened. He simply wanted to drink tea with the coachman again and converse on abstract topics.

Arriving in Sagar, he made inquiries. It turned out that both the Master and those two devotees had died. But the mental development of one of their sons had improved significantly, and the childless couple had given birth to two children, a boy and a girl.

For some time, Mr. Sharma sat at a tea kiosk, watching the bustling movement of pedestrians and cars. It occurred to him that the relationship between the Master and Hanuman had been quite close, if a simple coachman called the Deity "elder brother," and He responded to his invitations.

…Telling this story, Father Lalmani did not sound skeptical at all, nor did he try to append any moral lesson. In his narration, there were notes of gentle sadness, as if he were trying to recall the vision of a royal firebird that had flown over him without dropping a single magical feather.

It was time to return to the city. The road was bumpy; the bicycle wove between potholes. Father Lalmani's story had stirred him: the Master was a simple man, but his devotion proved unshakable. What nourished his faith? Can the wave of a mortal man's love reach the gods? And why did they respond, if people are indifferent to them? Is it possible to blindly believe in something without seeing the object of faith? The saying "seeing is believing" had always seemed one hundred percent true to him.

Then another thought visited him: having seen manifested divinity, one must make a leap of faith. If a miracle is near and can be known, one cannot remain blind. To comprehend the incomprehensible, there must be sufficient awareness and strength of desire. Thus he understood: faith requires courage and a readiness to submit to higher forces.

When Hanuman appeared before him years later, his mind was absolutely empty but focused. It is unlikely that anyone's stories can come to mind at such moments, and he did not try to compare his own impressions with Father Lalmani's story. In that moment, the story of his own life was being told; his beliefs and faith were being put to the test.

True faith is like a lightning strike, and after the initial shock passes, it is impossible to ignore it.

**On the Brink of a Military Career**

By about the age of sixteen, the idea of a military career began to seem attractive to him. Perhaps because he was the best marksman in college, was in excellent physical shape, and was much stronger than many of his classmates. Or perhaps because of the appeal of the military uniform, which had always been associated for him with order, fortitude, resolve, and stability. His parents did not pressure him in making the decision, but the very fact that there were already stories of successful military careers in the family was a convincing argument.

He had no other ideas about the future. In 1973, he joined the National Cadet Corps, the youth wing of the Indian armed forces; in October '74, he attended a training camp. College students were invited to such events for the preliminary selection of recruits for army positions, and he enthusiastically joined the selection process.

The following year, while pursuing a Bachelor's degree in Military Science, Economics, and Geography at Maharani Lakshmi Bai College, he completed another training camp and made it onto the Gwalior shooting team. His grandfather's genes undoubtedly helped: he took first place and was selected to participate in the Republic Day parade, where, together with the best cadets of India, he was to represent the state of Madhya Pradesh. Preparation for the event was conducted in a camp on the grounds of a garrison near Palam Airport, in Delhi. Aware of the seriousness of what was happening, he approached the training with extreme responsibility and took second place in the national competitions for marching and shooting. Life in the barracks and constant drill training were exhausting, but the excitement and enthusiasm of the other cadets were contagious.

"Beating Retreat"⁵⁹ – the official conclusion of the Republic Day celebrations – was observed on January 29, 1976. The arrival of the President of India with his bodyguards was marked by the trumpet sounds of fanfares, units presented arms, the national anthem was played, flags were unfurled. Hundreds of cadets marched before the stands, and it was a truly momentous day. Playing in unison, the army and navy bands marched, their movements forming intricate patterns. He could not see all of this from his column, but the sequence was known to everyone.

The winter sun was mercifully mild, and although they had been roused before dawn, the solemnity of the event compensated for the lack of sleep. Army bands took their positions before the presidential box, the drummers performed solos, the final drumroll sounded, a long bugle call greeted the sunset. The flags slowly descended, the ceremony concluded. Departing, the cadet units marched through Raisina Hills, past the brightly lit parliament building. Exhausted, he slept that night without dreams.

The next day, the best cadets were invited to the former Viceroy's Palace for tea, and it was wonderful! The largest residence of a head of state in the world, the presidential palace with 340 rooms, occupied 30 hectares, and it had everything: lush gardens, stables, and countless lawns. The tea with the President itself was formal, but the atmosphere was magnificent. The scale of the celebration, the presence of important state officials, inspired him and confirmed the correctness of his choice. Yes, he would become an officer of the Indian Army and serve his country!

The awarded 'C' certificate of the National Cadet Corps entitled him to appear before the Service Selection Board, and he submitted an application. Moreover, there was no need to take written exams; it was enough to pass a five-day selection process. If the outcome was positive, the candidate was granted the rank of Second Lieutenant. But the selection system was rigorous; candidates had to undergo additional training conducted in three stages. He applied for a month-long training at the Kamptee camp, near Nagpur in Maharashtra. There, among other cadets, he met a young sardar⁶⁰ named Harpal Singh Siyal, and they quickly became friends. His new acquaintance was an excellent conversationalist and as well-read as he was. It was great to discuss various interesting topics not related to the upcoming exams. In a conversation about the ideas of Vivekananda, Harpal mentioned yoga a couple of times, and then told him about *Autobiography of a Yogi*. "Sharma, you must read this, it's amazing! I guarantee you'll like the book." Being a bookworm, he noted this information to himself.

The final exams were held in Allahabad, and in November 1977 he went there. Over the following five days, candidates underwent complex psychological, physical, and motivational tests – the level of stress and anxiety was off the charts. The final test was an informal personal interview. According to rumors, this was the main one: the impression made by the candidates was as important as the scores obtained.

…The sense of humor of the high-ranking army officer conducting the interview was very peculiar. Or perhaps it was worldly wisdom; it's hard to say. One of the questions was simple, but even years later he did not know the answer.

"We have three people: a blind man, a mute, and a deaf man. One day, the mute saw the deaf man kissing the blind man's wife. He went to the blind man to tell him what was happening. Now tell me, how did the mute manage to inform the blind man about what had happened?"

The recruits offered various options, but none fit. Perhaps the correct answer simply did not exist. Still, he thought of a few convoluted ways to solve this puzzle: if the mute really wanted to ruin the deaf man's life, it would be enough for him to draw what had happened, and the blind man could read the sketch with his fingertips. But the probability of misinterpretation was high. And so he decided to let the fictitious illicit affair between the deaf man and the blind man's wife take its course, leaving the mute to helplessly watch the adulterers. He glanced at the examiner: it was quite possible he was just amusing himself, or that the test had no exact answer, only a multitude of probabilities.

Another question sounded less strange, but was also quite unusual:

– You hear a girl's cries for help. What are your actions?
– I will go to find out what is happening.
– Two hooligans are bothering her.
– I will try to protect her!
– Why, who is she to you?
– She is my sister; I will protect her, I will try to fight off the attackers. And I will tell her to run and call for help.
– And if she simply runs away and doesn't come back with help, then what?

There were many possible answers; he had nothing to lose, and he replied: "Then the fight will end, because it's a fight over the girl, and since she's no longer there, there's no reason to fight." The interview was over. Whether his answer satisfied the examiner remained a mystery, although he had no doubt in himself. Overall, the selection process and the strictness of army discipline awakened anxiety in him and left a heavy impression. Secretly, he wondered whether a military career was the right choice. Life in the barracks got on his nerves, not to mention the 4 a.m. reveille and daily shaving, yet he reproached himself for thoughts unbefitting a future officer. In any case, the exams were over, and backing out now would be cowardice. The final results came faster than expected: he had successfully passed all the tests and was recommended for the rank of Second Lieutenant. The future was inevitable, only now it seemed not a promise, but a sentence.

**Death, Wedding, and the Beginning of the Search**

The house greeted him with a hollow silence: everyone had gone to Mumbai. The servants averted their eyes; no one wanted to be the first to deliver bad news. Standing in the empty living room, he nevertheless heard the message that his elder brother Mahendra had died of a heart attack. His relatives decided that such news might greatly upset him during his difficult exams and chose not to inform him. His brother's death was a crushing blow.

By the age of 32, Mahendra was the pride of the entire family; great hopes were placed on him. Handsome and successful, he was married to an aristocrat, the princess of Jamnagar, Kumari Harshad Kumari; they had a daughter, and everything was simply wonderful. By 1977, Mahendra had starred in leading roles in several Bollywood films and had taken the stage name "Raul." But sometimes, suddenly acquired money and fame become an unbearable trial. His brother became addicted to expensive alcohol and other pleasures of the rich and famous, and the chief tax collector soon knocked on his door. Apparently, to collect the payment for a wild social life that ended so abruptly, right in the middle of one of those famous glamorous and noisy Bombay parties.

According to Hindu customs, the memorial ceremony...

The funeral rites for the deceased are held on the thirteenth day after death. All relatives must participate in the final rituals to ensure the soul’s safe passage to the other world, a favorable rebirth, or moksha⁶¹ – liberation. He had always thought that nothing was more important or sacred than the duty of bidding farewell to a loved one, yet this family tragedy was pushed into the background by another event, and no one but him seemed to see a problem in this.

His uncle refused to cancel his son’s wedding, which was scheduled for the same day as Mahendra’s final rites. The money for the celebration had already been spent; the show had to go on. He was home alone when his older cousin came to pick him up. Impatiently and insistently urging the importance of fulfilling familial duty, his cousin almost forcibly shoved him into the car. The demand to attend the wedding seemed insulting to him, but trying to explain the monstrous incongruity between his emotional state and attending the upcoming festivities was futile. A multitude of relatives had gathered for the wedding, which struck him as a grotesque violation of the sacred tradition of farewell. The wedding felt more like a celebration of his brother’s untimely death. The burning grotesqueness of the situation was unbearable: the obligatory attendance at his cousin’s wedding, the impossibility of mourning his elder brother… Surrounded by smiles and laughter, deafened by the noise of the lavish wedding, choking on the aromas of the dishes and the smell of heated bodies, he fell into deep contemplation. Gradually, his sorrow transformed into an awareness: all these bustling relatives were simply envious of his father’s status and his brother’s success. Well-hidden envy had slumbered beneath a thin film of prescribed, measured familial friendliness, but now it had been shamelessly torn away. Of course, the decision to throw a wedding banquet despite the family tragedy had a purely practical aspect. In the years to come, he would learn much about the commercial side of family relationships and the influence of money on human sincerity during mourning.

A month later, the uncle who had celebrated his son’s wedding with such pomp suddenly died of a heart attack. Another month passed, and another uncle, a successful lawyer who had once taken him on a tour of a prison, fell into a diabetic coma and died. Then the list of deceased relatives was joined by an ancient widowed aunt. He could not shake the thought that disrespect for the dead could bring nothing but misfortune. The festive atmosphere of the wedding evaporated like the scent of camphor, and the acrid taste of death and the pain of loss were now felt by many of those who had so recently rejoiced at the celebration.

Numbness gave way to a cold, restrained anger. No, he was not an orthodox believer, but he considered the final rites sacred. The relatives’ lack of basic human compassion and respect for a sudden tragedy had forever changed his understanding of duty, honor, and blood ties. The succession of deaths gave rise to a particular kind of reflection within him. Continuously pondering the nature of death and the meaning of life, he knew: the questions he was asking were called “existential,” and finding answers to them was difficult. And yet he struggled to understand what death was and what happened after it. Perhaps, to do so, he first needed to understand the paradox of life: why people come into this world, what the soul is, and where it resides before the body’s birth. Intuitively, he knew that answers could only come from within and that the very act of life contained all the necessary clues. However, gradual learning did not guarantee a diploma; only an express course could answer the questions. In the years that followed, yoga removed the useless lenses of illusion, doubt, and uncertainty; he came to know death and, understanding its meaning, began to doubt its inevitability. The discovery of the meaning of the eternal sowing and reaping of human souls made him think about the origin of spirit, not God.

For now, he continued turning the pages of his beloved books, returning them to the shelves. The wisdom of books did not answer the fundamental questions, as the authors specialized in individual aspects of existence, and no one was capable of describing the entire picture of the world.

*Autobiography of a Yogi*

The need to find answers overwhelmed him to such an extent that the decision to abandon the prospects of a military career came of its own accord. He announced that he could not cut his hair short, as required by the army, because he did not wish to part with his long hair. This was the only explanation he could think of. Articulating his inner turmoil and sharing his true thoughts and feelings with anyone seemed impossible. In the seventies (and even earlier), it was widely believed that a young man of his age simply could not be preoccupied with questions about the meaning of life.

His relatives were utterly astonished by this explanation. Everyone decided that he had lost his mind from grief over his brother’s death or was suffering from unrequited love. After all, there had to be a valid pretext for refusing a promising and stable career! He refused to go into details, and they stopped bothering him with questions.

Monotonous and dreary days dragged on. At some point, he remembered a book recommended to him by a young Sikh⁶² at the training camp. He later learned that Harpal Singh Siyal had moved to Great Britain, and they never met again; yet some seemingly chance encounters can change everything, including one’s destiny. Consciously or not, Harpal had delivered a message that changed his life. In December 1978, in a dusty little bookshop, he paid 16 rupees for *Autobiography of a Yogi*. And he read the book in one breath, overnight.

…Looking at the photographs of Lahiri Mahasaya and Ashutosh Chatterjee, who taught Yogananda Sanskrit, and at the image of Babaji, he wondered why all these faces seemed so familiar, as if he had met these people before. The names and events mentioned in the book echoed in his memory; the description of the spiritual search, the acquisition of the teaching, and the subsequent realization resonated with his own emotions and thoughts. Upon reaching the description of Kriya (rather vague, but conveying the general meaning), he understood with ringing clarity: this was the yoga to which he must dedicate his life. The thought was like an explosion, and the decision became irrevocable. From that moment on, he would practice only Kriya Yoga and nothing else. The people mentioned in the book had already walked this path, and he would become a Traveler, following in their footsteps. The author also touched upon immortality in the book. This astonishing theme was only hinted at in passing, but it ignited his imagination.

The Master of the tradition, the immortal Babaji, had communicated with Lahiri Mahasaya relatively recently, in 1861 – and this was the last of all known contacts between mortal men and eternal beings.

Yogananda’s teacher, Yukteswar Giri, appeared to his beloved disciple after death, and Yogananda firmly asserted that their communication was not a hallucination or a vision. Yukteswar’s testimony about life after death, about the ability to dissolve into atoms and restore the body anew, was vivid and convincing. At first, the theme of eternal life was not of paramount importance, but in the years that followed, he returned to the book’s text again and again. It seemed to contain a hidden message.

…Few heroes of Indian mythology had returned from the realm of the dead with such detailed descriptions as Sri Yukteswar. The story of a yogi who ascended into the astral world, moving between different universes, seemed absolutely authentic. For him personally, the story of Yukteswar Giri was proof of the truth of the Teaching. Kriya Yoga is a powerful instrument of knowledge, a science that leads beyond the ordinary into the realms of higher knowledge. Its concept had nothing to do with escapism, with an attempt to flee irritating daily life into an imaginary world. He also hoped that the practice would help dissolve the deep feeling of sorrow that had settled in his heart. Though still unexplored, this yoga could become his Guide, and he also thought of it as armor, the necessary protection on the battlefield of Life.

In the absence of any information about the technique itself, he decided to perform a thousand Kriyas each day until he attained samadhi. The goal was set, and it brought immediate relief. He needed to begin the search for a guru who could teach him all the practices so vaguely mentioned in the book.

Such a search is always accompanied by anxiety and doubt: will the seeker find a true guide, will he follow the right path? People can spend years searching for a teacher and never find one. Some sink into despair, or worse – into the ordinariness of so-called normal life.

Humanity possesses an incredible and astonishing ability to pursue abstract yet entirely real goals. Every living being strives to prolong its existence, no matter how painful the process of life itself may be. The bitterness of any failure has the taste of life, not death. Even a fiasco leaves unforgettable marks on the maps of destiny. All great adventures begin with curiosity, and the length and purpose of the journey depend on will, motivation, and imagination. Curiosity spurred Alice to follow the White Rabbit – and led her into the altered reality of Wonderland. He set out on his journey, holding only a book in his hands, but in it was described the highest triumph ever achieved by man: the yogic art of conquering Death through Life.

**The Hermit of Mount Amara**

A year had passed since he graduated from the institute. Obtaining a master’s degree in political science changed nothing; his only interest was yoga. The relentless noise of the city and the endless bustle of the streets only stirred in him a longing for solitude. He became extremely selective in choosing company, avoiding both his peers and adults.

In the summer of 1979, whenever the clouds provided even a semblance of coolness, he and his friend and neighbor, Padam Upadhyay, would go into the mountains. Absorbing the landscapes and the peace of the hills, he felt free and almost happy during these short expeditions. Taking a few flatbreads and filling his army canteen with water, they enjoyed the breathtaking views of Gwalior from the summit of Mount Amara. That day, they climbed for almost two and a half hours until the clouds finally began to disperse. Noon was approaching; they stopped to rest and ate unhurriedly, savoring the taste of fresh bread. Padam noticed something strange: small white crabs were scurrying around them. Everywhere, as if on a seashore, the sun-bleached remains of their shells were scattered, and it was completely unclear what it all meant. A trickle of water seeped from a crevice in the rock, but the ground was dry and scorched. The heat was growing more intense; it was time to go back.

---

⁶¹ Moksha – liberation, freedom. In yoga, the ultimate goal of practice, liberation from the cycle of births and deaths.

⁶² Sikhs – followers of Sikhism, a religion that originated in the 15th century in Punjab.

Descending along the rocky bed of a dried-up stream, they saw a small stone overhang jutting out above a barely visible path. Beneath the overhang stood a large trishul⁶³, marked with red sindur paste⁶⁴, and a dhuni⁶⁵ with fresh coals—a sure sign that a sadhu lived nearby. Continuing along the narrowing trail, they noticed another stone overhang with a wall built against it, constructed from large, uneven stones. The whole structure formed something like a shelter adjoining the stone body of the mountain. There was also a small door there. Intrigued, they tried to peer inside: they pushed the door and stepped into the darkness. Inside, they saw a hermit, gazing impassively at the newcomers; his black eyes shone from beneath bushy white eyebrows. Naked and lean, he wore only a loincloth, his dark body glistening in the dim light; his gray hair and beard were tied into knots, and his large mustache reached his ears. The ascetic’s legs were folded in padmasana⁶⁶, and his arms rested on the floor along his body. Astonished by what they saw, they struggled with awkwardness; he felt a pang of fear. And it was from fear, or perhaps in spite of it, that he politely addressed the seated figure: “Baba, we are thirsty, please give us water.”

The sadhu did not rise. Instead, with his legs still folded in padmasana, he lifted himself on his hands and took two “steps” toward a water pot. Filling a small, shiny brass cup on a long handle with water, the ascetic extended it, indicating that one should drink without touching the rim. He hastily drank some water and thanked the host: “Thank you, baba, we are leaving now…”

Remaining in the lotus posture, the yogi continued to silently watch the “guests,” and they hurried to leave. Though tired, the friends descended toward the highway almost at a run, and soon were sitting in a bus heading to the city.

They did not speak of the strange encounter; there was little to discuss, yet for some reason he kept thinking about this yogi. A couple of months later, they repeated the expedition, deciding this time to go even further. Passing the previous resting place, Padam Upadhyaya pointed out the same shells of white crabs, and they turned onto the path leading to the sadhu’s dwelling. The overhanging rocky ledge was in place, but beneath it there was no trace of the trishul or dhuni. Going further, they expected to see the improvised dwelling with the stone wall and door under the second overhang, but there was nothing there either, except for the vast stone flank of the cliff. Bewildered, they walked up and down the trail several times, finding no traces of the dwelling whatsoever.

Even years later, he did not know what to think of this strange story: was it merely a wandering ascetic, or Siddha Amara Guru himself? The local people believed that the immortal sage Amara still lived on the mountain. In any case, the identity of the ascetic with the shining eyes remained a mystery, yet he could not shake the feeling that the sip of water he had taken in the sadhu’s dwelling had produced some strange effect. His subconscious began to prevail over his logical mind, his intuition sharpened, and the urge to find a spiritual path was nearly driving him mad. It was impossible to guess what exactly lay ahead, but the premonition of approaching change became distinct and acute.

**The Valor of a Kshatriya, the Power of a Brahmin.**
**The Instruction of Anandamayi Ma**

Several more months passed; he still knew no one in Gwalior who could share practical information about Kriya Yoga. In June, passing by a huge gate that resembled the entrance to a palace, he noticed a sign reading “Kriya Yoga Group” and went in to inquire. It turned out to be more of an interest club than an educational program. The group, founded by Siddhoji Rao S., a young and highly educated descendant of the Rajput dynasty of the Sisodia royal clan, was open to all interested in yoga and philosophy. At last, he had found a place where he could freely discuss yoga with like-minded people, without fear of ridicule. So he began to visit the estate frequently, and the group became a long-awaited outlet for him.

Most of the time, Siddhoji Rao was in Pune; they managed to meet only after some time. But their friendship flared up instantly, despite the fact that S.R.S. was 13 years older. The new friend’s family belonged to the aristocratic Kshatriya lineage, tracing its genealogy back to the Solar Dynasty of Gohil-Sisodia; they were also related to the Maharaja of Gwalior. Their families were acquainted, and they joyfully discovered numerous connections and mutual friends. But what primarily brought them together was yoga and a thirst for spiritual discovery.

The endless, echoing halls of the vast estate and the spacious inner courtyard were entirely at their disposal. The members of the Kriya group practiced bhastrika⁶⁷ breathing, then moved to another hall to meditate. Even when S.R.S. was in Pune, some group members continued to practice at the estate, and he too was given keys. Now he could enjoy solitude in the palace—an ideal refuge from the bustle of city life.

Siddhoji was charismatic and charming; with his dazzling smile, black beard, and lion’s mane of hair, he strongly resembled Shivaji Bhonsale, the legendary warrior-king of Maharashtra. The visual resemblance was heightened by his clothing, as Siddhoji wore the same simple white kurta and trousers as the famous hero. Having inherited a vast library from his father and grandfather, S.R.S. was an insatiable reader. A shared passion for reading cemented their friendship, as both loved good books. Often, the subject of lively discussions was Kriya Yoga and the life of Yogananda; S.R.S. knew the *Autobiography of a Yogi* almost by heart, literally down to the line.

Every time he returned to Gwalior, S.R.S. would go straight from the train to his younger friend’s house, and they would talk for hours over tea. From yoga discussions, they would move on to the nature of the aura and the origin of astral beings, debate the hierarchy of ghosts, the levels and types of the afterlife. The conversations often continued long past midnight, and S.R.S. would stay overnight.

Both friends needed an interlocutor of equal intellect; they were extremely well-read and genuinely enjoyed each other’s company. Sometimes S.R.S. would pick him up on his scooter, and they would head to the hills, wandering there until dark, continuing their endless conversations…

The old palace of the Gwalior rulers, Jai Vilas, was another mystical backdrop for their nocturnal expeditions. They would swim for hours in the ancient reservoir, Ganga Sagar Baori, carefully avoiding water snakes, gazing at the starry sky; the moon was reflected in the surface of the dark water, and they drifted, lying on their backs in lotus pose…

S.R.S. was engaged in a property dispute with his uncle. One day, the uncle simply locked the passage to another part of the complex. Upon returning from Pune, S.R.S. found the doors closed; he could not locate his uncle, and no one answered the door or the phone. So S.R.S. turned to him and a mutual acquaintance, Mr. Padam, for help. Before this incident, S.R.S. had often spoken about the valor of the Kshatriyas, and this topic came up again during their unsuccessful attempts to break the locks with a heavy hammer. Taking the tool from S.R.S.’s hands, he exclaimed: “To the aid of Kshatriya valor comes the power of the Brahmin!”—and with one blow, shattered the lock. The palace was theirs again, and the Kriya yoga group could resume its sessions.

Remaining diligent students of Life, both sought to explore and test the boundaries of reality. Purpose and destiny sprouted from the same root. Analyzing the path traversed, one could glimpse destiny, but measuring the exact distance to the goal was impossible.

Stepping a little ahead, it is worth noting: over the years, both became renowned yoga masters, wrote books, and guided spiritual seekers. S.R.S. remained the same charismatic conversationalist and gifted orator, though his coal-black hair and beard had turned snow-white. Over time, the topics they discussed transformed into personal experience; answers came through persistent practice. In the end, their life stories proved that strong intentions indeed determine the future; everything depends on motivation and resolve.

…On July 11, 1979, one of the members of the Kriya group was conducting a fire ceremony, a havan, and he was among the invited. The ritual began around five in the evening; handfuls of grain were thrown into the crackling fire, ghee was poured, and the exclamations of “Svaha!” echoed from the walls of the inner courtyard. That day, he sat in meditation for the first time. Closing his eyes and concentrating on the point between his eyebrows, he fell into the Void within.
