At first, there was an almost physical burning sensation in the space between his brows, and then, somehow, everything he was became concentrated there. Thoughts and sensations ceased.

About an hour later, the ritual ended, and someone shook him by the shoulders, bringing him out of his trance. The astonishing depth and power of the silence of his own inner space and the secrets stored there were overwhelming. With piercing clarity, he understood: self-inquiry would become the meaning of his entire life; nothing else simply deserved attention. If Yogananda’s book had been the starting point, awakening curiosity, then meditation was the moment that changed the direction of movement, a confirmation of purpose—not just a sign, but a command to action.

The hot month of July was drawing to a close when he heard that Anandamayi Ma⁶⁸ was to come to Vrindavan. The famous saint, mentioned in the *Autobiography of a Yogi*, was warmly disposed toward the masters of Yogananda’s lineage, and he burned with the desire to absorb even a hint of the Divine that was felt in her presence.

Only a few souls among the billions born on Earth are born as siddhas. Gifted with supreme knowledge, these beings come to bless others with their presence and have no need for spiritual education or initiation. Anandamayi Ma, born in a state of omniscience, was one such soul.

Together with acquaintances from the Kriya Yoga group, Dr. D. P. Arora and the doctor’s relatives, Parampadya and Pachori, they decided to go to Vrindavan in the hope of obtaining an audience with the saint. Reaching Mathura by train, they hired a horse-drawn carriage to Vrindavan. A light drizzling rain temporarily cooled the heat, making the journey quite pleasant, and they arrived at the ashram in high spirits.

Introducing themselves at the ashram gate as followers of Kriya Yoga in the lineage of Yogananda, they asked permission to see the Mother. The wait took about two hours, which was a mere trifle given the countless number of people trying to see the saint. Finally, they were led into a small room on the first floor. Anandamayi Ma was sitting on a simple wooden bed; on either side of her stood two female sannyasis. In a simple white sari, with shining eyes and dark hair, the saint was still strikingly beautiful, though she was over eighty. The room was filled with distinctly palpable peaceful vibrations. Entering, they all bowed; they had been warned beforehand not to touch Ma’s feet. As he bowed in pranam, he blushed deeply; unexpectedly, Anandamayi Ma touched his head, blessing him, and then took walnuts, raisins, and apples from a box in front of her and handed them to him. Those around

Those present fell silent: he had received *prasad* directly from Ma… At that time, he did not yet know that whenever Anandamayi Ma visited Benares, she would invite his future Teacher and generously treat him to food she had prepared herself…

Being the youngest in the room, he remained silent, and only Dr. Arora dared to break the silence of the room: “Mother, how can I achieve progress in *sadhana*⁶⁹?” – “By doing, you will succeed (*Karoge to hoga*).” And Anandamayi Ma again immersed herself in a transcendent state.

He perceived this short phrase as a personal instruction, an *Upadesh*. The words sank into his soul, and in the years that followed, he performed his practice following the saint’s advice – simply by continuing to do.

Later, many remarked on how fortunate he was to have met this fragile woman, one of the greatest saints of India. And indeed, he was a lucky one.

⁶⁹ *Sadhana* – spiritual practice or discipline, performed regularly with the aim of achieving a certain experience, and at higher stages, self-realization.

**Out of the Body**

The year 1979 proved extraordinarily rich in signs and inexplicable events. The search for a Guru continued, and in the meantime, he tried to perform the breathing exercises he had learned in the Kriya Yoga group.

The group members practiced no actual *kriya*; they were like-minded people exchanging thoughts and ideas, and the yogic techniques they performed were rather preparatory. Still, he decided to raise the breathing practice of *bhastrika* to a new level, bringing it to two thousand repetitions. He usually performed *bhastrika* before meditation; it calmed the mind, and the overall state was pleasant, and he tried to maintain it.

That December evening was no different from others. Around eleven o’clock, he finished *bhastrika* and lay down to rest. No sooner had his head touched the pillow than he felt his body rise toward the ceiling, his legs lifting into the air first. It was incredible. When the subtle sheath separated from the physical, his head also lifted from the pillow, and he saw his own motionless body beneath him. Some tiny particle of logical mind was still working. Knowing of such cases, he realized he had left his body. The sensation was frightening: he hovered near the ceiling like a balloon. The very nature of vision changed; everything entered his field of perception at once, 360 degrees around. Actually, the feeling was not pleasant: the motionless body lying below was utterly defenseless, and this vulnerability looked pitiful. Drifting slowly through the adjacent room, he found himself in the courtyard. The new vision was truly magical; every blade of grass, every leaf on the trees shone with its own aura; objects shimmered, outlined by contours of bright light. The perception of time also changed. Now it was capacious, condensed; seconds contained hours, racing by with incredible speed. Probably, the dogs on the veranda sensed him. Running up, a large male German Shepherd, Jackie, jumped onto his chest, pushing with strong paws. The dog simply passed through his astral body, and the shock thrust him back into his physical shell. And for the better.

Later, he learned that such journeys were not at all safe, as bodies left unattended could be taken over by other spirits. Seeing abandoned property, a new tenant might settle into another’s body as if it were his own home. Sometimes, as a result of such sudden possession, souls lost their bodies, remaining without maps, compasses, or knowledge of astral customs, becoming refugees, pariahs of the astral jungles. No democracy was observed in the astral realm; the only law there was the power of thought, which all inhabitants of the astral felt and acknowledged. Later, he encountered many tribes and castes of the Other Side, and all of them lived by the code of the power of consciousness. This power was both currency, weapon, and protection. Gradually, he learned the rules and geography of the new kingdom, but he was in no hurry to move there for permanent residence. The value of the body was too high to neglect it. As for the out-of-body experience itself, he preferred to think of it as a journey of consciousness in the capsule of a phantom body. This definition was more eloquent than any other term, as all those “mental bodies,” “subtle bodies,” “air bodies” only created confusion. One thing remained indisputable: he had hovered above his own shell, while his mind remained clear and focused, confirming what was happening.

After this adventure, he continued to practice two thousand *bhastrikas* every evening. This type of yoga he could perform on his own until he found a Teacher. The future was hidden, but the present was more or less manageable. The only tools for carving the contours of fate from the invisible yet tangible fabric of life were firmness of intention and one’s own body.

Miracles continued to occur without prior notice. One day, he was stunned by an extraordinary “visual effect” that would repeat only years later. After practice, covering his strained eyes with his palms, he saw a dazzlingly shining ring of solar color, as if formed by an electrical discharge. The Sun, shaded against a golden background, was alive and intelligent; the luminous sphere radiated everything of which the world was composed, and everything beyond it: the light of Life, Knowledge, eternal peace, and infinite Truth. The name of this phenomenon was unknown to him, and there was no one with whom to discuss it.

**Father, Shri Himmat Bahadur Sharma, and Mother, Shrimati Gyani Devi**

**Parents, early 1940s**

**Mother, Shrimati Gyani Devi, at age 24, in riding gear**

**Shailendra Sharma, 8 months**

**Shri Gulab Singh, paternal grandfather**

**Shailendra Sharma, 5 years**

**After college, 1980**

**Puri, 1981**

**Puri**

**Ashram**

…New acquaintances from the Kriya Yoga group often visited the Karar Ashram in Puri⁷⁰, and some even stayed there. Their stories about life in the ashram and impressions from meditation lessons gave some perspective to his vague notions of yoga. Discussions, exchanges of ideas, and regular breathing practices outlined a general direction, yet did not bring him closer to the goal. It was time to move on.

Having inquired about the ashram in more detail, in March 1981 he set off for Puri. Friends helped him buy a train ticket to Orissa, Puri, where he hoped to meet advanced yogis, study Kriya, learn about *samadhi* and everything else connected with the practice of higher yoga. His entire budget for an indefinite stay in Puri was five rupees.

At that time, the ashram was under the guidance of a disciple and successor of Yukteswar Giri, Swami Hariharananda. The enrollment procedure was quite simple: the swami initiated people into Kriya, giving a short speech about Kriya Yoga. Beginners were advised to listen to the vibrations in the *chakras*⁷¹ and visualize light, and after some time, God was supposed to make Himself known. Together with 30–40 other new initiates, he listened to the instructions – and life in the ashram began.

The teaching seemed quite simple. The basic kriya techniques were given without *pranayama*⁷². *Khechari mudra*⁷³ – (a key component of the practice) was mentioned only in passing, and the decisive role of this technique was not emphasized; practitioners were simply supposed to press their tongue to the palate. He knew nothing about *Khechari* or other aspects of the practice, and simply followed the instructions.

If the head of the ashram was not traveling, a group session was held in the evening. The initiates would sit in a row before the swami, and he would guide the meditation process. Some claimed to feel unearthly bliss; he felt absolutely nothing and was a little ashamed of this.

The indescribable feeling of omniscience, unity, true bliss experienced during his first meditation in July did not recur, no matter how much he concentrated on the *chakras* or imagined light. He persistently continued to practice, yet no discussions were held, no further explanations were given, and the rare answers to his questions were vague. Intuitively, he felt that the practices lacked essence and a certain wholeness, and he continued to insistently seek explanations. One day, the swami became angry, rebuking him: “You should meditate more!”

An inferiority complex immediately took pale root, and he began to blame himself: “I must truly be an idiot, asking stupid questions. The swami is right, I simply don’t meditate enough…”

Before arriving at the ashram, he had been convinced that all who followed the teachings of Kriya Yoga were living saints, in a state of constant *samadhi*. But after some time, an almost tragic realization visited him: the people in this ashram were merely skilled speakers with only the most general understanding of the subject. Some were simply hiding from worldly problems, masking their own issues and complexes with the search for the Divine. Disappointment and uncertainty accumulated; the chosen path began to seem like a dead end.

And yet, trying to adhere to his own schedule and counting on solitude, he began to meditate regularly in the ashram’s garden temple. One day, a familiar song from a Bollywood film playing nearby made him look outside.

“*‘Ruk jana nahi’ – do not stop if you feel defeated.
Walking on thorns, you will find the shade of spring,
This is your test of life,
Keep going, guided by the strength of your heart,
O wandering traveler…*”

In Orissa, Hindi was rarely spoken. The song was a message intended exclusively for him, and he followed the melody. When he entered a nearby travel agency, the old radio was already playing the next composition. The middle-aged Sikh owner was glad for the visitor, even if he was not a customer. They shook hands. “You have exquisite taste, listening to such a song!” – and he was immediately offered tea (brewed as they did at home), two *samosas*⁷⁴, and even a *rasgulla*⁷⁵. Later, he could not recall this man’s name, but the owner’s friendliness and the taste of the fresh *samosas* remained…

⁷⁰ Puri – a coastal city in the state of Odisha in eastern India, located on the shore of the Bay of Bengal. Also known as Shri Jagannatha Dhama after the 12th-century Jagannath Temple located in the city. One of the four main pilgrimage sites in Hinduism.
⁷¹ *Chakra* – a direct translation from Sanskrit means “wheel” or “vortex.” In yoga, chakra means an energy center. Seven main chakras are localized in the Sushumna channel. They are in a dormant state, although they can potentially be activated and “awakened” by special intensive yoga practice.
⁷² *Pranayama* – an ancient yogic practice of breathing techniques leading to the expansion and control of life energy and power, as well as to higher states of consciousness.
⁷³ *Khechari mudra* – a special yogic tongue “lock” used during certain pranayamas to stimulate the flow of amrita nectar. *Khechari mudra* is performed by curling the tongue back and inserting it into the nasal cavity.
⁷⁴ *Samosa* – a savory pastry filled with various vegetables and sometimes cheese.
⁷⁵ *Rasgulla* – a dessert in the form of a ball of cottage cheese and semolina dough, boiled in sugar syrup.

lingered in memory. The song and warmth of hospitality lifted the spirits. All of this somehow promised better days, assuring that desires would be fulfilled.

Literally the next day, changes began: someone mentioned another Kriya Yoga ashram located nearby. This was a complete surprise: so, within the same tradition, there existed other branches? It turned out that from one river many streams flow, but… do all lead to the ocean?

The Guru Dham ashram, founded by a disciple of Lahiri Mahasaya, Bhupendranath Sanyal Mahasaya, was nearby. One afternoon he walked past the Govardhan Matha founded by Shankaracharya, turned several times onto narrow streets, and entered the gates. An elderly man reading a newspaper on the veranda asked his name and the purpose of his visit. Shailendranath Mukherjee was a disciple of Bhupendranath Sanyal and a yogi, and perhaps due to the similarity of their names, he took an interest in the young visitor. Having listened attentively to the account of the Karar ashram, the elderly yogi delivered his verdict: "What you are practicing is not real Kriya."

He remained silent in bewilderment, though not particularly surprised; the words merely confirmed his own suspicions. Seeing his confusion, Shailendranath asked: "Will you believe the words of Lahiri Mahasaya himself?"

"Of course, for he is the father of Kriya Yoga…"

Mr. Mukherjee advised him to read the commentaries on the Gita written by Lahiri Mahasaya, and even gave him 110 rupees to purchase the books.

The three volumes of *The Bhagavad Gita in the Light of Kriya Yoga* had the effect of a bomb exploding. What he read illuminated, inspired, and at the same time deeply disturbed him; there was an abundance of information, and his initial enthusiasm mixed with disappointment and irritation, as it was unclear where to seek this very real Kriya. The practices described by Lahiri Mahasaya radically differed from what he had been "taught" in the ashram. Who could guarantee the authenticity of the teaching? He felt deceived.

**Under the Banyan Tree**

Apart from his personal inner turmoil, life in the ashram was quite comfortable and rather dull. Breakfast was served around 9 o'clock; after a heavy lunch, tea with snacks was taken. The midday siesta was considered a sacred affair. At 9 PM, dinner concluded the unremarkable day, and by 10 PM everyone went to sleep. Thanks to a carbohydrate-rich diet, the ashram's inhabitants were rather plump and drowsy. No one attempted to meditate at night, and why would they?

Having established his own schedule, he meditated in the temple for 8 hours daily, trying to master the techniques. There was no one with whom to discuss the practice or possible signs of progress. Kriya was the only thing that interested him, but no one in the ashram could or would answer his questions, so he had plenty of time to ponder various ideas. After a while, one strange thought began to haunt him. Life was comfortable, meals were regular, there was no need to worry about money or shelter. But what would happen if he left the ashram? Would the power of God and the grace of Babaji sustain him? Would someone be found to literally put food into his mouth, as proof of the presence of higher forces in his destiny?

These thoughts became so obsessive that in the last week of October, he decided to test himself, fate, and the divine will, if such a thing existed at all. Early in the morning, he climbed over the ashram wall and walked towards Konark along the coast. Soon Puri was left behind. Turning into the jungle, he followed a small path. From early morning until sunset, he walked without stopping, having not the slightest idea of his destination.

Making his way through increasingly dense thickets, trying not to lose the barely visible trail, he thought: "I will eat only if someone puts food into my mouth." Only the purity of the experiment could confirm the connection between a mortal's fate and the higher forces that govern it.

The sun sets early on the eastern coast, and by 5 PM it was completely dark. Exhausted and broken, he collapsed under a large banyan tree and immediately fell into a deep, dreamless sleep.

When he awoke, the sun was already high. In the distance, on the rice fields, figures of farmers were visible, but no one disturbed him. Thoughts or desires were absent. His body felt light; he felt neither the slightest hunger nor thirst. He was indifferent; the dizziness and weakness were pleasant, bordering on a hazy, relaxed semi-consciousness. He slept for most of the day; time flowed imperceptibly.

In the countryside, strangers are noticed immediately. The vastness of the open spaces sharpens the villagers' eyesight. Probably the farmers in the rice fields had noticed him and told their wives. The next morning, around 11 o'clock, as far as he could tell from the sun's position, several women, young and old, approached the tree. They brought food in clay pots, a pitcher of water, and placed everything right in front of him. A wave of hunger and thirst rose within him. Food had arrived, and he could eat and drink!

However, he restrained himself: the vow was different. He had sworn he would eat only if someone put the food directly into his mouth. The aroma of freshly cooked food made his head spin. He turned away. The miraculously materialized provisions were not convincing. He had to be sure that every detail of his request was fulfilled. The women sat nearby for a while, then left.

The next day, he no longer felt his own body, nor hunger. His breathing slowed; he was gazing into another dimension. The rice fields turned into slowly swaying, soft green waves, and the distant trees changed colors and shapes. Around noon, the women appeared again; this time there were 25 or 30 of them, he could not say exactly. As if through a thick fog, he observed the swaying folds of bright saris, their dark faces illuminated by flashes of cautious, timid smiles. A short beard and mustache made him look like a sadhu; his simple white kurta-pajama was dirty and torn after the long journey through the jungle, yet he did not appear wounded or ill. He was simply alone in an unfamiliar place, and they knew this.

Again, a banana leaf with food was placed on the ground before him, and a clay water pitcher was set down, and again he turned away. A vow was a vow; someone had to put the food directly into his mouth.

Among the women was a girl of about nine, dressed in a traditional bridal outfit, according to the custom of child marriage in Orissa. An elderly woman gestured for her to come closer, and she squatted down beside him. Mixing the banana curry with rice, she hesitantly brought the food to his parched lips. He began to eat. The girl gave him four or five more portions, and he could no longer, and did not want to, hold back his tears. Tracks of tears also appeared on the girl's cheeks. Looking back at the silent group, he saw that the women were weeping silently. This was a moment of realization that forever changed his life: compassion was a quality inherent in all women; it lived in their hearts because they were created to create, nourish, and sustain life. They manifested within themselves the power of Life, and now this power imperiously called out to him: "Live!"

Until this moment, he had not considered women worthy of attention; they lived in a parallel, narrow world, limited to kitchen chores, household tasks, and gossip. Accompanying his mother to the market, he would usually walk at a distance, trying to dissociate himself from this petty, confined female universe. But now, in his time of need, only women had come to his aid. He was a nobody to them, a rootless and unknown stranger, and yet they had shown compassion. With work-worn hands, quick smiles, and soft voices, the women radiated the true primordiality of love. His heightened emotions unlocked a strange new ability: he no longer needed to look around; he saw and felt everything at once with his heart. The smell of the hot midday earth and warm grass, the taste of the food, and the scent of the child's hair became sharp and intoxicating. In a gesture of blessing, he placed his hand on the girl's head; she sat motionless. The women began to softly sing bhajans in the Oriya language; the tears dried. About an hour later, one by one, the women began to prostrate themselves before him, taking their leave, and he again touched the head of the little bride. Then they all left.

The request had been heard; it had been answered. Whatever force had taken the form of the village women had clearly manifested its will and presence. He felt not the slightest agitation, only gratitude and quiet bliss. Taking a sip of water, he dozed off, and towards evening he set off back to Puri. After a few hours, the jungle was left behind; bright moonlight illuminated the coast, and the road was clearly visible. He walked confidently, filled with an inexpressible knowledge that the true call of the soul always receives an answer. At dawn, he jumped over the ashram wall and, entering his room, collapsed onto the bed. He saw no dreams, but continued to feel the warmth of the earth beneath him.

**Sea Creatures**

The ashram administrator learned of his absence fairly quickly. Someone must have reported him. The manager decided he was involved in something illegal, and a quarrel broke out between them. He did not try to defend himself or explain his absence and its reasons. The conflict was smoothed over, but the tension remained.

Three weeks passed without incident, and he still had not the slightest idea when he would learn true yogic sadhana. The ashram was merely a waiting room in which he was stuck for an indefinite time in the company of random fellow travelers who, it seemed, did not themselves know where they were going. Yet they all tried to convince him that this was the destination. He did not feel this at all, and his restlessness grew.

That night he could not sleep. The room was stuffy, and he decided to go out to the ocean to get some fresh air. Using his proven method, he jumped over the wall enclosing the ashram. It was about two o'clock in the morning; the deserted beach was flooded with bright moonlight. The ocean breathed evenly and powerfully, and he suddenly felt a surge of happiness simply because he was alive and breathing. This night belonged to him alone; it was wondrous to listen to the roar of the crashing waves while bathing in the bright moonlight next to the vast, living ocean. He was acutely aware of himself as merely a tiny grain of sand floating in a vast, conscious Universe.

The waves rhythmically shook the foam from their crests onto the sand. Hypnotized by the surf, he did not immediately notice two moving silhouettes about forty meters away. Gleaming like copper in the moonlight, two figures emerged from the waves. A man, like a Hulk, six and a half feet tall, with bulging muscles, and his companion, equally broad-boned and powerfully built. The man had short hair; the woman's wet locks reached her shoulders. Both were completely naked, save for cast gold ornaments on their necks, arms, wrists, and ankles. The jewels shimmered in the moonlight on their damp skin.

Both had wings, leathery, like the wings of bats, partially folded behind their backs. A sudden fear washed over him. As if sensing this, the woman glanced in his direction and silently pointed him out to her companion. For a fraction of a second, the creatures studied him, and then, turning towards the ocean, they dove into the incoming waves. Four unfurled wings, with a powerful synchronous splash, created momentary whirlpools, and the pair vanished into the dance of the tousled waves.

A few minutes later, breathing heavily, he was entering his room. His mind registered what he had seen but refused to analyze it. There were no terms to describe these sea

There were simply no such beings. Or perhaps they were celestial creations?
Demons, angels, aliens—whatever these beings were, he now knew of their existence, and no one could convince him it had been a hallucination. Uncharted worlds were near, existing side by side with his reality. It was simply that not many people dared to peer into the invisible cracks between worlds.

**The Sadhu on the Beach. The Mantra of Death**

The beach was an ideal place for meditation, and he came there almost daily, always preferring the same spot. Nearby, a sadhu of indeterminate age would usually sit. Time after time, this man moved closer, and eventually they began to talk. From constant exposure to the sun, the sadhu's tangled long hair had a coppery sheen, but his short, thick beard was black. A loincloth of matting or coarse burlap, tied with a rope, covered his lean, dark body, and this man looked more like a beggar than a sadhu. It was hard to tell where this wanderer was from, but he spoke good, even literate, Hindi with a northern accent. Looking at his equipment, one could guess he was an ascetic: he had a *danda* staff, with a special support used for performing *japa*78. Some sadhus take a vow never to sit or lie down and use the *danda* to support the upper body during sleep or while performing other austerities. Despite having the staff, this man sat on the sand quite comfortably.
The stranger was certainly not an ordinary chillum-loving vagabond, as he spoke of captivating, sometimes frightening things. Very precise in describing details, this sadhu was clearly interested in continuing their conversations. Despite looking destitute, the stranger did not ask for money. The interlocutor made him write down several alchemical formulas. And although this science held absolutely no interest for him, he still obediently wrote them down. One of the "recipes" contained step-by-step instructions for obtaining solid mercury.
One day, the sadhu spoke of the *Maran mantra*, which grants the ability to kill at will. The words of the mantra were not in Sanskrit, but in a strange mixture of several dialects—Odia, Bengali, and Hindi. The ritual itself was quite simple: one had to recite the mantra for twenty-one days. Having received its power, by repeating the incantation one hundred and eight times and mentally concentrating on the image of one's opponent, one could kill any adversary. A painful death followed almost immediately; the victim would vomit blood and eventually suffocate. The ceremony for obtaining the mantra itself had to be performed at a cremation ground, in strict accordance with the rules. It was completely unclear why the sadhu had decided to share this dangerous knowledge; he only said that the *Maran mantra* was meant for protection and that every yogi should know it.
Their conversations continued for about three weeks, until one day the strange interlocutor simply disappeared. However, a small notebook containing names of herbs, alchemical formulas, descriptions of mantras and rituals was safely hidden in his pocket. Tired of the monotony of ashram life, he began coming to the Swargadwar *smashan* around midnight. There, until four in the morning, he performed *japa*, silently repeating the *Maran mantra*. He was fully aware of the nature of the experiment; it was nothing other than black magic. But he was young, curious, and weary of the monotony of life in the ashram.
Surrounded by frightening shadows and sounds, he spent twenty nights at the *smashan* without any major incidents. On the last, twenty-first night, he saw a "fresh" corpse. The deceased's family must not have been able to afford enough wood for cremation, and a single smoldering log lay on the poor man's body. The half-burnt corpse looked terrible.
Trying not to look at the dead body, he began to recite the mantra. Suddenly, a small explosion occurred near the corpse, and a blue flame flared up. The head turned and stared at him with its remaining eye. He wanted to run, but remembered the sadhu's warning in time: "If you run, you will go mad." Gathering all his courage, he finished the ritual. Although the corpse's gaze was terrifying, by the end of the mantra recitation he felt an inner calm.
Now a new, secret power lived within him. The ability to kill with a single movement of will was tempting, and possessing this power was captivating; however, he felt neither peace nor satisfaction. On the contrary, irritability grew, and an inner volcano of anger could erupt at any moment. It was pure luck that he did not attempt to use the mantra: at that time, any minor incident could have provoked him into irreversible actions. Outwardly calm, he seethed inwardly, trying to contain negative emotions. A year later, after he had found a spiritual teacher, the Guru noticed his state and inquired about the reasons. Trying to find the right words, he told the story of the *Maran mantra*.
The Teacher said: "This is a dangerous gift. No matter how calm and peaceful you are, there will always be something that can anger you. The temptation may arise to use this power, just to prove you are right. And this will trigger a chain reaction of negative karmas. As your Guru, I advise you to get rid of this mantra."
Apparently, the Teacher himself possessed certain abilities and knew well the possible consequences of their misuse. The method of getting rid of it and purification was quite simple: with a sharp nail on a copper plate, one had to describe what had been done: "I, so-and-so, desired to obtain such-and-such power, and it was given to me." Then, creating a *sankalpa*, a strong intention to renounce the deadly gift, one had to throw this plate into the waters of the Ganges: "Now, by the instruction of my Guru, I give back this power."
Black magic—*abhichara*—is still widespread in India; some deliberately seek such powers to instill fear, command respect, or for enrichment. *Abhichara* is used to eliminate competitors, cause damage and suffering; disputes over property and legal matters (especially between relatives) are often resolved in this way. However, the consequences always return like a boomerang to their initiator.
The path he had chosen was different and had no need for cowardly and base dark rituals. Respecting power, he cultivated it, relying on the strength of his own spirit, and gradually it began to guide him.
In November, shortly before the Diwali festival, on the embankment near the Guru's house, he gave the power of the *Maran mantra* to the Ganges, immediately feeling relief, almost happiness.

**Winning the Lottery**

Months passed, and one day he received an invitation to a festival at the neighboring ashram of Bhupendranath Sanyal—apparently, Shailendranath Mukherjee, thanks to whom he had bought and read the books of Lahiri Mahasaya, remembered the young visitor.
He considered the time spent at the Karar ashram as wasted. The visualization practices and breathing techniques had nothing to do with the *sadhana* described by Lahiri. The effect of Kriya Yoga should have been completely different, but getting any coherent explanations from the *sannyasis* in the ashram was still impossible; they continued to give motivational speeches and chew over empty words about God-realization. He no longer believed in "enlightened" ones. Needless to say, he arrived at the festival at the Sanyal ashram with a heart full of disappointment. More than two hundred followers of Kriya Yoga were present, including the then-head of the ashram, Mr. Jwalaprasad Tiwari, and Shailendranath introduced them: "This is a student of Kriya Yoga from the neighboring ashram; he would like to study it in its pure, original form." The head of the ashram immediately offered him initiation.
Jwalaprasad Tiwari looked noble and dignified, but appearance and charisma no longer impressed him—he had already met enough masters of flowery speech.
"Can you teach me all the *kriyas* that Lahiri Mahasaya taught? Tell me directly. If yes, I am your disciple. If not, there is no point in initiation, because I will continue to search for a true guru anyway." This was an extremely straightforward statement, bordering on insolence, and the conversations around them quieted down. But he continued: "Perhaps you know someone who is familiar with the complete teaching of Lahiri Mahasaya? If so, please give me his name and address!"
After a short pause, Mr. Tiwari replied softly: "Yes, such a teacher exists. It is the grandson of Lahiri Mahasaya, Satyacharan Lahiri, and he lives in Benares. However, he is quite old and, it seems, has stopped giving initiations. But you can try to meet him; perhaps you will be lucky…" And Mr. Tiwari asked an elderly woman to write down the teacher's address on a piece of paper.
Clutching the precious scrap of paper, he felt as if he were holding a winning lottery ticket in his hands. Somehow he knew: the search was over; this teacher from Benares would become his Guru.
In his commentaries, Lahiri Mahasaya called a special technique, *Khechari mudra*, a key element of the entire practice. In the Karar ashram, no one explained this technique; it was not even mentioned. Perhaps out of ignorance? Or was important information deliberately hidden so that new students would not progress in yoga? All this no longer mattered, because the master from Benares taught *Khechari*, and this was very important.
The people he met through Shailendranath were kind and open. Seeing the sincerity of his quest, they supported and encouraged him, unlike the *sannyasis* in the Karar ashram. Filled with inflated superiority, they did not even think about spiritual growth, as it might interfere with the accustomed comfort of their lives. Over time, he learned to recognize the unmistakable stench of corrupt "spiritual" organizations, hierarchical games, and empty promises.
No illusions about life in the ashram remained; he could leave immediately, yet he decided to wait for the head of the ashram, who was soon returning from a trip abroad. At a large reception before several thousand followers, he accused the head of the organization of violating traditions and breaking promises. It is unpleasant when your money is stolen, but it is doubly painful when your faith is desecrated. Some of those present tried to interrupt him, hissing: "A *sannyasi* should not behave like this!" However, no one could offer a coherent rebuttal.
In a pathetic attempt to save face, the head of the ashram turned away, pretending to be talking to someone and not hearing the accusatory speech. No one expected explanations from the leadership anyway; most of the ashram's inhabitants simply wanted to belong to any organization that provided support, protection, and a sense of belonging. In their view, spiritual awakening was supposed to happen according to a schedule, preferably between lunch and evening tea. To all these people, he was a troublemaker, a violator of order and established etiquette.
Exactly one year had passed since his arrival in Puri, but what a year it was! The discoveries about people, the surrounding world, his own abilities and intentions did not embitter or disappoint him; on the contrary, all this clearly illustrated the necessity of discrimination, of discernment. In March 1982, accompanied by his mother who had come for him, he left the ashram.
Returning to Gwalior, he discovered that his entire collection of precious comics, about 4000 books and magazines, had been sold as waste paper. This was a great blow, and he genuinely mourned the loss. His mother insisted that comics were a useless hobby, completely unsuitable for an adult man, which he should now become. Undoubtedly, he had matured, but his values clearly differed from those acceptable in the world of so-called normal people. For the rest, returning home was quite positive; his parents breathed a sigh of relief, as they had feared he would take *sannyasa* and become a monk, which would mean severing all ties with the family.
A few days after returning home, he wrote a letter to his future Guru. In the haste of leaving Puri, the precious scrap of paper with the address was lost, but this did not matter.

of great significance; fate had already sung him its promise.

He addressed the envelope thus: “Satya Charan Lahiri, Bengalee Tola, Benares” – and this proved sufficient for the letter to reach its addressee. A reply soon came, containing a precise return address and a practical suggestion: “Perhaps you should first settle your material affairs. Marry, find a job, or start your own business – and after that you can devote yourself to yoga. Otherwise, people usually run into problems.” He showed the letter to his father, who remarked that this Guru was a very sensible man. And yet, in his reply, he declared his firm resolve to dedicate his life to yoga and nothing else. The future teacher responded quite quickly: “Then come in September or October, and I will decide what to do with you.”

The Beginning

* * *

“Read no history, read nothing but biographies. For in them is life without theory.”
Benjamin Disraeli, Contarini Fleming (1832)

Benares. The Guru.

A familiar history teacher from his college wrote to a cousin, a pandit named Radhe Maharaj who lived in Benares. In the letter, the professor asked the pandit to find a room for his former student and to guide him to the required address through the tangled labyrinth of the eternal city’s streets. The same professor lent him money for the ticket, and on September 30, 1982, he arrived in Benares. It was the birthday of Lahiri Mahasaya, and he took this as an auspicious sign. At noon he reached the home of Radhe Maharaj, where he was warmly received and fed. Then the pandit led him to his destination. Into the embrace of fate.

An old servant led them to the upper veranda of a house situated near Chusatti Ghat⁷⁹. On the way to Benares, he had imagined that in the home of the Guru of the Lahiri dynasty he would be met by stern swamis. Instead, he saw about a dozen ordinary retirees; none among those assembled were younger than seventy. One of those present was reading a chapter from the Bhagavad Gita aloud; the others listened attentively. Embarrassed by his own youth, he felt out of place. Those gathered glanced at him questioningly, and the situation grew awkward: he had never seen the Guru and could not distinguish him among the other elderly men. To whom should he bow?

After hesitating, he bowed to the sacred book in accordance with Hindu tradition and quietly sat down in a corner. An elderly woman who entered bowed to one of those present – and the future teacher was identified! He rose and prostrated himself before an unremarkable elderly gentleman. Radhe Maharaj tried to introduce them both, but was gestured to wait, and soon slipped quietly off the terrace. He attempted to address the Guru himself, but was also stopped: “We will talk after the reading is finished…”

The agonizing wait seemed endless; tension mounted: would he receive initiation, or would he be refused? In his reply, the Guru had only said: “Come, and I will decide.” This guaranteed nothing.

After about forty minutes, the reading ended; those present accepted prasad and dispersed one by one. The Guru was ready to speak with him, but the conversation was interrupted by the arrival of some man. Later he learned that this was a high-ranking official who had already asked for initiation and been refused. Despite this, the stranger decided to try again. Raising his voice, Satya Charan Lahiri ordered the uninvited guest to leave. His heart sank: now the Guru would drive him away too! But his fears were groundless. Having finally ascertained who the young visitor was, the Guru said: “So, you are that Shailendra from Gwalior? Well, I have been waiting for you. Come tomorrow at seven in the morning, bring a notebook and pen. And buy flower garlands as an offering to Shiva, Babaji, and Lahiri Mahasaya.”

He left with a pounding heart and could not remember how he found his way to Pandey Dharamshala, where Radhe Maharaj had rented him a room costing one and a half rupees a night. Yet the money was wasted – from agitation he could not sleep.

Initiation

Early in the morning, he bathed in the Ganga. The sun was just beginning to color the horizon, gently illuminating the river and the ghats; the sounds of morning temple bells awakened a shy chorus of invisible birds. The river, at first grey and dark, turned deep crimson, and then gradually took on the color of shining silver. People around him were bathing and softly, in a chanting tone, singing mantras and raising their hands to the sun in greeting. The great river accepted all offerings: garlands of flowers, fruits, ghee, and rice, and the oil lamps floating upon it, whose light slowly faded, becoming almost imperceptible in the rays of the rising sun. The river meditatively absorbed half-burnt corpses, human sins, and the prayers of all hearts, in all languages. Whatever words he uttered within himself, the slow waters caught and carried away. It was time to go to the Guru.

In this city, nothing came easily or simply. The cherished desire that had brought him here was on the verge of fulfillment, and yet he was not entirely certain it would come to pass. Would the Teacher grant him initiation? However, the dawning day instilled confidence in the inevitability of a new beginning. The giant, eternal being that was Benares breathed with the chest of the Dashashwamedh Ghats. Assi Ghat was its head; the famous Manikarnika Ghat, its umbilical cord of smoke from funeral pyres, connecting the physical world to the astral.

Myriads of souls were enclosed within the body of Kashi⁸⁰; the narrow streets were the blood vessels of the eternal city. He strode along one of the lanes, accompanied by a sudden convoy of huge, black, glistening buffaloes, past braziers of peanuts and shops of multicolored fabrics, absorbing the sounds of puja bells from the houses. And beneath the hum of awakening Benares, an inner silence settled within him. At some moment, it seemed to him that his heartbeat echoed the inaudible yet tangible rhythm of the city’s heart.

Arriving an hour early, he waited in the courtyard. The Teacher came down at seven o’clock and told him to remove his kurta and sit on the floor. Turning his back to the teacher and looking expectantly at the wall, he was surprised by sudden sensations in his spine, caused by the touch of the Guru’s fingers. The energy knots, the future chakras, were activated, and from that day onward, he was to concentrate on these points during practice.

Satya Charan Lahiri asked why he did not wear the sacred thread of the Brahmins, the janeu. It is supposed to be put on after a special ritual, and both his brothers had undergone this ceremony before marriage. He was the youngest, a bachelor, and did not think it played any significant role for yogic initiation…

Alarmed, he fabricated a story that he had left the janeu in his hotel room, at which the Guru chuckled: “Why not simply tell the truth: you haven’t undergone this ritual, have you?” And he confessed.

The Teacher laughed, and then began to explain the first level of practice. Listening to the instructions, he realized that he already knew these techniques. The practice felt familiar, just as the names and faces in the photographs from *Autobiography of a Yogi* had once felt. Several times he could even accurately predict which kriya would follow the previous one. Noticing this, the Guru asked if he had practiced before. He was certain he had not, at least not in this life.

Years of swimming served him well. Performing breath retention, he was able to hold it for more than 200 counts, which pleased the Teacher. But Khechari Mudra seemed too difficult, and he doubted he could master it.

At that time, he still did not envision the length of the path lying before him; yet an unshakable certainty that yoga would become his life overrode all other emotions. The morning of October 1, 1982, marked the beginning of an incredible transformation.

The Guru invited him to spend the night at his house; this was thrilling, and he awaited further developments. But the next day, the Teacher said: “Now you may return to Gwalior. Come back when you have mastered Khechari Mudra, and I will teach you the next level of practice. Work diligently: I am already old, and it is unknown how much longer I will remain in this body. Therefore, do not waste time.”

As he boarded the train, he felt a surge of confidence. At last, it was clear what to do! Before this journey, nothing had been able to bring clarity to his search; he had felt only emptiness and numbness from philosophical ramblings, calls to focus attention on the divine and wait for revelations. None of the people he had met before made any visible effort to attain the highest goal of yoga, samadhi. As if striving were entirely unnecessary, and spiritual secrets should unravel themselves. The path of a manicured and respectable version of enlightenment was convenient in every way, but it was not his path.

Intuitively, he knew: the extraction of the precious ore of this yoga would be as difficult as the mining of gold.

War and Peace

Leaving Benares, he had no idea what awaited him. His thoughts were wholly concentrated on the complex techniques of practice, and he intended to move forward. There was no need to inform his family about the yogic initiation: although his father sympathized with his yogic pursuits, the others had no idea about sadhana. Moreover, the family was going through hard times. Within a month, by November 1982, they had to sell the house. His brother Gyanendra found work as a manager on a huge farm, which was very timely – they needed to move, and the brother was entitled to housing.

The company Gwalior Dairy Ltd. had leased a farm of over a thousand acres. A legal dispute between the state of Madhya Pradesh and the trust board managing the farm was in full swing, and for the duration of the litigation, the court had appointed a temporary farm manager, who was required to reside there. A huge warehouse was renovated and converted into a living space with nine rooms and a hall. Every family member gained personal space: the parents, Gyanendra with his wife Mala and son Avinish, and he himself. His two other sisters were married and lived separately, and his younger sister Pratibha had died of hepatitis in 1968. The elder brother was now the sole breadwinner, and he needed help. They lived on the farm for only a year, but what a year it turned out to be!

The farm had the potential to yield millions, and while the lawsuit was ongoing, local bandits and raiders were eager to grab a share. The enormous harvest was worth several million rupees, yet the profit vanished along with a large portion of the crop. When the new management tried to establish order, war with the raider clans became inevitable. Problems began immediately.

The very next day after moving in, they found themselves embroiled in a confrontation with one of the local mafia bosses, who had a bad reputation and a large gang that extorted money for “protection.” All the local farmers paid them. He and Gyanendra were physically strong, but more importantly, they were confident in their rights. There was no question of paying the bandits or backing down and leaving. The family’s well-being and safety now depended on this job, and they remained cool-headed and unyielding. The racketeer left. He had come alone, just to “get acquainted,” but the locals advised the new management not to resist, as the ringleader was a ruthless killer.

Just an hour after this unpleasant visit, representatives of another gang appeared: “Friends, we will provide you with weapons. Fight back against that bastard! We will support you!” There was nothing to do but accept the weapons from the “opposition”; among other things, the delivered ammunition included

hand grenades were included. Then a veritable hell broke loose.

The initial "one-on-one" skirmishes quickly escalated into a full-scale guerrilla war: heavily armed bandits raided the farm, always in different places, seizing the harvest and intimidating the workers. About a hundred villagers, unable to resist the gangsters, labored on the farm. Gyänendra, he himself, and a few reluctant watchmen had to confront the invaders, guarding the territory and the harvest. Thanks to hand grenades and double-barreled shotguns, they managed to hold on. The police, obliged to support the legally appointed manager, had taken to appearing only after the gunfights were over. In general, the police worked in a peculiar way: they would arrest groups of suspects and then immediately release them on bail. Much in the rural provinces had remained unchanged since the time of the Mahabharata, and reforms were far off.

The Wild West drama continued, raids following one after another. Sometimes armed gangs of thirty to forty people took part; fierce firefights could last until dawn. By some miracle, bloodshed was avoided, losses were insignificant, and people remained unharmed. Often he wondered how it was possible to emerge from such clashes without a single scratch… And he was grateful to Babaji – apparently, the Avatar⁸¹ was indeed watching over him.

The daily anticipation of armed conflicts caused stress and tension. All of them, both leaders and workers, were constantly on high alert, and this was exhausting. Few can adapt to a constant threat, but there was simply no other way out. These rather difficult circumstances revealed new, previously unknown facets of his own character: fear, anger, a sense of being right and of justice.

Many years later, having studied his natal chart, an astrologer commented on that period of life: this transit of Saturn was marked by dangers, confusion, and physical threat to life. The Mahadasha of the Sun caused waves of violence, indicating confrontations, intimidation, and illegal actions, including ambushes and murders. Well, the reading was accurate; all of this was part of the daily reality back then.

The siege of the farm continued for more than twelve months. Nevertheless, in the midst of this bedlam, the practice went excellently. Despite the daily clashes, he practiced every night, and the boundary between realities was thin but distinct.

Practice swept away the daytime confetti of thoughts and impressions; he entered into the boundless darkness, and the breath became the beam of a lighthouse, signaling to the Spirit.

**The Secret Mudra, the Eternal Spirit, and Kshetra**

In January, after altercations with workers and another day of dealing with uninvited guests, he was preparing for his nightly practice. Accidentally touching his wrist, he discovered the absence of a pulse, and the silence of the veins shocked him. He checked his carotid artery. There was no movement of blood flow under the warm skin. Feeling the artery again, he sensed a slow, hesitant pulsation. From time to time, the strange phenomenon repeated; the pulse simply disappeared, and there was no other choice but to learn to manage this. This led to gradual control over the blood vessels and the speed of the heartbeat. Experiencing what was described in yogic texts, he had no idea what exactly triggered these processes. Perhaps it was the effect of the Shvasahita pranayama⁸² practiced at the initial level. If so, it was incredible: only three months had passed since initiation.

He mastered Khechari mudra, a key condition for the next level of practice, in March. Initially puzzled by its complexity, he did not allow himself to doubt his own abilities and continued his attempts. He had strived for this yoga for too long, and now, having received all the explanations, he was so close to mastering this important stage of practice! Retreat was not an option, and there was no other way but to achieve Khechari. This mudra was the criterion of true sadhana, the key to doors that needed to be opened, simply by fitting the key to the lock.

All yogic texts unanimously acknowledged the uniqueness of Khechari; Shiva's beloved mudra was a protection, a means of clairvoyance, and a recipe for longevity. By drinking amrita with the help of Khechari, a yogi could free himself from aging and death. To obtain the precious nectar, the udder of the brain had to be "milked" with the tongue. To obtain the magic potion, one had to become a magician, and overcoming the difficulty of performing this technique was a perfectly fair condition for initiation into the secrets that lay ahead.

By day, he and Gyänendra and a small group of guards repelled the malefactors and resolved numerous economic issues; and by night, paying no attention to the occasional gunshots, he traveled to distant universes illuminated by the bright light of Kutastha, the immortal spirit enlivening the body. The first encounter with the Light, as he called it, occurred back in 1979, after his first out-of-body experience. It was astonishing, yet there were simply no words capable of describing that experience at the time. During his second visit to Benares, he gathered his courage and described in detail what he had seen to the Teacher. The Teacher was impressed.

"Kutastha is your Atma⁸³, and what you saw is a glimpse of Paramatma⁸⁴, the Great Spirit. To see it is an extremely rare fortune. Usually, people can only see the 'standard' Kutastha: a dark circle in a halo of bright light. You saw something special." After a pause, the Guru repeated: "To encounter Paramatma before receiving the practice is a great blessing. You are very fortunate…"

The unusual sphere, shot through with golden discharges of electric flashes, appeared on his "inner screen" several more times. By then, he already knew that the velvety-black circle with a deep dark-blue hue in its center was his spirit, Atma, and the golden outer ring was Jiva⁸⁵. He did not attempt to decipher exactly what this Spirit was. It could be his personal "self," Jivatma⁸⁶. In Hinduism, duality is perceived so deeply that accepting wholeness is far more natural than trying to understand each of its components. All efforts in Kriya were directed towards understanding the true nature of the "Self," that which was simultaneously both Shiva and Shakti, both male and female. Light and darkness were twins; an exhalation did not exist without an inhalation, and this order of things did not change. In Sanskrit, the word "Kutastha" meant "that which remains unchanged."

Names and terms were not as important as the effect itself. He knew: an eternal Spirit lived within him. Enlivening the body, It was not a part of his self; they had yet to get to know each other. Sometimes the splendor of Kutastha changed its shades under the influence of the season, the position of the stars, or the intensity of daily events. Whatever the reason, the luminous sphere of the Spirit was constantly present on his inner screen. Growing larger, it drew him in, and at times he would fall entirely into it. The Bhagavad Gita was the only reliable source describing this phenomenon that he could find, although even there the nature of Kutastha was mentioned rather sparingly.

"There are only two Purushas⁸⁷ in this world: the perishable and the eternal. All creatures are perishable, and only Kutastha (the liberated one) is eternal." (Gita 15/16)

It spoke of two kinds of primordial Energy; one was mortal, the other immortal. Observing, he understood that the Spirit is comprehended only through sadhana. Metal can be shaped on an anvil, but the anvil itself remained the same. Yogic practice is the foundation and anvil of magic; transformation occurred through opposition and endurance. At some point, it became clear that the changing colors of Kutastha were the language of the Spirit, and to learn to form words and begin to ask questions, one had to learn a new alphabet.

The Spirit was an ancient, powerful entity, an extraterrestrial. There was a feeling that the level of sadhana did not matter, as It could make contact at any moment, at Its will. Even Khechari mudra was not so important; everything depended on the readiness of the "vessel" to open, as the Spirit itself was always ready for communication. The Spirit was simply waiting, and the maturity of the practitioner was decisive for the beginning of the dialogue. The continuation of the communication depended solely on the Spirit, and it was impossible to predict what exactly would interest and amuse It. The enlivening of the body by the Spirit was influenced by a complex combination of karmic and planetary factors, and they were never random. The Giver of life, It also took it away when the time came to seek a new incarnation, shedding the worn-out body like old clothes. Imagining the infinite ocean of bodies that this Eternal Being had changed, he shuddered. There were many theories about the unity of Spirit and body, but one thing was obvious: the Spirit resides in the body as long as It is interested in the body's possessor. When the Spirit grew tired and bored, It moved on.

The Spirit could impart new knowledge to the body's owner, grant revelations, and to enter this "educational program," one simply had to meet certain requirements. Coming to witness and enjoy, the Spirit must be interested in communicating with the "master" of the body, and it was He who "leased" the body to the "operator."

It was wiser to wait for That One to make the "first move," and observing Kutastha, he tried to remain dispassionate and detached. Once, the Guru mentioned that the appearance of Kutastha during Yoni mudra⁸⁸ is a sure sign of a short

The goal of awakening Kundalini⁸⁹. This captivating assertion required empirical confirmation.

Constant skirmishes and endless managerial and domestic problems distracted and troubled him, yet his inner life was full of astonishing discoveries. The awareness of progress in practice fostered a growing confidence in his own abilities. External irritants were caused by people whose imagination never extended beyond the bounds of primitive desires, whether for land, power, women, or money. Their actions were the result of primal survival instincts, and understanding this, he did not take what was happening too much to heart.

Both his surroundings and the current situation were temporary, while his goal and intentions remained unchanging. He had to plough the body with the plough of breath, no matter what happened during the day. The *kshetra*, the field of mind and body, demanded constant cultivation, and he was determined to reap the harvest. At times, this felt like a battlefield; at the beginning of practice, the body would become sluggish and unresponsive, as the mind stubbornly continued to chew over the day’s impressions. Shifting the focus of attention was difficult, but the call of the breath did its work, and after a while, the incoming streams of pranic energy would begin an alchemical transformation, opening the treasure-house of the heart.

**Facets of Education**

During the intense confrontations on the farm, he had the opportunity to get a closer look at some of the hardened criminals who had taken their side because the other gang was trying to overpower the new management. The bandits proved to be curious representatives of the human species, and interacting with them was instructive. They were by no means intellectuals, but the motives for their actions were simultaneously simple and sophisticated. After one incident, he understood: one cannot always judge people by their actions, for human nature possesses a far greater duality, depth, and contradictoriness. Sometimes the mixture of the divine and the demonic is so thick and lumpy that it is unclear what will ultimately be baked from this dough.

One robber (this was his permanent profession) would typically strike his victim with a knife in the thigh to immobilize, but not kill, and then take all the victim’s belongings. The knife wound to the thigh was a kind of calling card, a “trademark,” while the robberies themselves were random in nature, nothing personal. The robber was a drunkard and a rather cheerful fellow, without the slightest sign of hidden malice. He was an “ally” of their team, and they could do nothing about his antisocial behavior. That day, he had robbed someone again, taking a tidy sum of three hundred rupees from the victim. That same day, the mother of one of the farm workers fell seriously ill with a high fever; her son was absent, and the old woman grew worse by the hour. Going to her, the robber gave all his “day’s earnings” to pay for the hospital and medicine. Yes, the criminal gave the money to another person out of compassion… And what was he to be considered now?

Two or three days later, when the robber was more or less sober, he asked: “Why did you do that?” The man shrugged: “Robbery is just a job, what I do to get by. The old woman needed urgent help, and I had the means to help.” This minor incident revealed to him the irresolvable complexity of human intentions and moral criteria. Individual actions and decisions occurred according to the will of *karma*, *dharma*, and the call of the heart. The latter were the true catalysts of destiny, but how did it all work? Causing harm with one hand and offering help with the other, casting nets of mercy and cruelty into the ocean of *samsara*, mixing black and white until these colors became muted, indistinct… Was there Divine will in all this, or is life a mixed stream of all three *Gunas*, a property of nature itself? *Tamas*, *Rajas*, and *Sattva*—inertia and darkness, active seeking and thirst for action, bliss and aversion—all of them are mixed both in the world and in the vessel of an individual human personality. The play of life consisted in the uneven distribution of blessings, rewards and slaps, smiles and sorrows—and all this nested in the human heart, prompting one to act according to personal qualities.

Any conclusions regarding the “case of the bandit” would have been erroneous, yet he learned the lesson, acknowledging the uselessness of attaching distinctive labels to people and the impossibility of clearly categorizing their actions. Most often, people’s words diverged from their deeds, masking their true motives.

As for the criminals and their code of conduct, their motivations were transparent and uncomplicated, but in many ways revealing. The bandits did not seem fierce and dangerous to him; they were just people, subject to both fears and complexes, as well as individual displays of generosity and mercy, and none of this distinguished them from ordinary law-abiding citizens. The karmic cocktail made them who they were.

…A year later, his brother’s contract ended, and the family moved from the farm to a rented house in the suburbs of Gwalior. Having won the war, the Sharma brothers had earned a solid reputation. Now, if any of their acquaintances had problems with the local mafia, they were asked to stop by or simply to walk through the neighborhood, and that was enough for the problem to disappear on its own. Rumors of the guaranteed effect of their presence spread quickly, and already complete strangers who owned shops began inviting them for a cup of tea. He did not immediately understand the reason for his sudden popularity and warm reception in almost every shop in the city.

The tension he had endured and the constant skirmishes gave him greater self-confidence and survival skills, but the feigned external toughness was merely a shell, armor concealing a growing sensitivity and refined emotions. He was filled with an unbearable tenderness for the fragility and incredible beauty of life, and his understanding of the world widened, sharpening his acute sense of self. Establishing contact with the inner Self, with the elusive, blinding light of the Spirit, was becoming the priority.

**Becoming Guru**

Eighteen months flew by in diligent practice. Now, in full control of his pulse, he could not only stop it by an act of will but also slow his heartbeat—and all this happened naturally, as if the knowledge was already a part of him and merely awaited awakening.

Having mastered *Khechari Mudra*, he could count on receiving the next level of practice, and having written to his Guru about his achievements, he soon received a reply: he was expected in Benares; he could receive the second *kriya*.

…The sun was already heating the city considerably, but in the mornings, the courtyard of the teacher’s house was still comfortable. Satyacharan Lahiri, pleased with his progress, even called out to the *pujari*⁹⁰ in Bengali: “Ramesh, he has done *Khechari*!” One of the residents of the large house, Ramesh Babu, occupied rooms on the first floor with his family. This modest man was the uncle of the famous sitarist Ravi Shankar. Hearing the Guru’s words, Ramesh peeked into the room, smiling shyly.

In the inner courtyard was a small Shiva chapel, in which was installed a beautiful, large black *Shivalingam*, and before it, two marble statues of Lahiri Mahasaya and his son Tinkori Lahiri, the father of the current Guru. Part of Satyacharan Lahiri’s daily practice was reading the Bhagavad Gita aloud before the statues of his ancestors. In this same temple, on April 28, 1984, Satyacharan Lahiri taught him the practices of the second level of *kriya*, *amantra* and *samantra*. Despite their complexity, they seemed strangely familiar.

Having completed the instruction, the teacher pointed to the statue: “Serve Lahiri Mahasaya while I read the Gita.” Having not the slightest idea how to serve anyone, let alone a statue, he asked what exactly was required of him. “Give him a massage,” and the teacher began to read. Despite the absurdity of the situation, he began obediently rubbing the marble shoulders of the statue. After about five to seven minutes, the cold of the smooth marble under his fingers turned into warm, living flesh, and he recoiled. Noticing this, the Guru commanded him: “Wait in the room.”

Overcome with a strange feeling, he tried to understand what exactly had happened in the temple—was it a hallucination? Perhaps the stone simply warmed from the heat of his hands… but how to explain the pliable softness of the marble? His mind simply dismissed the possibility of the supernatural. About ten minutes passed, and he saw the Teacher run out of the temple and hastily cross the courtyard, despite his age and severe asthma. An anxious thought flashed that he was in for a reprimand for some terrible blunder… Breathing heavily, Satyacharan Lahiri entered the room. “You are the successor. Everyone agrees. You will be the new Guru. From this day, you may give initiation to those you deem worthy.”

Visibly shaken, the teacher sank heavily into an armchair, trying to catch his breath. Utterly bewildered, not knowing what to reply, he bowed to the Guru. He was silent; it was so quiet that he thought he could hear his own heartbeat.

All of this was a complete surprise: to receive the second level of *kriya* and immediately afterward be appointed the successor of the lineage? Teaching others had never been in his plans; he could not even imagine himself in that role. His initial goal was to achieve the highest attainable heights in yoga, and perhaps, after that, he could give advice to others. But to be named successor at the very beginning of the path, after only a year and a half of practice, during his second visit to the teacher…?!

What was happening did not align with what he had read and heard about the great masters of yoga; it was all too simple. Perhaps anyone who had done *Khechari* and received the next level of practice automatically became a guru? Or maybe *guru* was simply a general term for those who had done *Khechari* and could transmit Kriya Yoga to others? At the next meeting, he quietly asked a disciple sitting nearby: “Who else among the disciples can give initiation into the practice?” The stunned look of his neighbor said more than the brief answer: “No one…” Overcoming his embarrassment, he turned to the Guru: “How often is a disciple who has mastered *Khechari* permitted to initiate others into the technique of Kriya Yoga?”

⁸⁹ Kundalini (Sanskrit for “coiled serpent”) is a form of energy sleeping at the base of the spine. Kundalini, coiled in the *muladhara chakra*, which represents the earth element in the human body, can be awakened by streams of *prana* and *apana* directed into the central channel—*Sushumna*. There is an ancient belief that every person carries divine energy within them, and certain yogic techniques can awaken, release, and harness this energy. Shailendra Sharma believes that Kundalini has far more than three and a half coils (as it is usually described in yogic texts). The coils of Kundalini represent various forms of the sound “OM.” Gorakhnath speaks not of 3.5, but of 8 coils of Kundalini.

⁹⁰ *Pujari* – a priest who conducts a religious ceremony or temple rituals.

"Never. You are the first," replied the Guru.

That is how it all came to pass. The fifth Guru of the parampara of Lahiri Mahasaya was only twenty-six years old.

Even years later he could not say with any certainty what exactly had happened then in the temple. The extraordinarily complex science of Kriya Yoga cannot be mastered in a few months, and the fact that a novice could become a Guru simply did not fit in the mind. Logically, the combination of all the factors and circumstances could be summed up like this: the Master was old and could leave the body at any moment. The parampara had always chosen its successor on the basis of the preferences of the living master and the will of all the previous Gurus, of their astral knowledge of the probable future. It seemed he had been chosen unanimously, since the Master had said: "All have agreed…"

Gradually he began to grasp the weight of the responsibility that had fallen upon him. Strangely enough, the capacity to give swift answers to the most difficult questions of visitors during darshans came almost at once. Ordinarily extremely shy and untalkative, he could now analyse any topic and immediately give well-reasoned replies. Answering questions about the most vital problems of life meant carrying responsibility for the subsequent decisions of the questioner, but he was guided by the strength and the blessing of the Parampara, as well as by his own dogged sadhana. In addition to the growing capacity to think more quickly and speak fluently and concisely, his physical strength also grew, as though the body were preparing for great burdens. Tracing all these astonishing changes, he could not help but recall the principle of Peter Parker, the hero of his favourite Spider-Man comics: "With great power comes great responsibility."

After April 1984 the Guru personally initiated no more than ten or fifteen disciples; now the Master simply observed in silence as Shailendra gave diksha[91] to the new ones. Over the following three years he came to Benares two or three times a year, usually staying for several weeks — and all those who sought initiation into Kriya had to arrive on those days.

In the presence of the Guru he gave initiation into Kriya to four hundred disciples. Satyacharan Lahiri began to redirect questions to him: "Let Shailendra answer," — and listened silently, attentively. Sometimes, after the last visitor had left, the Guru would discuss alternative replies: "This question could have been answered differently, consider such a variant…"

[footnote 91: Diksha — initiation into a religious tradition or spiritual practice. Diksha and Shiksha Gurus — there are two equally respected types of guru: the diksha-guru, or initiating spiritual teacher, and the shiksha-guru, or instructing spiritual teacher. One may have many spiritual teachers as instructors, but the initiating spiritual teacher, the diksha-guru, is one. He formally initiates the seeker into the line of transmission (tradition, sampradaya) by means of a mantra, touch, breath, or any other mystical, secret means. He takes upon himself the responsibility of leading the disciple through the stages of sadhana toward liberation — in this life or in the next. This guru is the mentor of those souls who are destined to undergo the process of education through future incarnations.]

The replies allowed only a passing assessment of the possible outcomes of the lottery of life, but the source of Truth was one, and he was learning to read Her dim crystal sphere for those who were ready to listen. At times this occupation was wearying and thankless.

The Guru's delicate yet unfailingly precise instructions widened his mind, allowing him to see the countless facets of individual karma, always tied to the general, collective one. The colossal mirror of karmas reflected all destinies, including his own. The Master had to make sure that the successor was capable of catching the reflections of the higher Truth, filtered through the medium of knowledge, and of refracting those gleams for those who were trying to make them out. The light of Truth was always different: at times blinding and cutting, at times soft and soothing, but always unchanging.

…Several times he went into the temple and discreetly touched the statue of Lahiri Mahasaya. But he felt only the coolness of the marble.

**A Voice Found, the Last Film, and a Head on Fire**

After his return from Puri he tried to warn his acquaintances from the Kriya Yoga group that the techniques being taught at the Karar ashram were utterly ineffective. His own experience confirmed the importance of studying the original teaching of Lahiri Mahasaya: those practices worked and differed cardinally from what was being taught at the ashram in Puri.

Choosing his words carefully, he described the atmosphere that prevailed at the ashram, the dubious morality and financial machinations of the administration of this spiritual establishment, and all of this in no way conducive to spiritual development. However, most of his listeners reacted warily to his stories, and even with hostility. Some even suggested that he had been driven out of the ashram, and that everything he had said was merely the bitterness of an outcast. Moreover, most members of the group were quite satisfied with their spiritual attainments. In their view, there was no need to challenge the customary balance of things in search of "true yoga." Only Siddhoji Rao, the founder of this small community and his good friend, believed him at once, though their conversation took place much later.

During this time his family moved several times — first to a farm, then to rented lodgings — and yet S.R.S. sought him out, and both were sincerely glad of the meeting. Siddhoji Rao was well aware of the meagre yogic progress among the members of their group — or rather, of its almost complete absence. For several years S.R.S. had himself been following the yogic techniques received at the Karar ashram, without particular success. But upon meeting he instantly saw the dramatic, inspiring changes that had taken place in his younger friend — and he listened attentively to the account of disappointment, of the miraculous finding of the Master, of the new practice and the astonishing experiences. The yogic path of which the two of them had so recently been reading and arguing had for one of them become a reality. When they met again, he was already a Guru, and S.R.S. asked him for initiation. The old friend became his 25th disciple, and some time later brought his wife, who also received initiation into Kriya.

…The members of his household did not even suspect that he had become the successor of a yogic tradition. For many people such things have no practical value, since they do not fall under the category of understandable achievements. Unless, of course, they bring commercial success. But he had no intention of engaging in self-promotion or attracting followers; Kriya did not work that way.

…He now left the house quite rarely, preferring not to leave his room without weighty pretext. Perhaps it was precisely the anonymity that began to draw attention to him.

Shivanath Vyas and his younger brother Upendra lived nearby, and they were the only contemporaries with whom he kept company in those days. Their father was a deeply spiritual man and practised meditation.

That day he needed to get to the market and hoped that someone might give him a lift; Upendra Vyas drove past and offered to take him along: "Only let us stop at my place for five minutes, and then I shall drive you over." In the house they were met by Upendra's father, visibly agitated. Holding a garland of flowers, Vyas the elder bowed and touched his feet: "Please, accept me as your disciple!" To say that he was surprised was to say nothing at all. Having heard out the story of Vyas-ji, he did not know what to think. It turned out that two weeks earlier, in meditation, Vyas-ji had received the following revelation: it had been told to him that in fifteen days, at a certain hour, a young man would enter his house who would become his spiritual mentor.

On the appointed day Vyas the elder waited on the threshold of the house, and everything happened exactly as had been foretold.

What was taking place seemed beyond the bounds of reality: a friend's father suddenly touching his feet, asking for diksha… Of course, he gave Vyas the elder initiation, and soon Upendra also asked for it. Then yet another friend of the Vyas family, called Shastri-ji, received initiation and became his faithful companion in various adventures.

Vyas-ji and Shastri-ji enjoyed an unimpeachable reputation in the town, and rumours of their initiation into Kriya Yoga spread quickly.

"If they have become disciples of this Guru, then there must be something in this young man!" Word of mouth ran without a hitch, and people began coming to see "this guru."
