Watching the steady stream of humans rushing straight into his room, his relatives could not conceal their astonishment: the unemployed dreamer had suddenly turned out to be incredibly popular, and visitors treated him with the greatest reverence!

His very first initiation into the practice, however, he had given even earlier, while visiting his cousins in Gwalior. There he had again seen Bhardwaj Dube, with whom he had been acquainted since his teenage years. That day Bhardwaj had heard his old friend speaking with people, and was struck. The one he remembered as a shy youth now astonished by his impeccable and original logic, his unusual ideas, his confidence and manner of bearing. Bhardwaj knew little of yoga; he simply needed friendship and support — and this kind and sincere fellow thus became his first disciple. He never could master the Khechari mudra, however, unlike Vyas-ji and Upendra, who mastered this most important part of Kriya. After this, Vyas the elder's life changed perceptibly. If before the initiation into yoga he had been gravely ill, then soon after beginning the practice he was completely cured and was filled with even greater spiritual zeal.

The incontestable presence of inner strength, charisma, infectious laughter, and an excellent sense of humour drew many to the young Guru, though he tried to impress no one. Usually, during spontaneous darshans, he was in fine spirits and joked a great deal, which in itself soothed and pacified visitors. Nevertheless, there existed a certain unseen line that no one dared to cross — he was a Guru, and that alone bespoke dignity, sincerity, and purity. Through him the power of the parampara manifested itself. By compelling people to think in a new way, he spoke to them of the wholeness of Truth, helping them to find the courage to pursue spiritual goals in the midst of the familiar everyday.

This was an interesting time. Some visitors came merely for a brief acquaintance, but returned again and again, as if drawn by a magnet. He had nothing against this. People who came to consult him on personal matters were impressed by his ability to resolve any situation and to offer unexpected yet practical solutions. The unusual answers and views provoked, made them reflect, and those who were ready to think and to change came back again. And began to take an interest in yoga.

Answers were given to the most difficult questions, the most intractable problems were analysed and resolved, the very essence of a person's life trials was uncovered. He could…

instantly analyse any topic, and the conclusions were weighty and truthful. His subconscious knowledge deepened exponentially with every question. The duties grew also. With each new initiation the Guru took upon himself responsibility for the disciple's future, and this commitment was for life. Enthusiastic followers were not encouraged; nevertheless, he was always glad of fellow travellers.

The varied discussions gave food for reflection and illustrated the complex palette of human characters and archetypes. Each new visitor projected onto the Guru's "inner screen" a complex network of karmic ties. No one need be recruited — people are already filtered by their own karmas, which brought them to yoga, to the teacher. Or, conversely, took them away to other variants of fate…

He was young, and nothing human was alien to him. As a fan of Marvel comics, he eagerly awaited the premiere of the Superman film. On the day of the premiere, having bought tickets, he stepped out of the cinema for popcorn, dressed in a black T-shirt, black jeans, and showing off cowboy boots with stacked, slanted heels. He was spotted by two elderly disciples; one exclaimed, "Guruji!" — and both Pandits prostrated themselves right there on the pavement at his feet. Dressed in traditional dhotis[92] and kurtas, grey-haired gentlemen in their sixties and seventies were touching his polished cowboy boots, asking for blessings.

[footnote 92: Dhoti — traditional men's garment resembling loose trousers. A piece of cloth is wrapped around the hips and legs and tied in a knot at the front or back.]

A crowd gathered around them instantly, expecting a spectacle or a scandal — something was clearly brewing; otherwise why would two Pandits be prostrate before a young man in jeans? He could scarcely persuade them to rise. Perhaps the passers-by took him for Superman, appearing on the streets of Gwalior, but it was doubtful that any comic-book readers were among the gawkers. Jokes aside, this was the last film he watched in a cinema. Wherever he went, a crowd spontaneously formed, and all of this was extremely uncomfortable, unnecessary, and made him keep away from public places. The sudden popularity wore on his nerves.

Meanwhile, the inner and outer changes continued, and some were quite unusual. At night he practised in a small room on the roof, and his mother would usually wait for him below to make sure he had eaten. She was troubled by his new "hobby"; she believed that all these long meditations, late awakenings, silent abstraction, and absent-mindedness had altered him greatly.

That night, still filled with bliss and stillness after intensive practice, he came downstairs and sat in an armchair without turning on the light. He wished to prolong this indescribable state. His mother entered the dark room carrying a glass of milk and a warm flatbread — and dropped the plate with a cry: "Your head is on fire!" Springing to the mirror, he looked at his reflection: above his head shone an aureole of golden flame. He quickly switched on the light. His mother was severely frightened and bewildered; he tried to calm her: "It is only a trick of the light, not flame, you imagined it!" Thus did he first see his own aura, and at once began to experiment with it. To learn to control the bright luminous outline took time, but in the end he learned to switch it on and off at will so as to continue conversations with visitors and friends. Otherwise, gazing at the bright halo above his head, people fell into a state of trance.

From time to time he could display the aura to some of his disciples for support and inspiration. In silent awe they would contemplate the bright nimbus, and he would casually remark, "It is only a side-effect of practice…"

Over the years the colour of the aura changed: at first a bright gold, it took on a greenish tint, and its shape also changed. For the last twenty years it resembled a Shivalingam and was a dazzling white. Charged by advanced pranayamas, the aureole pointed to ongoing changes in the development of the brain and bore witness to the highest level of yogic mastery. But this did not mean that one could relax and rest on one's laurels.

**Karmic Lessons**

One evening they were sitting with the Master before the temple in the courtyard. The reading of the Gita had ended and people were leaving the satsang[93] when an unexpected guest came in. Old, lame, half-paralysed, he moved with great difficulty, leaning on the shoulder of a woman who accompanied him. Becoming uncommonly severe, the Guru did not even glance at the visitor. Trying to bow, the man fell, breaking his face on the stone floor; blood streamed from the old man's chin and nose.

A wave of compassion swept over Shailendra, and he half-rose to help the cripple. The Guru, however, remained dispassionate and said coldly, "You have paid your respects, now leave." Then, addressing the visitor's companion, he added, "Take him home."

The old man limped, dragging the paralysed leg, and some time passed before the woman practically carried him out of the courtyard.

[footnote 93: Satsang — traditionally a gathering in the presence of a spiritual guru (sat guru) for discussion of the principles of Truth and other important concepts and ideas. Nowadays satsang is also called any gathering in which discussion of the spiritual, meditation, or teaching takes place. It may include the singing of kirtans or philosophical discussions on dharma.]

Turning to him, the Guru said: "You were overcome by compassion. But you do not know this man. He was a cut-throat who passed himself off as a priest, luring pilgrims into deserted places where he robbed and murdered them, and threw the corpses into the Ganga. This scoundrel killed more than three hundred people at Kashi Karvat. And now he reaps the karmic consequences of his deeds, having lost everything, including his health. He is cared for by his widowed daughter… The suffering of others stirs compassion in us — this is wholly natural. But first try to see the causes of human misfortune; they are never accidental." What had taken place made him reflect on the extreme tangle of the web of human fates. Can one unriddle the substance of someone's character without knowing the whole history of his life and the circumstances that led to this or that outcome? The Guru saw human karmas and at the same time understood their roots. It was expected that the successor would also develop this faculty.

There existed methods of determining a predisposition to one or another kind of behaviour by analysing body type, the length of fingers, the shape of skull and toes. External traits could tell of a person's various tendencies. Broad hips in men, long or crooked fingers, luxuriant hair or baldness, birthmarks or strabismus were marks of previous karmas. The science of the link between outer appearance and inner characteristics had ancient roots. The Indian siddha-sages followed the ancient Vedic Samudrika Shastra[94], a treatise on "the knowledge of bodily features."

[footnote 94: Shastra — sacred book; a science. Shastri — one who holds a special degree permitting him to teach. In Sanskrit the word means "commandment, rule, manual, compendium, book, or treatise." The Shastras are predominantly post-Vedic literature, appearing roughly after 500 BCE.]

However, to look into the depths of the human heart was the specialty of an entirely different science, requiring a developed inner sight. The Master taught him to see human karmas.

…Two years later the former cut-throat died a terrible death. The daughter, having gone to work, left the electric stove in the kitchen turned on. Trying to reach for something, the old man lost his balance and fell chest-first onto it. He could not get up, and was slowly burned alive. The Master did not remain indifferent to the troubles of the old cripple's daughter, a modest schoolteacher, and helped her with money until she found a permanent post in a state school. The karma of this woman was quite a good one.

Another story illustrated a different variety of karmic entanglement. The town of Shivpuri, where their family lived before the father's retirement, was beautiful, green, and peaceful. The father's high post provided them with life in a great mansion with serving staff. One of the fifteen servants, a thin, ghost-like man with very dark skin, was called Devi Lal, and his story they first heard from one of the servants.

On one not-too-fine evening Devi Lal died, but the next morning, during the cremation, he unexpectedly came back to life. Strewn with garlands of orange flowers, wrapped in white funeral cloth, Devi Lal suddenly sat up on the funeral pyre, staring blankly at the crowd of mourners — just before the burning torch was about to be touched to the wood. Lavishly seasoning the story with details, Devi Lal readily told it to anyone willing to listen. The children especially rejoiced, never ceasing to wonder and to ask questions.

…On that fateful evening, when Devi Lal had lain down after supper, two dark-skinned and menacing men in identical white loincloths suddenly appeared in the room and ordered him to rise and follow them. The frightened Devi Lal tried to protest, but he was roughly dragged from his bed with a huge fishing hook that one of the strangers stuck into his cheek. The other began to beat him with a staff studded with thorns. From the unbearable pain Devi Lal saw nothing of his surroundings, and it seemed to him that this torture lasted an eternity.

They arrived at a strange place where they were met by a grey-bearded man, surrounded by bright light, filled with dignity and inner strength. He said: "This sinner has been appointed to suffer longer; he has not yet exhausted all his sins. Why have you brought him so soon? Throw him back!" One of the fierce escorts yanked the hook from Devi Lal's mouth, the other kicked him in the rear, and he fell into an abyss, coming to himself on the funeral pyre. His buttock permanently lost sensation in the spot where the kick had landed. As proof, Devi Lal would stick a needle into his own flesh without blinking, and became a true hero of the children — not because he had risen from the dead after nine long hours of death, but because he could endure pain. All of this seemed to the children extremely thrilling. Sometimes Devi Lal's unique abilities were demonstrated to visiting relatives: "Look, I can drive a needle into him and he will feel nothing!"

When the family moved to Gwalior, he once met one of the former servants from Shivpuri and inquired about Devi Lal's fate. The old servant had been ordained to suffer, and so it turned out — Devi Lal fell ill with leprosy, the family turned away from him, and he was forced to beg. His death was lonely and painful. The one who had sent him back into his body had seen to it that the old servant received his due. The story was not a very cheerful one, no moral could be drawn from it, since absolutely nothing was known of Devi Lal's past. No one could say for what he was being punished, and indeed there was no longer any need for the investigation.

The laws of karma are inexorable, but they may be softened by certain actions. One such method is yoga, capable of changing the lives of the worst of criminals. But to be granted such a chance, one must have karmic predisposition, luck, and the grace of a Guru. He did not yet know with certainty how burdensome his own past had been, but was filled with resolve to pay off whatever debts there might be, and try not to incur new ones. Of course, with each new initiation, with each new disciple, yet another bond was formed, but this karma was of a special kind.

**The Last Meditation of an Aghori**

Benares has always been known as the city of the Aghoris, although these fearsome-looking ascetics may be met all over India. Though Aghoris are not outcasts, people approach them only in the most difficult and unusual life circumstances. But here, by the burning pyres on the ghats of Varanasi, one could meet practising Aghoris in their "natural habitat." Smeared with ash, adorned with amulets of human bone, shaggy and half-naked sorcerers and their strange paraphernalia attracted the attention of tourists hungry for photo-shots.

Watching the Aghoris with cautious curiosity, he tried to understand how one could come to know God while living amid death and filth. Throughout India the path of Aghora[95] is associated with darkness and black magic and is considered "impure." It seemed that the aim of all these horrifying rituals contradicted the very notion of purity, so revered in orthodox Hinduism. How else could one call life in cremation grounds, if not a continuous celebration of death and denial of the light?

[footnote 95: Aghora — a spiritual tradition arising in Northern India. It is a simple and natural state of consciousness without fear, hate, disgust, or division by discriminating signs of religion, race, or sex. Though Aghora is universal and transcends the particularities of religion or culture, the teaching does not lend itself to easy categorization. Its followers maintain that every time people "discriminate," their selfhood, their wholeness, is limited, and is mastered by destructive emotions such as anger, fear, jealousy, greed, and lust. The chief postulate of Aghora is that at his root every human being represents an individual whole, one with divine consciousness. The Aghoris hold that, by learning to identify with one's own wholeness, one is freed from limitations. As a result, the energy previously invested in limitation may be redirected toward self-realization. Some scholars and researchers classify Aghora as a non-dualistic (Advaita) subdivision of the Hindu philosophical school.]

And yet, even the "dark" sadhana strives toward the same goal. Like yogis, Aghoris seek liberation from the cycle of reincarnations. The road to moksha is one for all seekers: through awareness of the individual "Self" and its identity with the Absolute. The Aghoris hold that all opposites are ultimately illusory; "pure" and "impure" do not exist. For them life and death are merely two sides of one coin, and so they accept darkness by way of various rituals. For it is part of the Light. Striving to comprehend non-duality, they overcome social taboos and reach altered states of consciousness. Living among corpses, smoking ganja and consuming other intoxicants is a radical approach, but it gives some sense of the illusory nature of customary attitudes. These people might well be walking one of the roads leading to truth. And yet he did not believe that Shiva enjoys being worshipped amid stench and decay. In darkness, in the abyss of icy eternity — yes, but not among rotten half-burnt remains… Mahadev loves the cremation grounds, but certain rituals were simply disgusting. Moreover, how many true Aghoris were there among the sorcerers and tantrikas who appropriated this title in order to increase their income? The Guru said that real Aghoris are extremely rare, and they are powerful. The Master was acquainted with the most unusual persons…

In November 1985 Satyacharan Lahiri sent him to one such ascetic with the words: "A friend of mine, who has attained the heights of the Aghora path, is beginning his final meditation. You must visit him." To visit an Aghori, one of those people sitting on the ghats? Only years later did he understand: Satyacharan Lahiri wanted him to receive certain hints about the future.

One of the servants' children was charged with leading him, and he followed the boy through the narrow labyrinths of streets. At the door of an old house he gave the name of his Guru, and was invited in; chance visitors were not admitted to an Aghori in his final meditation. He went up the stairs. Sitting on the roof of the house, facing the Ganga, the old man was sunk in deep trance; his eyes were open and absolutely motionless. The ascetic appeared to be about ninety, wrapped in a shawl, with a woollen cap on his head; clean-shaven, he had a pleasant appearance. There was no trace in him of the ugliness or wildness usually associated with the cult of Aghora. From the old man emanated unusually powerful vibrations. Approaching the motionless figure, Shailendra silently bowed and quietly sat on the floor. He had been strictly told to say nothing that could disturb the ascetic, and simply to wait. The minutes dragged on extremely slowly, and he felt uneasy. Even being a Guru, he was utterly inexperienced in matters of preparing to leave the body.

Half an hour later the meditator finally spoke: "Who are you?" Naming his Guru, he added: "I am his disciple and follow the same tradition. Please bless me and, if it seem fitting to you, say something to inspire me."

The old Aghori was silent; nearly twenty minutes passed before he spoke. Once, when he had lived on the cremation grounds, he had ardently wished to see Parashurama[96], one of the eight immortal sages. Parashurama is considered the Guru of all Aghoris, so the desire of the still-young ascetic to confirm a spiritual link with "Rama of the Axe" was nothing unusual. Persistently questioning his own teacher about methods of invoking Parashurama, he persuaded his mentor to give him detailed instructions for performing the rite. For forty days he was to recite a mantra and perform a particular ceremony.

[footnote 96: Parashurama ("Rama with the Axe," Rama Jamadagnya, Rama Bhargava, and Virarama) — the sixth incarnation of Lord Vishnu. One of the Immortals, Parashurama is endowed not only with martial fierceness and valour but also with composure, control, will-power, prudence and patience. It was foretold that Parashurama would appear on earth at a time when evil would triumph and when the Kshatriyas, warriors endowed with weapons and might, would begin to abuse their power. It is held that Parashurama will restore the cosmic balance by destroying all kshatriyas twenty-one times over. In the Mahabharata he is presented as the mentor of many heroes: Bhishma, Drona, Rukmi, and Karna.]

"On the fortieth day Parashurama stood before me, and I saw him as clearly as I see you now. On the earth behind the Avatar I noticed a Sri Yantra[97], drawn in red vermilion. When Parashurama vanished, the yantra disappeared with him, and on the surface of the ground, among the sand and fine gravel, there was not a trace of red paint…"

Tearing his gaze away from the river, the old man glanced at him sideways. The November sun stood high, and the Ganga shone like molten silver. The Aghori was silent, and it did not seem he would say anything more. Probably this was the appropriate moment to take his leave.

[footnote 97: Sri Yantra or Sri Chakra — a form of mystical diagram (yantra), used in the teaching of Sri Vidya. Nine intersecting triangles, representing Shiva and Shakti, surround a central point, the Bindu, the universal centre. The triangles represent the cosmos and the human body. All other yantras descend from the Sri Yantra.]

Unexpectedly the old man uttered: "You shall see — one day the Soviet Union will vanish, and after some time the United States will cease to be united. Now go." Greatly puzzled, he bowed and left, pondering the ascetic's last words; at the time he had no interest whatsoever in geopolitics or in the constitutional structure of the United States.

…The peculiarity of Indian philosophical schools is that information is often given without explanation, and the questioner must himself reflect on the obvious or hidden meanings. To demand an immediate explanation is considered improper, since realisation must be obtained on one's own, must be earned. To question an Aghori on the brink of non-existence about the concrete meaning of what he had heard and what had been foretold would simply be inappropriate.

The next day the old man was found sitting in the same posture, gazing at the Ganga with frozen, unblinking dead eyes. It turned out that no further visitors had come after his departure; he had been the last to speak with the Aghori. A "water burial" was rapidly arranged: the body of the Aghori was wrapped in white cloth, decked with garlands of yellow marigolds, and committed to the river in accordance with the custom for saints of his rank.

Did the Aghori bless him? There was no way to know; one had simply to keep on living. But why had the ascetic spoken at all of the Soviet Union and the United States? When he passed the words of the Aghori on to the Master, the Guru smiled: "You will know the meaning of these words when the time comes. Aghoris are known for seeing the future."

At that time it was impossible to imagine that such a mighty state as the Soviet Union would cease to exist. However, within five years the powerful citadel of socialism collapsed, and rather quickly. In those days ordinary citizens of the USSR could not travel abroad freely, but after the fall of the Soviet empire seekers of yoga began coming to India. Some became his disciples. Yes, this part of the prediction undoubtedly came true, and the changes touched him personally. As for the rest of the prognoses, the coming transformations, evidently, were still gathering before they descended upon people's heads. But already in 2021 the world watched militants and radicals storming the Capitol building in Washington; on the brink of large-scale civil unrest, the United States was becoming less and less united.

From time to time he reflected on all this, trying to decipher the codes of destiny. It seemed that the meeting with the Aghori had sown the seeds of his own fate, for the Guru had sent him to the departing ascetic for a reason. The Master knew: the destiny of the next, the Fifth Guru of the line would be linked with Aghora, and the blessings of a follower of this cult could be not only useful but prophetic. After this meeting, an interest in the life of Parashurama awoke in him — he was a part of the charade of his future. And he was not at all surprised, nineteen years later, to receive a personal message from "Rama of the Axe" himself, even though it came in someone else's dream. As for the mystical Sri Yantra, it came to him under astonishing circumstances, and indeed with precise instructions for its installation. This tantric three-dimensional diagram of the cosmos had its own important role in the play of his life.

Time continued to unwind its eternal thread; all that remained was to contemplate its patterns, trying to follow the windings of the yarn. Everything in the world was bound together and interwoven. Absolutely everything.

**Darshan of Vishvanath**

In March 1986, Benares was preparing for the celebration of the great Night of Shiva, and he decided to remain in the city to spend time with the Master. He had never been particularly fond of going to temples and tried to avoid public places during the great religious festivals, when the complex cocktail of human hopes and prayers spills generously into the air amid unimaginable crowds. The thick brew of thoughts and expectations boiled in the gigantic cauldron of the eternal city; millions of devotees stood in long queues at the temples, to lay flowers and offer prayers. But Satyacharan Lahiri insisted: "Since you happen to be here during Mahashivaratri[98], you must go for darshan to Vishvanath[99] Ji!"

In the morning, weaving through the narrow lanes, he set out for Vishvanath Gali, one of the oldest and busiest streets of the city. There stood the chief Shaivite temple of Mahadeva, known as Shri Vishvanath or Vishveshvar. Its domes, plated with a ton of pure gold from the Sikh empire,

[footnote 98: Shivaratri — "the Great Night of Shiva," observed monthly, but the chief celebration of Maha Shivaratri is held once a year, in February-March. One of the principal religious festivals in India, dedicated to the marriage of Shiva and Shakti.]

[footnote 99: Vishvanath — one of the names of Shiva, meaning "Lord of the Universe."]

gleamed brightly, while in the blue sky pigeons circled, absolutely indifferent to everything happening below. When he reluctantly drew near the end of the queue, the crowd was already quite dense. Trying to block the ragged currents of human emotion, he focused on the temple's history.

…Dedicated to the Lord of the Universe, Kashi Vishvanath had been built on the very spot where the first of the twelve self-manifested Jyotirlingams had appeared. Breaking out from the earth as a pillar of light, it shot up into the sky, beginningless and endless, like Shiva Himself. The cause of its appearance had been a dispute among the Trinity as to who was the strongest — then Mahadev had pierced all three worlds with the infinite column of light. Taking the form of the wild boar Varaha[100], Vishnu[101] rushed to dig under the column, while Brahma became a swan to fly up to its very top.

[footnote 100: Varaha — third avatar of Lord Vishnu; in the form of a boar he was sent to save the earth, which had been drowned by the demon Hiranyaksha. The dynasty of the Bharatpurs worshipped Vishnu, and this may explain the origin of the legend of the golden statue of a boar on the bottom of the pond by the royal Smashan.]

[footnote 101: Vishnu — one of the tridev (trinity), defender and preserver of the world created by Brahma. His role is to restore the balance of good and evil in troubled times. Vishnu has incarnated nine times in the form of various beings (avatars). It is held that His tenth and final incarnation will be the Kalki Avatar, who, according to prophecy, will bring an end to the Kali Yuga.]

Exhausted by fruitless attempts, Brahma lied that he had reached the summit of the pillar, calling upon the ketaki flower to witness. Enraged by the lie, Shiva took the wrathful form of Kala Bhairava[102]. Severing Brahma's fifth head, Mahadev cursed Brahma together with the ketaki flower. The Creator ceased to be worshipped, and the ketaki flower was never again used in offerings, and centuries later the situation had not changed. In India only a handful of temples to Brahma have been preserved. Watching the festive bustle, he fully endorsed Mahadev's decision: "The Creator has made nothing but chaos…" It was only ten o'clock in the morning, and many hours of waiting still lay ahead. As an offering he had bought a datura flower.

[footnote 102: Bhairava (or Kala Bhairava) — a Shaivite deity, a mighty manifestation of Shiva, principally connected with destruction. In Kashmiri Shaivism (the Trika system) Bhairava represents the Supreme Reality. Usually in India Bhairava is also called Dandapani ("the one who holds the staff in his hand"), the punisher of sinners. Another name of Bhairava is Svasva, which means "the one carried by a dog."]

…The Jyotirlingam is regarded as the pillar supporting creation, its centre, the axis of the world, around which the spindle of the planet revolves. But standing in the slowly moving queue, he felt no symbolic link between earthly and heavenly; this place packed with people did not at all resemble a sacred point of contact between heaven and the firm earth.

…The queue had been creeping along for more than five hours; the flowers in his hands began to wilt. By four in the afternoon he felt nothing but weariness and irritation. A small group of Tamils standing behind him began softly to chant the Lingashtakam, an eight-stanza poem of veneration of the eternal manifestation of Shiva: "…revered by Brahma and Vishnu, pure and shining, he destroys the sorrows which from birth pursue each of us."

Brahma-Murari-suracita-lingam
nirmala-bhasita-shobhita-lingam
janmaja-duhkha-vinashaka-lam
tat-pranamami sadashiva-lingam…

One queue was for men, in the other stood women, and all this joyously-stirred human mass, at first sight wholly chaotic but well-organised, gradually flowed toward the entrance of the temple. Hundreds of hands stretched toward the Shivalingam set in a square depression; mountains of garlands of yellow marigolds were repeatedly cleared away, baring the dark-brown glossy stone. Stunned by this sight, some pilgrims froze on the spot, and the temple staff had practically to push them out of the door to make room for ever more devotees. "Only one touch! Move along!"

Having laid down the flowers, he had barely managed to touch the moist surface of the Shivalingam when someone shoved him. Losing his balance, he fell, striking the left side of his chest against something hard. It turned out he was stuck in a small silver depression that surrounded the relic, and now lay directly on top of the dark glossy stone.

Two Brahmin attendants helped him up; in anger he turned to the elderly woman who had caused his spectacular fall — but she, in no way embarrassed, was already offering Vishvanath Ji her own offerings. He held his tongue; all that needed to happen had already happened. It remained only to push back through the crowd to the safe harbour of the Guru's house. Still irritated, feeling not the slightest spiritual elevation, he told the Master what had happened. Satyacharan Lahiri laughed like a child; never before had he seen the Guru so amused. Wiping away tears, the Master said: "It seems Vishvanath Ji has accepted you as an offering. That was a true darshan."

So then, he had offered himself to Shiva along with a datura flower, and this spontaneous offering had been initiated by none other than Shakti? Who else could have shoved him with such precision that he landed on one of the holiest relics of India?

Yes, Shiva had struck him in the very heart, and fateful vibrations pierced his whole being; whether this was an embrace or a taking, only the future would show.

At the Guru's insistence, from November 1985 to March 1986 he visited several other interesting places and people, and all of these visits turned out to be fateful. Mahadev's embrace at Vishvanath was the prelude to a personal audience, but all this was still to come.

**Pain, Bonuses, and Inner Time**

Sometimes the practice could last more than ten hours, and all of that time it was necessary to remain in the lotus posture. Such a duration of meditation was not obligatory — physical capacities vary among individuals — but those who strove toward the highest goal of yoga had to overcome discomfort. The boundary of overcoming could be stretched as far as ambition and motive allowed. The individual karmic predisposition to hard asceticism was part of the programmed life-plan.

He himself was filled with the determination to step beyond the boundary of known, well-studied, generally accepted norms, and this determination was accompanied by pain that could no longer be ignored. To withstand the sharp, gnawing pain in the joints and to endure the dragging spasms in the limbs required persistence and patience.

In its boundless variety, pain challenged the protective mechanism of the nervous system, which sends frantic signals to the brain. The legs folded in the lotus went numb, but the implacable pain, having first tested pelvis and back, eventually spread over the whole body. Counting his breaths, he remained motionless.

…Was complete immersion in the practice the key to success? Can progress be measured by the number of hours spent, or is quality more important? He knew: without quantity there comes no realisation of quality. Stimulated by the duration of practice, yoga slowly elevated the mind and deepened the experiences. The guarantee of success was regularity of sadhana, together with duration. At first, long hours of practice were less important than making it part of daily life — until yoga became the principal component of his life, displacing the other priorities.

In the "Autobiography of a Yogi" Yogananda mentions an interesting fact about Ram Gopal Muzumdar: the "sleepless saint" daily spent twenty hours in sadhana. This raised a puzzle: how can one practise for twenty hours a day if there are only twenty-four hours in a day? There was no one to ask. But the yogic texts said that, before attaining the results of sadhana, one must first perform the great austerity, maha-tapas[103], and he kept on extending the boundaries of the possible. From April 1985 the time of meditation increased to twenty hours. To squeeze the practice into the frame of a single day was impossible, and he established his own schedule. He slept after yoga, ate and exercised as long as he wished, and no longer confined himself to 24 hours. External, solar time governed the alternation of day and night,

[footnote 103: Tapas — in yogic practice means super-efforts leading to purification, self-discipline, and austerity. "Tapasya" comes from the Sanskrit root "tapa," meaning heat or energy, and may roughly be translated as asceticism or penance.]

but the ancient yogis knew of the existence of an inner time, to which organs, blood circulation, and brain impulses obeyed. And this time was based not only on biological cycles.

Falling into rhythm with the melody of inner time, pranayama sang to the body its lullaby, slowing the myriad neurons but not putting the organism to sleep. One ancient text, the Svacchanda-tantra, even specified the precise number of exhalations and inhalations: the outer day contained twenty-one thousand inhalations and exhalations; during the inner "daily cycle" there were six hundred breaths — and this is plain, non-yogic breathing.

The steady streams of Prana and Apana[104] filled his body, lengthening, expanding the hours cut out from the cloth of the everyday. The sense of Time was renewed: the body had become breath, no longer measured by hours.

Meditation established a new cycle of sleep and waking, and the biological clock adapted to the new schedule. The adjustment had done its work: now he could increase practice-time without cutting back rest. No one can stay awake twenty hours and perform the higher pranayamas without adequate rest. The Yoga of the Immortals was intense, and the body required proper restoration.

[footnote 104: Apana — one of the five pranas (life forces), moving downward and responsible for elimination, digestion, and reproduction.]

For the next six months he practised for twenty hours, then ate and slept like a dead man for 12-14 hours. He saw no dreams — all dreams exhausted themselves in the process of practice. Waking, he was ready to practise again. The problem of time was solved, but the factor of pain remained.

Stiffness, cramps, sharp twinges and aches became constant obsessive companions, whose irritating, tormenting company could only be waited out. At some point he understood: resistance is useless, and simply opened the gates to the invading armies of Pain. After some time (actually, after several years of Kriya practice) the blood circulation began to slow, the numbness of the body intensified until it reached the neck. And then… the pain went. Wholly assimilated by the body and dissolved as salt in the ocean, pain sated its hunger, and the organism accepted it: now the sensation of discomfort existed only in the mind. And then the whole body became mind, forgetting its bodiliness.

However, just to straighten out a leg after Padmasana took him about an hour and a half. The reviving of the entire "earthly space-suit" required still more time.

…Eight years later he wrote of touching the horizon, the thin line of contact between sky and earth, using a romantic metaphor. And yet this illusory line clearly marked off the worlds. Different realms intersected; inhalation followed exhalation, life met death. The conscious mind, like a lotus, sent its roots into the nourishing soil of the subconscious. With the death of the body, the journey of the mind

continued, and almost always the mind found a new vessel. Spent bodies again and again disintegrated into the elements, and each time the soul had to undergo its training anew. Yet there existed ways to preserve the flower of developed consciousness and the seeds of all memories without losing the body that sheltered them.

Kriya was the yoga of action, and progress in it was attained only by practice. It was performed by the mind, but the body was a necessary instrument, a complex apparatus. The words once spoken by Anandamayi Ma were still with him, and he continued to live according to this short but mighty mantra: "By doing, you will succeed."

As an action, Kriya had to be accompanied by Iccha[105] and Shakti. Iccha was will, resolve, the blade of intention. And the power, Shakti, came to one who called Her only voluntarily — for Her there were no marriages of convenience. Shakti could grant power only out of love. He who possessed Her was called Shaktiman, Master of Power. The presence of physical strength was one of the conditions of this transaction, for Shakti never chose weaklings. She came at the call of the Will. These three forces, these three factors, were indispensable conditions of the search for the Self, of Knowledge of God, of Unity, by whatever names these goals are called. He himself called it "Awakening."

[footnote 105: Iccha — will, desire.]

Divided into inhalations and exhalations, the movements of Prana formed the outline of Life. He stood at the centre of this ancient mandala[106] and observed the rotation of forces and elements, the flowing of the invisible but constant stream of consciousness. What awaited him: immortality, the transfer of mind beyond the earthly spheres, astral existence as a mighty spirit among beings like himself? It was useless to guess; anything was possible. For the moment the absence of pain was enough for him, and twenty hours of unbroken practice were a victory, an achievement no one could dispute. And, most importantly: these six months of unrelenting practice predetermined the events that followed.

[footnote 106: Mandala — a specific geometrical configuration of symbols, used for meditation and the practice of concentration.]

**Samadhi**

In 1985 the family moved back to town. The neighbourhood was rather secluded; only a few residential buildings stood beyond the engineering college. Whole hectares of green fields, where festivals and weddings were usually held, and the local smashan, insulated the residents from the distant urban noise. The place was almost idyllic.

It was the last week of September; the night sadhana felt familiar, almost routine, when, after the exhalation in Yoni mudra, something inside clicked and his mind flung itself open like doors. Surrounded by a bright ring of light, the dark sphere of Kutastha became ever brighter and larger until it wholly engulfed him — and he fell into the dazzling radiance. The heart and breathing stopped, consciousness entered every room and corner of the city, where people were going about their daily business. His mind bore witness to the consciousness of every living being; he could see (or rather, feel and know) all that they felt and knew. People were eating, defecating, having sex, weaving intrigues against neighbours and employers; the thoughts of some smouldered like coals of hidden anger. Sexual desires, the need to possess someone or something, were tangible, voluminous, and all of this cocktail of multiple emotions now poured into his wide-open consciousness. He was aware of all the emotions of others at the same time, and it overwhelmed him, as though he were being made to drink a mixture of nectar and poison, evoking both revulsion and delight. Moreover, the smell of humanity was simply unbearable. The reek of putrid mental projections and impulses outweighed the subtle aromas of piety, selflessness, and goodness.

The yogic texts described samadhi as bliss, but his own sensations were by no means pleasant. The romantic descriptions of the state of suspended animation did not say a word of the mad squall of emotions that overwhelmed him. Later he learned to pass beyond such states, blocking or avoiding the waves of human thought, the cacophony of others' emotions flooding his mind, but at that moment…

Acutely and entirely aware of himself, he by no means dissolved in omniscience and omnivision; he did not forget who he was. The core of individuality remained untouched, and at the same time, along with this sensation, he remembered everything he had forgotten and lived through in different bodies, in earlier births and lives — to forget them again was now impossible. All these memories once more became part of his being, and this knowledge filled him to the brim.

All colours, emotions, configurations and forms, kinds, sex and consciousness of the living and the dying, swept through him in sharp, stabbing waves. The body remained lifeless, breath and circulation absent, but he became aware of this only when he began gradually to come to himself. Circulation restored itself slowly, causing almost unbearable sensations. Years later, answering questions from disciples about this moment, he would ask them to picture a numb hand or leg and the tingling of circulation slowly restoring sensitivity to the limbs. And then to imagine the whole body in such a state, numb and lifeless, tormented by millions of pricking and itching tiny needles piercing the inner organs, bones, and nerves. To endure this was unbearable; he summoned all his will-power not to cry out…

His wristwatch showed that he had been in the state of suspended animation for an hour and a half (he usually marked the time before performing Yoni mudra). About what had just happened he had no doubt; he knew precisely what had befallen him. It was like the sensations of a traveller, exhausted by hunger and thirst, finding after a long journey a safe and hospitable place. The long agonising thirst had been quenched, and he was filled with relief, gratitude, and satisfaction.

He woke up late. His anxious mother was calling him to breakfast, which he had successfully overslept. Forcing himself to rise, he recalled the abyss that had opened up in the night, the range of his own emotions, the numbness of the body.

…The consequences of this first expedition into the Void were rather disturbing: he began to age rapidly. Knowing nothing then about the existence of higher pranayamas that permit mind and body to bear such trials, he felt unceasing fatigue and depression. Most of the time he lay staring at the ceiling, recalling the details of what he had lived through; all desire to practise yoga had gone, together with his appetite and his interest in life. Nothing could shake off the lethargy that had seized him.

His mother shook him, tried to make him at least eat regularly. She was convinced he was suffering from unrequited love, and he did not try to dissuade her. After some time he did try to tell her and his cousins about what he had experienced, but everyone looked at him with a strange expression. And then carefully suggested that he was suffering from a psychiatric disorder and needed special treatment. After this he shut himself up entirely. Only the Master could understand him, for such experiences lay beyond the comprehension of ordinary people who consider themselves normal. It was then that he began to discern the invisible line dividing laymen from spiritual seekers.

Some of the discoveries of the first samadhi were shocking; it was by no means a state of bliss as the uninitiated imagined it. In addition to the bodily changes, samadhi entirely altered the perception of reality. The freeing of the mind from its conditioned state laid bare the sad truth about the institutions of religion and power — these were phantoms. The postulates and truths transmitted by parents and other adults had turned out to be relative. He soon understood how thorny the path of spiritual seeking can be, if a yogin lives in a family — the unceremonious interferences and admonitions can become unbearable.

An inspired seeker could move in the stream of imagination while the people around remained immobilised. They mainly tried to stop the one who was changing before their eyes, to talk him (or her) out of this spiritual nonsense, calling self-realisation "a waste of time." Well-wishers appealed to the duty or responsibility of the seeker toward his family, society, country, even humanity, demanding total submission to generally accepted rules and norms. The impractical dreamer was shamed for shirking these ephemeral duties, accused of selfishness, and reminded of all that had been done for him. Another's progress made people feel their own incapacity for development, and this was unbearable to them. The breaker of rules and norms had to be warned, shamed, scolded, corrected, stopped. In short, the situation of those who have experienced yogic samadhi while living in the "normal" world was unenviable.

Once, taking a shower, he noticed grey hair on his chest — and suddenly everything fell into place: samadhi had been a meeting with death; the body had perceived the absence of heartbeat and breath as the sure sign of approaching end. And, hesitating, it had obediently begun to age, racing toward the prescribed and unavoidable disintegration. Under the influence of the state lived through, the mind had begun to consider any activity useless, and the ending — irreversible, since the body had passed through the gates of death. The mind needed a hint, a direction, that could return it to life, making it feel the pulse and the tension of muscles, the smell of sweat, the flow of blood… The body had to be taught again to live, and he stubbornly lifted weights and performed countless mahamudras[107].

[footnote 107: Mahamudra — in Sanskrit "mudra" means "seal," so "Mahamudra" literally means "great seal." In Kriya yoga, mahamudra is a complex action involving the simultaneous performance of a seated posture combining bandhas (energetic locks), mudras, including khechari mudra, and the movement of attention along the energetic centres of the body.]

At first even rising from bed was hard, but unrelenting physical exercise stopped the progressing ageing, and even some of the grey hairs darkened again.

Samadhi was not a destination but the next chapter, a step, the beginning of a new journey.

**Trick or Attainment; the Lost Meaning of Samadhi**

Shri Satyacharan Lahiri first entered samadhi when he was well over fifty. By that time the Master's father, Tinkori Lahiri, had already left the body. When asked about samadhi, the Guru never went into details: "You will know all that is necessary when you experience it yourself." Of course the Master was right, and the brevity of his answer was supported by the well-known allegorical parable: a virgin present at childbirth cannot fully understand the meaning of what is happening. Without understanding the events that preceded the birth, all the feelings and emotions of the mother, by observing someone's labour it is impossible to feel what it means to give birth to a child. And the same applies to the transcendence of the mind, the state in which consciousness passes beyond all, except itself — and becomes at once everything.

It is known that after the first samadhi, every time he closed his eyes, the Guru could see his own soul, the Jiva. It appeared in the form of a golden human figurine, only a few centimetres in size, radiating light.

About cases of samadhi the Guru did indeed speak, and once told his listeners a story which had happened in Benares at the end of the 1930s. A sadhu, having arrived in the city, began to

request permission of the British authorities to conduct an experiment. This ascetic intended to remain underground without food or water for six months and asked for an official guard, which would confirm the authenticity of the trial. Permission was granted, and the sadhu shaved all the hair from his head and body so that no insects would lodge in it. Then he meticulously cleansed his intestines and stomach by various prakshalanas[108], so that not a trace of organic matter would remain inside. Afterwards the sadhu drank a little ghee to lubricate the gastrointestinal tract, and got into the pit dug specially for him. The underground was closed with boards and a guard of soldiers, appointed by the local authorities, was posted. The sadhu entered the state of suspended animation.

[footnote 108: Prakshalana — Sanskrit word meaning "cleansing"; yogic techniques of cleansing the digestive system, including the whole gastrointestinal tract.]

After six months the improvised cave was opened and the quite alive ascetic was extracted; this fact was documented by the British authorities, who usually monitored such cases. Because of the absence of sunlight the sadhu's skin had yellowed; there was still not a single hair on his body. He looked freshly shaven, and even his nails, cut six months earlier, had remained short. The sadhu's helper or disciple began carefully to massage his neck and shoulders, pressing on certain points and whispering a mantra into his left ear. Forcing the ascetic's teeth apart, the assistant poured a little ghee into his mouth to start and cleanse the intestines. Having become extraordinarily sensitive to sunlight after the long confinement, the sadhu spent several days in total darkness. Every day the door was opened slightly, and the assistant gradually raised the curtains, and at last the sadhu came out of the room. It took about a week for him to begin walking without help. Several weeks later he was able to speak, gained renown, and grew wealthy, receiving numerous donations.

This sadhu had heard of Lahiri Mahasaya and therefore agreed to meet his grandson, Satyacharan Lahiri. Over lunch and tea they spoke for quite some time. Satyacharan Lahiri asked about the techniques that had allowed the sadhu to remain alive in a breathless state for six months, about the thoughts and emotions experienced in the state of suspended animation. The answers were quite honest: "I felt absolutely nothing; my state was like a coma. All the functions of the body ceased, and, unlike yogic samadhi, the mind was empty and dark. I had no thoughts or emotions whatsoever, only the absolute darkness of non-being…"

The personal history of this man was remarkable. At thirty he had lived through a tragedy and, in deep depression, had decided to put an end to his suffering by throwing himself down from the hill of Chitrakut. At the summit, to which hundreds of steps lead, stands the temple of Hanuman, and on that day, unable to cope with his emotional trauma, the unhappy man almost ran up. As he approached the edge of the cliff he was preparing for the leap when someone called out: "Hey, brother…" A stranger who had come out of nowhere altered the course of events with his call. An unremarkable sadhu in orange robes appeared to be in his sixties; he looked like an ordinary wandering sadhu but had read the suicidal thoughts of the young man. "If you really mean to die, you should not leave the body in such an unworthy and primitive way. Come with me, and I shall teach you to die properly…"

So the despairing man became this sadhu's disciple and was instructed in the art of dying by stopping all functions of the body, including breath and the circulation of blood in the veins and inner organs. The mentor explained how, by pressing certain points on the neck and at the base of the skull, one may "sever the head" and arrest the blood supply to the brain. At that time the new convert was young and physically strong, and this allowed him to bear the colossal loads on the heart and nervous system during the risky "experiments."

In India under the general category of sadhu fall all who do not meet social standards and criteria. Such people wander about, doing quite strange things in pursuit of earnings or in search of the Almighty.

Strictly speaking, Satyacharan Lahiri's new acquaintance had never been a yogi, since he had practised no discipline other than the "art of dying." But the ability to die and revive by act of will was indisputable and very similar to the yogic art. The general public saw no contradictions in this; a miracle was a miracle, and people were not interested in the motives or aims standing behind such austerities. Everyone wanted to see a man who had conquered death — and, paying tribute to his courage, people left generous donations. They literally showered him with money and called him a "great siddha." And indeed he had achieved the impossible — who else could boast of being able to die and return to life? Although the staged "death" was not the result of yogic tapas, it required great courage.

…In the course of the conversation the sadhu said: "You have an entirely different way of attaining samadhi; you abide in a true yogic trance, your consciousness expands, and you experience astonishing things. Therein lies the difference between our states. When I enter death, the state is like deep sleep or oblivion; I see neither dreams nor visions; I have neither revelations nor any special wisdom."

Undoubtedly, the experience of death endowed this uneducated man with a particular wisdom, but did not give him highest Knowledge. The secret of Khechari he did not possess, though he had heard of this mudra — he had not been taught it, for his kind of "death" did not require Khechari. The secrets of his knowledge the sadhu shared with no one, though almost 80 years later it became known that there existed a certain rare herb growing in inaccessible jungles. Properly prepared and taken in the right proportion, it induced a state resembling death. Combined with particular mantras and pressure-point massage, the magical herb could halt all physiological processes without ceasing life itself.

No less important were the techniques of subsequent revival of the body, which required the presence of a knowledgeable helper. The art of dying was a complex science and demanded a profound understanding of the process. Why should the living cross the threshold of Death uninvited at all, and then return? What seemed a trick, a fakir's gimmick, was an investigation of one's own capacities, limitations, and aspirations. For yogins, this death-like samadhi was the gate of the portal of Knowledge, leading to absolute fusion with the world.

…In 1990, visiting Chitrakut, he recalled the Guru's story of the "never-dying" ascetic and inquired about him among the local sadhus. Some remembered the story, but the man had disappeared many years before — perhaps he had died of natural causes. There was, of course, the intriguing possibility that before his final journey the sadhu had used his tried method and remained between worlds, in a state of non-life and non-death. But how long could the herbal elixir keep the body in suspended animation? There was no one to answer these questions.

…Satyacharan Lahiri also told of another case, again happening in Benares and documented by the British authorities. A yogin spent several months in samadhi underground and came out of the pit in which he sat alive. The site of his samadhi was covered with a stone slab and earthed over, and over it a field was sown with wheat. After the harvest the ascetic was dug up quite uninjured.

The authorities methodically registered such cases, but some stories are known only by the accounts of witnesses. In 1985 one of the Guru's disciples related that during construction work near one of the ghats workers had found the body of a yogin. The man sat in the lotus posture, his body looked undamaged. Almost all the muscle-mass was well preserved, although the skin was ash-grey. The man was half buried in earth, since the walls of his underground refuge had been damaged in the excavation. At first everyone was so terrified that they feared to touch him, but then one of the workers did the most foolish thing — tried to open the yogin's mouth with the end of a crowbar. From the corner of the ascetic's pressed dry lips blood emerged, his eyelids slowly parted. Drawing a deep breath, almost in a whisper, with effort moving his cracked lips, the yogin uttered: "What year is it now? Who is ruling now?"

One of the workers, who had not entirely lost the gift of speech, answered: "Baba, we have democracy, the government is in power; there are no kings any more." With visible effort the yogin straightened his legs out of the lotus posture: "So this is what it has come to…" Some time later the workers pulled the yogin out of the pit and helped him to his feet. Naked but for a loincloth, he was tall and thin, but did not look emaciated. Having taken a few unsteady steps, he entered the river up to his waist and stood there a long time, as if performing a ritual ablution. Then he plunged into the water and did not appear again. The stories were only stories; in Benares there were millions of them, forgotten or many times retold. But the narrator himself was in charge of that construction work and knew these workers well — simple people incapable of inventing such an unbelievable story and, what is more, of agreeing on the details when retelling it.

…Another case happened with a famous saint, a woman by the name of Ram Sia Sarkar, who lived near Gwalior, not far from his old cottage. One of his acquaintances, Surya Kaant Sharma, showed Ram Sia a photograph of a yogin riding a white bull, and she demanded a personal meeting with him. When they first met, this woman was in her thirties. Although she was revered as a saint, Ram Sia prostrated herself before him, reverently touching his feet. In Indian villages there is still a custom: when a young woman eats, she covers her face so that her father-in-law shall not see her chewing. Ram Sia Sarkar's attitude toward him was just as respectful.

She told her story. In the village of Tarot, beside the temple of Hanuman, there is a small cave where Ram Sia spent a year in the state of samadhi. When he asked about this experience, she quietly replied: "The spirit of my Guru, Shankh Baba, was with me all that time." She remained underground without water, food, or sunlight, while the villagers without break, twenty-four hours a day, sang kirtans beside the cave. This concert lasting a whole year guarded her, ensured her safety, and confirmed the authenticity of the seclusion. Before entering the cave, Ram Sia addressed her devotees: "I shall come out after a year, naked and dishevelled, but do not be frightened. On that day it will rain and there will be a thunderstorm…" And so it came to pass. After a year, naked, covered only by her own long matted hair, Ram Sia appeared before thousands of people who had come to see the miracle. A few minutes later the storm broke, and the downpour began to pour over the streets, the people, and the animals, washing the cobwebs and dust from Ram Sia Sarkar's ash-grey skin.

This saint had never practised the secret Khechari mudra or other yogic techniques, but the spirit of her Master was always with her, sustaining her through the long trance. Childlike and innocent, Ram Sia could see and sometimes predict the future. Therefore local politicians often visited her ashram, seeking blessings before elections. She did not experience the constant bliss of samadhi, but her words and actions were righteous.

Once she came to him, asking for a blessing before

parikrama. That day she was wearing thick woolen socks, and he remarked with a smile that after a 21-kilometer walk they would surely tear. She replied, “Oh, I will walk lightly, without touching the earth…”. No socks can withstand a multi-kilometer walk, but Ram Sia’s socks remained without a single hole…

…The hidden meanings of the well-known episode described by Yogananda in the *Autobiography of a Yogi* were revealed to him only many years later. Having first met Babaji in Ranikhet, Lahiri Mahasaya spent seven days in a cave in a state of continuous intense samadhi. It was unclear how such a thing was even possible, if Lahiri had not previously performed intense yogic practices – his nervous system and body were absolutely unprepared. Nevertheless, Lahiri Mahasaya spent seven days in a state of suspended animation without any serious consequences! In Lahiri’s own diaries, nothing is mentioned about this. One could, of course, assume that Babaji’s power extends to disciples, protecting and supporting them, but this would sound far too contrived. The physical body lives by its own laws; no metaphysics can turn the weak into the strong. Without preliminary preparation, enduring samadhi is practically impossible, and he knew this not from hearsay. However, Lahiri Mahasaya’s diaries offered a clue: before entering the trance, Lahiri was given a little oil to drink, and he underwent a thorough purification. Perhaps this was the preserving, saving antidote that allowed him to withstand the most powerful currents of energy and return from samadhi unharmed?

…In the twentieth century, the meaning of the word “samadhi” changed radically. It began to be used as a respectful term applied to deceased politicians, public figures, and other respected people: “He entered samadhi, here is the place of his samadhi.” This has not the slightest connection to yoga. Samadhi, the brilliance of its mystery, and the aura of admiration that surrounded the ascetics of the past, were now borrowed and widely used to honor those who had achieved high status and wealth, those who lived for the gratification of their senses and desires, rather than separating themselves from them.

He often thought of the Masters who stepped into immortality, choosing the continuity of existence. They contemplated the infinite existential spectacle, having passed through yogic death in order to never cease the cognition of Life.

**Visions of the Future**

In November, about a month after the memorable night of the out-of-body experience, he saw what could be interpreted as a vision of the future. Static, colorful images came independently of the state of samadhi; there was no expansion of consciousness. Rather, it was a side effect of the trance he had undergone. He saw majestic palace buildings, but their location remained a mystery; there was only some subtle hint that this place must be somewhere near Vrindavan. A few days later, the next vision came, and he viewed the set of still frames again; they resembled fragments of a bright kaleidoscope. The full panorama was hidden, yet details and fragments could be discerned.

All three visions occurred around noon. He had barely turned the page of a new issue of *The Phantom* comics when his consciousness switched and threw him into a time tunnel. Initially disoriented, he took it for hallucinations, but the visions repeated, and each time the portal opened for about half an hour. That was enough to recognize the frames of the future. It seemed someone was holding a magnifying glass to his eyes, bringing the scenery closer, and there was no doubt: this was a slideshow of disparate scenes from his future. All three times, the same frames appeared with varying degrees of detail, but with new aspects. He saw palaces with domes and arches, an old pond and a large tree, unfamiliar faces and some animals, and all of this was so intriguing that he wanted to share the impressions with someone. However, family members were completely unsuitable for this. A couple of months later, during a visit to Benares, he told the Guru about what he had seen. There were several other disciples in the room, but he felt not the slightest awkwardness, so great was his desire to share. It seemed only the Guru believed him, but no one dared to express skepticism or doubt. After darshan, everyone present went up to the roof. There was a kind of custom – before heading to the Ganga for a bath, the disciples would massage the Guru’s back; he loved this procedure very much. During the massage, the Teacher suddenly turned to him, saying: “Yes, you will live in a place resembling a palace. Only it will be a shamshan, and many people will pass by its gates. Some will come to you. And if you continue to diligently practice yoga and maintain celibacy, then by the age of forty-four, the sign ‘Om’ will form in your brain.”

He listened, not daring to ask for clarification; imagining life at a shamshan was completely impossible, and he took these words as an allegory. The Teacher’s words about the sign “Om” also sounded like a metaphor. Perhaps it was a hint at supreme wisdom? What else could the sign of Creation in his brain mean, and how could it be discovered there?..

Yes, there was a story about a woman who sang the name of Lord Rama as a mantra all her life: “Ram, Ram, Ram…”. After her cremation, it was discovered that the divine name was “engraved” on the frontal bone of her skull; natural, finest grooves formed the letters in Sanskrit and Urdu. Such was the consequence of diligently performed mantra practice and the highest devotion – similar cases are known, and no one disputed their significance. After all, India is a land of wonders. But to imagine that, as a result of yoga practice, the sacred symbol of the Absolute could appear in his own brain was beyond any fantasy.

As for the vow of celibacy, he took these words as a direct instruction. To no one except him had the Teacher ever recommended adhering to abstinence. Besides, most of the students were married householders. In general, the Guru never prescribed a specific lifestyle to anyone. Knowing human nature and the laws of karma well, Shri Satyacharan Lahiri did not interfere with them, except in urgent cases. Nor did the Guru predict the future; his methods were more subtle. Seeing the best path of development for a particular person, the Teacher assessed the totality of the person’s chances, abilities, and predispositions before giving recommendations and hints.

After the Teacher’s departure, he had to continue guiding students, and he used a similar approach. Interference in someone’s destiny could affect the natural course of events even at the level of indirect predictions, and the subtle threads of karmas should not be entangled. Time itself changed the patterns already manifested on His loom, and these ornaments were called “destinies.” They never repeated the cycles of a previous life exactly, and yet the evolution of the soul depended on decisions and actions performed in the past, influencing the present and future. The Guru could only recommend and advise. And people, ghosts, and even Gods bore responsibility for the choices they made.

Karmic paths were solitary routes, though they passed through the busy crossroads of collective destinies.

**Meeting on the Train**

…The relationship between him and his father was always good; perhaps not openly tender, but loving and respectful. Himmat Bahadur Sharma did everything possible for the family and, despite his demanding work, managed to give his children unconditional love, support, and the necessary guidance. When, shaken by the death of his elder brother Mahendra and torn by contradictory uncertainty, he informed his parents of his decision not to begin a military career, his father did not object. “It’s up to you…”.

Times were different; parental expectations were not so high. Families of the middle and upper classes did not push their children towards corporate, government, or military careers; parents did not try to raise “achievers.” Regarding the choice of profession, a more or less relaxed atmosphere prevailed in the family. His father never tried to set any of the children on “the true path” and believed in his youngest son. In essence, his father shared his interest in yoga, supporting his progress on the spiritual path. The first person to whom he demonstrated Khechari mudra was Mr. Sharma senior, who understood its significance. The respected judge himself had practiced asanas for many years and was aware of this complex and mystical mudra; he knew that Khechari was not some trick, but a yogic achievement.

The first book on yoga he read was his father’s *Gheranda Samhita*. The family library contained many sacred texts of Hinduism and most of the Puranas. He devoured them all greedily.

…On September 16, 1984, Himmat Bahadur Sharma died of cancer at the age of 72, and this was a most severe blow to him. Feeling orphaned, he concealed his own grief, trying not to aggravate the pain of his mother, brother, and sisters.

A year passed. He was visiting his elder sister in Lucknow and had to return to Gwalior by train. Having gone to the station, he hoped to buy a ticket without a prior reservation, but he was unlucky. At the ticket counter, he was cheerfully assured: “No problem, we’ll find something!”, yet every single berth was taken. Nevertheless, he boarded the train and stood in the aisle with a small suitcase, preparing to spend the night in the vestibule. There could be no question of asking strangers to let him sit on the edge of their berth. Suddenly, an elderly man called out to him: “Son, you can sit here.”

“Thank you for your kindness, I’m fine…”. The man insisted, and he accepted the generous offer, sitting on the edge of the berth, trying not to take up too much space. While making his bed, the stranger asked where he was going.

“Returning home, to Gwalior.”

“Oh, a judge lived there, a great man! He saved my life and made me who I am. Everything I have achieved in life, I owe to him…”

“What was his name?”

“Judge Himmat Bahadur Sharma. Do you know him?”

“He passed away last year… I am his youngest son.”

The chance fellow traveler burst into uncontrollable sobs. This was completely unexpected. He waited until the man calmed down. Finally, dabbing his tears with the sleeve of his kurta, the old man began to speak.

Several years ago, he had been framed and became the accused in a complex legal battle. It was said that the judge had most likely taken a large bribe from the plaintiffs, and his case was hopeless; he would surely lose and end up in prison.

The judge was Mr. Sharma, who examined all aspects of the case and delivered a just verdict – the accused was found innocent. After the hearing, the judge summoned him to his office: “We both understand that fighting a syndicate is difficult. The people who tried to frame you are corrupt and dangerous. They are unlikely to let you resume your business in this city. I will give you a letter of recommendation so that you…”

...could start their printing business anew in Jhansi."

Acquitted of all charges and armed with recommendations, this man moved to Jhansi, began again from scratch—and succeeded. The judge’s fairness and support not only saved him from disgrace and bankruptcy but also paved the way to success and prosperity.

…He stood quietly by the window, watching the thickening darkness and the flickering lights racing past. If his father had not delivered a just verdict back then, he might now be traveling with a bitter and broken man. Or with an indifferent man who might have demanded that the conductor throw the boy without a seat off the train. Instead, a chance fellow traveler had magically become his benefactor. The old man insisted that the son of the respected judge lie down on the berth and sleep.

“Thank you, sir, I am fine as I am. Please, rest; I will sit here, at your feet.” His heart filled with warmth, and for the first time he felt that someone was watching over him, that he was under protection. Ancestors help the living in many ways. What remains after people pass through the gates of death? Only the memory of their deeds, the echoes of how they treated others. The incident on the train seemed staged, as if the old man was obliged to deliver his father’s message to him—to remind him of honor, justice, compassion, and the significance of every action.

…The old man slept, wrapped in a gray woolen shawl.

Flickering shadows danced on the walls of the compartment, following the rhythm of the train’s motion. Half-asleep, he thought of his father’s uncompromising, unwavering honesty and innate moral ethics, and also that, though a judge by profession, Himmat Bahadur Sharma had never, in his humanity, judged anyone.

**The Promise**

As a rule, important life events are milestones on any person’s path. People measure the passage of time by promotions, professional achievements, or relocations. Disgrace or illness, joy, triumph, marriage or the birth of a child, divorce or the death of loved ones mark the calendar of every life, evenly distributing peaks and troughs of emotional surges between them. Once someone asked about the life milestones in his own destiny, and he answered without hesitation: every moment was and remains a bright flash. So it was, and surrounded by bright flashes, he lived each moment of the present totally, from which the wholeness of being was composed. The awakened mind could concentrate on the minutest details of every second of life, fully present within them.

Life was at once both whole and porous, like a pebble at the bottom of a river and the river itself. Time flowed both through him and over him. Of course, there were also important, fateful events, though the past seemed to him a half-forgotten dream. Living each moment is important: an endless stream of impressions and emotions replenished the lake of consciousness. The expression of fleeting Time, emotions were precious; their bubbles enlivened the monotony of existence, and all the experiences of biological and astral beings were pores on the skin of Time; life seeped from them like sweat.

Practice had smoothed over many memories, yet he could return to the feelings and emotions whose seeds had been sown many years ago—and harvest the fruits that had grown from them. Incidentally, those fruits turned out to be neither sweet nor sour.

One of the strongest feelings he had ever experienced was the relief after fulfilling a promise. One might speculate about the spiritual dimension of such an experience or the importance of the act itself, but the moment of fulfilling the promise was beautiful, though a little sad…

His father was still working in Shivpuri, with only a couple of years left until retirement, when one of his brothers approached him with a delicate but urgent request. His father’s brother was a contractor and constantly in need of money; he urgently required funds to secure a new contract. That day, their sister, Sharda Pathak, lent her gold jewelry to the contractor brother. The father gave his word to his sister as a guarantee for the loan’s repayment. It was agreed that Sharda would be paid interest, and the gold would be fully returned after the successful completion of the project. The jewelry was her dowry, a family heirloom, and she was very proud of it. Under the guarantee of the father’s word, the uncle received over half a kilogram of gold.

…Over the years, it became clear that often, when a relative is the creditor, large sums are never returned. If money is borrowed from those with whom one has no familial ties, the debt is almost certain to be repaid. The uncle’s business failed; all projects and commercial schemes collapsed, and the aunt’s gold sank into the river of time. The contractor uncle never fulfilled his obligation—perhaps because he never restored his financial standing, or perhaps because he believed that debts to relatives need not be repaid. But it was the father’s word that had guaranteed the transaction between brothers and sister.

The uncle never spoke of compensating for the gold, and the father took this painfully, recalling the failed deal and broken promises with bitterness, blaming himself. It was his assurances that had convinced his sister to part with her dowry.

Over the years, significant changes occurred in the family, mostly problems related to ongoing financial difficulties.

In 1984, while visiting his father in the hospital where he was being treated for cancer, he gave him his word that he would return the half-kilogram of gold to his aunt. Fulfilling the wishes of the dying is considered very important, so that the spirit, burdened by unfulfilled obligations, does not remain in the lower realms.

The promise became a persistent reminder of unfinished business; year after year, during the ritual of remembrance for the departed, he mentally reaffirmed to his father his intention to keep his word.

Sharda Pathak, his aunt, had always helped him. Especially when he was searching for his palace. She did not consider his dreams and plans strange or insane. Together with her husband, she unconditionally supported her nephew, providing shelter and giving love, even though his character was not an easy one. Along with four cows and two dogs, she took him in when he returned to Mathura from Sarnath, and encouraged him during his temporary stay at an ashram in Vrindavan. But most importantly, she continued to believe in him when he finally moved into his magical palace, which more closely resembled the ruins of a horror film.

…Over the years, her memory had turned into a handful of dry leaves, and it was hard to tell what exactly she remembered and what she had completely forgotten. When the aunt was widowed, her daughter—his younger cousin Devahuti, who had accompanied him on many adventures and escapades—invited her mother to live with them at the *smashana*.
