Frail and thin, in a fine cotton sari, the aunt would sit in the sun by the house, blessing some visitors who stopped to bow to her; the touch of her light fingers was special…

Thirty-two years had passed since the conversation with his dying father. His agricultural activities had brought him enough means to finally pay for half a kilogram of gold, and there was no reason to delay repaying the debt. There could be no more procrastination; the aunt was already bedridden. That day, arriving in Mathura, he returned the gold to her, adding four gold bracelets of his own as a token of gratitude and the promised interest she had never received. The aunt was very weak and not quite herself, yet she understood the reason for his visit. Accepting the small, weighty bundle, she said quietly, “You are a very good boy…” The gesture was more symbolic, for in the place where the aunt was soon to depart, gold was of no use to her—but a word was a word. Completion brought a feeling of lightness, and he treasured that feeling. His father’s last wish had been fulfilled, and Sharda Pathak’s valuables had been returned to her; memories of the past had become fertilizer for the nascent future and nothing more.

In one of the poems he wrote in 1993, it was said: “Time has no emotions, but every emotion has its time.”

**Blessings on the Path.  
The Saint of Boiled Potatoes**

…While in Benares, he decided to visit his elder sister in Lucknow. It was already dark when the train unexpectedly stopped. Some passengers began gathering their luggage and disembarking, intending to walk to the city; one by one, they disappeared into the deepening twilight. A group of five or six elderly people were questioning the conductor about the reason for the delay. It turned out that the railway tracks ahead were badly damaged, and repair work would take several hours. Lucknow was not far, and everyone decided to head towards the city. Picking up his travel bag, he followed the others and began walking along the railway tracks. It was a moonless, cool night; a strong wind blew at their backs, and in the pitch darkness not a single light was visible, as if they had been swallowed by the darkness. The elderly man walking ahead was breathing heavily, someone sneezed, the gravel crunched rhythmically underfoot—only these sounds were discernible in the night.

Suddenly, a distant whistle of a steam engine sounded, and the Guru’s voice rang out in his head, loud and clear: “Get off the tracks immediately!” He shouted, “Get off the tracks!” and everyone scattered. They had barely taken seven or eight steps when a huge iron garland of carriages thundered past, washing over them with a wave of hot air. Due to the direction of the wind, no one had heard the approaching train; moreover, the engine was running with its lights off. Cold sweat broke out on him. Exhausted by the sharp surge of adrenaline, people sank to the ground right where they stood. They thanked him, but he fell into a kind of stupor and could not utter a word. Mentally, he bowed to the Teacher.

When a diesel railcar appeared, one man from the group, a former railway worker, called out to the driver: “How far to Lucknow?”

“Are you all from that train? Climb on, I’ll give you a lift.”

Climbing onto the open platform of the railcar, he and his companions rode in silence, holding onto the steel handrails; the distant lights rapidly approached, and within twenty minutes they were in the city. By about three in the morning, he reached his sister’s house, where he slept for ten hours straight. He had no dreams, but he heard the Guru’s voice: the words were indistinguishable, though they sounded soothing.

…Once, while visiting his aunt in Mathura again, he called on his cousin to join the search for the palace that was soon to become his home. The only thing left was to find it. Intrigued, the cousin was glad for a distraction from daily routine and ready for any adventure. Wandering through Vrindavan, they came upon dilapidated estates, abandoned ashrams, and half-ruined temples, but none of the places fit the category of a palace. The image of majestic structures had been clearly imprinted in his memory since the very first vision in November 1985, and he was determined to find that place. Intuition told him: the palace was somewhere nearby; he only needed to read the signs of destiny correctly, generously scattered all around.

…That day, they were waiting for a bus to Mathura. The heat was unbearable; an elderly woman peered out from a small tea stall: “Boys, come inside, or you’ll get scorched. When the bus comes, you won’t miss it; it will stop right here.”

Inside the stall, they were immediately given a cup of sweet tea and boiled potatoes with salt. He tried to pay. “Oh no! I don’t take money from children.” Looking more closely at the elderly vendor, he saw that her aura was remarkably bright. He had already seen many saints whose astral halos were clearly visible, and from time to time he experimented with the bright radiance of his own aura, but the spectrum of this elderly woman’s aura was impressive. An almost blinding halo above her head sparkled and shimmered with neon light, and he realized that this woman had seen Krishna Himself: in the radiance emanating from the vendor, His shadow was discernible.

Realizing that he not only saw her radiant halo but also understood its meaning, the woman smiled at him. Bewildered, he could only ask: “How did you manage…?!” She playfully snapped her fingers: “Just like that…” This brief dialogue would have baffled any witness, had there been any.

Marked by the constant presence of the dark-blue God, who, as is known, is rather selective in His contacts, the elderly woman lived extremely modestly, continuing to brew tea in a tiny roadside stall on a dusty verge, boil potatoes, and feed the hungry. Having moved to Vrindavan, he visited her almost every day, and the woman always treated him to traditional tea and a boiled potato sprinkled with coarse salt…

When he was leaving, she came to say goodbye and, to his great surprise, the woman who had seen Krishna Himself bowed and touched his feet. And she offered 51 rupees – the traditional offering to a Guru. It was the warmest of all the farewells in his life. She was well past eighty, and their meeting turned out to be their last. The life of the old vendor embodied the Buddhist saying: “Before enlightenment – chop wood and carry water. After enlightenment – carry water and chop wood…” The simplicity of people who had attained the highest levels of realization amazed him more than the spiritual heights they had reached.

Around the same time that the saint of the Boiled Potato was treating them to tea, he encountered another woman, and the memory of this small episode always brought a smile. He was in Vrindavan again, waiting again for the bus to Mathura, when an elderly beggar woman approached, asking for money. He said regretfully, “Mother, I would give you money, but I have just enough for the fare to Mathura.” The beggar woman immediately pulled out a one-hundred-rupee note: “Son, if you need money, take it!” He smiled: “Mataji, your blessing is enough.”

His path continued to be marked by unexpected encounters, and often the ability to bestow spiritual gifts belonged to the most unremarkable characters.

The Taste of a Mango Drink and the
Subtle Art of Begging

In the absence of his brother Gyanendra, he stayed with his maternal uncle, who lived in a government-allocated house. There, people visited him daily, for he was a Guru and could give advice on many practical matters. His uncle never inquired about the strange influx of visitors, and everything went smoothly. Officially unemployed, he intended to remain so, devoting himself exclusively to yoga, without burdening himself with a career or family obligations.

A cousin arrived from Mathura, and they trained for hours, lifting kettlebells and barbells. The intense exercise stoked their appetites so much that within a month they had spent all their money. Their budget dwindled to 15 rupees, but asking his uncle for money was improper and even humiliating. The financial future looked rather bleak, and they joked that tomorrow they would sit by the roadside and beg, but today they desperately craved a fresh mango cocktail.

A rusty scooter sped them to the market, and they ordered two drinks, 5 rupees per glass, and 10 of the 15 rupees were spent. Slowly sipping their cocktails, they stretched out the pleasure: tomorrow was unknown, the mango season was almost over, and they had no urgent business.

Enjoying the last sips, they noticed a sobbing boy, no older than eleven. Holding a stick with balloons tied to it, the little vendor was crying desperately, wiping his tears with his sleeve. “Little brother, what happened?” It turned out that he had earned five rupees for the whole day and was going to buy flour for flatbreads, lentils, and medicine for his mother, but had somehow managed to lose the money. With the money, the hope for dinner and medicine was lost…

He and his cousin exchanged glances; it turned out they both had the same thought. He took out the last five rupees and gave them to the boy. Instantly ceasing his sobs, the boy stared at them in disbelief, and then, hardly believing his luck, clutched the coins in his hand and ran down the street, the balloons floating above him marking his triumphant dash.

With empty pockets, they headed home. On the way, the petrol ran out, and they had to push the scooter the last two hundred meters. Yet they were singing songs, and their mood was excellent.

The next day, a visitor arrived whose son had been in an unpleasant situation a few months earlier. Somehow, he, the Guru, had managed to help this family. Bowing, the grateful father said, “You helped my son, I am immensely grateful. Please accept three hundred rupees as a token of my appreciation; it would be a great honor for me…” For the first time, someone was trying to offer him money for advice. That was how it all began. People thanked him for support, an encouraging word, guidance, for the inexplicable but tangible healing energy that emanated from him, for the state of peace and tranquility they felt in his presence. Caring for the Guru was a tradition – visitors brought clothes and food, shared small sums of money if they could afford it. He reserved the right to accept or decline offerings. Sometimes, if the gift was offered by an unworthy person or someone clearly trying to buy his blessing, he refused. True grace was not for sale.

There were plenty of other gurus, charismatic and skilled orators, many of whom turned out to be calculating spiritual charlatans. The very phrase “Kriya Yoga” had long become a commercial commodity, and he was well aware of this. Having received the priceless gift of yoga, he shared it freely, for it was impossible to put a price tag on it. In most cases, he simply tried to help people; life is difficult enough, and everyone needs support. Having never acquired the habit of keeping a tally of good deeds over the years, he simply tried to see the logic in the chain reactions of karma and follow it.

He never met the little balloon seller again, but sometimes the taste of the mango drink reminded him of the light of hope and happiness, multiplied many times over by the prism of quickly drying tears in a child’s eyes.

…Delving deeper into causes and effects, one could discern the motives, goals, and results of any action. He had always liked the saying, “A beggar should not be choosy…” But was that really so? The art of begging was no simple matter, and the results could lead to unexpected consequences.

One day at the station, an old, dirty beggar stretched out a weak, trembling hand to him, asking for alms. An unusually strong fate line on the not-so-clean palm of the beggar intrigued him. A man with such a powerful line could not be an ordinary beggar; it contradicted the principles of palmistry. Obeying an impulse, he decided he would give the beggar all the money he had if the man asked for it. That day, his wallet held nearly ten thousand rupees.

“How much money do you need?” The beggar was stunned by the sudden turn of events, for usually people tried to avoid him and shooed him away. Hesitating, the old man asked for five rupees, and the specified amount was immediately given to him. He could have become richer by a full ten thousand if he had phrased his request differently…

Even after this fleeting encounter, the unusual fate line on the old man’s palm did not leave his mind, and he asked friends to find this person. They made inquiries, and an astonishing story came to light: it turned out the old man was not an ordinary beggar at all, but the leader of a large clan of beggars. A stern ruler, a ruthless tax collector, he commanded authority, and without his blessing, none of his “subjects” could marry. Everyone paid him a tax for the spots they were allotted. That day at the station, begging for alms, the old fox was pretending. In reality, he was conducting a weekly inspection, checking whether his subordinates were at their posts and collecting enough money. The old swindler had a profitable business, and the powerful fate line correctly indicated the strength of his character, his luck, leadership, and wealth… This curious encounter made him think about the self-imposed limitations people set. As a rule, when asking for specific things, people describe the results of their expectations in words. It is precisely these formulations that impose limitations on the results of wish fulfillment; a lack of imagination often guarantees a predetermined meagerness of fate’s gifts. The king of beggars could have said, “Give as much as you can,” but he limited his expectations to a limit of five rupees.

The universal laws of the Universe worked on a similar principle: instead of asking for specific benefits, it was wiser to ask the Gods for what they themselves were willing to share. Fate keeps generous gifts in the semi-transparent cellophane of chance.

The Successor

It turned out that the Teacher knew of the existence of the successor to the lineage long before receiving the letter signed “Shailendra Sharma, Gwalior…” This astonishing fact came to light in 2016, quite by chance, when a relative casually mentioned a meeting with Shri Satyacharan Lahiri in Benares.

In 1968, this relative, Mr. Sharma, worked as a senior engineer in the irrigation department of Benares. The city was actively promoting a training program for civil engineers, and mixed Indian and Sri Lankan teams of specialists worked on development projects, often traveling to remote villages for surveys. During one such trip, Mr. Sharma shared a room with a trainee from Ceylon. On the trainee’s bedside table stood a photograph of Lahiri Mahasaya, and he regularly offered prasad to the guru, then distributed the offering to his colleagues. This trainee dreamed of visiting the home of the famous teacher and asked Mr. Sharma to help him find the address of Lahiri Mahasaya in Benares. Thus they came to “Satya Lok,” the Teacher’s old house on Chausatti Street, and met Shri Satyacharan Lahiri, the fourth Guru and grandson of Lahiri Mahasaya. They visited him several times, and during one of these visits, in the context of discussing the future of this spiritual tradition, Mr. Sharma asked the Guru about the successor. Shri Satyacharan Lahiri replied: “A physically strong, excellently educated, and intellectually developed young man is preparing for this mission in Gwalior… He will continue after me.”

This casually told story shocked him: the Guru himself had never once hinted that he had known of him earlier. In 1968, the Guru was about sixty years old, and his future spiritual successor was only eleven; eleven years remained until their meeting. He recalled his first impressions of visiting the Guru’s house, the intense curiosity with which the elderly disciples looked at him. Perhaps it was explained by the fact that the Teacher had told them of the arrival of a potential successor, but at the time he was greatly embarrassed by the close attention of the old kriyabans¹¹². As it turned out, his coming had been predicted and expected. This story confirmed the suspicion that the Teachers of the parampara always knew both about the next successor and about the paths and time of departure of each guru.

All this might have seemed merely a hypothesis, but over time he became convinced that this was the actual state of affairs.

Five years under the Guru’s guidance flew by quickly; diligent practice brought the most fascinating discoveries. The essence of human hearts was revealed to him, and he was able to establish a friendly relationship with his own spirit, a being that announced its presence in the darkness of the Void.

¹¹² Kriyaban – one who has received initiation into the practice of Kriya Yoga.

the golden light of Kutastha. The spectacle was so captivating that it left no time for reflection on the nature and location of the so-called God. Whoever lived within him was omnipresent, and this discovery was not bookish knowledge; he realized it with his entire being.

…In the Guru’s house, students were permitted to read the diaries of Lahiri Mahasaya; sometimes he even practiced in the Master’s room, inhaling the legends and history together with the strangely dry, odorless air. Here, in this old house, four generations of masters had meditated and attained realization, and their presence was constant and powerful. The priceless revelations described by Lahiri Mahasaya in his diaries were staggering. No one had described spiritual experiences so profoundly; to leaf through the yellowed pages, to see the Master’s handwriting, was a great honor. The words, written nearly a century ago, were permeated with nostalgia; even the greatest spiritual achievements became the past, but he was here to continue, to learn.

Babaji chose for his first disciple to become known to the world the life of a humble householder. Yet the life of Lahiri Mahasaya, illumined by the mystical teaching, proved to be extraordinary. The son and grandson of Lahiri continued the tradition, but the power of Babaji’s blessing and the might of Kriya itself could be preserved in one family for only three generations. This was confirmed by an indirect, though well-known, fact: according to Hindu tradition, the last rites are performed only for three generations of male ancestors…

The fourth Guru, Shri Satyacharan Lahiri, represented the third generation of the dynasty. He was born on January 28, 1905, during the dark half of Ashtami Tithi¹¹³, in Kashi, in that very house where his famous grandfather had lived, taught, and practiced. Satyacharan was the youngest son of Tinkori Lahiri Mahasaya, the third Guru and eldest son of Yogiraj Lahiri Mahasaya. When the older children left the nest, Tinkori kept Satyacharan with him, and for many years, until his own passing, prepared him for the role of successor to the tradition. An undeniable master of yoga, Shri Satyacharan Lahiri was a man of knowledge, a Teacher and spiritual father to many. But above all, he was a guide to the realm of Spirit.

…One day he asked the Teacher: why do most people spend the greater part of their lives “asleep” and do not even wish to “wake up,” why among those seeking enlightenment are there so few who are realized? The Guru replied succinctly: “Because only a few wish to awaken, and not everyone among the ‘awakened’ attempts to attain realization.”

Finding common ground with the Guru was not always easy, although he was very democratic and seemed open and approachable. Having been widowed early, Shri Satyacharan Lahiri did not remarry; his adult children started their own families, and the Teacher was surrounded mainly by disciples and visitors. Often silent, not standing out much among the elderly students, he was a true spiritual giant. Although the burden of knowledge did not weigh upon Satyacharan Lahiri himself, he knew the delicacy of the process of knowing, and he taught, carefully observing each seeker. In accordance with tradition, the Guru gave exactly as much knowledge as the seeker was ready to absorb at his individual level of development and preparation. People came to Kriya Yoga for different reasons, with different goals and motives; their individual karmic predispositions also varied. Each student required a personal dosage of knowledge; otherwise, they could choke on it, in the literal sense.

Having become the successor after the Teacher’s passing, he continued to use the same methods, for he bore responsibility not only for the students’ progress but also for their failures. Sometimes regular practice could be interrupted by life circumstances; in rare cases, the inner demons of the practitioner himself could spiral out of control. In such cases, karma prevailed over yoga, and the student might cease sadhana, temporarily or indefinitely. All he could do was inspire, observe, and wait. As his Teacher had done.

**The Teacher’s Passing**

In the summer of 1986, Guru Purnima¹¹⁴, the day of honoring all teachers, was celebrated in the home of Shri Satyacharan Lahiri, and the small inner courtyard could not accommodate the guests. Ashok Kumar Chatterjee, one of the old disciples, presented his book *Puran Purush*, a biography of Lahiri Mahasaya compiled at the Guru’s request and containing numerous quotations from the Master’s personal diaries. After the hour-long presentation of the book, Shri Satyacharan Lahiri called upon the new Guru to deliver the concluding speech. Most of those present were disheartened, expecting another long speech, but he was brief: “The previous speaker has already said everything. I wish to emphasize the main point—practice to the utmost of your ability and attain Kutastha, receive the darshan of the immortal spirit.” These words became the culmination of the evening, and those gathered applauded with relief.

The uneven, jolting black-and-white footage of a documentary film captured the Guru half-reclining in an armchair, absorbed in his thoughts and clearly far away, and him standing beside the Teacher. Broad-shouldered, in a simple white kurta, with jet-black, closely cropped hair, he spoke with restraint but confidence. Having respectfully addressed the Guru and those present, he concluded his short speech with the traditional namaste gesture. Watching these frames years later, he felt a pang of nostalgia. To tell the truth, the atmosphere that day was not at all festive, though no one could name the reason. At one point, the Teacher asked him, along with several other disciples, to read the eighth chapter of the Bhagavad Gita and left the room. Later they learned that on that day the Guru was determined to leave his body, as he considered his earthly mission complete. However, the other Teachers of the parampara, or perhaps other higher forces, deemed otherwise. In the courtyard temple, Shri Satyacharan Lahiri stayed only a short while; his request to depart was denied, and he came out into the courtyard, sobbing like a child. Seeing him cry was difficult, and no one guessed the reasons for his distress. As a consolation, the Teacher was told the exact date and time of his departure: it was to occur six months later, on his eighty-second birthday. In the following months, the Guru weakened so much that he had to be carried upstairs; he could no longer move on his own. Yet the Teacher’s mind remained clear and sharp, and he continued to conduct satsangs and darshans.

Their last meeting was touching: he prostrated himself at the Guru’s feet, and the Guru placed his hand on his head, saying, “I am very pleased with your progress; continue. I have taught you everything I knew myself.” This was the last time he saw the Teacher alive. In accordance with the prediction, the Guru left his body on January 22, 1987, remaining in full consciousness until the very last moment and performing Omkar Kriya¹¹⁵, the supreme yogic technique for transferring the spirit to the astral plane. Two people were with him: his Bengali disciple, who looked after the household, and another person who had entered only a few minutes before Satyacharan Lahiri abandoned his body. The Guru’s relatives were absent, and the body was placed on ice to preserve it until cremation. After two days, there were still no signs of decomposition.

The fourth Guru of the Kriya Yoga lineage of Babaji, Shri Satyacharan Lahiri, was cremated at Manikarnika Ghat. According to custom, the bone remnants were collected from the ashes of the funeral pyre; they were tiny and few in number. This meant that on the earthly plane, Satyacharan Lahiri no longer had any karmic obligations. Although all four previous Gurus of the Parampara were great yogis, they were still householders, not recluses, and they were cremated; in India, only sannyasis are buried. Both Shri Yukteshwar and Yogananda Paramahamsa were monks, and therefore their bodies were interred.

…A couple of weeks after the Guru’s death, the tall black Shivalingam in the inner temple of Shiva developed a crack. Made of black kasoti stone with a mirror-like surface, adorned with the memorial inscription “Shyam Sundareshwar,” the Shivalingam was over two hundred years old and had stood there when Lahiri Mahasaya bought the house. Now a long crack ran across its surface; it was repaired, and those inclined to seek symbolism in such signs could interpret the event as they wished. In any case, the Fourth Guru had departed, yet the lineage continued. The fate and purpose of all the Teachers of the parampara were predetermined by Babaji himself; their appearance and departure occurred according to schedule.

Without the teacher’s support it was difficult, but he did not grieve excessively: the Guru’s passing was expected. The fact that the Teacher was to leave his body in six months was known in advance. And yet the Guru did not abandon him; when the Teacher’s advice was needed, he received it. At times it was enough to recall what the Guru had explained to him during his years of discipleship, and the essence of phenomena would reveal itself anew. Answers and hints were now stored in memory, surfacing upon request or responding to specific situations. Only careful analysis, an investigation of the very root of the problem, was required. Even during the Guru’s lifetime, he had stopped troubling him with unnecessary questions.

Sometimes people asked whether the Guru could take a new birth. This was extremely unlikely: Shri Satyacharan Lahiri was not destined to return to this world; he had fulfilled his mission. Now the Teacher could travel further and visit the myriad other worlds intended for those who have attained the heights of spiritual development.

Practice helped him cope with the loss, and he continued his sadhana for 14–16 hours. Yet the apathy was almost paralyzing; the inability to advance to the next levels of Kriya robbed him of hope. “I never found out many important aspects of Kriya… Who will teach me these techniques now?” At the level he had reached, higher yoga could only be discussed with advanced practitioners, but among the yogis known to him, there were no such people. All of this was extremely disheartening.

**“Om” and Other Signs**

The news of the Guru’s death reached him in Bengal, near Calcutta, where he was on family business. An ancient Kali temple in the town of Chuchura belonged to the relatives of his aunt’s husband. This was perhaps the only place in India where animals were never sacrificed to the Goddess, and therefore She was called the Merciful Mother there. Every ten years, the numerous clan appointed someone from the family to look after the temple for a year. Their turn had come to manage the temple affairs. Together with his cousin, he organized the daily puja and the distribution of finances. It was not so bad; he even managed to meditate in the temple every evening. On the day of the temple’s founding, when more than ten thousand people had gathered for the celebration, he finally underwent the janeu ritual, putting the “Sacred Thread” over his shoulder. This made him a brahmin officially. The telegram about the passing of Shri Satyacharan Lahiri arrived twenty days after this event.

…All train tickets were booked, and he managed to reach Benares only on the twelfth day after the Teacher’s cremation. On the thirteenth day, the final rites were performed. One of the disciples invited him to Asansol, back to Bengal. The community of Kriya yogis there was eager to see the successor, and the time for the official

...the timing of the introductions could not have been more fitting. The visit turned out to be rather exhausting: every day around 150–200 people gathered for darshan, and for the first time he received them alone, without the Master. People came to pay their respects to the new Guru, to see him in the flesh. A sharp, poorly concealed curiosity was seasoned with sincere devotion.

He was from the north, which came as a surprise to many, since all four of the Masters before him had been Bengalis. He was not related by blood to the previous teachers, and this too surprised, though it did not shock. The adult children of Shri Satyacharan Lahiri had little interest in their father's spiritual practices and were absorbed in practical matters, careers, and families.

...A few days later he noticed a group of five bearded men who would come and stand near the doors. All five were of the same height, fit, and dressed in identical short-sleeved shirts and dhotis. Like the other visitors, they listened attentively, examining him with curiosity, but their manner and gestures were subtly different from those of the others present. On the sixth day one of them approached and politely asked permission to see the soles of his feet.

Puzzled by the unexpected request, he agreed. The man produced a large magnifying glass and began the inspection. For almost a minute he stared intently at one sole, then the other, then turned to his companions and exclaimed in Bengali: "Achi! It is there!" Drawing nearer, his companions in turn began excitedly examining the discovery through the same lens. Intrigued, he waited patiently, hoping he had not become the object of attention of some strange fetishist sect. Then all five prostrated themselves before him.

"Please, tell me what you saw there?" — and they recounted a deeply striking story. Once they had been followers of a guru who had left his body at the age of nineteen. Before his death, that guru had told them that many years later they would meet a young yogin with the sign "OM" on the soles of his feet, and after that meeting their spiritual work in this life would be finished.

He did not know how to react. One of the group said: "We have seen many yogins before we met you. And for all of us this is a great day." Asking for blessings, they took their leave, and he never saw them again. Back in Gwalior, he mentioned the incident during darshan. One of those present immediately offered to examine his feet — and froze in astonishment: the capillaries beneath the skin clearly traced tiny "OM" marks. The glyphs on his right foot were in Sanskrit, while on the left the "OM" was "drawn" in the Bengali alphabet.

The disciples took photographs, and for a time the colour prints lay on his coffee table as a kind of curiosity. Life ran its course. Daily existence was filled with other, no less astonishing surprises and domestic problems. Though the discovered glyphs intrigued him, the spindle of life kept unwinding many threads of different events at once, leaving no time to dwell on the meaning of the sacred symbol upon his soles.

Ten years later, after many adventures and trials, when destiny had ceased trying to disguise its intentions, small whitish curving lines emerged on the nail of the index finger of his right hand, forming the sign "OM". When giving initiation he would usually "switch on" the disciple's potential chakras by touching their spine with this finger — and with every new disciple the mark on the nail became more distinct. If the intervals between initiations were long, the pigmentation would fade. Reflecting on this phenomenon, he suddenly recalled the words of the Guru: "After your 44th birthday, the sign 'OM' will form in your brain."

Before his 45th birthday, he discussed an (almost scientific) experiment with his disciple Dr. Dwivedi from Agra. At Dr. Gupta's institute in Agra he underwent an MRI procedure while holding his tongue in Khechari mudra. The investigation was intended to document the fact that Khechari mudra is in fact attainable and had been performed personally by him.

The original idea belonged to a great-grandson of Lahiri Mahasaya, Banamali Lahiri. Professor B. Lahiri, himself an advanced yogi, had advised establishing clear criteria for the Kriya practice required in this line, along with documentary proof of one's own attainments. Banamali Lahiri was acutely aware of the trends in the yogic "markets", and seeing the real intentions of some self-proclaimed "teachers", he often said that too many false Gurus claim false yogic attainments. They break the lives of their followers, promising salvation and liberation while having no authority or right to be spiritual guides. For such "masters" yoga was merely a profitable business. Even Shri Satyacharan Lahiri himself warned his disciples against creating any organisations, rejecting the overtures of numerous "followers and founders" of groups who wished to secure his blessing to teach Kriya practices and philosophy under the trademark of the Lahiri name. The Guru said that running such organisations requires enormous effort and a rigid timetable, which not only lowers the quality of spiritual practice but also negatively affects the ego and self-esteem.

...The MRI results surprised everyone: on the images one could clearly distinguish a sign "OM" formed above the region of the pituitary gland. The contrast shadows outlined the precise curves of the Devanagari glyph, though for the doctors it was simply a sign of normal frontal-lobe function. For him personally the scan was confirmation that Satyacharan Lahiri's prediction had come true, and he felt enormous satisfaction and gratitude — for the Master's faith in him, for the miracle of yoga, and for the fact that his progress was being confirmed. There are many interpretations of the universal sign "OM", but above all it designates the name of Shiva. The symbols that had appeared naturally on his soles and nails, and then in his brain, spoke of the grace of Shiva, for only Mahadev could brand a person with his own mark.

It was impossible to explain all this from the standpoint of logic; the only relevant reference was found in Patanjali's Yoga Sutras [footnote 116: The Yoga Sutras of Patanjali — a collection of Sanskrit aphorisms (or sutras) on yoga. There are 195 sutras compiled by the sage Patanjali, who synthesised and systematised knowledge on the subject of yoga. The Yoga Sutras speak of ashtanga, the eight elements of practice that should culminate in samadhi. The principal aim of the practice is kaivalya, the recognition of witness-consciousness as distinct from the cognitive apparatus. The modern yoga tradition considers Patanjali's Yoga Sutras one of the foundational texts of classical yoga philosophy.], in the chapter "Samadhi Pada". The commentary read: "He who stands beyond karmas, obsessions, and attachments is called a special Purusha and is marked by the sign OM…"

The power of the sound itself filled every cell of every living body, and all the flesh of the world was a myriad of notes of this eternal, primordial melody. The yogic body was a perfect instrument, with each new life performing a new score in an infinite symphony conducted by Time itself.

[footnote] Yoga Sutras, Samadhipada, shloka 27:
"The Lord is Purusha, distinct from others; afflictions, actions, the fruits of action and karmic consequences yet to bear fruit in the future do not affect Him ||24|| In Him is the seed of all-knowing, unsurpassed ||25|| This Ishvar [footnote 117: Ishvara — in the various schools of Hinduism may signify a personal, chosen deity, or the principle of supreme consciousness. The name is preferred mainly by devotees of Shiva; the analogous term "Bhagavan" ("Lord") is generally used by followers of Vishnu. Hindu communities differ in how they interpret the relationship between Ishvara and Brahman. Some assert that they are identical. Adherents of Advaita Vedanta hold that the "personal" Ishvara is a limited and inadequate conception of Brahman.] is the Guru even of the first (gurus), for He is not bound by Time ||26|| The word by which He is called is Pranava — AUM ||27||"

MRI of Shailendra Sharma performing Khechari — https://shrishailendrasharma.com/khechari

Kumbh Mela and the Scent of Karma

On the occasion of the Maha Kumbh Mela in February 1988, Allahabad was flooded with nearly 15 million pilgrims. Acquaintances of his disciple, Dvarka Prasad Shastri, had promised to find sleeping places and accommodation in one of the camp stations, and it was decided to take advantage of this opportunity. He was ready for a distraction from the monotony of his event-poor life, whose only adornment was the night sadhana that transformed the darkness of night into the brightest daylight. Accompanied by two other disciples, he arrived in Allahabad by train, then walked for more than six hours across the vast territory of the Mela in search of the right camp. He had seen great crowds before, but nothing could compare with the ocean of pilgrims arriving at the confluence of the Ganga and the Yamuna for the ritual bath. To witness such a vast assembly of believers, to be part of this event, was truly astonishing.

The Shahi Snan [footnote 118: Snan — the ritual of bathing or offering of water in worship, used to bathe the deity.] (Rajyogi Snan), the bath of the saints and their followers, is one of the most important days of the Kumbh Mela. Thousands of sadhus, religious leaders, and their devotees moved toward the river, on foot and on chariots drawn by camels, horses, and men. Swaying on the backs of slowly moving, lavishly decked elephants, showered by the sun-bright rain of marigold petals, the mahants of various ascetic orders towered over the crowds in their lofty howdahs, while marching squadrons of naked, grey-ashen ascetics led the procession to deafening music. The roar of trumpets, the whistles, the sound of mantras and the melodies of bhajans shook the air, and this cacophony of life's celebration issued from a vast invisible dome of the unborn sound "Om" that covered the valley.

That sound had begotten all beings, all inanimate objects and forms. Born from the seed of myriad fathers and the eggs of countless mothers, all the living were descendants of this Universal vibration; the Sound pierced them, and Its echo would now recede like the surf, now sweep back upon the shores of human bodies.

...The extreme sensitivity that had developed in the course of sadhana had endowed him with extraordinary perceptiveness. He watched the slow waters of the Ganga and the human mass on the riverbanks, smelled the scents of incense and food, inhaled the pungent fragrance of dust ground by millions of bare feet into the finely milled flour of destinies — and this whole existential cocktail of the rich elixir of eternity, mingled with the scorching fragility of fleeting Life, was maddening, intoxicating. The magic of yoga allowed him to see the endless train of incarnations: even here, at...

...the Kumbh Mela, spirits were entering the capsules of embryos already beginning their slow transformation into corpses, the preparation for the next transformation. At the moment of death the rind of the over-ripe fruit burst open, spilling the seeds of samskaras [footnote 119: Samskaras — mental imprints, unconscious and residual memories that form the basis of karmic impulses and tendencies. In Hindu philosophy, samskaras are the basis for the development of karma. Every action or intention leaves an impression, an effect, an imprint, or a samskara, in consciousness. The samskaras then await the volitional fulfilment of a particular individual in the future as hidden expectations, circumstances, or a subconscious sense of necessity to act. Samskaras manifest as tendencies, karmic impulses, subconscious impressions, the makings of habits, or innate predispositions.], unfulfilled desires, sorrows and attachments — and the draft of Time caught up and carried away the fragrance of the soul, of consciousness. All these bodies were sentences of Time, and human desires were wandering echoes through the rooms and labyrinths of fates. The intensity of urges and longings (often pedestrian, if not base) prolonged the duration of the prisoner's stay in the cell of the body. Tamed and pacified, they could turn the prison into a palace. Here, in the material world, spiritual progress depended only on motive.

...At last they found the right camp, and after a brief flurry of settling in they hurried to the river to make the bath in time: the day was the most auspicious for that rite. The water turned out to be icy, and bathing became a real ordeal, but once dry he felt magnificent. Toward evening the pilgrims began returning from the river, and he fell into conversation with one of them. It transpired that this sadhu was staying at the camp organised by a swami whose name was well known to him. Some time ago, in Benares, he had personally initiated that swamiji into Kriya on the Guru's instruction. The sadhu escorted them to the proper place, and the swamiji was overjoyed to see him. He was given a genuine VIP reception, including free lodging in a personal tent that more closely resembled a bungalow. By the standards of the Mela this was luxury. For two days they revelled in the atmosphere of the festival; yet, despite his occupation and the new impressions, his thoughts kept returning to the meaning of the ritual bath.

Picking up various mental waves, he began to suspect that the messengers of the Lord of Death — the Yamadutas [footnote 120: Yamadutas — astral beings, servants of the God of death, Lord Yama.] and other astral beings — who analysed human karmas and judged people by their deeds, tracked their "wards" exclusively by scent. The aroma of temple incense and the bittersweet smoke of burnt offerings was an ancient code intelligible to spirits and gods. And the "mental scent" was a marker, a tag, from which one could not be rid. All human karmas — past, present, and future acts and intentions — formed an enormous library, and the book of every fate was read by those responsible for the upbringing of souls. Whatever acts were committed, whatever thoughts arose, all of them were begotten by the mind, evoking a corresponding chemical reaction in the body. Every thought and emotion — be it anger, lust, love, or hate — created a unique scent that accompanied particular karmic actions. The unmistakable karmic fragrance became a personalised tag of the human being. Those who took the lives of animals smelled pungent and sour; murderers of men gave off a peculiar stench, like the smell of metallic rust; purity and piety were marked by the warm, soothing scent of the early monsoon. Sometimes the teachers had to track down errant disciples by it.

During the Kumbh Mela the constellations stand in a particular alignment, and the stars amplify the effect of the bath. By entering the river together with millions of other pilgrims, one can wash off the smell of one's personal karma, deeds, and thoughts. The molecules of one's individual aroma mix with the trails of thousands of other karmic fragrances, thereby throwing the astral pursuers off the scent. And the messengers of the Lord of Dharma can no longer distinguish sinners from the righteous. For a time they lose the trail, and then men are free of their surveillance. By having "washed" both good and bad deeds in the gigantic pitcher of the Kumbh Mela, one can confuse the karmic tracks. When human sweat mingles with the waters of the sacred rivers, a peculiar chemical reaction occurs. It is known that any physical body, with all its karmas and odours pleasant or otherwise, is seventy percent fluid. Any fluid is based on water, and water preserves the memory of previous experiences and thoughts. To bathe in the Ganga at an auspicious time meant also to mix the inner liquids of the body with the waters of the sacred river at the molecular level, since the person was immersed in the ancient, divine, and power-laden memories of the Ganga herself. For this reason the Kumbh Mela bath exerted a powerful influence on human consciousness and, accordingly, on the events of one's coming life. Of course, this theory of karmic scents was only a hypothesis, though a quite plausible one.

...After the fifth immersion the water no longer felt scalding cold to him. He was already coming out onto the bank when Dvarka Prasad Shastri, who had caught up with him, suddenly clapped his hands over his eyes. "What's the matter, Shastri ji?" — "Please, do not look that way, Guruji — several Punjabi women have stripped to the waist!!" Holding back laughter, he said: "Shastri ji, I am so grateful that you care about my purity. Please continue to watch them while keeping my eyes shut. Take your hands away only when the ladies are fully dressed and I am out of the danger of seduction!" At last, some five minutes later, Shastri ji released him. He glanced at the matrons shivering in their wet saris; they were deep in their sixties, but to the defender of his morality they still seemed to be potential seductresses.

...The time came to set off back, and the return threatened to become a real problem, since the railway stations and bus stops were still packed with crowds of departing pilgrims. Fortunately, their hospitable host kindly arranged a comfortable car to Chitrakut, from where they boarded a train to Gwalior.

The Kumbh Mela left an indelible impression: intuitively he knew that the trip marked a new beginning. So it proved; the coming April changed his life irrevocably.

Babaji

One of the Guru's disciples, who worked as a yoga instructor at the Hindu University of Benares, ran into trouble at work and turned to him, persuading him to put in a word with the dean. It was a fine pretext to visit Benares again, and in the last week of March 1988 he boarded the train. On arrival in Kashi he went directly to the University and spoke with the dean, satisfying himself that the disciple's career was in no danger. With that the official part of the trip was finished. A room had been reserved for him at the Kashi Mumuksha Ashram, where at noon a large group of participants in the yoga study programme came to meet him. The visitors left rather late, and he was able to begin his practice only at half-past eleven at night. Immersed in deep meditation, he lost track of time.

The night was quiet; sitting in the lotus posture, in deep trance, he did not at first hear the knock at the door. Annoyed, he rose from the concrete platform-bed, assuming it was one of the ashram's residents. It was around two in the morning; the main gates were already locked, and at that hour outside visitors simply could not get into the building. The switch clicked, and a dim lamp lit up beneath the ceiling. He opened the door. The broad-shouldered man standing on the threshold was about 170 cm tall, a couple of centimetres taller than himself. The stranger, of solid and muscular build but not at all heavy, wore a cream-coloured short-sleeved shirt and a dhoti in the Bengali style; his skin was neither dark nor fair, but had a slight golden tint perceptible even in the uneven, faint light; he was clean-shaven. The visitor appeared to be about thirty or so.

Not trying to hide his irritation, he asked: "What do you want?" — "I am the one known to you as Babaji," — and he saw at once the radiant aura of the night guest. Nervous, almost frightened, he bowed at the feet of the Guru, not daring to touch them. Entering the room, Babaji sat down on the platform-bed and crossed one leg over the other in a rather relaxed manner. Now he could see the visitor's face more clearly. The eyes usually reflect a lifetime of experience, and Babaji's eyes were ancient and piercing. Stunned by what was happening, he lowered himself to the floor and tried to master his emotions. He did not succeed very well. Babaji began to speak — softly, gently, yet seriously. The deep sound of his voice filled the room, as though they were inside a huge bronze dome, and the resonant timbre echoed off the walls, piercing the heart. Only the Guru spoke, and he listened, trying to absorb every word.

That night the Master spoke only of yoga, explaining some very important details of the higher Kriya techniques. At one moment, describing a difficult technique (the passage of the "knot of Rudra"), Babaji suddenly said: "The smashan awaits every yogi who reaches this level; it will simply draw him in…" The prediction sounded frightening. He could not imagine living in a cremation ground. Babaji went on: "The next level, the passage of the 'knot of Rudra', can be mastered only on a smashan…"

He let these words fully penetrate his consciousness, absorbing them. Then the Master added: "You are performing the yogic techniques given to you correctly," — and the confirmation lifted his spirits: at least he was moving in the right direction! That night he received an incredible gift: a detailed and meticulous explanation of all the Kriyas, even the highest levels whose mastery he could not have dreamed of. Like a table set with a lavish feast, this knowledge was now fully at his disposal.

Babaji bade him take notes, and he hurriedly wrote down the instructions, trying not to miss anything. In closing, the Master gave him a complex of separate, entirely new techniques which he was to begin only after his 57th birthday. They were not to be taught to others.

At the very last, the Guru added: "Many of your disciples will reach the highest levels in the practice," and to hear such a prediction was wonderful, since he considered himself a beginner on the path of yoga. Babaji's last words were: "Ishwar chahega to fir milenge" — "God willing, we shall meet again…"

The conversation lasted about forty minutes, but time seemed to stand still. He bowed to the Master; Babaji placed His hand on his head — the touch was warm, human. The Master turned toward the exit, and only then did he notice that the Guru's hair was bound in dreadlocks. No thicker than half a centimetre, shaded a dark bronze as if from prolonged exposure to the sun, the locks reached the Mahavatar's shoulders.

Yielding to impulse, he rose from the floor and followed the Teacher, but Babaji turned and, as one might to a child, wagged a finger at him. The door closed with a soft click, and he again slid down to the floor, feeling at once empty and brimming with faith, enthusiasm, and contentment.

The rightness of the chosen path was confirmed: he had just met the first Guru of the parampara — not in a dream or a vision, but in waking reality! All that was described in Yogananda's book was true. Babaji watched over every disciple! And He could personally meet those who practised sincerely and devotedly, those worthy of teaching. The personal meeting and conversation with the Guru strengthened his certainty that the heights of yoga are attainable, that the miracles described are real.

Trying to gather his thoughts, he understood: this incredible audience had been granted to him not only because of his persistent practice and his connection with the tradition, but also because he had already passed through the experience of samadhi. Babaji had come because the present successor of the line possessed potential, had a goal. All this new information had to be consolidated, arranged…

...and to begin to apply in practice, for the parampara expected that he would press forward. That night he could not sleep at all, reliving everything he had heard and seen. Going through the notes, he even sat in the very same place where Babaji had been sitting moments before, revealing to him the most secret particulars of the higher Kriyas.

...The confidence the Guru radiated was captivating, contagious, yet in Him there was not a trace of pretension. Most people rarely act in accordance with their own nature, because society drives everyone into the frame of "acceptable standards", forcing them to shed Nature's original codes. Babaji's behaviour was strikingly simple and at the same time filled with power. Realised beings act according to the essence of being which they have known through meditation and tapasya. This might sound trite if it were not one of the hardest attainments of self-realisation. Siddha-yogis live in harmony with the laws of Nature and can grasp her mysteries through self-inquiry. Perhaps this was the key to the mystery of their supernatural abilities: they reached the highest states while remaining in unity with Life itself.

The first of April is known as "April Fools' Day", but for him this day became forever the threshold of the most inspiring and fateful meeting of his life.

Babaji
A detailed description of Babaji's features and physique was given by Sh. Sharma. The image was produced by the joint efforts of Sumit Jadiya, Sarvadaman Johari, and artificial-intelligence technologies.

Shri Lahiri Mahasaya, 1828–1895
Shri Tinkori Lahiri, 1863–1934
Shri Satyacharan Lahiri, 1905–1987
Shri Shailendra Sharma, 1957–

Shri Satyacharan Lahiri, Fourth Guru of the Lahiri Mahasaya line. A few days before leaving the body.

After being declared successor of the line, the Fifth Guru, 1986.
After the completion of the work on the commentaries to the Gita, May 1992.
A month after the meeting with Babaji, May 1998.

Places and Books

* * *
"…the book of life is never too long and ought not to be boring."
— from a conversation of Shailendra Sharma with his disciples

On the Farm

After meeting Babaji he left Benares inspired, brimming with joy and excitement. At home he told his relatives about the meeting with the immortal Master, but no one attached particular significance to the story. The members of the family cared little for his strange encounters and aspirations; yoga was simply an impractical hobby, and sadhana a waste of time. Moreover, the atmosphere at home was bleak; there were many reasons for it, including a declared bankruptcy. His father's pension fund no longer secured the family's means of subsistence.

With every passing day he was more convinced that, after attaining samadhi, a yogin must try to live in seclusion. The forced daily contact with relatives was becoming more and more explosive; the constant carping and reproach worked on his nerves; he was continually reminded that only thanks to the family did he have food and a roof over his head. But there was nowhere to go. Yet a possibility arose to build his own house on a plot of land jointly owned by him and his brother Gyanendra. Borrowing fifty thousand rupees from a cousin, he began, with the help of friends and disciples, the construction of a small cottage. The building demanded far more time and money than expected, and nearly two years passed before he could move in. Tension in the family was rising, for everyone was now living in a rented house in which no one had any chance of privacy. But the practice was magnificent, and the bright star of Kutastha rose every night on his inner sky.

The reproaches about the absence of career plans and the worry over the future were laughable. The future as such was being shaped with every breath of pranayama, with every movement of Apana toward the heart. The awareness of the body as a unity of the elements of Matter, born of Void and Sound, with Spirit, was growing. The recognition of Spirit proceeded from within; the deep cognitive process required solitude, peace, and a steady balance of discipline. But there was no sense in trying to explain any of this to others.

On the 10th of July 1990 he turned thirty-three, and that birthday proved especially stormy. After a fierce quarrel with relatives he took a few books, some clothes, and went to the farm, knowing he would not return. Several disciples, among them his friend Rambabu, helped him move his things and settle in. And although the house was not yet finished, the place was secluded, far from town, and belonged entirely to him alone.

In the evening it was discovered that, in the rush, the gas stove and the cylinder — critical to survival — had been forgotten. The chances of dinner were becoming frighteningly uncertain. Noticing his worry, one of the workmen asked: "What's the matter, Guruji?" He explained.

"Nothing to worry about," — and the workman at once contrived an improvised hearth from two bricks, gathered firewood, and lit a fire. A broken clay jar, helplessly ending its days in the bushes, was set upon the bricks, and within a couple of minutes the rotis were ready. Then the workman plucked a few wild tomatoes and crushed them with his fist; an unearthed onion was likewise mashed and mingled with chili pepper and salt. Dinner was served, and it turned out to be the most delicious meal he had ever eaten. For the first time the power of the earth was felt by him so keenly: everything needed had already been provided by Nature and was waiting; he had simply been blind, absorbed by petty cares. This culinary master-class became one of the new lessons: opportunities exist always and only wait to be discovered; all that is required is to see them.

Each time he recalled that first night on the farm, he tasted again the flavour of tomato and the sweetness of red onion wrapped in hot bread; it was the taste of freedom. The next day one of the disciples came from the town to cook food and brew tea. The new life on the farm had begun.

Naga

For some reason the unwavering certainty that he would stay in this cottage for two years did not leave him. And this was quite well, for the place helped him to recharge, to be rid of the pressure of city life. Here one could hide from the noise of people's thoughts and the toxic slag of their intentions. All the cares connected with the move proved soluble; the main thing was the luxury of solitude.

When they began to dig irrigation ditches and plant trees, the rainy season had already started. The territory teemed with termites; during construction the insects had vanished, but their tall mounds rose everywhere; some reached two metres in height. The workers broke down several of these high-rise castles, and the gaze was met by complex systems of underground labyrinths. A workman from a neighbouring village came to demolish the termite mound that stood before the eastern entrance to the cottage, whose nearly two-metre walls were hard as concrete. The work went slowly, yet by the end of the day the ant citadel had fallen. He was in the house when he heard the cry: "Guruji!" Rushing outside, he saw a great black cobra crawling out of a hole at the foot of the ant-castle. Moving uncertainly, as if in a drowsy trance, the snake disappeared. Cautiously peering inside the ruined foundation, they saw in the darkness of the tunnel a red, unblinking eye looking fixedly straight at them. He cursed the stupefied workman so as to bring him back to himself, and his masterfully chosen profanities had a livening effect. The workman was told to come back the next day, while he himself returned alone to the remains of the mound that evening.

...The image of a serpent atop a termite mound is considered one of the most sacred images in India. Villagers regard these mounds as entrances to the underground kingdom of the nagas [footnote 121: Nagas — in Hinduism and Buddhism, a semi-divine race of beings, combining superhuman and serpentine qualities and inhabiting the underworld. There is a belief that from time to time the Nagas assume human form. Rituals dedicated to the Nagas have been performed throughout South Asia for more than two thousand years. Nagas are depicted as fully human with serpents around the head and neck, as ordinary snakes, or as half-human-half-serpent.], to Bhogavati. It is believed that there dwell magical serpents with precious stones hidden in their heads; such stones, called "Nagmani", can heal diseases and bring wealth to their possessors. Sometimes termite mounds formed around ascetics who had remained in samadhi. Remaining motionless for months or even years, the saints could endure the bites of termites. It is said that the snakes which often dwell in abandoned mounds live on the bodies of such yogins. Some temple traditions hold that cows pour their milk over the mounds, making a kind of offering to the deities dwelling there. On the ruined foundations of the ant-fortresses one can often see images of Shiva or Vishnu, since in country places the termite mounds are venerated as seats of spiritual power.

But for all his respect for folklore and tradition, the termite mound directly in front of the entrance to the house was a problem to be solved. Carefully placing incense before the dark hole, he tried to establish mental contact with its occupant. Addressing the red-eyed being, he said: "I am here only to ennoble this land, and I will not trouble you any more, whoever you may be…" The fragrant sandalwood smoke flowed slowly down into the dark opening, as though the message had been heard.

That night he had a most strange dream: before the house stood a man almost three metres tall, clad in a silken dhoti. The stranger's black hair seemed glued to his head; his skin was yellow, the colour of turmeric. The colour of his eyes was hard to determine, but the dark pupils were vertical. The man said: "Please do not harm my children, they are small and defenceless. I have always been the guardian of this land and will protect you as one of them…" Then the speaker pointed to an opening in the earth; there was something resembling honeycomb, only in each cell, coiled, lay tiny golden serpents. He respectfully answered the Naga: "I am only trying to plant a garden here, and I will not trouble you and your children." — "Continue. You are under my protection."

The next morning he woke with a heavy head. The dream, which had felt utterly real, had had a stupefying effect, and his body was strangely elastic and supple. The heat was beginning. He stepped out onto the veranda and at once met the steady gaze of a huge golden cobra coiled in loops; her throat and underside were white, and her red forked tongue rhythmically appeared and disappeared between her half-closed fangs. The snake's eyes were red and slanting, like a human being's, and the whites had a bluish cast. This beautiful but lethal creature looked at him without blinking. Brushing aside the thought of the double-barrelled gun standing behind the door, he guessed that the snake's appearance was connected with the recent dream, and at the same instant the snake disappeared beneath the base of the mound. Soon the workman who came was in no hurry to do anything near the serpent's nest. Hurling a new portion of curses at the poor wretch, he took up the spade himself and began to level the mound, taking care not to destroy the foundation. A peaceful agreement had been reached, and he was sure that the nagas would no longer trouble the workmen.

Although the opening in front of the house was filled in, sometimes a light, almost invisible vapour rose from the earth, and the soil remained warm even during the harsh winter months. By spring the ground near the mound had acquired a strange whitish hue. There was no need to investigate the cause; the inhabitants of the farm minded their own business. As they went on improving the territory, the workers planted kadamba trees and laid out a garden — and the plantings grew at incredible speed. The lawn before the house was cleared, climbing creepers draped the cottage in lush green and became a refuge for countless butterflies and humming-birds. From time to time snakes were seen, but the Naga himself no longer appeared in his dreams.

...And other encounters

The hot September forced him to do his sadhana late at night. He usually went out of the house around two in the morning and gazed at the clear dark sky. Yogic meditation calmed thought, the night was filled with moonlight and peace, the divinity of life pulsed in the fragrant darkness, and the earth breathed with a thousand sounds. One night he saw two blue lights to the right of the wall enclosing the territory. The lights drew nearer, and at a distance of thirty metres he began to make out two human figures, male and female. Moving half a metre above the ground, they did not soar — they walked. The couple "came up" to the house, and in the bright moonlight he could see their luminous bodies, as though composed of diamonds; even their hair sparkled with myriads of tiny flashes. Naked, without ornaments, they were fantastically beautiful. To describe in words the serene bliss radiating from these splendidly made beings, the flowing grace of their motion, was impossible. When they were less than ten metres away, the woman noticed his presence and gave a sign to her companion. A wave of laughter reached him, and he felt a rush of inexpressible happiness. Passing him, the couple turned the corner of the cottage and, having gone around the house, reappeared from the other side, "floating" above the ground only two metres from him. Then they vanished, like a hologram suddenly switched off.

He tried to find some traces of their presence, but found none. And yet the rapture and awe their appearance had caused were comparable only to the emotions he had felt after meeting Babaji. The next day two blue-throated jays appeared at the house. Usually shy birds, they perched on trees right by the lawn, not at all disturbed by the presence of people. This pair began to come every morning, and if he slept in they would flutter at the doors, uttering impatient trills. Clearly an alliance had formed, and he ordered two poles to be set up on either side of his chair on the lawn. The birds sat on them like figurines, remaining at their posts for hours, watching the stream of visitors and listening in on the discussions.

...He had not been home for almost six months and his mother was worried. Like every mother, she wanted him to find a good job with a stable income that would let him start a family. These commonly accepted patterns of social obligation had worked for countless generations, and one was not to step out of them. His mother loved him but was insistent and stubborn. As he was, because he was her son, though his stubbornness was bent on achieving entirely different ends. The similarity of their characters created the dilemma: it was impossible to explain his aspirations to his mother; even strangers understood him better. He used to joke: "It is not for nothing that one's kin are called blood-relatives. They drink your blood!"

In any case, worried about him, the mother dispatched the elder brother Gyanendra on a reconnaissance mission. He was to find out whether the younger son was lying in rags on the bare ground, destitute, hungry, and rejected by all, like Devdas [footnote 122: "Devdas" — a 2002 romantic drama by director Sanjay Leela Bhansali based on the novel of the same name by Sarat Chandra Chattopadhyay (1917). The film tells the story of Devdas Mukherjee, a wealthy London-returned law graduate, who has come back to marry his childhood friend Parvati. The family refuses to grant permission for the marriage, provoking the hero's emotional collapse and descent into alcoholism.], the hero of the Bollywood film.

Gyanendra arrived after lunch. He had recently received a large loan from the local bank, and the first man he saw was the bank manager who had granted him the loan. The man was washing tea-cups.

— Sir, what are you doing here? — asked the astonished brother.
— Oh, just serving the Guru…

Passing through the kitchen, Gyanendra met a lady — a well-known town judge — bringing in a teapot of freshly brewed tea. Entering the cottage, he found there the entire staff of the city's department of agriculture, including the director-general and the manager. One of the senior officials was ironing the Guru's kurta. Darshan was in full swing, a heated discussion was going on, and no one paid the newcomer any particular attention. Gyanendra sat quietly for some time, drank a cup of tea, and returned home, reporting to the family that the younger brother was doing very well indeed.

Life on the farm seemed serene, but many subtle, unobvious events stimulated deep insights. The land had accepted the calm and solitude of his practice, and the cycle of pranayamas was tuned to the rhythm of Nature's mighty breathing. The earth was satisfied with the care given to her, and his sadhana reached new heights. Sensing his growing inner power and the depth of his spiritual insights, people kept coming to him…

Just before the festival of Diwali a disciple from Haryana brought to the farm two cows and a tiny calf only a month old. He named him Gandhi. The playful, charming calf became a kind of household pet, and his briefly-visiting cousin Devahuti happily played with the new member of their small herd. The next day she was returning to Mathura, and standing near the grazing cows he watched her go. Devahuti turned around, he waved at her, and in that instant the two blue-throated jays landed right on his head, then a moment later flew off.

At first this unusual omen aroused a feeling of unease, but the following weeks and months brought only positive changes.

The Art of Non-Expectation

To his Master he had told the recent incredible experience rather laconically: "It happened," and at once moved on to describe the side-effects of samadhi — the apathy, the inertia, and the rehabilitation of the body through physical exercise. Visibly pleased, the Guru smiled: "Well then, your first 'journey' has taken place, but this is only the beginning. It will be repeated, but for that some time may be needed. Just keep practising." That was the only counsel and instruction he had received from the Teacher.

Now, surrounded by endless fields, he dwelt in solitude. The noise of the city and the splashing of the waves of human thought no longer interfered with his concentration.

Samadhi was a shock; the secret curtain over the stage had been raised, but it revealed a wholly unexpected production. He had not merely been shown an epic spectacle — he had been made an active participant in it. Samadhi proved to be by no means a blissful contemplation; it was a funnel that had sucked him into another dimension and had changed his life forever. There, in the Void, the subconscious could at last meet the Spirit, the inner all-knowing essence, and this Spirit could invite the householder of the body on an excursion into a vast museum, into a circus, into a safari park, onto a battlefield, into the personal Disneyland of the Lord God.

...Nearly five years passed. He had practically ceased to expect a repetition of the first experience, when suddenly something inside switched, and he again entered the state of non-thought. Or, rather, the realm of Super-Consciousness. The physical sensations were the same as on the first occasion: the numbness, the gradual return of circulation, the slow and uncertain resumption of the heartbeat and the unaccustomed sensation of breath re-entering the nostrils. But this time the samadhi was peaceful, without a trace of the mental excitement caused by the irritating flood of others' emotions simultaneously assaulting his consciousness. During the first samadhi the whirlwind of others' experiences had risen in his consciousness, and his own mind had scanned the minds of all the beings in the city and its surroundings, and this had caused a fierce inner turmoil. Now, in solitude, he was not troubled by the presence of people, though he sensed thousands of other creatures around him.

There were night birds, insects, various reptiles, wild deer and buffalo. He felt the hunger of a jackal, for a moment entered the cunning mind of a fox hunting nervous mice, admired a pair of cheetahs in love, regal in their concentrated grace. The vibrations of the animal kingdom were peaceful and on the whole serene, in contrast with the bustling, spasmodic ripples of the mental waves emanating from human beings. Ordinary smell sharpened greatly and became almost unbearable. Already rather sensitive to scents in his ordinary state, he was now acutely aware of them — they passed through the body like discharges of electric current. The astral fragrances of the animal kingdom were generated by the mental contours of its inhabitants; the vibrations of beasts and birds could not remotely compare with the mental miasmas of human beings. Not a single smell of the animal world disgusted him; the animals behaved according to their nature, and in their mental landscapes there was not a trace of artificial and conditioned intentions.

Now he could relish the most exquisite tastes, but he had to remain cautious, since the mind could also reproduce the most repulsive ones. The taste of his own saliva could become nectar, wine, milk, or poison — anything consciousness might wish. He, the yogin, had become a bhogi, an enjoyer, capable of indulging the senses in whatever his mind focused on. In the ordinary process of pleasure a normal man uses about 2% of the mind; the yogin enjoys with fully activated consciousness, and the expanded mind multiplies pleasure many-fold. In the middle of the dark fields he revelled in the happiness piercing through him. The bliss of samadhi is enjoyment of the world — now he fully understood the meaning of the Shiva Sutras [footnote 123: Shiva Sutras — a collection of 77 aphorisms forming the foundation of the spiritual mysticism tradition known as Kashmir Shaivism. The work is attributed to the sage Vasugupta, who lived in the 9th century CE. It is said he received this teaching from above. Vasugupta is said to have lived near Mount Mahadev close to the city of Srinagar. Legend has it that in a vision a siddha, a semi-divine being (or Lord Shiva himself), instructed him to find the stone on which the shlokas were inscribed. The Shiva Sutras represent deep philosophical revelations and the great postulates of Sanatana Dharma. Historically the text is classified as part of the Tantric or Agamic tradition.].

And still all sensations and reactions had to be subdued and controlled, though he allowed them to flow through him like the waters of a mighty river. The long-awaited second samadhi lasted about an hour and recurred three or four days later. Then, after a brief interval, the state of suspended animation became regular. Repetition helped him learn the art of control and balance. Travelling "between" the worlds, he understood the principle of entering this state and the rules for leaving it.

...The absence of expectations was an important condition for the recurrence of samadhi. The most difficult task in yogic sadhana is not to wait for the results of one's efforts while still striving for them day after day. The inquisitive human mind wants to see the fruits of every action, despite everything the ancient texts say about action without attachment to results. Expectations still go on living, especially in those who have already crossed the barrier of action and tasted the flavour of attainment. Anyone who has achieved success is bound to crave more.

Of course he had waited for results, and that wait had lasted nearly five years. Every day, sitting in his practice, he anticipated a new samadhi. Secretive and shy, expectation lived deep [in the mind]…

...in the depths of the mind, like a ruffled chick. Seemingly quite harmless, it actually undermined the mind's ability to return to the state of unconscious-ness that had opened the door into the Void the first time. Yet to recreate the same mental state which had allowed the invisible barrier to be crossed for the first time was impossible, and in this lay the chief "technical snag."

People who have undergone supernatural states crave their repetition; but the thirst for new adventures is so great that it does not allow this to happen. Almost five years were needed before he could quiet the mind and master the subtle process of relaxing it; one cannot simply switch consciousness on and off at will. His condition in those days could be described as joyful excitement.

He was utterly convinced that Babaji had honoured him with His presence because He knew of the first "journey." Otherwise the Guru would not have taught him. There were other decisive factors as well: he was young, strong, and resolute; he was the next Guru; and he practised for hours, testing his own capacities and the abilities of his body. Nothing besides yoga did he desire.

Most likely, if he had gone on practising without ever meeting the Mahavatar and without receiving instruction in sadhana, he would still have made progress, though hardly have reached the subsequent levels. He would in all probability have got stuck in memories of the great experience of Samadhi, until that memory became like a yellowed old photograph — an unbearable memory of flight, the dream of a wingless bird.

It was unlikely that anyone could understand all these new experiences and emotions, and he did not seek to share them with anyone. Some of those who had known him before the transformation took his newfound self-confidence for arrogance. They found it hard to understand the cause of the happiness that overflowed him: "What is he so jubilant about, why so self-assured? He has no job, earns nothing! Whereas we slave away, trying to get somewhere in life, while he lives carefree and obviously enjoys it!" Of course no one said these words aloud, but they were written on the faces of some of the visitors.

The confidence came from the incredible satisfaction of progress and attainment in the practice. The spiritual hunger had been quenched; he had reached the highest state of yoga, yet this was still only the beginning of the next stage, and he knew it.

...In those days his aura was golden, and those who came to him felt reverence. To subdue the enormous energy boiling within him he had to swing the heavy Indian clubs — the mudgars — no fewer than five hundred times a day. Regular samadhis began to affect his hormonal balance and muscle tone — the changes had been set in motion by the awakened mind. His physical strength rose sharply, not so much through exercise as through the stimulation of consciousness. The mind, having survived yet another meeting with death, now commanded the body to live.

He began again to lift disciples, holding them on outstretched arms above his head, and this was an altogether different kind of strength — in former times one would have called it supernatural and divine. Perhaps it was. Divinity manifested in the body as Spirit, and the two recognised one another. Reflecting on all these changes, he did not mention them when speaking with the Guru. The Master had never discussed the consequences of samadhi with him, and only once touched on the subject, rather obliquely: "You will learn and understand all you need when it happens." After such a statement, pressing for details was awkward.
