Emptiness pervades Time, and Time, the root lying at the foundation of the consciousness of this conscious Emptiness, pervades Her. The result of the union of Time and conscious Emptiness in the act of love is conscious Matter, the body of this world, perceived through the sense organs. The Shakti of the material world is Emptiness, which, being the basis of all movements and actions occurring within it, itself remains unchanged. The Prana of Matter and Emptiness is the Great Time itself, which, although it pervades all that exists, always remains beyond it. He who has comprehended Time and recorded these sutras gives a hint as to how the great yogis developed their consciousness in the body-womb and came to know the Prana of all that exists, Shiva Mahakala – or Time…”

…Having finished the last sentence, he felt immense satisfaction. At last, topics about which it seemed almost impossible to speak without listeners falling into a state of trance had been described, and the experiences of recent years and months had been expressed and explained. The long day was coming to an end, but his real “working day” was only just beginning. With the departure of the visitors, he rose from the cot, stretched, and cracked his joints; the time for night sadhana had come. A sadhana in which the answers to all questions were contained.

But from a practical point of view, things were going so-so. There was not even a hint of the materialization of the palace from the visions. Busy with household chores, preparing food, and replenishing supplies, Devahuti was worried: the ashram was only a temporary refuge, and there was no certainty about the future. During these weeks, she had written a poem about her despair and uncertainty, and about the support she found in him, her Guru. She decided not to show him this poem, since their situation was difficult and hardly conducive to conversations about poetry. There was little money, or rather, none at all, and the question of further survival became more acute than ever.

At night he had a strange dream: a monkey-like man with the black face of a langur sat casually and relaxed against the wall of an old fort above a river. Suddenly this strange creature laughed, as if from being tickled: “Yogiraj, don’t worry about anything, I will make sure everything is fine.” Pushing off from the wall, he flew into the air and, crossing over the river, dissolved into the pale sky. The monkey-man kept his promise: very soon they were caught up by another wave of events.

Sarnath, “Thorns and Roses,” “The Wanderer”

A telephone call from a disciple was the prelude to a new beginning. Lal Baba persistently urged the Guru to visit the newly built ashram in Sarnath and stay as long as he wished. The offer was worth considering; there were no funds, their small herd had increased, and the cows needed more and more fodder; students and visitors drank tea by the bucketful, and provisions were dwindling.

…Lal Baba’s real name was Gupteshwar Singh Shastri. They had been introduced in Sarnath in April 1988, just a couple of days after the momentous visit of Babaji. Upon seeing him, Lal Baba immediately prostrated himself on the floor and requested initiation into yoga: “Please, become my Guru!” Over the following days, the new disciple enthusiastically took on the task of organizing a meeting with the Dalai Lama, who had recently received the Nobel Prize and was traveling to the holy places of India. Lal Baba managed to persuade certain lamas to arrange an audience for them, but the event itself turned out to be not so significant. A pleasant elderly man, the Dalai Lama presented them with traditional white scarves, said a few kind words, and with that, the visit was over. They politely took their leave and exited the hall, leaving behind the lamas in their ochre robes, the scent of incense, and the friendly head of Tibetan Buddhism. He was under the strongest impression from the meeting with Babaji; nothing had greater significance than sadhana and the desire to explore the new techniques given by the Master. The white ceremonial scarf was given away to someone; he valued souvenirs of a completely different kind. A small notebook with records of the kriya techniques dictated by Babaji was safely hidden in the pocket of his kurta – and this was his most precious possession.

…After Lal Baba’s telephone call, tense discussions lasted almost a week. In the end, he and Devahuti decided to accept the invitation. Sarnath could be a welcome break from the daily worries about money and food, allowing him to focus on sadhana.

There were no funds for the move, but suddenly 20,000 rupees materialized, and one of the new disciples, an employee of a transport company, volunteered to pack all their belongings.

Before leaving for Sarnath, it was decided to perform one more parikrama around Govardhan Hill. He was in a foul mood. And, having covered only half the way, he addressed the Deity of the hill: “Giriraj, please help me find a permanent home, otherwise I will not return, I will not be able to complete this parikrama…” It is believed that a parikrama must be completed, otherwise wishes and prayers will have the opposite effect. It is unlikely that the Dark God perceived his words as blackmail; rather, as a cry for help. Constant homelessness was unsettling, the necessity of yet another move was depressing. But the decision was made, and their belongings were again loaded onto a truck: two Dobermans, six cows, several crates of books, heavy mudgars, and kitchen utensils. The possessions of refugees.

Three disciples departed on the truck with the belongings and animals. Vrindavan was shrouded in milky-white February fog when he and Devahuti boarded the bus to the station. He did not look back.

…Located just 10 kilometers from Varanasi, near the confluence of the Ganga and Varuna, Sarnath is not as popular as its famous neighbor. Yet the town is well-known and visited, especially the Deer Park, where Gautama Buddha taught the Dharma. Sarnath, or more precisely, the village of Singhpur, is also the birthplace of the eleventh tirthankara of Jainism. Overall, this place is much quieter than Kashi, oversaturated with people and sacred vibrations.

The place they were heading to was called Ashram Shakti Peeth. After Lal Baba built a Shakti temple there, the ashram became extremely popular. Located near a busy railway station, the place was surprisingly isolated and quiet; an ancient dried-up lake conveniently shielded the ashram from the city, providing the seclusion that all who have ever tried to meditate in sacred but extremely noisy pilgrimage sites seek in vain.

Lal Baba, materially well-off and highly respected among the elite of Benares and even the mafia, was involved in politics and was the right-hand man of the then Prime Minister Chandra Shekhar Singh. The ashram was built for holding political symposia, seminars, and meetings; two large halls and a reception area could accommodate several hundred people.

News of the arrival of Lal Baba’s spiritual mentor spread like wildfire. Members of criminal groups, renowned sadhus, heads of other ashrams and politicians, as well as hundreds of people from neighboring towns and villages, attended the darshans daily. Everyone burned with curiosity: such a respected and powerful man as Lal Baba could not be a follower of an ordinary spiritual teacher. In addition, one of his gurubhais, a disciple of Shri Satyacharan Lahiri, now a high-ranking official in the tourism department in Varanasi, began sending buses of tourists to the ashram. They were promised an audience with the greatest yogi of modern times. Sometimes among them were Western tourists, seeking not only wisdom but also a good pull from a chillum. To their great disappointment, such an item was absent from the ashram’s menu. India attracted both sincere seekers of truth and hashish lovers; often these interests were not mutually exclusive.

He had no intention of becoming a crowd-pleasing commercial guru. He dreamed only of a quiet place for practice, but still found none. Unwanted attention and sudden fame seriously interfered with his sadhana.

…On Shivaratri, he proposed conducting a march to the famous Sarangnath Mahadev temple; this was meant to be a symbolic procession, just like the one a year earlier in Gwalior. More than 300 participants gathered. Along the procession route, people came out of their houses to see the unusual march, led by a broad-shouldered yogin with rudraksha necklaces on his bare chest and long, loose hair. The participants carried drums and saffron-colored flags – symbols of victory and strength.

The event also caused some problems: the abbot of a nearby Buddhist monastery called the police and complained about a possible invasion of Sarnath by radical Hindu fanatics. When they had already returned, the ashram was suddenly surrounded by several police jeeps. Two police officers entered. He introduced himself and handed them each a copy of the Bhagavad Gita. The sacred book always acted as an excellent credential, the best of recommendations and business cards; the non-existent problem was resolved. The authorship of the commentary on the sacred book, backed by Lal Baba’s reputation, dispelled all possible complications, but the episode itself was quite unpleasant. In fact, it illustrated the state of affairs in the spiritual world: unfortunately, clan wars and battles over territory and influence were common.

Shortly before the celebration of Holi, Devahuti, embarrassed,

She handed him a folded sheet of paper with a poem she had written.

Unable to find suitable words, he sat in silence for some time. His sister's devotion was pure and strong; her poem spoke of uncertainty, destiny, faith, and the unknown future that awaited them. Devahuti was ready to step into that future alongside him, no matter what.

Moved and filled with gratitude, he felt his emotions surge, demanding an outlet.

Devahuti's poem offered a possible format for expressing his own feelings, and that same day he composed the first 19 poems—about the distant horizons a traveler strives toward. A stranger to any poetic ambition, he had never attempted to rhyme words, though he admired the exquisite poetry of Kabir, Omar Khayyam, and Surdas. Some old Bollywood songs were filled with deep philosophical meaning and were also romantic; he knew many by heart. The words that came were not marked by refined rhymes, yet they were created by the most powerful intention to reach the goal, to touch the boundary between worlds. This intention predetermined the outcome of the journey; everything else was secondary. The true meaning of the seemingly impossible task of touching the horizon symbolized yogic heights, and this was the goal and meaning of his life.

Fifty-two poems appeared in three days. He named the anthology "The Traveler"—it became a manifesto and a source of inspiration for seekers walking the same path. There were many of them, and to each he said that he was not gathering followers, though he welcomed fellow travelers.

…Over the course of several months, he constantly recorded random fragments of thoughts, and Devahuti diligently collected these scattered pages and scraps of paper, until both realized that these seemingly unrelated reflections on life and yoga deserved publication.

The collection, titled "Thorns and Roses," began with the words: "I gave away all the best that I had. I gave all my treasures to those who came to me, always removing thorns from wounded hearts and adorning them with flowers; I accepted the worst from others and gave my best. The only thing I regret is that I could not share the pleasure of all that was done with others. Let this be my farewell message when I am destined to leave here…"

Poetic, often sentimental words described what he would continue to do in the decades to come. The path of knowledge was also a path of healing, both for himself personally and for those whom he "healed" from the poison of illusory or real reality.

Around the same time, he registered the "Siddha Siddhanta Yoga Academy¹³⁸," as he was certain that more and more people would become interested in Kriya. The name came of its own accord—it is the name of one of the six main philosophical traditions of Shaivism, as well as the name of a well-known text attributed to Siddha Gorakhnath¹³⁹.

…If anyone were to ask about his occupation, he would answer without hesitation: a Guru of yoga, a guide appointed by the parampara. The specifics and scope of these duties were not yet fully appreciated by him, but he knew: actions leading to a meaningful, lasting result were also called "Siddhanta."

The Beginning of Spiritual Democracy.
Another Life of Yogananda

Sarnath was not far from Varanasi, and he sometimes visited Banamali Lahiri, the nephew of the late Guru. They had met before, their relationship was friendly, and he was always a welcome guest at Banamali Lahiri's, who still lived in the old house of Lahiri Mahasaya.

Tinkori Lahiri raised six sons: Kalicharan, Abhyacharan, Ambikacharan, Taracharan, Umacharan, and Satyacharan. Shri Satyacharan Lahiri was his teacher, and Banamali's father was Abhyacharan Lahiri.

…The narrow lanes leading to the old house of Lahiri Mahasaya had not changed at all in the last hundred years. A small shop nearby, now empty and locked, belonged to a merchant who, observing Lahiri Mahasaya as he walked to the Ganges to bathe in the pre-dawn hours, would joke: "Look at this Bengali, already drunk since early morning!" Emerging from his night samadhi around 4–4:30 a.m., Lahiri was still intoxicated by the visions and experiences of his sadhana.

The times when Babaji decided to bestow this mystical yoga upon a humble householder were still monarchical. According to the scriptures, yogic knowledge was to be transmitted to descendants of royal blood. Babaji could initiate a raja or a noble prince. But, given social restrictions and caste affiliations, members of the royal family would not have been able to continue transmitting the art of self-realization to others. Human history was approaching a new cycle of irreversible change, and spiritual democracy was expressed in the fact that a middle-class brahmin, a clerk, a husband and father, was initiated into yoga. Ancient texts spoke of rulers who left their kingdoms to perform austerities; in contrast to this tradition, a humble family man became a significant spiritual figure. Lahiri Mahasaya proved: spiritual evolution within a single lifetime is accessible to all. Although he was a yogi in a past life, by all standards Lahiri Mahasaya was an ordinary man, a petty clerk with no particular social status. And yet, he became a spiritual giant, from whom even rulers sought blessings. Thus began a global spiritual awakening—everyone could now discover the divine principle within themselves.

The stigma of caste still existed, but yogis treated everyone equally—caste, gender, and age were not to become limitations on the spiritual path. Being a high-caste brahmin, Lahiri Mahasaya made no distinctions between classes. Unlike some of his disciples. There was a man belonging to the Jaiswal caste, which had branched off from the Kshatriya caste many centuries ago; some people of this clan became traders, others winemakers. Due to the rigid caste hierarchy, the brahmin disciples in Lahiri's circle expressed displeasure at the initiation of a low-caste Jaiswal into yoga and objected to his presence at group meetings. The tension grew until one day Lahiri Mahasaya invited this disciple to sit beside him. In this way, he demonstrated the equality of the yogic brotherhood to the snobs; the disciples apologized.

…He and Banamali Lahiri were sitting in Lahiri Mahasaya's room, and a most interesting discussion was in full swing. Suddenly, a tall, well-built, and handsome man appeared in the doorway; he looked to be just over forty.

The newcomer was very serious. The visitor bowed to the pair of canvas shoes of the Teacher and to his folded dhoti lying on the wooden cot where Lahiri Mahasaya usually sat. Then he bowed to Banamali Lahiri and quietly sat down in a corner. The large wooden wall clock, bought by Lahiri Mahasaya himself, ticked with a rustling sound; the house was quiet. After five or six minutes, the man stood up, bowed again, and left the room, without losing his focused gaze and straight posture.

After the door closed behind the visitor, Banamali asked: "Do you know who that is?" — "Please, enlighten me." — "In his past life, his name was Mukunda Lal Ghosh; later he became known as Yogananda. In this new incarnation, he lives in a house on the banks of the Ganges, having inherited a good fortune. Finally, he can devote all his time to yoga. He is not married and has little contact with people. It seems solitude is his best friend. As is his sadhana. Sometimes he just walks along the Ganges and comes here…"

This was a staggering revelation. It had always been believed that after completing his great mission, Paramahansa Yogananda joined the group of immortals of Babaji. Banamali Lahiri smiled: "He is only preparing to join that group. It is unknown what 'examination' one must pass to become a companion of the immortal Master. Perhaps he is undergoing some special training; as long as a person is not free from disease and has not learned to endure any weather, he cannot follow the lifestyle and discipline of that group…"

After Yogananda popularized Kriya yoga, making the lineage of Babaji world-famous, many new Kriya teachers gained recognition.

Undoubtedly, Yogananda was the greatest yogi who gently challenged the Western mentality and changed it, opening new spiritual perspectives for seekers. And yet, he was reincarnated in a human body, living in seclusion, anonymously, in complete obscurity, concentrating only on yoga… What is the meaning of this birth? Had he not attained spiritual heights?

"You see, the tasks of the subsequent birth of any Guru of the parampara are always determined by Babaji himself, and no one else."

He paused for a moment and asked Banamali:
– Does this man know about his past life?
– I am sure he remembers everything.
– Did he not become a Guru of Kriya yoga in this, his current life?
– No, he no longer needs to be a teacher.
– Does he have a Teacher guiding him on the Kriya path in this reincarnation—since he continues his practice and may need guidance?

Banamali Lahiri replied softly: "On this earthly plane, he no longer needs a Guru. He remembers everything he was taught. Yogananda was a disciple of Yukteswar Giri, and he developed and perfected himself surrounded by the direct disciples of Lahiri Mahasaya. As you know, his journey to America and his mission were a direct instruction from Babaji—and Yogananda succeeded. He accepted a new birth to prepare for the next tasks. A new life is necessary for working out accumulated karma. Being a spiritual leader is hard work. You have already felt this yourself, haven't you?"

Banamali was right. The Guru takes on responsibility for his disciples and heroically fulfills the role of a connecting bridge between them and God. A spiritual teacher cannot change the direction of a disciple's destiny, but he can help them burn karmas, even if the smoke proves quite toxic. Such intervention always has personal karmic consequences for the Teacher himself.

They sat in silence for a while; he recalled lines from Yogananda's autobiography, where the author spoke of a strong desire for a peaceful life on the banks of the Ganges, filled with reflection and meditation. It seemed Mukunda Lal Ghosh had finally found the balance he dreamed of. He was given what he so desired: obscurity and complete freedom for spiritual realization, unburdened by responsibility for the fates of others. His new life was devoid of obligations to society, he fully retained the memory of his past personality—was this not the highest reward for a yogi?

…In 1997, under similar circumstances, he saw this man again. And although they did not exchange a single word, both knew of each other's path and destiny. It was his last trip to Kashi. Soon, another city firmly bound him to itself and held him for the subsequent 30 years.

The Name of the Spirit. Devahuti's "Discovery"

That evening, while rotating the heavy mudgars, he was in a foul mood. The uncertainty was dragging on. They were still in Sarnath, and the lack of money and prospects for finding a permanent place to live (not to mention a palace) in the foreseeable future weighed heavily upon him. The splendor of the inner cosmos could not fully compensate for the necessity of solving everyday tasks. Moreover, the astral worlds were not inhabited by friendly fairies and cute unicorns. Fierce ancient spirits spoke in the dialect of the subtle worlds – and it was a language of power and will. Only steadfastness of mind and the strength of yogic will could contend with the predators of the astral expanses. The present uncertainty about the future again hindered concentration.

Occupied with these thoughts, he paid no attention to the “white noise” that had been pursuing him for several days, until a sudden realization came: this persistent melody signified the name of his own spirit.

The sound lasted about six or seven minutes; the variations were subtly fine, elusive, impossible to reproduce. He tried to repeat the motif aloud, but the limited speech apparatus failed: his vocal cords simply could not recreate either the amplitude of these notes or the specificity of the phonetics. For some reason, the name of the spirit remained unpronounceable, but someone or something persistently continued to call out to him from the subtle plane. Definitely, the spirit was trying to introduce itself.

The name of the soul is given by its astral parents, and it can be pronounced only in the astral worlds. After souls pass from the earthly plane to the astral, Time itself calls out to them by name, summoning them to Itself.

During samadhi, the sense of individuality never dissolved or disappeared; he clearly knew what and who he was, felt the power of his own essence, and that essence was his soul. Atma, or Spirit, whose power helped preserve and maintain the sense of “I” with all its memories and aspirations. Contrary to some “spiritual teachings,” getting rid of the sense of “I” was the worst of all possible ideas, since the “I” is the core and pivot of any living being. He always remembered his earthly name.

But what did the name of the Spirit mean? Sometimes, encountering astral beings “on the other side,” he knew their names, which could resound as a melody or vibration. And this was not surprising: if physical bodies were generated by the universal Sound, the name of each soul could certainly be Its echo. And although the very birth of souls remained a mystery, he believed: one day it would be revealed.

…Meanwhile, the dynamics of life in Sarnath were changing, and the energies were becoming quite turbulent, often negative. Groups of visitors began to gather whose interests were far from yoga; most were interested only in religious rituals and philosophical debates, some expected miracles and personal blessings, the percentage of idle onlookers was increasing with alarming consistency. Even outright shady individuals began to frequent the darshans. It was becoming increasingly difficult to maintain the strict daily regimen that supported his sadhana and the necessary seclusion. All these factors influenced the decision to send Devahuti back to Mathura: the atmosphere in the ashram was becoming unsuitable for a young unmarried woman.

She was furious, but upon returning to Mathura, she stubbornly continued her search for a house that might please them both. One of the inquiries was answered by a broker, who casually mentioned the royal shamshan in nearby Govardhan. A large plot of land with buildings was for sale, but the broker did not bother to mention that the Department of Archaeological Research had declared the complex a protected historical and architectural monument. This meant a ban on any changes or alterations to the existing structures, which was why no one wished to acquire the shamshan as property, at least not legally. Furthermore, the place was believed to be haunted, but all these details came to light much later. Meanwhile, having chosen an unconventional negotiation tactic, Devahuti sent the Crown Prince of Bharatpur a copy of the yogic commentaries on the Gita written by her brother.

The owner of the shamshan, the old Raja of Bharatpur, was alive but in poor health, and all financial affairs were handled by his son, Prince Kumar Vishvendra Singh. He read the book with great interest and was impressed: the yogic interpretation of the sacred text differed radically from all the academic and religious expositions known to him. Brilliantly educated and possessing a sharp mind, the heir to the ancient dynasty considered that transferring the ancestral cremation ground to a yogi, rather than a trader or businessman, was the right decision. The Prince only wanted to be sure that the new owner would treat the history and memory of the Bharatpur dynasty with respect.

After fairly short negotiations, the preliminary decision to sell was confirmed, and Devahuti called Sarnath to announce the news.

“I found your palace…”.

**1992**

March of the Yogis, Gwalior, Mahashivaratri 1992

Abhishek at the Gupteshwar Temple, Mahashivaratri 1992
Shailendra Sharma lifting disciples during a break in the work on the Gita commentaries

**The Land of Liberation**

**Arrival**

Devahuti came for him in Sarnath, and in mid-May they returned together to the heat-scorched Mathura to inspect the property and finalize the deal. While the legal procedures for the purchase were underway, he stayed at his aunt’s house. A couple of days later, they contacted the Raja’s secretary, who arranged a visit to Govardhan. One sultry evening, around 8 o’clock, the secretary picked them up, and in the gathering dusk, the car set off. When, after winding through narrow lanes, the car pulled up to the gates of the shamshan, it was completely dark.

A sudden memory brought vivid images of a cold December night, the first parikrama, and the tall, gaunt stranger wrapped in a blanket who had come out of the gates to bow to him. They stood at those same rusty gates. Who was that man? The place was uninhabited, and no one could answer. The secretary sent for the caretaker, who, slowly approaching the fence, pushed the key into the rusty lock. The heavy iron gates creaked open. The secretary invited them to enter and look around, but he did not intend to accompany them himself.

The outlines of the palace were barely discernible in the thick, scorching darkness; they entered the courtyard before the first building when suddenly, from the lower colonnade of the palace, a woman ran out to meet them, dressed in a traditional red Rajasthani dress. The hem fell just below the knees, gold embroidery glittered in the dense twilight, and her antique shoes were adorned with precious stones. Always attentive to details, he instantly absorbed all the nuances, forgetting to be surprised. A streetlamp dimly illuminated a small area near the gates, and in this uneven light, the stranger’s dark face, her beautiful, cow-like eyes, could be clearly seen. She laughed almost soundlessly, showing white, even teeth, and placed a garland of jasmine flowers around his neck – and disappeared into the darkness. He and Devahuti exchanged glances, simultaneously realizing that the stranger was an astral being, perhaps the spirit of this place.

At that same moment, a welcoming chorus of invisible peacocks burst forth, and the silhouettes of a flock of bats, pursued by the shadow of an owl, glided against the palace walls. The shadows were a product of the night; this place was a kingdom of twilight beings, a refuge of mirages and memories. In the sultry darkness, they suddenly felt the touch of a cool breeze, and the voice of his heart whispered to him: “Now this is your home.”

On June 6, 1993, he and Devahuti went to Bharatpur to sign the final documents. The streets of Mathura were overcrowded; a huge procession of richly decorated elephants and fifty-two horses was crossing the city; they had to stop due to traffic jams. It was a celebration of a folk hero, the legendary Maharana Pratap, the 54th ruler of Mewar from the Sisodia Rajputs, and he thought that encountering this grand procession was an auspicious sign. And so it was. The signing of the deed of ownership went smoothly and without any complications. The old Raja placed the ink imprint of his thumb on the document next to his signature to authenticate the transaction – and all formalities were completed. He became the new owner of the royal shamshan. The next chapter was about to begin, and it promised to be interesting.

**The Legend**

The locals called this place “Rajawali Chatri” – “the memorials of the Raja,” but to the rulers of Bharatpur, it was known as Moksha Bhumi – the land of liberation. When the first Raja of the dynasty was seeking a place to build a fort, he noticed a sadhu sitting under a tree by a pond; nearby, a leopard and a cow were peacefully drinking water. Seeing this, the Raja bowed to the saint with the greatest reverence and asked him to indicate a place suitable for the construction of the future structure.

The sadhu showed a good place for the fort and palace, just 20 kilometers from Govardhan; today it is called “Dig.” Before starting the construction of the fort and royal residence, the Raja ordered a Bhumi Puja – a traditional ceremony of purification and worship of the earth before any new construction – to be performed at the chosen site. The saint was honored to dig the first handful of earth, but on the thirteenth stroke of the shovel, the Raja stopped him, deciding that the hard work would tire the honored guest. The sadhu turned to the Raja: “For the honors and respect shown to me, and for the recognition of the Divine power, I grant prosperity to 13 generations of your lineage. But a royal title does not protect from the cycles of rebirth, and even the most pious, after death, will have to visit both various heavenly worlds and the underworld. However, I will show you a place of true liberation: anyone who is cremated there will attain it.” The ascetic led the royal retinue to Govardhan and indicated the boundaries of the future shamshan. As the sadhu had predicted, the cremation ground served the Bharatpur dynasty for thirteen generations.

…In the daylight, looking at the majestic silhouettes of the palace-mausoleums, he recognized the place he had first seen in meditation in the autumn of 1985. These were the very buildings he had tried to describe to his laughing relatives as a child. The predictions of Satyacharan Lahiri about the crowds passing by the gates, the words of Babaji about practicing at the shamshan, his own “fantasies” and recent visions – everything fell into place.

The old tree growing in front of the huge white house also looked familiar. Quite recently, in Mathura, he and Devahuti had seen it and the white-bearded old man sitting beneath it during a spiritual séance. It was from under this tree that the sage (whoever he was) had conveyed to him the message about the need to begin work on the Gita commentaries. Was that vision a projection of the future? It seemed that anything was possible in this place.

Built for the Bharatpur royal dynasty, the palaces not only symbolized the former grandeur of a bygone era; they were becoming the stage upon which the drama of his own future was to unfold. And it awaited its unveiling.

**Ciphers.**

Built in 1823, two memorial palaces neighbored a large white house – it was there that he and his sister took up residence. No one knew why this house had been built on the *smashan*. Perhaps members of the royal family planned to stay there while visiting the cremation grounds of their ancestors. He, however, preferred to think that the house was built exclusively for him, as no one had ever lived in it before.

Large, with elements of colonial architecture, the white house stood at a kind of crossroads of internal roads, and the center of these roads lay exactly in the middle of the room he had chosen for himself. It seemed there was a certain metaphysical meaning in the location of the building. In many cultures, crossroads are considered magical places, portals to other worlds. Indians believe that, along with other, not necessarily friendly forces, the creative energy of Shakti dwells there. So crossroads are places for the distribution of various energies, both divine and infernal, however people might try to classify them. In any case, the architect who placed the large white mansion in the center of the *smashan*, right in the middle of the *parikrama* around Govardhan Hill, certainly had some special, unknown idea.

They counted one hundred and eight doorways in the house. Of course, almost all the doors had been stolen or broken. Houses in India are rarely built with so many entrances, as doors are costly, but this building was erected for kings, and they could afford much. Gradually, all 108 heavy wooden doors were restored and replaced.

In the upstairs room, they hung a large photograph of Lahiri Mahasaya, but something kept happening to it: either the glass cracked, or the frame broke for no reason, or the photograph simply tore loose and fell. Numerous attempts to repair or rehang the photo were in vain; the incidents continued until he simply ordered the photo taken down from the wall. Lahiri Mahasaya and the *parampara* were always with him; he respected the Guru, but here, on the *smashan*, special energies reigned. Not everyone could live here permanently, and not all photographs could hang here.

…Often, people carry within themselves the vibrations of the earth; its silent melody is born not only from the planet itself, but also from the earth element present in human bodies. With the help of these GPS trackers, each individual is "connected" to the planet. People are by no means conductors, but merely musical instruments of a great orchestra, and the earth itself plays its ancient hymn on the strings of bodies and hearts. For millennia, the melody of Govardhan Hill sounded, mingling with loud music, the high voices of *pandits* and street vendors, the tunes of *bhajans*, and the falsetto of mantras. On auspicious days, at full moon, the melody of the ancient hill was nourished by the powerful and continuous hum and vibrations of millions of bodies – this overture swelled into a crescendo. He came here to become part of the symphony.

**Giriraj**

The story of Govardhan Hill, Govardhan Giriraj, or the King of Mountains, as he is also called, is known throughout India. And yet, it is worth telling this story once more in the context of the biography of the current "spiritual lodger" of Giriraj. The other *sadhus* and holy people who lived on the Hill until recently were evicted by officials or driven out by the police.

Long-awaited improvements in this area began almost twenty-eight years after he and his sister arrived in Govardhan. The most radical of all was the campaign to demolish illegally built ashrams and temporary tent cities that had sprung up over the last two hundred years or so. Where all these *sadhus* went after the eviction remained unknown.

However, the harsh vibrations of Govardhan changed, becoming more peaceful, even subtle. A new fence was installed along the entire 21-kilometer perimeter of the hill – now he remained on the Hill alone, as no one else lived on Giriraj permanently. Strangely, the noise of the crowds of pilgrims on the *parikrama* did not affect his solitude at all, and he almost enjoyed this hum. The fence prevented the relentless littering of the surroundings: human worship and devotion generate tons of garbage. Govardhan Hill turned green again, as in the days when the *rishi* Pulastya<sup>143</sup> placed Him on the land of Vraja…

The son of the Creator Brahma, the *rishi*<sup>144</sup> Pulastya once visited a mountain called Drona Giri<sup>145</sup> on a special matter. The mountain was a powerful conscious being and a great ascetic – a *tapasvi*.<sup>146</sup> Upon reaching the abode of Drona Giri, Pulastya *rishi* saw the hill Giriraj playing on his mother's lap. Legend does not mention her name, but it is quite natural to assume that mountains give birth to hills. The child-hill was beautiful: grapevines bloomed on it, streams murmured, caves darkened, birds sang, deer grazed, and peacocks danced among its greenery. At that moment, the *rishi* had a revelation: this hill would grant liberation to all who seek to free themselves from the *samsaric* cycle of births and deaths…

Pulastya bowed to Dronacharya: "O great Drona, lord of mountains, the gods worship you, you are rich in healing herbs, you heal people and prolong life. I have come from the eternal city of Kashi, the abode of Shiva. Sinners who die there are liberated; the holy Ganga flows there, and the Lord Vishwanath himself has chosen this city as His abode. But there is no mountain there. Therefore, I would like to bring your son to Kashi. I desire nothing other than to perform asceticism on the slopes of your son's divine body."

Pulastya knew: if he brought Giriraj to Benares and worshipped Shiva, performing continuous austerities on the Hill, miracles would occur.

Hearing Pulastya's words, Drona felt a surge of sorrow; his heart was pierced by fatherly love, and tears flowed from his cave-like eyes. He said: "My son is very dear to me, but I fear your curse." And he addressed Giriraj: "Go with this sage to Bharata-varsha, the land of karma, the land of liberation." The young hill asked the *rishi*: "How will you lift and carry me? My length is eight *yojanas*,<sup>147</sup> my width five *yojanas*, my height two *yojanas*, and I am heavy…"

Pulastya replied: "My dear, come into my arms, and I will carry you to Kashi. Only promise that you will stay where I place you…". Giriraj bowed in prostration at his father's feet and sat on the *rishi's* right hand. Moving slowly and carefully, carrying the hill, Pulastya traveled for some time until he reached Vraja. Remembering his past life, Giriraj said to himself: "I must not leave this place: here, on the banks of the Yamuna, Krishna will appear. Beholding Him, I will attain perfection." The hill grew heavy; the tired Pulastya forgot his promise not to place Giriraj anywhere except Varanasi. Exhausted by the heavy burden, the sage set the hill down on the ground, and it moved no further. The *rishi* tried to lift it again using mystical powers. However, despite embracing Giriraj and gently coaxing him, the son of Drona Giri did not budge an inch.

Giriraj said softly to Pulastya: "O sage, you have placed me on the ground, and I will not move from this spot, for such was my vow." Then, abandoning all attempts, with lips trembling with anger, Pulastya cursed the hill: "Presumptuous hillock, you did not fulfill my wish and refused to follow me to Varanasi! Therefore, you shall sink into the earth, submerging yourself daily by the size of a sesame seed, until you disappear entirely!". Since then, Govardhan Hill has been steadily sinking downward by a quarter of a millimeter per day, a fact confirmed by geological research.

Another peculiar and less known detail is mentioned in the *Yogini Tantra*: "Having gathered the ashes remaining from the body of the *daitya* Keshin, the Creator sprinkled them with water from his *kamandalu*<sup>148</sup> and raised a mountain, neither high nor low, abounding in food for cows. Eating this grass, the cows were satisfied and happy. And then the Creator gave the mountain the name Govardhana." *Yogini Tantra* 15:27–29.

The name "Govardhan" means "nourisher of cows," and as long as they graze on the Hill, Brahma's karma also diminishes, for the Creator, without a doubt, made a terrible mess of this world. As a result of the power struggle between the new and old gods, Indra tried to flood Vrindavan with torrential rains. Krishna lifted the Hill and held it for seven days like an umbrella, protecting the cattle and people. Indra was forced to retreat, and Govardhan Hill became a symbol of Krishna's triumph. This victory symbolized the decline of the Vedic gods and the rise of the new cult of Krishna. Another, less known belief held that as long as the hill stands, the power of Kali Yuga<sup>149</sup> is restrained. Legends were everywhere; they nourished the very soil of Vraja, and the best thing about them was that sometimes they came true.

In 2005, after the cremation of the Maharaja's cousin, two 90-year-old Jat elders visited the *smashan*. They sat in the *darshan* hall for some time in silence, sipping tea. The old men remembered many stories and legends about the Jat dynasty, but that day they spoke of an unusual prophecy: a certain *rishi*, whose name history did not preserve, predicted that Pulastya's curse would be lifted if a king were cremated on Govardhan Hill – then in his next incarnation he would become a yogi. This yogi would find a Shivalingam and install it at the site of his own cremation, there where his body was consigned to fire in his past life. Then Govardhan Hill would stop sinking, and the curse would be lifted.

The Jats were not trying to flatter or play up to him; they were too wise and old for that. Analyzing other stories about the Hill and what he had learned about his past lives, he decided not to draw any conclusions. One thing could be said with certainty: he was luckier than the *rishi* Pulastya, for it was he who was fortunate enough to live and perform yogic austerities on the holy hill. Something greater than mere luck had brought him here, to the most powerful *smashan* of northern India. To the land of liberation.

**The Battle of the Latrine Fields**

It was only in September 1993 that he was finally able to move permanently. Throughout the long, hot summer, he lived in a guesthouse on Radha Kund, overseeing the clearing of the land and the construction of necessary facilities; the place had been abandoned for many years, with neither running water nor toilets…
Life on the royal *smashan* was far from peaceful and meditative for several more long and harsh years. The Bharatpur Rajaon ki Chhatri, the Royal Memorial Palaces of the rulers of Bharatpur, seemed majestic only from a distance; the territory was in a state of decay and desolation. For many years, the palaces had been used by local gangs as a base for overnight stays and illegal trade, and everything that had not yet been stolen or broken was subjected to systematic vandalism. Obscene graffiti adorned the walls and intricately carved colonnades. From these choice specimens of primitive folk art, he learned much that was previously unknown, about humanity in general and the sexual preferences of the local inhabitants in particular.

Assessing the scale of the devastation and the vileness of the barbarism, he felt a cold anger. Excrement was everywhere: the territory was used as a public open-air toilet, and even the cremation site of the Raja had been desecrated. Cow dung, monkey feces, and dog droppings were not as repulsive as the filth left by humans. Perhaps the aggressive marking of territory symbolized an intention to destroy and defile the very idea of the past, which the majestic palaces recalled. Defecating on the king’s grave literally symbolized the state of consciousness of the contemporaries: they did not care about historical monuments, they felt not the slightest respect for the memory of the former rulers of Bharatpur. The harsh realities of life in the North Indian province prevailed over any sentiment, feeling, or memory.

The wall surrounding the *smashan* was broken in several places, the territory was unguarded, and therefore belonged to no one, which made it an ideal place for morning evacuations. Nearly 800 local residents still continued to relieve themselves on the *smashan*. The wretchedness of life could spur them to nothing but the regular production of feces, the spewing of obscenities, and the perception of the rest of the world as a giant latrine. The past did not exist, the present was shitty, and for everyone else it was to become the same. When the local shit-stirrers began to challenge the new master of the *smashan*, the anger that filled him poured out. Almost daily, he had to defend his right to this territory with his fists.

Western spiritual seekers may find it difficult to imagine a Yoga Guru using choice curses while slapping, kicking, and landing tangible blows on local hooligans. However, there were no other ways to defend the right to the territory; only the strength of muscles and a stick were weighty arguments. The guards he hired watched the battles from a distance; being natives of Braj, they did not need complications with local authorities. The show was repeated almost daily; he acted as the main hero, while the rest of the cast, consisting of provocateurs, marauders, and other riffraff, changed constantly.

Meanwhile, the epic “Battle of the Latrine Fields,” as he called the operation to clear the territory, was about to begin. But even the untouchable sweepers refused to enter the territory. Not because of prejudices or frightening tales of the evil spirits of the *smashan*, but because the place was desecrated and befouled beyond all measure. The *smashan* was defiled in the literal sense. In the end, a plan emerged: he began daily to buy several liters of local moonshine for the cleaners. Wrapping their faces in rags, slightly staggering under the influence of the neutralizing alcoholic elixir and muttering curses, the sweepers set to work. It took them several months to clear the nine acres—only then was the Battle of the Latrine Fields won. Ahead of him lay many other, no less difficult, campaigns.

**Prisoner of the Dark Lord**

Cultivating the land was practically impossible, not only because of the lack of water and funds, but also because of the unceasing problems with the locals. Bandits of all stripes, together with the so-called mafia of *pandit*-priests, tried to pressure the newly arrived outsider who had taken possession of the most famous “haunted place” in Braj. All conflicts began the same way: the guards would report that so-and-so was waiting at the gate and demanded a meeting with the owner.

For most inhabitants of the holy city, the sanctity of the place left no impression at all. Dialogues began with absurd claims or insulting demands for money and quickly turned into outright mockery and an attempt to provoke him. Selling land and real estate transactions in small Indian towns are always complicated and troublesome. No one believed a piece of paper, even if signed by the previous owner, the old Raja himself. He constantly had to prove his right to own the desirable plot of land located in the very heart of Govardhan.

Instead of buying off the raiders with money, he chose to fight and wait to see what would happen next. There was simply no money, nor any reason to yield. He knew the unwritten rules well: if you pay even once, the extortionists will return again.

A heated exchange of insults and threats was usually followed by a fistfight. He was in excellent shape and much stronger than his emaciated or overfed opponents, and he offered no arguments other than brute physical strength and the rage pulsing in his veins. The land belonged to him, the property was acquired legally, and he intended to live here. Yogic *ahimsa*¹⁵⁰, that is, non-violence, is an excellent principle, and it should be adhered to as long as your family is not threatened and your home is not under siege. A lone stranger in these inhospitable lands, he was determined to stay.

…In those years, the renowned Pandit Gaya Prasad Ji, or simply “Pandit Ji,” a respected man and, by common opinion, a true saint, lived in Govardhan. Vaid Ji, or Gangadhar Upadhyay Vaidji, an old Ayurvedic doctor who often visited the new resident of the *smashan*, was a follower of the Pandit and once brought him a book with yogic commentaries on the Gita. The saint had it read to him three times, and in the pure Brajvasi dialect, he delivered his verdict: “This man is now a prisoner of the Dark Lord of the Hill. And once it takes someone prisoner, the deity never lets go. This yogi will remain here forever.” For a usually silent saint, these were quite loud and unusual words, and Vaidji hurried to the *smashan* to convey the strange message.

…At that very moment, another skirmish had just ended. The new owner stood at the gate; the dusty area had already become an improvised boxing ring for receiving malicious visitors. He was wiping sweat from his brow, still in a rage, and this was by no means the best moment for Vaidji’s excited story. Still not cooled down, he listened with half an ear; the message made no sense, scoundrels disturbed his peace daily, the place itself was hopeless and befouled—and he would leave this shitty backwater right now. Let’s see who would try to take him prisoner. He ordered Devahuti to pack their bags, as they were leaving immediately.

The useless gatekeeper was told to call an auto-rickshaw, and the yellow-green, rattling shell appeared almost immediately. Seeing his anger, no one dared to reason with him. They loaded into the auto and headed for the railway station in Mathura. When the rickshaw reached the nearest temple, located just 100 meters from the *smashan*, the auto’s chain broke. He cursed the driver, and they had to walk back to their gate. After a while, the watchman stopped a jeep, and they set off again. They drove up to the temple, where the broken auto-rickshaw still stood, when the jeep’s gearbox emitted a dry crack—the vehicle stopped dead in its tracks: the transmission was broken.

At that same moment, his cousin drove into the *smashan* courtyard, hoping to show off his recently purchased car; he was ordered to take them to the station immediately. What happened to the car next was unclear. Emitting a screeching grind, it stopped right behind the ill-fated jeep and auto-rickshaw. Using his considerably expanded vocabulary of curses from the last few months, he told his cousin everything he thought of his car—and strode back to the *smashan*.

There, an acquaintance from Agra was waiting for him, who had just arrived from the car dealership in a brand new Maruti van. “Guruji, please bless the new car; I plan to start a tourism business…” He promised to do so at Mathura station immediately after a successful trip. Their luggage was loaded into the car for the fourth time, everyone settled in comfortably, the motor made a few coughing and growling sounds—and fell silent.

By that time, his anger had cooled, and he understood: someone (or something) was indeed trying to prevent him from leaving. The fact that all four vehicles had broken down simultaneously could not be a simple coincidence. He turned to Devahuti: “We are not going anywhere. I am staying and will accept whatever is destined to happen…”

A few weeks later, he decided to visit the saint, and was about to do so when an inner voice said: “Do not go now; after meeting you, the saint will not live long…” The voice sounded so distinct that it could not be a figment of the imagination; its only possessor could be the ruler and deity of the place, Govardhan Maharaj himself. The visit was postponed. Several months passed; the saint fell seriously ill and slipped into a coma.

When Vaidji suggested visiting the dying man, the same familiar voice sounded: “Pandit Ji will not attain liberation until you come to him.”

It was early evening, around five o’clock, when they came to Pandit Ji’s house. The saint was unconscious. There was nothing much to do; he bowed to the dying man and returned to the *smashan*. That same night, at 4 o’clock in the morning, Gaya Prasad Ji left his body. In Vraja, there is a widespread belief that a peaceful death at this age near the Holy Hill is nothing other than the grace of the Dark Lord of the Hill, Giriraj Maharaj. But what exactly was this grace?

Recalling the farewell visit, he gradually realized its symbolism: the last true saint of Braj had given the last *darshan* to the first yogi who dared to settle on the *smashan*. Times and places were undergoing radical changes: the land, connected for millennia with the name of Krishna, was opening to new energies and influences. In a few years, he would begin to worship Shiva here, and this would create a new dynamic, giving rise to other tendencies. The time was coming to support and preserve, to hold on to the present and let go of the past.

**The Last Confrontation**

The first winter on the *smashan* was cold and harsh; they slept wrapped in woolen shawls under thick blankets, drinking endless cups of hot tea. In the evenings, they tried to warm themselves by a smoking fireplace, and the stars watched them through the gaps in the roof. The large-scale clearing and repairs on the territory proceeded apace. He managed to juggle numerous responsibilities, remaining a Guru and practicing yogi, as well as a farmer, contractor, and boxer. Continuing to defend himself against the insolent local bandits who harassed him almost daily, he held on.

At night, during *sadhana*, the essence of the Universal laws and the power of *pranic* energy filled his heart with wonder at the preciousness of life and the grandeur of death, and these pacifying energies contrasted sharply with the coarse, angry tension of the days.

One day, his sister returned from Mathura with alarming news: confirmed rumors were spreading that an assassination attempt was being prepared against him, no less. They waited all night, but no one came. A couple of days later, the whole story became known.

In Mathura, there was a gang of raiders known for the merciless murder of many unfortunates over property deals. Some time ago, they had tried

To acquire the royal cremation ground, but the old raja had sold the property to a young yogi. All legal formalities were observed, the transaction was completed – but not for the gang. After a series of planned attacks and provocations ended in failure, the thugs hatched a new plan – simply to kill the new owner. The plan could be carried out relatively easily, since he lived alone, except for a few guards who would not think of putting up resistance. Having finished him off, the bandits would gain the property; they were certain there would be no investigation.

When the gang was discussing the raid, the name of his uncle somehow came up – and the wife of the mafia boss heard it. Some time ago, the uncle had saved the life of the gang leader, her husband. That day, bloodied and wounded, the mobster had fled through the streets of Mathura, pursued by assassins, and tried to take shelter in a doctor's house.

The doctor hid the unexpected "patient" under a large table in his office and calmly continued filling out papers, while the gangster, enclosed on three sides by heavy oak panels, crouched at his feet. It was a perfect scene from a Bollywood film, except the blood and knives were real, and the doctor was certainly risking his life. When the gangsters burst into the office searching for the fugitive, the doctor sent them on a false trail. With restrained dignity, giving explanations to the uninvited guests, he felt the hot breath of the man hiding under the table on his calves. The bandits left empty-handed, and the story of the miraculous rescue became legendary. Now this same gangster was planning to murder the nephew of his savior.

Realizing who the target of the assassination was, the mafia wife flew into a rage: "You bastard! You're going to kill the nephew of the very man who saved your worthless life! Woe is me, I am married to an ungrateful scoundrel!" The mobster was genuinely surprised: "So the yogi from the cremation ground is the doctor's nephew?!" – and he called off the hit. Certain notions of honor live even in hardened hearts. Though confrontations continued nonetheless.

The time when he helped his brother manage a large farm had toughened him; he was no stranger to conflicts involving threats, shootouts, and fistfights. Back then they were outnumbered, yet they survived through perseverance and will. Besides, they had nowhere else to go. Just as now, when he had to defend his right to this place, to life here. This time he was alone and felt not the slightest fear, only irritation and disgust.

…In Gwalior, while visiting the gym, he often observed the so-called "tough guys" when they came to work out. Having studied their behavior and manners well, he was not particularly impressed by their "posturing and showdowns" – they were the most ordinary people, and all the harshness and roughness were cultivated, contrived. No one was capable of sustaining constant rage. The motives guiding these people were primitive, and the only language they understood and respected was the language of brute force. Here in Govardhan, it was exactly the same.

Most of the local thugs and hired bandits came from one notoriously "criminal" village, whose inhabitants "specialized" in various crimes. Any native of this glorious place could be hired for extortion, robbery, or murder. Even the police took precautions when entering their area.

…Carefully carrying a heavy rifle as if it were a shovel or a broom, the guard entered the room with an apologetic look: "Sir, there is a man who has come to fight you, waiting at the gate…" Just like that, an invitation to yet another brawl was delivered by his employee, who was supposed to protect the owner from uninvited guests. "Why keep this useless idiot at all? Why am I paying him?!" The answer, of course, was well known: being a local, the guard could not stand up to local crime bosses. This was the "privilege" of the employer.

"Please, sir, hurry! This is a very dangerous man with a terrible reputation, and he insists on meeting you."

Enveloped in a cloud of his reputation's glory, the mobster waited at the gate. To begin with, they exchanged a few introductory words, then came obscene abuse, seasoning the fight. He gave the uninvited visitor a thorough beating, making sure the blows landed on the most painful spots. Growling with pain and wiping blood, the bandit rose from the ground: "You think you're so strong, but you're all alone here – and tonight I'll bring all my men! We'll storm this place with four or five tractor trailers and break every bone in your body!"

"Come, I'll be waiting." Spewing obscenities, the beaten gangster limped away.

Hearing about the threats, Devahuti grew seriously worried: "What will happen when these bandits show up?" Trying to dispel her nervousness, he calmly replied: "Let's see what happens next." There was plenty of reason for concern: they were just the two of them, not counting the useless guards and a few hired workers. This "guard" could not compete with hardened criminals, and yet waiting it out was better than fleeing.

Meanwhile, it grew dark. Around seven o'clock, the watchman brought news: ten minutes after the bandit had left the cremation ground, he was hit by a bus right in front of the police station. Crushed to pulp…

The news was rather sad. A sadness of the kind that usually comes over one upon seeing a dog or cat run over on the road, though he could not even remember the bruised and bloodied face of that man. His knuckles still ached from the blows to the cheekbones of one who was already a corpse. Flattened by the bus wheels, rolled in blood and dust, the recent problem had turned to ashes on a funeral pyre and vanished. This incident added spice to his reputation; for a while, the number of hunters after others' property diminished, and those wishing to "settle scores" with him disappeared for a long time. Almost forever.

**Saving the Worker Fateh**

Shyam, the chief cowherd, had worked for them for twenty-nine years, ever since the small herd had arrived at the cremation ground from Vrindavan. At first there were few cows, but Devahuti persistently continued to pick up injured homeless bulls and cows, and their number gradually grew. They gave as much milk as they wanted, they were not separated from their calves – such was the custom of Braj since the time of Krishna.

Even when he was first hired, the cowherd did not look particularly young; his appearance fully matched the number of years spent under the relentless sun, in the winter cold, and in hard labor. It was quite possible he was the same age as his employer. It was hard to tell. A smile never stretched Shyam's thin lips; his narrowed eyes looked out with mockery; he was clever and tenacious. The cowherd was born and had lived his entire life in Braj; he had seen enough sadhus and so-called swamis and did not consider the young, broad-shouldered yogi to be anything special. He was certain the newcomer would not stay long.

What this yoga was and exactly what the new owner did at night was of no consequence. Yoga was an exotic craft of wandering fakirs, a whim. The workers only knew that during the day the master held darshans for visitors, and at night he was occupied with "bhajans" until dawn.

In the local slang, "bhajan" meant any kind of religious worship; little was known about any other kind of spiritual practice; pranayamas and meditations were considered quirks, side effects of religious fervor.

For the last 400 years, love for the Divine, bhakti, has been considered in Braj the "specialization" of Krishna's followers, and true Brajwasis worship the Dark Lord of Govardhan Hill.

Repair and restoration work continued through the winter of 1996; the house needed major reconstruction, and he intended to return the building to its original state. Some bricked-up doorways were reopened, internal partitions were erected, forming new rooms. Huge stone slabs and ceiling beams weighing around 350 kilograms were stacked outside, on the lawn in front of the house. That day, a large stone beam needed to be placed onto the load-bearing walls of a doorway. About 15 workers labored under the direction of the chief mason, who always asked him to be present during the most critical work: "Guruji, please just be here while we do this."

Using ropes, wooden blocks, and bars, the workers began to lift the beam, guiding one end onto a supporting column. The other end of the beam had to be raised and placed over the doorway. One of the workers, Fateh, stuck his head under the raised beam to make sure all the angles were perfectly aligned. Suddenly someone slipped, people shouted: "Oh! It's slipped, it's going, it's falling!.." – and everyone recoiled, dropping the stone block, afraid they would be crushed.

What followed next could probably only be explained by an adrenaline rush: throwing his ever-present walking stick aside, he stretched his arms above his head without hesitation, supporting the beam. Fateh froze, expecting to be crushed to pulp. Sensing the impending tragedy, the workers stood motionless. Realizing he was holding the 350-kilogram load alone, he shouted: "You bastards, where have you gone!!?" The workers rushed to help, and in a single surge, the beam was laid over the doorway. That day, all the workers, one after another, touched his feet for the first time as a sign of respect. The cowherd bowed before him first.

For the inhabitants of Braj, the manifestation of supernatural strength in life-and-death situations confirmed the divine presence, while also reminding them of the fragility and transience of life. These "uneducated" people knew: only a true brahmachari could hold such colossal weight alone, and only the patronage of Hanuman, merciful to those who observe celibacy and purity, could explain the reason for such a miracle. These people never sought God, knowing exactly where He was. In India, any low-caste sweeper or child will say without hesitation: God is everywhere. That day, people saw the manifestation of His power in the superhuman strength of their taciturn employer, and from that day on, he was unconditionally accepted as a true saint, since no other proof could have convinced the Brajwasis of the authenticity of his spiritual power. A few days later, he unobtrusively tried to lift one of the stone beams still stacked on the lawn in front of the house – and could not move it a single millimeter.

Whenever in a good mood, the worker Fateh would tell the story of divine intervention and the superpowers of Guruji to anyone willing to listen. And the townspeople listened, because cheating death is not easy, and besides, the story was told by an eyewitness and participant, one who had happened to be in a place famous for spirits and ghosts, to look death in the face and remain unharmed. All this could only have happened by the grace of the gods.

Before this incident, the workers thought the master only ate, slept, and preached to random simpletons who wandered in for a cup of free tea – this lifestyle was common among the local "holy acharyas."

Several years later, his presence in Govardhan began to be associated with pious, if impractical, deeds. During a drought, and later without any special pretext, he provided farmers with access to water from his plantation, cleared lands, planted trees on barren soil, and hired people to clean the road.

The coolers with water were installed at the local police station of the Siddha Siddhanta Academy, and dormitories were built for the patrol officers – and from time to time, officers with heavy carbines would come to pay their respects and drink a cup of tea in the coolness of the darshan hall. They blended perfectly into the motley crowd of peasants, lawyers, teachers, bakers, and visiting Europeans.

Gradually, he built a solid reputation in Braj as a philanthropist and a truly religious man, who had not abandoned his sadhana for a single day in the last thirty years – and this in itself was astonishing.

Debts and bills were paid on time; he was dependent on no one, not even well-wishers. Apart from the support of the parampara and the grace of Govardhan Hill, he could count only on himself, and he had no intention of changing the existing state of affairs.

**On Visitors, Prophets, and the Fruits of Karma**

The stubborn cactus growing everywhere had to be uprooted, the dilapidated buildings demolished, and the system of internal roads and irrigation ditches still needed to be completed. More than a hundred workers were hired, promised payment at the end of the month; two days remained until the appointed date, but there was nowhere to obtain the funds. It was naive to think the day laborers would agree to wait for an indefinite payday – they had families to feed. From the stress and anxiety over the situation, he could not sleep. Until it dawned on him: the same forces that had brought him to the smashan and compelled him to begin the work of improvement and clearing would take care of everyone involved in the process. He was merely a manager, an intermediary; the money would appear, and the people would be paid – because all of this was part of a larger plan. With these thoughts, he fell asleep.

The next day, an unexpected visitor arrived from Bengal, whom he had seen only once. Back then, in 1987 in Asansol, hundreds of people had come to see the new Guru – this man was one of the visitors. He had asked for help. Once, a successful goalkeeper for the oldest and most famous sports club in India and Asia, the Mohun Bagan Athletic Club, he had tried to stop a penalty kick and, receiving a powerful blow from the ball to his chest, lost consciousness. This man never fully recovered from the injury; he continued to cough up blood and occasionally lose consciousness. Since he was an excellent goalkeeper, the club had fully covered the costs of his treatment in Germany and England, but there was no improvement.

Having heard the story, he felt a hot wave of compassion rising to his heart, followed by an instantaneous flash of a vision of the future, and in this vision, the goalkeeper was back on the field. Obeying an impulse, he felt in the pocket of his kurta for a pellet of solid mercury and handed it to the visitor: “From today, everything will be fine.” The magical pill was to be kept under the tongue. Within a few weeks, the alchemically processed mercury healed the goalkeeper. The footballer decided to give all the money he had received as compensation for medical expenses to his savior. On that miraculous June evening, he arrived in Govardhan with a donation, and the sum was mysteriously equal to the amount that needed to be paid to the workers. Sometimes the laws of karma worked perfectly.

Around the same time, an elderly relative visited him. A former military man, he carefully observed the activities on the campus, spending whole days calculating the possible expenses, the number of workers, their wages, and the volume of work. Then, unable to restrain himself any longer, the retired uncle demanded answers: “How do you manage, financially speaking?”

“By the grace of Lord Govardhan Hill, Babaji, and the parampara…” “Spare me all that spiritual nonsense, answer honestly – how do you manage the finances?!”

He sighed: “Give me fifty rupees.” Caught off guard, the uncle reached into his wallet and handed over the requested sum. He accepted the note: “This is how we manage…” Jokes aside, times were hard. Money was tight, although somehow he managed to pay wages on time. At the end of each month, they barely scraped together enough to cover expenses, as if some invisible accountant was making sure the show went on. He took no payment for initiation into yoga, gave no paid lectures, and conducted no seminars. He had no intention of going anywhere to give talks; he had seen the world, and the world had seen his best and worst sides, and both knew enough about each other. He had plenty of other things to do, and the nightly sadhana required complete focus and presence.

For someone living on the smashan, any commercial activity would be a contradiction of the yogic code. In ancient times, yogis managed to look at society objectively only by severing all ties with it. If a person does not participate in any schemes of enrichment, the system may reluctantly loosen its grip. And even despite the minimal contact between society and the smashan, the cremation ground was still part of the social infrastructure, as it provided final, one-time services. Although in India, smashans are avoided due to superstitions and gloomy thoughts of death, everyone ends up there at the journey’s end.

None of the teachers in this lineage ever charged for knowledge. Shri Satyacharan Lahiri was very strict in this regard and accepted only voluntary offerings from students, allowing each to determine the amount themselves. He followed the same principles, and putting a price tag on the art of yoga seemed impossible.

Having never worked anywhere, he often joked about it, saying he was the most famous unemployed man in Gwalior who happened to own a palace. Moreover, the lack of a steady income saved him from the “necessity” of marriage. No family would agree to give their daughter in marriage to an unemployed man – and this suited him perfectly. Marriage had never been on his list of priorities.

After selling the land that had belonged to his deceased father, he received a share as an inheritance. Apart from voluntary offerings, there were no other sources of income. His personal needs were minimal, but the large estate required constant financial input, and every rupee that came in was invested in the repair and maintenance of the farm, the house, and the plantation. The land paid him generously for his care with the hard currency of the ever-changing beauty of the seasons, the blossoms of the trees, the quiet whisper of the harvests, and the serenades of the birds.

Over the years, he received various “commercial offers,” and some were quite unusual. For instance, an invitation to go abroad (to fill stadiums with newly converted yoga adepts) came from a high-ranking officer of a foreign state, who was involved in a program for researching paranormal phenomena. This man represented a government-funded group searching for people with extrasensory abilities. Over time, they had managed to assemble a team of fairly strong mediums. Having stumbled upon information about Kriya Yoga on a website, the officer printed out a photograph of the young guru and showed it to one, or rather one of the mediums, a ninety-three-year-old African American woman, a kind of chief oracle, like in the film *The Matrix*. She said: “You are asking about Shiva? This man’s Muladhara chakra is the brightest on the planet; he possesses the most powerful earth energy. Connected to Jupiter, he is one of a kind…” This statement was intriguing, though it explained absolutely nothing.

It is worth noting: all this took place in 2001, two years after the release of the famous science fiction film *The Matrix*, directed by the Wachowski brothers. Who knows, perhaps the film’s characters were “sketched” from real people…

The officer promised him considerable fees for participating in the research. Skilled PR managers and promoters were to ensure the crushing success and popularity of the new “spiritual messiah”; luxurious residences and private jets were only a matter of time… But there was a catch: the organization would take full charge of organizing his schedule, tours, and seminars. In other words, he would become an employee.

…Watching the disciples prostrating before the Guru, this visitor declared that he felt an immense energy flowing from the teacher to the devotee. And this was indeed happening: sometimes his heart would involuntarily “open,” pouring out a stream of compassion and blessing; however, it was impossible to measure or classify.

As a courteous host, he tried to entertain the guest and once demonstrated his radiant aura. The officer was astounded. His interests revolved mainly around paranormal abilities and their impact on the psyche; yoga was a secondary subject. Upon closer examination, the goals of the organization he represented were not particularly original.

For him personally, the role of a “business guru,” an advertised “enlightener,” held no interest whatsoever. Any commercial scheme inevitably creates a web of financial obligations and dependencies, and in the end, can become an obstacle to the only thing that concerned him – yoga. He valued his independence. Moreover, he was well aware that people trying to profit from spiritual teachings almost always end up embroiled in all sorts of scandals.

The visitor returned three times, and conversing with him was quite interesting. But leaving the smashan was out of the question. He was exactly where he was supposed to be.

**The King is Dead, Long Live the Yogi**

At the end of June 1995, around noon, hundreds of vultures began to gather under a huge pipal tree. One after another, more than 300 large, brownish birds landed in the northern part of the garden, leisurely folded their wings, and hurried to drink from the puddles and irrigation ditches. Having quenched their thirst, the birds huddled in a tight circle in the shade of the tree, sheltering from the scorching sun. Something in the air had subtly changed, as if the summer heat had become charged with electricity. The last to descend majestically, slowing before landing, was the king of the vultures. He was at least one and a half times larger than the others. The leader slowly strode to the water, and the flock parted. Taking only three sips, he rose again into the air and, powerfully beating his wings, headed northwest. The flock followed him.

The vultures were a real scourge of the area; they persistently occupied the roofs of houses, their toxic droppings corroding even plastic and galvanized coverings. Getting rid of them was problematic; they invariably returned. But that day, they were seen for the last time.

Five days after this event, a small stone canopy adorning the spire of the dome of one of the palaces cracked and fell. He immediately thought this could mean bad news for the royal family – and he was not mistaken. The last ruler of the Bharatpur dynasty died the next day. Maharaja Brijendra Singh¹⁵¹, duly anointed upon his ascension to the throne, was a fading reminder of the past glory of Indian kingdoms, and with his passing, the echo of the past fell silent. The era of kings was over; like any era, it was finite.

The old raja was accorded a state funeral, with a guard of honor, a military escort, and a ceremonial gun salute. On July 8, 1995, thousands of people accompanied the deceased raja on his final journey. Towards evening, the solemn procession reached Govardhan, and crowds flooded the smashan.

When signing the documents for the transfer of the royal smashan to the new owner, the parties had agreed that the last Maharaja of Bharatpur would be cremated on the land of his ancestors, and this agreement was fulfilled. Although they had met only once, the Maharaja had impressed him as a true ruler and a worthy man.

…The cremation ended, the visitors dispersed. It was quite late. Several disciples from Bengal joined them from Devahuti; dinner was served. From the terrace, the amber glow of the embers was visible; the raja’s funeral pyre was still smoldering, the atmosphere was eerie.

¹⁵¹ Maharaja Brijendra Singh (01.12.1918 – 08.07.1995) – ruler of the princely state of Bharatpur from 1929 to 1947.

By custom, the royal family left four or five guards at the funeral pyre. This custom has its basis: sometimes tantrics try to obtain bones or other parts of the royal remains. In India, it is believed that such relics can be used in certain rituals, as they possess the most powerful magical properties. Braj is famous for sorcery and black magic, and the precautions were far from superfluous.

The time came to walk the dogs, who had been locked in the house all day and were now whining impatiently. Two Dobermans, with eyes burning in the darkness like red coals, rushed towards the pyre, and the guards fled in panic, jumping over the fence. Yes, they had been told that the new owner remained in the house, yet they could not overcome the terror at the sight of the broad-shouldered figure emerging from the darkness, accompanied by terrible creatures with burning eyes. Perhaps they decided that Bhairava himself, with his fearsome hounds, was making his rounds of the shamshan.

…Prince Vishwendra Singh had extensive connections, as well as very real chances for a bright political future. Many of the VIP guests who had gathered to bid farewell to the Raja were influential people. And they all knew that the original resting place for the ashes of the Jat kings had been given to a stranger, an unknown yogi. The guests examined the Aghori Baba, as some called him, with open curiosity and undisguised suspicion. Who was this stranger who dared to become the owner of royal property and looked so self-assured? What did he intend to do here—perhaps perform tantric rituals, or worse? Rumors and innuendos left a certain aftertaste; some formed a bad impression—and this could lead to various undesirable complications, including reputational ones.

On the third day after the cremation, the prince returned to collect the ashes from the funeral pyre, as custom required.
