And for the first time, they and the prince had time for a substantial conversation, after which they became friends. The prince expressed a desire to distribute the yogic commentaries on the Gita, written by the new inhabitant of the shamshan, among the guests who had attended the last rites of the old Raja. There were reasons for this: the distribution of 700 copies of the Bhagavad Gita was meant to defuse the atmosphere; everyone was to learn that an educated author, a spiritual seeker, and not some shady random character, now lived on the royal shamshan. The prince's plan worked—idle rumors and gossip dissipated.

The ashes of the old Raja were taken to Haridwar and scattered over the Ganga, and his spirit joined the ancestors of twelve previous generations, already dwelling among the echoing colonnades of the memorial palaces.

Ten years after the death of the old Raja, a group of elderly Jats entered the gates of the shamshan. There were about fifty of them, and they were not random pilgrims. The royal shamshan was the resting place of their rulers, and they had been here before. The prosperous kingdom of Bharatpur was founded by Raja Suraj Mal in the eighteenth century, and his royal palace was located just twenty kilometers from Govardhan, in the neighboring city of Dig, where a large Jat community still lived.

The guard, Digambar Singh, who struck up a conversation with the visitors, learned that they were from the same village as himself—and let the pilgrims onto the grounds. The Jats wandered through the garden, admiring the lush greenery; they all remembered the shamshan as abandoned and desecrated, and now rejoiced at the changes that had taken place. In the distance, they noticed a man in a white kurta; that day, as usual, he was inspecting the improvement works, but his quite ordinary appearance made an unexpectedly deep impression on the Jats. Being devoted to the ruler of Bharatpur, they prostrated themselves on the stone terrace of the palace, whispering among themselves: "The King has returned, the King has returned!"

They were invited into the darshan hall, and for some time the Jats sat, gazing at him. Tea was served, some legends were discussed, and new stories were told. Never neglecting devotion and faith, he respected their expressions, especially if they were sincere.

Once, his disciples obtained old photographs of the royal family, taken at the turn of the century, and an old, black-and-white documentary film. His double was captured everywhere: a handsome, unsmiling young man whose features bore an incredible resemblance to his own. Rumors and theories about his origin were not encouraged, though neither were they completely dismissed as tall tales. He did not try to refute belonging to a royal lineage in past lives; people could believe what they wished. And yet he preferred to keep the knowledge of past lives to himself, even if it coincided with theories and guesses. Yoga provided access to fragmentary memories of previous incarnations, and he knew perfectly well who and what he was in this life—and that was enough.

All fairy tales have roots, and even fairies need a refuge. The shamshan was an ideal sanctuary for legends and forgotten tales, for the magic of yoga. As for legitimizing the theories of his royal lineage, he narrowed it down to a saying: "Tapeshwari so rajeshvari, rajeshvari so yogeshvari." The ancient proverb meant roughly the following: when a person performs great tapas, he becomes a king; and if, being a ruler, he performs just and great deeds, he becomes a yogi. This is an ideal description of the laws of spiritual evolution and predestined karmic justice.

He was certain that the last sip of water taken by the king of vultures was directly connected to the passing of the last Raja of Bharatpur. The disappearance of the vultures symbolized the end of dynasties, the decline of royal lines, and the disappearance of monarchical power in general. Not only on the Indian subcontinent, but throughout the world, new forces were coming into play, new trends in the development of societies were spreading rapidly. And this was happening not only on the earthly plane, but also in the astral spheres.

**The Snake Charmer's Mantra**

One evening, workers informed him of an unusual snake spotted near one of the palaces, and he went to look at the reptile. The first thing he saw was a tiger's tail lying between the columns of the arcade; then the tip of this "tail" stirred slightly. They decided to call the local snake charmer, Bheem Nath, who belonged to the Sapera clan.

The Sapera were originally from Rajasthan, a branch of the Kalbelia tribe; the word "Kalbelia" itself meant "catcher of black (snakes)." Villagers often call the Sapera "yogis" (the term usually means a holy man, not a practitioner of yoga) for their ability to tame snakes, which are considered divine incarnations of the Nagas—descendants of the sacred serpent Vasuki, depicted coiling around Shiva's neck. Although the Sapera revere the ascetic and saint Kanif Nath as their Guru, they themselves do not belong to the Nath sampradaya of yogis or to any other yogic clan. Despite this, in some states they are still called Naths. In any case, these people preserve a part of ancient knowledge; their folklore and traditions reflect fragments of the teachings of the great yogi Matsyendranath.

…It is said that once Kanif Nath came to a lavish feast hosted by his rival, the immortal yogi Gorakhnath. Gorakh received the guest cordially: "Any refreshment you desire will be served." To shock Gorakhnath and demonstrate an absence of disgust, Kanif Nath demanded that roasted snakes and scorpions be served. Offended by such provocative behavior, Gorakhnath cursed the descendants of Kanif Nath with disgust: "They will all be snake charmers." For centuries, the life of the Sapera has been connected with preparing potions and antidotes from the venom of these reptiles, with healing from bites and the like; "snake charming" is their life. Some Sapera clans remain nomads, others lead a settled life. Not far from Govardhan, there were eight or nine Sapera villages; their inhabitants earned a living by playing at weddings and removing venomous reptiles from peasants' homes.

…The snake disappeared before Bheem Nath arrived, but he, having heard of its unusual coloring, was eager to obtain the rare specimen. Usually, Bheem Nath searched for new snakes during the rainy season, traveling all over the country. Upon bringing a snake home, he would perform a special ritual, taking a vow to release the captive after one year. The contract is never broken, due to the antiquity of the laws themselves and the possible consequences of breaking the oath; on the appointed day, the snake is released at the Ganga, often in Rishikesh. The Sapera treat the snakes as guests; they are well fed, sprayed with water in summer to cool them, and in cold winters the snakes sleep in beds with their "employers," warming themselves with the heat of their bodies.

…The next morning, Bheem Nath arrived with his younger brother, and they immediately set to work, scattering salt in all directions and reciting a mantra. Then Bheem Nath began to play a special flute, the been pungi, holding it almost to the ground so that the sound would create a vibration to attract snakes. This thousand-year-old art was perfected through long practice and preserved as a tribal tradition. While playing the flute, the Sapera use continuous breathing, nakosans. After inhaling, the musician can play without interruption, sometimes for hours, without breaking the flow of breath. This pranayama is nothing other than a yogic technique that could have come to the snake-catchers only from the Nath tradition.

Bheem Nath had been playing for just under 10 minutes when a snake, appearing from nowhere, lunged at him with incredible speed. It was not the sought-after "tiger's tail," but a strange subspecies of cobra. As it turned out later, the snake had thin, bristle-like whiskers of a gray-green color, standing vertically like antennae. The Sapera believe that such whiskers are found only on very ancient snakes, and they grow when cobras live for more than 250 years. And after reaching the age of 500 years, they develop the ability to fly. Not with the help of wings, but through mental power arising from a high concentration of venom. It is said that if such a snake flies over someone's head, the person can be paralyzed. In addition to these charming abilities, after 1000 years such a snake becomes a werewolf and can take on any form: human, animal, or inanimate object. Bheem Nath had plenty of such tales, but watching him at work was even more interesting.

…The snake moved swiftly, but the Sapera reacted instantly, grabbing its long, furiously writhing body. Wriggling free, the cobra bit him on the thumb. Ignoring the wound, Bheem Nath shoved the snake into a mat bag and tied it tightly; within a minute, his skin turned yellow and he began to shake with convulsions. No one knew what to do; Devahuti and the workers frantically tried to find out which hospital to call, but Bheem Nath told everyone to calm down. "I will remove the venom myself; I have that knowledge, that vidya." And he asked Devahuti to cut his thumb so that blood would flow. She hesitantly followed the instructions, but there was no blood—the venom had already begun to take effect. They made another deep, half-centimeter cut; a few drops appeared. Bheem Nath pressed something resembling a piece of black resin to the wound, a so-called toadstone, which is sometimes found in the heads of large toads. The snake-catchers...

They always carry this natural antidote with them, and some
snake charmers use *nagmani*—a stone extracted from a snake’s head, considering it even more effective.

Meanwhile, Bhimnath sent for neem leaves.
Then, carefully enunciating the words of a mantra, he began to move
the brought leaves along the arm, as if drawing out the venom.
The used leaves were tied into a bundle and thrown away:
apparently, they had somehow absorbed the poison. Then Bhimnath
drank 50 or 100 grams of ghee to reduce
the heat from the venom. The gathered onlookers watched the act with amazement,
and for him, this incident became a moment of truth. In
a situation of mortal danger, all philosophical
discourses are useless, and if someone had tried to
convince the snake charmer that he was not a body but an embodiment of the
immortal spirit, Bhimnath would have been dead. Spiritual clichés
are impractical and useless in matters of life and death.
The secret knowledge of his ancestors—that is what saved Bhimnath’s life.
A Sanskrit sutra from the Vishnu Purana states: *Sa Vidya,
ya vimuktaye* — “Knowledge liberates.” Usually, he interpreted
this expression as “knowledge that liberates from rebirth.”
But on that day, he understood that the sutra spoke of
deliverance from immediate problems. The depth of practical
wisdom could free one from dangers and obstacles,
so that one might live long enough for the great liberation.
Obstacles must be removed so that a person can approach
the stage of liberation—for this, people must be able

to save themselves from accidental premature death. The ancient
sages were eminently practical.

He politely asked Bhimnath if he could
teach him the art of venom extraction. The snake catcher
replied that only his Guru, who lived
somewhere in Orissa or Bengal, could grant permission. A request was sent, and after
some time, a response came: “You may teach this yogi.
Living on the *smashan*, he has severed his connection with society, therefore
he is worthy of the knowledge.”

As a rule, *sapera* do not pass on their skills to outsiders;
the tribe protects both its secrets and customs, and its members. They
do not even hand over criminals from the tribe to the police, but conduct
their own courts, where justice is dispensed by a council of elders.

Bhimnath personally chose the day of initiation and performed the
ritual in one of the corner rooms of the palace, teaching him
two mantras in ancient Hindi. The mantras were to be recited
a certain number of times, and exactly at midnight, he was
to receive confirmation that he had been accepted into the tradition.
Everything happened just as it was said. Exactly at midnight, he
had a vision: two coal-black, silent women
of the tribe appeared in his room, one quite stout,
and her companion bony and thin. Without blinking, the women
looked at him, and he saw that their dark pupils were
vertical in shape.

The next day, Bhimnath came to inquire about

the results of the ritual and, upon hearing of the vision, was very
pleased. Two days later, he appeared again, dressed up, in a red
turban, carrying ritual implements and a basket
with a huge black cobra. Bhimnath insisted
that the snake must bite the *pujari* so that Guruji could demonstrate
his newly acquired ability to heal. “Otherwise, how will you know
that you truly possess the *vidya*?” The cobra looked seriously dangerous.

He addressed the *sapera*, trying to make his words convincing:
“I know for certain that I fully possess this knowledge.” Everyone present breathed a sigh of relief and
relaxed a little, especially the *pujari*, who had been preparing
for the inevitable. Disappointed and not at all convinced,
Bhimnath reluctantly left, but the plan came to fruition two
or three weeks later. One day, the *sapera* was cycling
past the gates of the *smashan*, and the guards called out to him,
having just noticed a *Raktavanchi*, a special kind of cobra. It is said that these
snakes are descended from Takshaka—the lord of cobras, who in Vedic
times killed the ruler of the Kuru clan, Parikshit.
They are considered the most venomous of the *nagas*. Of course,
the reptile in question could not produce documents
confirming its royal lineage. It fought
fiercely, but in the end, Bhimnath caught this descendant of the King of the *Nagas*. He then made the snake bite him on the
finger. Triumphantly, he ran towards the house, still holding the
angrily writhing cobra. The specimen was indeed

rare, with a red hood, a red tail, and a yellowish
belly.

“Guruji, now that I am bitten, you must remove the venom!
Begin!” Nervously, he began to recite the mantra
and applied all the techniques shown to him, and it seemed the venom
was successfully neutralized. The knowledge worked! Bhimnath drank
some ghee and left, carrying the bag with the captive
*Raktavanchi*.

…Several months later, workers reported that
Gandhiji the bull was showing all the symptoms of a venomous
snake bite. The bull was usually tied up in an old building
that later became a Shiva temple, and this place was a favorite
haunt of snakes. He would not get up and would not eat, and by these signs
one usually determines that an animal is unwell. Due to the bull’s
great weight, the snake venom acted slowly, but Gandhiji
was clearly unwell. Patting the lethargic giant
on the withers, he recited the mantra; almost immediately, the huge bull
struggled to its feet and very soon began to chew its feed.

When the forestry department allocated an additional plot of
land for the Siddha Siddhanta Academy, active work
began on clearing the area and establishing a plantation. The
snakes inhabiting the jungle resisted the invasion, and hundreds of
reptiles, disturbed by the heavy machinery and people, flooded the
*smashan*. Giant cobras and “horse-chasers,”
super-venomous snakes capable of moving faster than a horse, hid in the most unexpected places, including the

lower levels of the palace. One of the rooms was simply teeming with snakes,
which clearly wanted only one thing—to be left in
peace. The snake charmers were called again. As a
warning, a loud noise was raised at the doors, but despite the
precautions, one of the snake catchers was bitten on the leg the moment
he stepped inside the room.

Pale and already beginning to sweat profusely, the *sapera*
identified the snake that had attacked him as a *Padam Naga*, a small but
deadly venomous cobra, after whose bite the victim usually
dies within six minutes. Hurrying to the scene,
he applied a *toad stone* to the wound of the bitten man,
reciting a mantra, and the actions again had an effect. Fortunately,
Bhimnath had given him a piece of black fossilized resin, and
since then, he had worn it as a ring.

Overall, the science of venom neutralization proved practical
and effective, and the price for the knowledge was not so high.
Bhimnath agreed to teach him on the condition of paying *dakshina*—
the traditional fee for teaching. The miracle mantra cost
a portable television with an accompanying battery, as
Bhimnath’s village had no electricity.

Observing Bhimnath’s work, he saw again and again
how, after the recitation of the mantra, the cobra would freeze, and only
its black eyes would follow the charmer’s actions. Focused,
completely absorbed in his work, Bhimnath was confident and calm,
almost relaxed. This work was his *dharma*, his life and destiny, determined by the blessings

of his tradition and ancient wisdom.

Their acquaintance grew into a strong friendship, and the snake
catcher shared some secrets with him, one of which was
the invocation of the power of Kanif Nath—
the Guru of the *sapera* tribe. This was an unheard-of honor and the rarest of
rewards. Such secrets had never before been revealed to outsiders,
but Bhimnath considered that his friend was in no way inferior to any *sapera*.

Every time Bhimnath came to the *smashan*, he would begin
with a traditional greeting: “*Dekho, Guruji! Guru pat kisi ka kabja nahi, aur Guru kisi ek ka nahi*,” and only then
would he proceed to the business he had come for. In this phrase lay
the greatest wisdom: “Listen, Guruji! No one can possess the Guru,
and the Guru belongs to no one!”

Years later, while working on commentaries to a little-known
ancient Nath text, the *Gorakh Bodh*¹⁵⁴, he understood:
Bhimnath was repeating the words of the great Guru Matsyendranath.
Perhaps the *sapera* did not even suspect that he was speaking of the Spirit
who was the mentor of the mind. The Spirit-Guru belongs
to no one and is attached to none of the bodies in which he
temporarily dwells. Although it is quite possible that the snake charmer
knew the hidden meaning of this wisdom.

¹⁵⁴ *Gorakh Bodh* – a little-known ancient text of the Nath yogis, written in
the form of a dialogue between Gorakhnath and his guru Matsyendranath in the “twilight language.”

**The Royal Guardian**

The ritual pond was over five thousand years old, but its
stone steps were built only two hundred years ago. More than
a hundred gradually narrowing steps descended to the deep
bottom, which no one had ever seen. There were rumors
that at the bottom was installed a golden idol of the boar Varaha, the *vahana*¹⁵⁵
of Lord Vishnu, but no one had either the resolve or the resources
to verify the truth of this assumption.
Attempts to partially drain the *kund* and clean the steps of
centuries-old growths of algae and settled duckweed ceased
when the hired workers began to fall ill one after another,
and then refused to continue the work altogether. Hiring
someone to work at the famous *smashan*, known as
an abode of all kinds of unclean spirits, was practically impossible.
Stories of all sorts of horrors discouraged superstitious locals from
appearing there even for money.

The majestic palaces were reflected in the sleepy surface of the pond;
sometimes the dark water changed color, becoming milky
white. A few days later, the pond could become turquoise, and then
the water would again acquire its usual brownish
hue, remaining transparent. One could explain these

¹⁵⁵ *Vahana* – a mount (bird, fish, insect), literally meaning in Sanskrit “that which carries or pulls.” This term denotes a being used by a deity as a means of transport.

changes by temperature variations, underwater currents, or
the release of algal spores affecting the color of the water, but all
this was not so important. Just another sign of the presence
of something supernatural. Braj was considered the land of
Krishna, and milk symbolized His presence and divine
blessing.

Water, the embodiment of purification and eternity, represents
one of the five primordial elements, and therefore Indian
temples, memorials, and palaces were built near bodies of water. From a purely
practical point of view, it was good to have at least some
reservoir with water supplies here. At first, when they
had just moved to the *smashan*, the pond was a help
for household needs. That summer, the temperature rose
to 55 degrees Celsius, and he would drift for long periods in the *kund*,
lying on his back with his legs crossed in lotus, just to
cool down somewhat.

One day, someone mentioned an unusual sound coming from the
direction of the pond, resembling the hissing of a steam locomotive. Barely
perceptible but continuous, the sound lasted from sunset to
sunrise, and it seemed the inaudible resonance was beginning to affect
the surroundings. Something was changing; the water in the pond turned black,
a large tree growing nearby withered. Some of the workers were clearly
frightened; village people are generally very
sensitive to signs of nature, and those who worked at the *smashan*
were doubly attentive to such signals.
The sound did not disappear; it continued for another couple of months, causing

...stirring up unease and deepening the already grim reputation of that notorious place. Locals began to avoid the road that ran along the smashan's wall, especially in the evening hours. The sound grew louder, and some visitors asked about its origin. Birds were being found dead along the perimeter of the pond. After discovering eight dead owls, the gardener hung their carcasses on the trees to determine the cause of death. In Braj it was believed that, when a snake bites, the body remains intact for a long time due to the preserving action of the venom. After three or four months no signs of decay appeared on the small bird-corpses, nor any foul smell. The blackened, mummified remains of the owls were buried.

The closest association with the "melody" of this sound was the hiss of a gigantic serpent, which prompted the thought of summoning specialists — snake charmers. Sitting by the pond, the sapera listened for a long while. They knew their craft excellently and agreed that this could indeed be an enormous serpent. Then they departed without taking any action. The only explanation he could pull from them was that the capture of such a creature lay far beyond their abilities.

There was no way out other than to find the source of the sound. After sunset he began coming to the pond, to the lower level of the palaces, where the two towers connected by a small bridge stood. There, above the dark, motionless water, he lit incense and mentally addressed the unseen inhabitant of the pond: "Please, speak with me…" This went on for a couple of months.

On 27 January 1995, a small bronze statue of a nameless goddess was found before the gates — and this seemingly unrelated incident later turned out to be of great importance for the smashan and its inhabitants. That same evening, around eight o'clock, he received a mental signal: "Come alone. Tonight I will speak with you."

He hurried to the pond; a few moments passed, and the upper body of a gigantic serpent silently rose above the surface of the water. The creature's head was about a meter across, and judging by its size its length stretched several meters. Moreover, the jaw of this anaconda-like serpent was adorned with a fiery-red goat's beard, water dripping from its tip. Without moving and without attempting to analyze what was happening, he absorbed the creature's mental transmission: "I have lived here for more than four hundred years; my blessings granted power and prosperity to the dynasty of the Bharatpur rulers. You have come here at my invitation, for I am the guardian of this land. From now on I shall guard you as well."

The giant serpent listed the names of the people it had destroyed. Some tantriks and black magicians knew of this ancient Naga; trying to catch and destroy him, they perished themselves. The serpent went on "speaking" of the secrets of this land, of its spirits and its living. It all resembled a science-fiction film, yet his logical mind switched off and he simply absorbed the "narration," feeling a slight dizziness. Noticing this, the serpent "told" him to keep at a distance, since his breath was poisonous.

The meeting was over. He could not say precisely how long it had lasted — half an hour, forty minutes. Without a single splash the serpent vanished beneath the water, and he, barely dragging himself home, slept for fourteen hours straight. Yes, he loved comics and ghost stories, but he had never planned to live inside them. The encounter had happened in waking reality — only the nature of that reality was different, requiring a different understanding. The spirits of the smashan were far more real than the so-called realists, with all their logic and healthy skepticism. The smashan was a portal into another reality, yoga was the key to that entrance, and the Naga was one of the deities of that locality. It seemed the gods and spirits of the place did not object to a yogi's presence, and as for the skeptics — they could be advised to find their own smashan and see what would unfold there.

In any case, the hissing stopped. From time to time the pond's inhabitant allowed himself to be seen and smelled. A wide funnel would appear on the surface, accompanied by a peculiar sour-vinegar smell; this meant the guardian of the smashan had risen to the surface of the pond to breathe fresh air.

Musing over these mysteries was entertaining but led nowhere. He preferred the spontaneous unfolding of events to guesswork — it alone could yield the keys to the secrets of the past. Nothing remained but to wait for the mysteries to reveal themselves.

## The Dying Man, the Wounded Bull, and the Absolution of Monkey Sins

In the summer of 1997, an unconscious man was found at the gates. The blazing summer heat had emptied the parikrama road, and there were no witnesses who could identify the victim. There was no identification in his dusty bag, and the few objects found there gave not the slightest hint of who he was. Devahuti came to look in on the new patient; the dark-skinned, bony man covered in sticky sweat and dust, in an old dhoti, looked to be over fifty. They notified the police and received a laconic answer: "If the unknown man dies, just cremate him. After all, he came to your gates…"

At the hospital they said the patient could not be saved and refused to admit him. The limp body was returned to the same spot before the gates. Then they called the local pandits, hoping for the power of mantras, prayers, or any rites suitable for such a case. But not one of the venerable acharyas wanted to approach or touch the dying man. Devahuti's patience ran out, and she shouted to the gatekeeper to carry the stranger inside. A cot was set up next to the guard post, in the lower arcade of the palace; someone brought intravenous saline and medications. After five days the stranger regained consciousness. He stayed on the campus for another three weeks, until he began to stand and walk. He could not explain what had happened to him; he remembered only his name and that he had come from Shivpuri, but the circumstances under which he had fallen unconscious remained unclear. In the end they gave him a little money for travel, and he left with a bow.

What stayed with them was not the stranger's miraculous recovery but the reaction of the local police, who had suggested simply cremating the body without bothering with formalities; the doctors who refused to help; and the so-called holy men who usually sing bhajans in the name of divine love. As a rule, places of pilgrimage harden their inhabitants rather than adding to their compassion. In many "sacred places of power" empathy is considered an impractical weakness; pilgrims often become victims of robbers, impostor-pandits, greedy merchants, or dishonest healers.

He and Devahuti could not accept the cynicism of such an attitude. Yes, current circumstances were difficult, but their core values remained unchanged. Mercy was the natural response: those in need of help should receive it, and everyone deserved a chance. They were not about to change their convictions. Knowing the value of every life, the uniqueness and fragility of every breathing jiva, Devahuti continued to care for the suffering and the sick. And she always rejoiced when she witnessed someone's return to life, to a world full of sounds, smells, and tastes — to the refreshing monsoon, the cold of winter's foggy embrace, the heat of summer. Everyone had the right to remain in the mosaic of the turning existential kaleidoscope, to be part of the magic of being. Each colored shard of glass was a precious, necessary part of the great mosaic of Life.

He called her "Dr. Dolittle"; she smiled shyly and plunged back into her household chores. Healing, doctoring, and caretaking were part of her nature and her dharma; with them her path was paved.

Once, returning from Mathura, she found a large brown bull lying gravely wounded before the gates and ordered him brought onto the property. The unwarranted expense of feed for yet another being did not trouble her; prolonging life for life's own sake seemed entirely logical to her. The bull survived, was given the name Bhola Shankar Tewari, and attached himself to his new masters with his whole great heart. Whenever they entered the shed, the bull would weep from love and gratitude. Bhola Shankar Tewari would lay his massive head on his master's belly, and tears would stream in rivers, soaking through the kurta of his benefactor. The scene was unbearably sentimental, but these were tears of sincere love. Scratching the heavy head of the bull, he felt a fine pulsation beneath the warm skin; the whole enormous body of Bhola radiated the high frequencies of love.

The entry gates were rusty; the squeaking metal panels needed paint. But despite their unpresentable appearance, they separated from the world a smashan in which the laws of society lost their cumbersome meaning. The cremation ground was the final destination, yet in rare cases here one could receive a chance at healing, a last wish could be fulfilled, a new beginning could appear (in one form or another).

Usually hordes of monkeys invaded the territory in spring, but in April 2006 the scandalous primates behaved unusually quietly. One morning workers noticed a small procession: a group of monkeys was carrying a large skull, passing it from one to another. At some point the distracted mourners simply left the skull on the steps of the pond. It was picked up and examined. The rather old and unusually large skull was buried right where it had been found, and from that day on the monkeys began to bring the bodies of their dead to this very secluded corner by the pond. They came here to die; usually these were enfeebled old ones who sat motionless in one place for several days, until life left them.

In the hot noon, when all beings were hiding in the vanishing shade of the trees, an old lame male monkey walked around the house several times, as if performing parikrama, then sat down on the kitchen terrace and breathed his last. That same evening, on a walk with the dogs, Devahuti met a pair of monkeys: a mother had brought her dead infant. Before climbing the tree, the monkey placed the tiny body on the ground before Devahuti, as if asking her to take care of it. It seemed the animals had officially appointed them representatives of the funeral bureau…

And what was one to make of the case of the old male macaque who had climbed into the Hanuman temple and sat quietly in a corner? The pujari was told not to drive the visitor away, and the gaunt animal was present at three evening pujas. On the fourth day the monkey could not stand; the macaque lay before the gates, paying no attention to the people passing by or the curious dogs. The workers placed the almost weightless body on burlap and carried it to the plantation, where they laid it in a shallow irrigation well, so the water might cool the insect-bitten skin and ease the animal's suffering. The next day the sufferer was still alive. He and Devahuti went to visit the dying creature, and seeing them approaching, the monkey lifted himself up with effort, tore several stalks of dry grass with his lips, and held out a paw to them, as if asking for help.

The ever-screaming loudspeaker on the neighboring temple gave a wheeze and fell silent. In the ensuing silence he recalled an ancient, almost forgotten ritual described in the Puranas. Pleading for forgiveness, the suppliant was to hold several stalks of straw in his mouth as a sign that he was surrendering to the mercy of the one he had wronged. Dry grass in the mouth meant: "I am a silent

sacrificial cow; I have nothing left but dry grass, and I surrender to your mercy…"

They were witnessing a desperate plea for final forgiveness. Obeying an impulse, he addressed the weakening animal, which was clinging to the walls of the irrigation pit, trying to hold itself upright: "I do not know you, nor what you are guilty of in this life or the past. But I forgive you; your last wish has been fulfilled. Go in peace."

The monkey closed his eyes and, with visible relief, sank back on his hind legs. The witnesses to this scene were few: Devahuti, several disciples, a pair of peacocks, and a white cow indifferently chewing the same dry grass that hung from the corners of the monkey's mouth. A couple of hours later Devahuti returned to the plantation with water from the Ganga and a paste of rudraksha and offered it all to the dying animal. Pursing his lips into a tube, the monkey drew in the mixture given to him from a teaspoon. It was like a final communion, but no one nearby would have thought to make such a comparison. It was quiet, and only the distant melody of bhajans hung in the still, thick air. An hour later, lying with his head to the south, the monkey gave up his spirit; a light breeze stirred the stalks of dry grass beside the motionless body.

## Gandhiji

The horn through which milk was poured during puja as the Abhishek-offering to Shiva had been given to him by a friend. The huge white bull Gandhi, who had lived with them since 1991, had donated his own ornament and symbol of manhood for the ritual.

The tiny calf, along with his mother, had been gifted to them by one of the disciples. Born on October 2nd, the birthday of Mahatma Gandhi, the bull-calf was named after the great leader of the nation — and this was no mockery or disrespect. Fourteen years later, after the bull's death, his horn was cleaned, hollowed out, and filled with milk each night. After some time they began to use another vessel during puja, and the old horn lay in the upstairs room, gleaming with the tarnished silver of its setting. Sometimes he blew into the horn, simply out of sentimental impulse, and the long, drifting sound stirred nostalgia in him.

When they first arrived at the smashan, Gandhiji roamed everywhere completely free. There were as yet no doors on the ground floor of the house, and the bull would wander into the main hall, sprawl on the living-room floor, and watch as his master, lying on his back, pressed the barbell. Sometimes, entering from the northern terrace, the enormous animal would slowly trudge through the whole house and out through the southern veranda, like a melancholy white ghost — the spirit of the smashan. Bulls rarely allow anyone to sit on their back, but Gandhi was ready to do anything for his friend. Lowering himself, the bull would bend his knees, allowing his master to mount behind the huge white hump and pat his neck. Rising with heavy grace, he carried the rider effortlessly. If anyone dared to address his master in a raised voice or attempted to touch him in his presence, Gandhiji would fly into a rage and lunge at the "transgressor," often tearing his own chains. But most of the time he behaved peacefully. He loved to wrestle and play, rising on his hind legs like a circus horse and placing his front hooves on his master's shoulders. Of course, the hind legs bore most of the enormous weight, but the witnesses of these games were convinced of the Guru's ability to tame and even lift the gigantic animal. The largest of all the breeding bulls in the district, Gandhiji became the father of many mighty sons, and breeders sought them out to continue the bloodline of giants.

The bull recognized only Devahuti, his master himself, and one more cowherd who tended him; the giant would let no one else approach, stroke, or feed him. But mostly, resembling a white mountain, full of primordial peaceful strength, the bull watched the surrounding world from beneath half-closed eyelids quite serenely.

He had a sense of humor. For several years the cows remained on the verandas of the palaces, tied to walls and columns. When the monsoon season arrived, they had to undertake a hasty evacuation of the herd, and the cows were being driven to shelter. On that day they woke to the sound of thunder, and Devahuti hurried: "Get all the cows inside the palaces!" Slipping in the mud, she fell, and suddenly someone behind her laughed with a deep, rolling laugh: "Ha-ha, ha-ha!" Looking back, they saw Gandhiji, gazing at Devahuti with a peculiar expression. The bull was watching her try to extricate herself from the puddle, and it was definitely his laugh — unless some sarcastic ghost happened to be nearby.

In the summer of 1996 the ISKCON administration approached them with a request to use the territory of the smashan for the celebration of the centenary of Srila Prabhupada's birth. The organization needed to find a safe and sufficiently large place capable of accommodating more than a thousand guests from one hundred and nine countries. They were all gathering to honor the founder of the International Society for Krishna Consciousness, known as the "Hare Krishna Movement." The planned celebration was to be the first international summit ever held in Govardhan, and because of the significance of the event he agreed, on several conditions. After the festival, ISKCON was to organize a thorough cleanup. And further, no leaflets, posters, or banners — only an advertising board at the entrance reading: "The Siddha Siddhanta Yoga Academy welcomes all spiritual brothers and sisters."

Large tents with food and drink were set up; a garlanded boat in the pond depicted a scene from Krishna's Lila. Together with friends and disciples they watched the festivities from the upper terrace of the palace, reserved for VIPs. Hundreds of devotees dressed in pastel-pink robes sat on the steps of the pond and strolled through the garden; all were astounded by the grandeur of the architecture and the lush greenery of the garden.

A flock of Bulgarian girls from ISKCON timidly approached Gandhi; they had never seen such an enormous creature with a protruding hump, and for them he looked no less exotic than an elephant. They asked one of the Indian escorts: "What kind of animal is this?" Forgetting the English word, he answered shyly: "This is the husband of cow," which is technically correct. The girls wanted to pet the animal, and one of the guests warned them: "If your hearts are full of devotion to Krishna, the bull will not harm you. Otherwise, he is dangerous." Before anyone could stop them, the girls surrounded Gandhi, hugging his neck and stroking his huge head; their eyes filled with tears, and the bull began to lick their faces. This unprecedented sight stunned those present.

As always, the crowds of devotees were extremely heterogeneous. When some of the female ISKCON devotees learned that the attractive silent yogin — so different from the shaven-headed "prabhus" in pink robes — was not only the owner of a magnificent estate but also a bachelor, the most varied propositions began coming his way. They ranged from offers of marriage to requests for cohabitation without the obligations of a marriage ceremony. There were more than fifty interested candidates, but many were more enchanted with the real estate than with the potential bridegroom. Politely and patiently he explained that permission to marry must be given by his Guru, and that no immediate answer to their proposals would be forthcoming. Some of the "candidates" persisted, and he had to repeat that the decision about marriage was still under the consideration of the Master. Devahuti giggled, they exchanged jokes, but the persistent attempts at matchmaking began to irritate him. In the end the test of "marriage proposals" exhausted itself. Yoga was the only thing that truly moved him, since the innermost can be realized only in solitude.

Gandhi was standing in his usual place, tethered to a post, when another bull attacked him — one that had somehow broken free; their massive bodies turned into deadly torpedoes, and the animals locked horns. When the workers rushed to the cowshed, the attacker lay on the ground, six meters from Gandhi, all four legs broken, and had to be put down. In the duel Gandhi's horn had broken off, and Devahuti tended to him for months, changing dressings and applying salves. The bull licked her hands and followed her like a puppy when she led him by the chain. But the wound would not heal. Gandhi was diagnosed with a malignant tumor that had developed right beneath the broken horn, and on 25 May 2005 he died. He was buried right there, on the cattle yard where he had lived his whole life. Several years later a stone statue was erected on his grave, stylistically depicting Gandhi in his prime.

In the evenings the intoxicating aroma of night flowers mingles with the rich smell of dung, the sound of the horn reaches the cowsheds and for a while floats over the rooftops, lodging in the branches of the bougainvillea. When the puja ends, the ghost of the enormous white bull slowly trudges back to the cattle yard, to remain there until the next evening, until the next call of the horn.

## The Magic Baroncha

In the winter of 2001 unexpected visitors arrived. He was sitting in the cold darshan hall, talking with someone, when Devahuti entered: "We have guests, and they need to be fed." About three hundred stray cows, who had entered the smashan grounds through the broken fence, were greedily grazing, hurrying to fill themselves.

It turned out that some "philanthropist" had collected sizable funds for the "relocation of sacred animals" from Rajasthan to the land of Krishna, then brought the herd to Govardhan and abandoned them. The "patron" had simply left a thousand cows on the outskirts of the town. He was later arrested on charges of negligence and abdication of responsibility; the cows were taken to a state shelter in Vrindavan, but before any of that happened, more than three hundred emaciated and wounded animals had become guests of the smashan. Devahuti stitched up their wounds and treated their injuries, and the cows followed her gratefully wherever she went. In the end, all the animals were gathered and tied up by one of the palaces. The nights were so cold that two large tamarind trees had to be cut down for firewood. The fire helped to warm the bony and exhausted "guests." The food for each cow cost 300 rupees a day, and they had to borrow to ensure an uninterrupted supply of fodder. The "bills from the cow restaurant"

continued to arrive for several more years.

When at last the cowsheds were built, the herd had to be led out of the palace and onto the street, then through other gates into the new barns. The animals received the relocation with alarm, deciding they were being driven out into the street again. The herd had grown, with several stray animals rescued by Devahuti added to it, and these were the first to grow uneasy. The panic spread to the rest. The cows bellowed and wept, with drawn-out sobs shedding real tears. Despite coaxing and encouragement, the herd continued to wail and balk. He and Devahuti had to go out into the street with the cows, and their presence calmed the animals a little. Finally, the herd was led into the new sheds; the anxious lowing and weeping subsided, though the animals were under heavy stress. For the next several days he and his sister had to stay with the cows until they became accustomed to their new home.

Looking at the small herd in the cattle yard, he sometimes mused on the proverbial Indian love of cows. Sometimes this strange attachment was ridiculed, and the essence of the tradition could very rarely be explained. In ancient times, when a seeker came to a sage, he was sent into the jungle with several cows, and he could only return when the herd had grown to the number set by the Master. Several years would pass. Tending cows in the jungle, eating nothing but roots, wild fruits, and milk, the seeker developed subtle mental abilities and learned to view any idea from different sides, to see the essence of truth. Living with a herd, such an aspirant began to perceive animals, plants, insects, and people as part of a single system, to recognize the subtlety even of gross material objects, their hidden nature. The mental waves of the cows, their meditative and simple thoughts, their peacefulness and deep humility, influenced the mind of the man who served them.

When such an ascetic returned, the Guru gave him Upadesh — teaching — and this could be one short phrase. But these words enlightened the disciple, whose ripened mind had prepared to absorb the highest wisdom. A person capable of understanding the subtle abstractions underlying the manifestations of Nature and her creations could instantly grasp the meaning of a single phrase. The blessing of Mother Cow made a man into a sage.

Since he had begun to live in Braj, the land of cow-veneration, he gradually understood: contact with cows was one of the basic conditions for success on the spiritual path.

He first noticed the strange effect upon the arrival of the first cows, when he began to take care of them. Telepathic perception developed, intuition increased, and he could feel the essence of any idea on which he focused. The cows had a very specific quality: in their presence the human mind opened. Watching the cowherds who labored from early morning until sunset, and the melancholy cows, he saw that the animals and people were happy. A special atmosphere of stillness and peace filled the garden with quietude, nested in the house, invisibly wafting around the columns of the palaces. Almost tangible, meditative, earthly serenity and peace remained unchanged.

From time to time the cows brought a truly magical gift to their owners. In the local dialect these small oblong oval objects were called "baroncha," and only a few old men knew of their properties. Composed of entangled hairs formed in the throat of a cow, baronchas possessed mystical qualities; it was said that they granted their owner peace, wisdom, and prosperity. Those who had a baroncha never lacked for anything: neither on the material nor on the spiritual planes.

However, cows gave a baroncha only if they were happy and loved. On commercial dairy farms, where animals are simply a product, not a single baroncha had ever been found. It was a symbol of attachment and love, and the smashan's herd "gave" two baronchas a year. Their collection grew to 16 balls of slightly varying size and color. Usually the baroncha emerged when a mother-cow was licking her calf and the calf's mother was simultaneously licking her daughter. If one of them had a baroncha in her throat, it came out in these rare moments when the cows were caught up in mutual tenderness. There were also exceptions: two small light-brown balls were "gifted" by two young bull-calves, which is extremely unusual.

The baroncha was not mentioned in any of the books on animal husbandry. Some alchemical manuscripts described the bezoar, a calcified ball of hair extracted from the stomach of a cow or bull. They were prized in medieval alchemy; bezoars were thought to possess magical curative properties. They were used as a universal antidote, on the belief that a bezoar placed in a glass would neutralize any poison poured into it. In India it was known as gorochan, and it was used in Vashikarana, black magic, for controlling people, especially women. But the baroncha had different properties, and it formed not in the stomach but in the throat of the cow.

When he put a baroncha in his pocket or wore it on his neck as a pendant, his mind became so motionless that any desire to speak completely vanished. So he kept the gray-brown woolly balls upstairs in the bedroom, where they filled the space with a soothing silence.

With the baroncha was associated another belief — that it helps to determine the next incarnation, to choose birth in particular circumstances or surroundings. This is a very rare possibility, since the decision about the next birth is usually made in advance and is determined by a person's karma and inclinations. To have the right of choice in such matters is the luxury of only a few souls.

Devahuti often carried a small brownish baroncha with her, and perhaps this amulet played an important role in her next incarnation and her return to the place she loved so much. Perhaps the baroncha was a lucky ticket that allowed Devahuti to book her desired incarnation in advance. Who knows — perhaps the cows gave her this precious pass to earthly life for all the love she had shown them.

## The Keepers of the Archives

It was known that their family line descended from Ashvatthama; they were high-born Brahmins belonging to the Bhardvaj gotra. The genealogy of the family was tracked by a special clan of archivists who entered into their catalogues all information about living and deceased members of the Bhardvaj lineage. They had been doing this for five thousand years. Following age-old custom, the keepers of the archives visited the families whose data they preserved, to read the names of ancestors and register newly arrived members. This was both their sacred occupation and their livelihood; the ancient records were recognized even by the Supreme Court of India.

Possibly because their large family had relocated many times, the connection with the archivists had been lost. There had been no news from the man who kept their family's archive for more than ten years, and it was time to make inquiries. In June 1995, his manager and adviser Vakil Sahab (as everyone called the man who had been working for him for some time) was tasked with visiting the archivists' village to request the existing records. The trip was a success, and soon about a hundred kilograms of books were delivered to the smashan, accompanied by an archivist. The words on the yellowed pages were recorded in a special code known only to the keepers of the archives.

The reading of thousands of ancestral names began, taking several hours. There was something completely biblical about the style of the records and the narrative itself, reminding him of the Old Testament. "In the beginning there was nothing; then the formless God incarnated as Brahma, Vishnu, and Shiva, and from Brahma came the first rishis…" Their family's genealogy went back to the days of Creation; the names of every generation were known; the reader slowly approached the twentieth century.

As if in a trance, he listened to the names, remembering half-forgotten stories and nicknames, tales of former splendor, glory, and adversity of long-departed ancestors who silently entered the room until it grew crowded. The air in the room thickened with the karmic mist enveloping it.

The family roots of the lineage were traced to ancient Tibet; the forefathers belonged to the tight inner circle of Lord Rama. After His death, some of them followed Rama's son Lava to Lahore, while others joined his brother Kusha, heading into the fertile valleys of Kashmir. Five generations of the family were known, and the keeper of the archives found all the names of the ancestors on the pages of the old books. Their clan was indeed descended from Ashvatthama, who had received the blessing of Shiva and become a Brahmarishi, one of the immortal sages. Then the hero had married. By that time he had already reached a venerable age, and remained immortal, unlike his wives, who gradually departed from him. Ashvatthama married several times and became the progenitor of seventeen different lines, which for a rishi was no difficult matter.

The keeper of the archives also confirmed: "Shiva is the deity of your family; His worship has not ceased in your clan since time immemorial."

Less than a year remained before the arrival on the smashan of the Lord of the Universe and his personal Ishta Devata.

## Vultures in the Shade of the Tree

The Shiva temple (right) after the installation of the Shivalingam, 1999

The memorial palaces of the smashan after the flood of 1996

Smashan, 1995
With the bull Gandhiji

Devahuti gives Ganga water to a dying monkey. August 2010

Mathura, 1990

Applying an antidote

## Gods, Avatars and Forces of Nature

### Games of the Gods

Only one who has personally lived through miracles can sincerely believe in them. In order for the logical mind to accept the incredible, it must become part of lived experience. The subconscious — the intuitive core of the personality — must recognize unexplainable phenomena. Setting aside his initial skepticism, he became acquainted with yogic states usually considered hallucinations, visions, or mystical trance. Such terms are eagerly used by those who have never experienced and have no plans to experience anything of the kind. Any manifestation of the supernatural or the divine can be experienced only empirically; otherwise it is considered a play of imagination.

There were plenty of stories about gods appearing to people in their dreams, and they usually sounded extremely romantic. People are generally inclined to take the imagined for the real, but the predictable plots of spiritual experiences often betray the poverty of the imagination, however fueled by faith. His own mystical experiences, however, were too real to be dismissed as mere banal hallucinations. One of those experiences

(completely insane, terrifying, but absolutely real) took place on 16 June 1996, around noon.

There exists a certain method by which one may summon any deity; no special ritual is required, nor mantra — only the most intense concentration and the summons itself. Upon reaching a certain level, yogis no longer need mantras to call deities. The address to one of the most powerful gods of the Hindu pantheon had absolutely unexpected and far-reaching consequences. He later admitted that he had done it out of pure curiosity, on impulse, simply to see whether the method would work.

It worked. Vishnu appeared at once, as if He had been expecting the invitation. The deity's head almost touched the 5-meter ceiling of the central room where the whole spectacle unfolded. Astonishingly beautiful, magnificently built, the Preserver of the Universe looked at him for a split second, and then immediately seized him, holding him under his right arm, and he felt dizzy and terrified. As it turned out, this was only the beginning. Another participant in the phantasmagoria appeared in the room — Shiva. He was furious. Tossing the black iron trident from his left hand to his right, Shiva pointed the weapon at Vishnu, and then with a monstrous roar, like a rag doll, he snatched him out from under the Preserver's arm. The locks of Mahadeva's jata waved like enraged serpents; the black cobra coiled around His neck slid lower and, spreading its hood, lunged toward Vishnu. He dematerialized as suddenly as He had appeared. There was a brief pause. He was still in Mahadeva's hands.

Fighting dizziness, he tried to concentrate on details. Light-skinned, very tall and lean but not overly muscled, Shiva looked slender compared to broad-shouldered Vishnu. His bearing was regal, and His hips were wrapped in elephant skin. Later, in some old texts, he read that Shiva wears the hide of the demon-elephant Gajasura. Mahadeva's neck was adorned with a garland of skulls. Accidentally brushing his elbow against the smooth surface of one of the skulls, he instantly knew — as if someone had thrown a switch in his brain — that the skulls belonged to the avatars of different epochs, and somehow he discovered their names. At that moment, however, this had no significance.

Shiva was sweating. When Mahadeva tore him out of Vishnu's hands, he felt the momentary burning touch of the Auspicious One's sweat on his own skin. Lifting his head, he met the gaze of the Destroyer's half-open third eye, and was horrified. Usually meetings with the divine are described in romantic and elevated tones; the actual events can stun and frighten. Fear — a much stronger instinct than curiosity — almost paralyzed him.

The Destroyer left the house, still holding him in his hands, and headed toward the pavilion of Raja Jaswant Singh. Everything felt almost normal, if one can consider normal the rhythmic swaying in time with the steps of the Deity carrying him under His arm. A thorny bougainvillea branch scratched his wrist… He must have lost consciousness, for he came to in his bedroom. His forearm was still damp with the Devata's sweat; the tiny scratch on his wrist was bleeding. He was shaking slightly, gooseflesh crept across his skin, and a new, strange energy was slowly filling his body to the brim.

This incident can scarcely be called a divine revelation; it was, rather, traumatic. Emotional equilibrium was restored only after several months, and even then he was not sure that it was possible to recover from such an experience completely.

As for the third eye of Lord Shiva… He is called "Tryambakesh" — three-eyed — but only three "cases" are known when Mahadeva opened the third eye. And each time it brought either the birth of a demon or asura, or the destruction of the god of Love. It is commonly known that the Destroyer lifts the heavy lid of His dark, destructive optical sight when the end of the world is near. Why then was the Deity's eye open? Did it mean that the apocalypse was approaching? However, all these events somehow gave a positive impulse to the present; besides the inner changes, the appearance of Shiva set in motion external processes. Before this incident the land around the smashan was barren and desolate; only cacti and thorns could grow on the calcified soil. The garden was a completely utopian dream; there was not a drop of groundwater. But 10 days after the appearance of Shiva, Govardhan was flooded, and remained underwater for three months. The flood affected the quality of the soil: it became fertile, fresh greenery grew quickly, attracting animals and birds. Even the faces of the locals somehow softened.

Where Mahadeva had stepped, rudraksha trees grew, which was a true miracle. These climatically sensitive trees never survive on the plains of northern India. Some wise people with whom he discussed the events pointed to an undeniable fact: the only confirmation that Shiva had appeared to a mortal was the finding by that person of a natural Shivalingam, a sign of Shiva. Otherwise all the words would have been just the fantasy of the narrator.

Two years after the appearance of the gods, the Shivalingam was found. Perhaps the Destroyer had come to inspect the property in advance. The smashan was His home, and any home needed a thorough inspection before the owner moved in.

## The Coming of Yamuna

Water for domestic needs had to be bought: six cisterns of fresh water were emptied weekly; costs were steadily rising, eating into a non-existent budget. Besides reflections on the miraculous appearance of the gods, his thoughts were focused on purely practical tasks: clearing the land, building, and agricultural work. Of a million pressing problems, the water question was the most acute. So much so that he even turned to a medium: "What is to be done about water?" The medium advised making an offering to the river Yamuna and asking her for help. This seemed quite reasonable, and on 25 June 1996, together with Devahuti and his disciple from Delhi, Umashankar Sharma, he went to Vrindavan. The heat was unbearable, and even being close to the river brought no relief, as if the river herself were sweating, suffering from the scorching breath of summer. They poured milk into the waters of the river, and to their surprise the white streaks vanished instantly, as if swallowed. He asked Yamuna to come, to revive the smashan with water, to honor them with her presence — and only later did he realize that the invitation had been accepted literally.

That same day a local resident came to him to discuss a recent purchase of a water well located nearby. The owner of the plot adjoining the smashan, Mohan Khandelwal, offered to install a pipeline from the well to the smashan on his own land. They went to the wall enclosing the territory to assess the possibility of such a solution. Approaching the wall, they both heard loud laughter, as if a giant were laughing behind their backs. They froze, and Mohan whispered: "Guruji, who is that?" He had no idea. Later it became clear: the gods of the place themselves were laughing at the plans to bring water to a spot that was destined to be flooded.

Climbing the wall, they looked around and judged the task entirely feasible, but a few seconds later a strong gust of wind almost threw them to the ground. Rushing to the house, they fell several times, dodging flying branch-fragments and whirlwinds of sand. The wind died down, but the rain did not start.

The next morning he woke with absolute certainty: a flood was coming, and the cows had to be evacuated from the lowland at least to the second level of the palaces. The stunned workers were ordered to lead the herd up immediately — they tried to protest, saying there was not the slightest reason for concern. While they were arguing, a heavy downpour began. Everyone rushed to untie the cows and lead them up the ramps to the palaces, and this redeployment continued all day. By evening only two bulls, Gandhi and Madhav, remained below. It was almost dark when, through the northern gates, water poured in from the overflowing irrigation canals located a kilometer from the smashan; angrily seething and swirling into whirlpools, the current rushed toward the pond. To their good fortune, the completely drained pond became an excellent reservoir for the furious flow.

The water rose quickly. Looking around uncertainly, the bull Gandhi stood before the ramp leading to the upper level of the palace, and he and the pujari tried to lead him to safety. Already chest-deep in water, gripping the chains, they pulled the bull toward the house, and with great difficulty made him climb up onto the veranda, where Gandhi calmed down. Towering over the terrace like a huge wet mountain, the bull stood motionless, the bulk of his white body gleaming in the darkness. They rushed back into the swiftly rising waters to look for the other bull, who was slightly shorter than Gandhi and clearly needed help, but he was nowhere to be found. He remembered that the kitchen supplies had been left outside and asked the pujari to try to save something — Mahatmaji almost swam in the direction of the kitchen. The water was already up to their throats.

Standing in the middle of the seething current, he felt a sudden painful blow to the abdomen. It was followed by a powerful clap of thunder, and with a flash of lightning he saw the tail of an enormous yellowish python swimming away. The pain was unbearable, and he shouted to the pujari to drop everything and return to the house. They had done all they could; staying outside was unsafe.

Upstairs in his bedroom, he saw that blood, seeping from the umbilical depression, had soaked his clothes and was dripping on the floor. Feeling a strong urge, he urinated, but only blood came out. The internal damage must have been serious, since he continued to urinate every 2–3 minutes, and the blood did not stop. Pressing a towel to his stomach, he noticed that his nails had grown white from blood loss. He weakened with every minute, his head spinning violently.

Mentally turning to Babaji, he prayed: "Give me a sign if my time has come to depart; I will sit in padmasana and raise prana and apana — allow me to leave the body in the proper, yogic way. But if I am destined to live — please help…" Babaji's voice answered at once: "You will urinate a couple more times, and the bleeding will stop."

At that very moment, a huge figure appeared in the room, not entirely human, emitting a golden radiance. Seeing a long, lion-like tail, he understood that before him stood Hanuman, and the thought flashed that from blood loss hallucinations had begun. But then the figure moved. Lifting himself with effort on the bed, he tried to focus. The deity's face was indistinct; Hanuman was standing with his back to him, but he clearly saw the gleaming, intricate ornament of His belt, adorning a red dhoti.

It is hard to say how exactly Hanuman moved from the room to the rooftop, but he continued to observe the Deity through a kind of tunnel vision. In his head flashed swiftly moving frames: here was Hanuman, holding onto the flag-

staff, standing on the roof and holding in his other hand something like a stick. He brandished this object, and hundreds of black shadows, resembling firewood, began to gather around Him. The Lord of Spirits made another motion, and another cloud of black shadows appeared. From somewhere a large rope loop appeared, and the objects (or beings) began to lay themselves into a bundle, like brushwood. Then the enormous sheaf vanished. He could not explain how he had witnessed the "gathering" of dark spirits, but he was sure of what he had seen.

Hanuman appeared in the room again, and His voice was reassuring. "You called great powers — and they answered the invitation. You asked Yamuna Herself to come here, and such an outcome was to be expected. But now everything will begin to settle." With these words Hanuman vanished. The bleeding stopped, the pain almost passed, and he felt relief.

When Devahuti, with the other guests who happened to be in the house at the time, came upstairs and saw the bloody trail on the floor, she rushed to his room. Of course he had to tell of the python incident, and those present began with one voice to lament: "Why didn't you call us? We must take you to the hospital at once…" That was exactly what he was trying to avoid. Even in an extreme situation, he could not leave the smashan. His absence would have interrupted certain subtle processes and disappointed the beings recently arrived or already dwelling here. The forces that had answered his call had every right to expect to be treated as long-awaited guests. The drama set off by the calling of Yamuna was unfolding here and now; pain, destruction, and healing were part of this play.

In the morning he felt almost well, except for a swollen belly horribly bruised. The young bull Madhav was found safe and sound: it seemed he had climbed the ramp to the other cows on his own.

Several months later, on October 2nd, they were celebrating the birthday of the bull Gandhi, and Devahuti carried sweets up to the upper terraces of the palace, where the herd now resided. But she came running back at once, calling him: among the columns of the palace lay a huge python. Seeing people approaching, the snake tried to climb to the upper level of the palace, then, abandoning attempts to flee, coiled into rings, hiding its head as if in shame. Most likely it was the same python that had struck him in the abdomen. Local snake-charmers were called. The giant serpent fought furiously, tearing the bag into which they were trying to put him. The next day, subdued and sleepy, the 4.5-meter prisoner was brought for a proper introduction. He weighed 40 kg. Someone laid the heavy yellow body of the python across his shoulders and took a photograph, which then hung for a long time in the cowshed. With time the image faded, and only the frame reminded one of the events of that stormy night.

The water receded slowly, and the earth needed almost three months to absorb all the moisture. But overall the consequences of the flood were positive; in the following months the quality of the soil improved sharply, the pond filled with fresh water, and there was no need to pipe water from the well.

What had happened quickly became part of the past. There was no need to doubt reality — it tested him daily. The appearance of the gods was as normal (and expected) as any other natural phenomenon. After all, the gods too were part of the highest Deity, Nature, whose breath sustained everything around.

## Puja to Hanuman

In 1992, a visitor came to him at the ashram in Vrindavan. The son of a well-known royal astrologer from Gwalior asked for initiation. The man was most amusing: with monkey-like grimaces, lively facial expressions and mind, and an equally agile character. His request was granted, and the astrologer's son began to take his first steps on the path of yoga. About three years later, this man's life changed radically, and the reason for this was not only Kriya.

In October 1995 he arrived unexpectedly, visibly excited, and declared that Hanuman Himself wished to speak with the Guru. The story was rather unusual. Out of the kindness of his heart, the astrologer's son had been caring for a dying old man who had been abandoned by his relatives. He had been a devotee of Hanuman and a powerful medium, whose body was used by a powerful spirit. The astral being "entered" the medium during the so-called durbars, special spiritistic gatherings at which cases of possession by spirits are reviewed and resolved, along with other complications caused by ghosts (and sometimes by people), and the medium usually pronounces the verdict. Such trials of ghosts were conducted in remote places of northern India, and these events brought the medium good money and fame. And also illness, since interference with the karmas of ghosts and people always has consequences.

Two weeks before his death, out of gratitude (or at the insistence of higher powers), the old medium transferred his abilities to the astrologer's son, who became the new channel for the spirit through which Hanuman would proclaim His will. Providing a "direct connection" with the Deity became his duty. The spirit itself was obliged to work for the good of humanity, relieving people from pain and grief, restoring shattered hopes, and giving another chance. Usually astral inhabitants and deities do not get involved in human affairs, although occasionally they intervene to protect the interests of other spirits.

And now the astrologer's son sat in the darshan hall. Nervously but resolutely, he announced that he had brought "a message from Hanuman ji." He pondered. Asking a medium to prove his abilities was unwise, since to summon such forces without a valid reason is simply dangerous. He nodded to the guest, inviting him to begin, and the man spoke in short, enigmatic phrases interrupted by pauses of heavy breathing. Waves of unusual vibrations passed through the medium's body. Closing his eyes, he rhythmically and mechanically raised and lowered his hands, clapping them over his head. In a half-trance, from time to time he bent his arms at the elbows as if holding reins, and sitting cross-legged, slightly swaying, he imitated the movements of a rider.

When the spirit inside him spoke, the medium's breathing stopped for 2–4 minutes. Because of this, the voice, deprived of the circulation of air, sounded strangely muffled — it was the spirit within him that spoke.

Fixing his gaze on the medium, he addressed the being within: "I want to speak with you without an intermediary. Directly." The wish was granted, but not on that day.

At first he refused to believe that the Deity itself was speaking through the medium and the spirit within. But within a few months several events occurred that confirmed details mentioned in the conversation with the medium. This forced him to acknowledge that he had indeed spoken with Hanuman, one of the eight immortals remaining on Earth until the end of the current Kali-yuga, and that His will was announced by the man sweating profusely before him.

Almost a year had passed since the first contact with Hanuman, and during this time he had several remarkable conversations with the Deity. These "sessions" expanded his horizons, for Hanuman spoke of the secrets of the afterlife, the journey of souls, and the principles of reincarnation. The patterns of karma opened up to him anew, in the light of universal laws rather than human beliefs.

In December 1996 a learned shastri suggested to one of his disciples that they conduct a special puja dedicated to Hanuman. The purpose of the ritual was to attain long-term prosperity and well-being. The ceremony required a secluded place, preferably near a large body of water, since water played an important role. The disciple thought of the ideal secluded place, the royal smashan, and having asked for permission to conduct the ceremony, received consent. On a sunny, cool day a group of pandits arrived in Govardhan, and all of them, together with the aforementioned disciple and several of his friends, settled on the steps of the pond. He joined them. After initiating them into the complex parts of the ritual, the priest began the puja. In the bright sunlight the flames of the oil lamps were pale and almost invisible. It was still and windless; the smashan watched and listened; ghosts peeped through the dark arches of the palaces; monkeys and birds patiently awaited the remains of the sacrificial offerings. Absorbed in the rhythm of the mantras, he did not suspect that the ritual would become an annual event for him for the next 12 years. For some reason, consciously or not, the old pandit passed the sankalpa for the puja to him, rather than to the disciple who had initiated the ritual.

The general meaning of sankalpa is a vow, an obligation to maintain the striving for the highest truth, resolve, intention, and promise. Such an intention is not formed by the intellect; it comes from within, from the depths of that essence which determines who a person is. The sankalpa then informs the logical mind of the specific decision that must be carried out and completed.

The ritual became his duty. Several months later, a pandit who came on a visit learned of the peculiarities of the ritual and exclaimed: "Oh, now you will have to conduct these ceremonies for the next 12 years!" Nobody had warned him about this, but after a brief hesitation he decided to continue. The sankalpa had been given on the smashan, in the presence of powerful forces — the promise was too serious to be broken.

For the next twelve years the puja to Hanuman was performed each December. Two months after the first puja, the old shastri died; another priest had to be found, and Pandit Janardan ji from Gwalior took on the religious part of the rite.

One part of the ceremony greatly puzzled some Western guests: mirrors, lipsticks, combs, powder-cases, and other cosmetic items were lowered into the water. But there was nothing unusual about this; the body of water was worshipped as yet another form of divine feminine energy, Shakti. She was given everything that might please the Goddess, for She was a woman, even if Divine. In this puja the pond of the smashan was worshipped as Lake Pampa, one of the five sacred lakes of India.

It is said that the Goddess Pampa, one of the many forms of the goddess Parvati, meditated on the lake created by Her father Brahma. Impressed by Her devotion, Shiva offered Her any boon, and She chose to become His wife. The lake still exists, located in Karnataka, among the magnificent ruins of ancient Hampi (whose very name derives from "Pampa"). The Ramayana states that this lake is also dear to Hanuman, possibly because nearby is Anjaneya Hill, the place of His birth. Therefore on the day of the puja to Hanuman, the pond of the smashan in Govardhan was called "Lake Pampa." The landscape of distant Hampi "overlaid" itself upon the Govardhan smashan, reproducing the Vedic episode.

The archaeological archives named the pond of the smashan "Vrishbhan kund," and on Google maps it was marked as "Brahma kund," but none of this mattered. The choice of the place for the ritual needed no "legalization"; by every measure the water at the smashan was a powerful and effective alternative to the sacred Lake Pampa, and the puja continued.

The final, carefully orchestrated puja, which took place in December 2008, became a solemn event. More than 600 people were invited, including thirteen Brahmins, and the fire sacrifice lasted several hours. The 1,000 names of Hanuman were read aloud; those present threw handfuls of grain into the fire,

exclaiming "svaha" at the end of each name pronounced by the pandit, and the vaults of the lower arches of the palace caught up the prayers, reflected off the surface of the water. The celebration turned out solemn, colorful, and serene.

At the conclusion of the ceremony people circumambulated the pond, paying their respects to the power of Shakti. Sitting on the steps, he watched them, savoring a long-awaited and always short-lived solitude. Colorful silhouettes were reflected in the gleaming mirror of the pond and disappeared; people took their leave and dissolved into the amalgam of the next day. They were all his spiritual children; he supported each of them. They had to return to the ocean of worldly life, only to come back once more into the world of spirits and legends, where everything, including ordinary logic, was inverted and called into review. Battered but not defeated by Maya, the disciples grew wiser, and each had to find inner peace in the daily feat of yoga.

Looking at the majestic palaces, at the gleaming surface of the pond, he thought about how everything was temporary: inanimate objects, living beings, the combinations of basic elements that composed their bodies. The elements themselves, however, were unchanging. This place, this smashan, had been built to withstand time. Not, of course, indefinitely, since there was a limit to all resistance. The duration of a human life was shorter than that of landscapes and buildings, yet humans had a truly ambitious — though hidden from themselves — goal: self-realization. The cycle of rebirths was created for the achievement of this single goal, and souls had simply no other mission. And the smashan supported this goal, if people tried to attain it. More often, they were unaware of the meaning of life and marched into oblivion without ever learning the reasons for their own incarnation. In his case, incredible events stimulated awareness and the carrying out of the mission, helping him patiently to await its fruits. The rest depended on Time and Destiny.

The puja was over. The next night one of the female disciples dreamed of a gigantic gray langur, watching her intently and seriously. In the morning she saw the same monkey, now back to its usual size: a large male with a black face and white beard sat by the pond, lazily picking up the grains and crumbs of sweets remaining from the puja.
