She approached, carrying an orange; the monkey waited. The fruit was placed before the langur, who unhurriedly ate it, and the ritual was completed in yet another way. Many participants in the rite reported signs of inner and outer transformation: memories surfaced; the parting with painful and traumatic events of the past arrived. He too noticed changes in himself; not only the nightly samadhis but also the daily pujas and yearly rituals worked on the mind. Now the mind knew where the mysteries were born, where the rites came from.

After some time Hanuman began to speak with him directly, without a medium. On such days he would receive a mental signal and leave the darshan hall early. Upstairs in the bedroom, in the corner, always in the same place, an indistinctly outlined figure of an old man would appear. The silhouette looked like a hologram and would not vanish, even when he tried inconspicuously to narrow his eyes. Their communication took place on the mental plane and required complete concentration and clarity of inquiry, though he never asked any questions. Honestly, he tried not to speak at all and made no attempt to summon the Deity. From time to time Hanuman appeared in His traditional image, but always standing with His back to him. After such meetings, he could remain silent for days, shifting the focus of attention from one plane of reality to another, without attempting to evaluate or measure them.

Quite strange information came in 2005 from another medium. This man was originally from Kanpur and also maintained a connection with Hanuman; Hanuman instructed him to visit the smashan on a delicate mission. After discussing the principal matter, the medium added: "Hanuman ji said that he has known you for the last 33 years." Such a surprise… 33 years ago he had been a student at college… Truly, the paths of karma are unfathomable.

Did Hanuman know about Babaji? He had no doubt about it. Even the gods could not stand between Guru and disciple, and whatever missions Hanuman entrusted to him, all of them were, no doubt, sanctioned by the Guru parampara.

This communion deepened his perception of the scope of events. What he was learning confirmed the inviolable law of balance between the world of men and the realm of spirits. Hanuman and his helpers intervened when angry, offended, or harmful spirits acted upon the lives of people. Otherwise, the gods kept their distance and were not at all concerned with the welfare of humanity. Divine beings had entirely different goals, but they maintained the balance between the worlds. Only occasionally did the gods assist certain mortals, if their karmas were somehow connected with the gods themselves. Yes, even the Gods had karma.

Over time he became convinced that the world was governed by spirits: some took on human births, others had never had a human nature. As the ruler of the world of spirits, Hanuman helped both those who lived in astral bodies and those who dwelt in human ones.

The puja at the pond became part of the daily evening ritual. The short ceremony in honor of Shakti was filled with power, peace, and the promise of blessing. Standing over the dark water, the pujari rang a bronze bell; on the stone platform above the water he scattered marigold flowers, sprinkled grains of rice, and lit incense. One by one, people briefly passed their palms over the flickering flame and touched their foreheads, sealing the blessing, and then walked away into the darkness. The fragrant plume of incense smoke rose to the starry sky, and the spirits hidden in the thick darkness began to feel the rising mental waves of the man they knew as "Yogiraj." By midnight the gathering storm of his expanding consciousness was charging the darkness with thunderous energy.

## Prayaschitta. Abhishek. Milk

Before the puja to Hanuman, the pandit made everyone undergo the ritual of purification — bathing in the pond. Standing knee-deep in the dark water, he remembered the words of the Guru, who had once said that prayaschitta was an important part of initiation into the yogic lineage, and that if he could ever conduct it for his own disciples, he must by all means do so. Recognizing the powerful effect of the ritual, he decided to perform an annual prayaschitta for the new initiates, and it filled people with calm and peace. Set in motion by the ancient mystery, human destinies began to change. Many of the initiated felt an intoxicating happiness and lightness; some withdrew into themselves; some had a mild fever; but almost all dreamed vivid dreams. Timed to the beginning of yoga practice, prayaschitta brought about global life changes that occurred over the following year or two. More often than not the changes were painful and radical, but always led to spiritual transformation. The ritual was usually conducted in February or early March, after the celebration of Shivaratri. The last prayaschitta took place in February 2009.

When the initiates lined up on the steps of the pond, the water turned out to be surprisingly warm. Some of the new disciples were silent and serious, others seemed animated and excited. Trying to get the most out of the exotic action, students took photos and cracked jokes. People came to the lower tiers of the palace, where, right by the pond, men by tradition had their heads shaved. Hair was considered "a carrier of karmas," too many memories accumulated in it. Each participant hoped to start anew, and the clean page of the future was to be filled with sadhana, will, and discipline.

Serious and focused, in a saffron-colored kurta, he walked through the variegated crowd accompanied by Devahuti. The cheerful noise, chatter, and laughter died down. Behind him appeared the pandit, methodically approaching each new initiate with ritual offerings: honey, milk, yogurt, flowers, grain, cow dung. Smearing these substances on their faces, first hesitantly and then with growing enthusiasm, people dipped themselves into the water, leaving rose petals and oily yogurt stains on the surface. Emerging with childlike smiles and shining eyes, they laughed, awaiting the next signal from the pandit. The sun was bright, the air filled with the anticipation of change and a newfound faith in destiny.

Invisible karmic fossils and slag cracked and dissolved in the dark water, and the long-forgotten but eternal essence of self triumphantly emerged from within. From this day on, the mind began to work differently, awakening spiritual sensitivity and intuition. In the process of difficult transformation, intellect developed rapidly, hurrying after the rebooted subconscious. The ritual washed away the burden of the past along with doubts about the future.

Of course, the effects of past events and deeds did not vanish completely; rather, they were paused, put away into a distant drawer, allowed to dry out and mummify, so that mind and heart might no longer be weighed down by the heaviness of regrets.

Resembling clumsy seals, the disciples crawled out onto the steps; now they listened to the long list of sins that the pandit read out. The list was endless, and each was certain that he had erred at least once. On that day the water of the kund was clear, but from time to time it turned milky-white, as if chalk had been dissolved in it. He joked that the pond was completely filled with human karmas and could no longer accept anyone's sins.

Two or three months after their arrival at the smashan, the water in the pond turned absolutely white, but at the time they had attached no particular significance to what had happened. When it happened again, he suspected that the milk used for offering in the temple across the street was seeping into the pond of the smashan through a system of underground springs. In Braj this phenomenon is known: once every 20–30 years streams of milk would burst from underwater springs of the famous lake Manasi Ganga, and sometimes white jets were seen in another well-known place — Radha's pond. Although these bodies of water never became completely "milky," only separate white streaks were visible, beating up from somewhere on the bottom.

According to local legends, Lake Manasi Ganga came into being when Shri Krishna's foster parents Nand and Yashoda stopped here for the night on their way to the Ganga, and in the morning Krishna summoned the Ganga by the power of his own consciousness. Thus the body of water and the temple received the name "Ganga of the Mind." Although the lake was located across the road, it was hard to believe that temple offerings could somehow fill the pond of the smashan with milk. After some time, the dark, transparent waters of the pond slowly became milky-white. Out of pure curiosity he sent a sample of the water to a laboratory, and the test showed a high level of lactose. Further investigation was pointless.

In November 2016 the water in the pond remained milky-white for fifty days, then suddenly, within two hours, changed color again to the previous one. Braj was generous with miracles, but this phenomenon had no scientific explanation and was not connected with a change in weather, temperature shifts, or the level of oxygen released by algae. It could not be tied to any particular

events or religious holidays; the logical conclusion was simply absent. However, they had been fortunate to witness this phenomenon, and they decided to keep it secret, cherishing their hard-won seclusion. Otherwise thousands of pilgrims would have begun visiting yet another miracle-place.

Milk was love and life, a manifestation of the Universal Mother and Her acceptance of all beings. The offering of milk was as ancient as the earth itself; performed in the name of righteous purposes and with benevolent intentions, the Abhishek could favorably influence the destiny of the one making the offering. That is why countless pilgrims walked around the Hill with pots of milk, pouring it on the earth in thin streams and droplets that marked their path, and stray dogs unceremoniously licked it up. No one chased them away, since this was a sign that the offering had been accepted. Agnostics were amazed at such wastefulness and wondered whether the milk could be donated to the poor instead. It is pointless to try to explain to people the logic of the Great Equilibrium of things, but sometimes he did try: everything returned to the Source by various paths, and everything was destined to reach Him.

Milk came from the earth, when rains and the heat of the sun fertilized the soil. Filtered through the stomachs of cows, grass and the force of Nature were transformed into milk, and then it was offered to the fire as ghee, and it filled oil lamps, boiled on kitchen stoves. Turned into yogurt, it became a ritual offering, was fed to great and small. Then, having completed its service, the substance of life and love returned again to the soil, becoming rain. Such was the great cycle; milk was yet another sacred element belonging to Earth herself.

In 2017 they performed the milk offering for almost a year. Every night 101 liters were poured over the Shivalingam, and the soil of the smashan received about 16 tons. When the soil ceased to absorb it, the ritual ended. There was no particular reason for the offering; he simply felt that the time was right; moreover, thanks to the grace of the sacred Hill, the new dairy farm was working excellently.

Over the years he heard many discussions about the nature of the soul, and all these conversations usually ended with the general statement of its omnipresence and immortality. But people possessed of immortal substance still continued to die. What then is immortality?

Butter is always present in milk, as invisible potential, a variant of transformation. It just needs to be processed and extracted, and then ghee does not spoil for hundreds of years. Despite the presence of an immortal substance in milk, raw milk spoils and turns sour.

Like ghee, the human soul symbolized hidden potential. But without complex yogic austerities, no one could understand or experience the immortality of the soul. The statement of its omnipresence was too general. How could one see this omnipresence, realize it, make it manifest? Everyone has a body and a soul; every cow gives milk that potentially contains ghee.

Milk must be heated, then cooled, turned into yogurt, and at the next stage churned. The freshly extracted butter was heated again, and what was obtained as a result was the immortal essence of milk. The process of extracting ghee was akin to yogic discipline.

In the development of consciousness, one could succeed only by the strictest performance of yogic practice; only then could consciousness be considered a separate entity. This was the sorcery of yoga — it "churned" the mind so that the seeker could realize its nature. Freed from identification with the worldly "I," consciousness slowly came to recognize itself as its own immortal substance. But first it had to be discovered. The aim of Kriya was the unveiling of the essence of the Self, and the Guru oversaw the extraction of the ghee of the soul from the milk of the mind, obtained from the cow of the body.

## The Second Meeting with Babaji

21 December 1995 fell on a full moon; moreover, it was the birthday of Dattatreya. Otherwise it was just a cold December night, indistinguishable from others. In the pre-dawn hours of 22 December that followed, around three in the morning, he woke up because someone was licking his face.

Almost all the doors in the house were broken or missing entirely, and at night one of the two huge Dobermans was left downstairs for guard duty. But now the dog was rapturously greeting him with disordered wet kisses on the second floor, in his bedroom. The door leading upstairs was usually locked from the inside, and it was the only door that locked. He sat up on the bed, wondering how the Doberman had managed to enter the room. The first thought that came to mind was a break-in. Invasions were a common matter; confrontations with the locals continued, and the dogs were more reliable than hired guards. But tonight… Gripping the large Nepali knife, he went downstairs. The door of the central hall was open, and someone said loudly: "Cretin, come inside." In the local dialect the words sounded far more offensive. Enraged, ready for a fight, he stepped through the door.

Legs crossed, Babaji was sitting on a wooden cot, facing north. Stunned, he nonetheless managed to bow and lowered himself to the floor before the Guru. Leaning his back against the wall, beside the fireplace, sat another man on the floor: dark-skinned, covered only with a loincloth. It was cold, but he felt his face burning. The notorious fierce Doberman burst into the room and, like a puppy, began to lick Babaji's feet, then crawled under the bed and fell silent.

On that cold winter night, Babaji's hips were wrapped only in a piece of white cloth. The strong, muscular, and completely hairless body of the Mahavatar did not resemble the body of a bodybuilder; at the same time, the Master's appearance had nothing in common with the slim youth widely depicted among practitioners of Kriya — and again he wondered why iconography differed so sharply from the original.

The Guru was clearly in an excellent mood, and this only added to his nervousness. When a mighty immortal being cracks jokes, anyone would become nervous. He felt as though the velvet pads of a powerful lion's paws were playfully touching his face. He was overwhelmed at once by fear, caution, delight, and curiosity, and he tried to control all these seething emotions. The fact that he was seeing the immortal master for the second time brought no relief at all. Now he sat before the one who knew every thought of his, and this produced in him a strange mixture of guilt and excitement.

He had heard tales from people who claimed to have met Babaji. They described long spiritual discussions with the Master and His detailed advice on diet and career success. He kept his face impassive. "These lucky ones were fortunate not to soil their pants," he thought. The ancient power emanating from the Master was not conducive to casual conversation. Yes, such beings could appear before a mortal to give instructions, directions, or assignments, but the purpose of the meetings was never mundane.

Babaji described the "tasks" he was to perform, and this time it was not about yoga. The stranger who had been sitting motionless by the fireplace stood up and handed him a heavy bundle wrapped in burlap. Inside was a bulky object, and he recognized a Shri Yantra. Made of metal resembling brass, it was rather heavy, about 20 kg, but there was no time to examine it in detail; he had to listen attentively to the Guru. Babaji spoke about the meaning and significance of the yantra, instructing him in detail about the time of its installation. It was to be consecrated a few minutes before five in the afternoon, when the necessary constellations stood in the auspicious position, and the ritual had several layers of meaning at once, but he had no right to discuss this with anyone.

The visit lasted almost two hours; around five in the morning Babaji rose. "Well, the time has come for you to attend to the assignments. And I must be going." He followed the Guru to the exit. At that moment Devahuti came down to make morning tea and let out the dogs — and froze in astonishment, staring wide-eyed at the visitors. He whispered: "This is Babaji," and gestured for her to bow. Trying to contain her emotions, she made a pranam. Babaji smiled, raised His hand in blessing, and stepped out the door.

The winter night was slowly melting, and the outlines of the trees were beginning to emerge, radiating a dim milky light. He and Devahuti stood on the threshold, watching the immortal yogi slowly walk along the alley toward a half-ruined old building. Mahavatar's silent companion walked in front. Climbing the stairs to the roof of the structure, the Guru paused for a brief moment, then waved his hand in farewell and disappeared from view.

He looked at Devahuti. Her mouth was half open, and both of them were in a half-trance. Then the realization of what had happened hit like an accelerating truck, and his legs turned to rubber, as if all the energy had left his body. He slid down to the floor. Being in the presence of the immortal Guru for more than two hours had produced (in him, who had never considered himself particularly sensitive) the strongest physical effect. Devahuti silently walked to the kitchen; the familiar morning sounds helped collect their thoughts. For some time they drank hot tea in silence.

The second meeting with the Guru opened new perspectives, granting a sense of mission. What had been said was to remain a secret, and besides, the scale of the predicted events was not entirely clear, although he did not try to decipher all the details of the information he had received in one sitting. It was all like Kriya: in order to take the next steps, one had to go through the initial levels.

Once every four or five years, without any visible logic or regularity, Babaji sent him mental messages, "showing" and "transmitting" what He deemed necessary; the messages came as visions, detailed scenes with a voice-over. Words sounded simultaneously with images that flashed before his inner sight, and this could happen at any moment. After such a transmission, he fell into a state of ringing detachment, and this unusually strong feeling could last a couple of weeks. There was no need to recreate the visions; they were already imprinted in consciousness. The main thing was the constant sense of mighty force, confirming its own existence.

The last time he "viewed" a news summary from the Guru was in autumn 2019. Babaji let it be known that He had been living in Benares for more than two years, in a small rented house on a narrow street in the Old City. With two tiny rooms, a tiny kitchen, and a toilet, this rather modest dwelling apparently provided the immortal Master with complete anonymity. Babaji's jata had vanished; His hair was cut short. The visual "message" was clear as high-resolution video, and the audio carried the rather loud sounds of nearby construction.

Benares was undergoing significant changes; the city was to become Suramya Kashi (picturesque), Samunnat Kashi (progressive), Surakshit Kashi (safe), Sanyojit Kashi (organized), Nirmal Kashi (pure), and Ekikrit Kashi (unified). The scale of the transformations was enormous, and the reconstruction affected more than a thousand acres, including the replacement of sewerage and water-treatment systems. The old streets in front of the Vishwanath temple were cleared of debris; some old buildings were demolished, and to the surprise of archaeologists and historians, in the inner courtyards old, unknown and previously unseen shrines and temples were discovered. However, the outer shell cracked and split; it was patched and repainted. The invisible Kashi remained unchanged, inhaling Time and exhaling the souls leaving the earthly plane.

At the end of Diwali 2019, Babaji left Benares, and after that the vivid images and mental messages "switched off." It is unclear why Babaji had let him know of his whereabouts at all: perhaps it is simply His method of staying in touch. But what kept Babaji in the eternal city for almost two years? Did the immortal yogi influence significant changes throughout the world, having chosen Benares as his temporary headquarters?

The presence of the Mahavatar in the city of Shiva could not have been accidental, just as His visit to the royal smashan, the country residence of Mahadeva, had not been accidental.

## The Identity of Babaji

Satyacharan Lahiri never met Babaji in person; it seemed the Master was not interested in the mystery of the origin and formation of the personality of the Guru of their parampara. Perhaps because he remembered too well his father's experience. Overwhelmed by an irresistible desire to see the great yogi, the 19-year-old Tinkori Lahiri vowed not to move from his place until the Mahavatar Himself appeared before him. He spent 14 hours in meditation, and his desire was fulfilled. Babaji appeared, but the details remained unknown, since the meeting was interrupted. Lahiri Mahasaya hurriedly descended from the upper floor and, gasping for breath, burst into his son's room: "What are you doing! Never repeat this!!!"

Tinkori received a stern rebuke; the father ordered him never again to disturb the Guru with requests to appear in bodily form, much less to demonstrate miracles. Once, the Mahavatar had reprimanded Lahiri Mahasaya himself for the same thing, when he had summoned Him to satisfy a friend's curiosity. Such requests were a sign of disrespect and meant only the absence of maturity. Familiar with the signs of Babaji's presence, Lahiri Mahasaya had instantly felt the powerful vibration coming from his son's room and intervened before Tinkori's summons provoked possibly irreversible reactions from the Guru.

Who then was this immortal yogi, and why was his name surrounded by such mystery? One of the many suppositions about the identity of the Mahavatar said that he was Gorakhnath, one of the immortal Nathas.

Remembering Gwalior in the seventies, he smiled: at that time he and Sidhoji Rao Sitole would discuss Kriya yoga and its masters for hours. Enchanted, almost obsessed, with the personality of Gorakhnath, his friend masterfully developed the most interesting parallels and made various suppositions about the connection between Gorakhnath and Babaji. Years later, S.R.S. even wrote a book about it — "Babaji: Frozen Lightning." But there was a catch: all the known texts describe Gorakhnath as a 15–16-year-old youth, while Babaji looked older, from 25 to 35, and wore none of the Natha accessories (earrings, bracelets, whistle). Definitely, both legendary yogis were great — but absolutely different.

There also existed detailed "testimonies" that Babaji was a native of south India. Books were written and published, lectures given, fascinating stories told about his childhood, abduction, and training under Rishi Agastya and Boganathar. Allegedly Babaji was one of the Tamil Siddhas, and in a former life his name was Nagaraj. It was believed that Babaji was born on 30 November 203 AD, in a small coastal village in Tamil Nadu, where the river Kaveri flows into the Indian Ocean. As it happened, the child was born under the same rising star Rohini, under which Krishna had been born. The future immortal came into the world during the celebration of Kartikai Deepam, on the night before the new moon, and his parents belonged to the Nambudri caste of Brahmins and were supposedly migrants from the Malabar coast. Reading about all this was interesting, but when he had met the immortal Guru in person, Babaji spoke Hindi as it is spoken in north India, and in his speech there was not a trace of a southern accent. He looked like a native of western Uttar Pradesh and was fair-skinned, with rather sharp features. There was not a trace of southern softness in him.

The diaries of Lahiri Mahasaya and the memoirs of Yogananda also mention that the Mahavatar spoke Hindi, not Tamil or Bengali. But all this was unimportant — except for one circumstance: a son of mortal parents, Babaji, had attained immortality. Sadhana had granted Him eternal life. His teaching definitely helped to prevent old age and death, since Babaji himself had succeeded in this.

Some authors claimed to have personally met the Mahavatar. Reading the elevated descriptions of their dialogues with Babaji, he could not hide his dry smile. "My son, you should eat two eggs a day; the protein is good for you…" Immortals who have lifted the veil of eternity do not hand out dietary recommendations. The narrators simply lacked imagination.

Another story about a group of immortals chosen by Babaji made him laugh outright. Allegedly, enlightened yogis lived near the Master in the Himalayas, and every morning Babaji's sister led them to morning rituals. After bathing in the cold springs they were allowed to bow at the lotus feet of the Guru. The description of a group of immortals seemed to him most amusing. No, it was not disrespectful, but… the description resembled some sort of kindergarten for the realized.

Those who have attained the highest steps of development could hardly live in groups, much less perform common daily rituals. Babaji himself would not have endured the constant presence of "spiritual followers." Moreover, however advanced they might be, they would not be able to bear the mighty energies of the Mahavatar himself for any length of time.

He remembered the searing touch of fear at his first meeting with the Guru, which had not entirely disappeared at the second meeting either. Babaji was a laughing lion, and he himself a helpless child. Despite Babaji's playful disposition and the entire informality of the meeting, it was clear: He was an ancient mighty being possessing a completely different level of intelligence and perception of reality. Perhaps for Him the world of men was simply another alternative reality.

That the Mahavatar appeared at the Kumbha Mela was well known; occasionally He visited big cities as well, but these were one-off cases. Overcrowded urban areas emitted the mental miasmas of human intentions and projections, too offensive and suffocating for an immortal. Of course, Babaji could endure anything, but seclusion and solitude were still synonymous with sadhana. Unless He was carrying out some mission and had to appear in the megalopolises.

There were also speculations that Sai Baba, Jesus, Guru Nanak, Adi Shankara, and many other religious and spiritual figures were disciples of Babaji. Why not? Sri Yukteshwar maintained that Swami Kebalananda, Swami Keshabananda, Swami Pranabananda and others had stated that Lahiri Mahasaya spoke of Babaji as the secret spiritual guru of Shankaracharya. And this too was quite possible. One case from the life of Shankaracharya is well known: once he was returning after bathing in the Ganga, and right in front of him a dirty and ragged chandal passed by. People of this caste were considered so impure that they literally could not cross the road of a Brahmin. More despised than the untouchables, chandals live in smashans surrounded by dogs. Shankaracharya exclaimed: "Get away!" and the chandal asked: "To whom do you speak? To the all-pervading Jiva, or to this illusory body?"

Brahma Satyam Jagat Mithya Jivo Brahmaiva Na Aparah… "Only Brahma is real; this world is unreal, and the Jiva, or individual soul, is not distinct from Brahma." Philosopher, theologian, and famous propagator of Advaita Vedanta, Shankaracharya instantly realized: these words shattered all his dogmas. As well as belief in a single eternal unchanging reality; the illusory state of multiplicity and differentiation melted, and Shankaracharya fell prostrate before the chandal. It is said that the untouchable was Shiva himself, but he could just as well have been Babaji. The chandal who met Shankaracharya at the ghats of the Ganga and initiated him into Kriya yoga could easily have been the Mahavatar himself, since the common philosophical concepts were closest to the ideology of this yoga. The transformations that Shankara brought to the Indian philosophical school and society could have been inspired by the influence of his diksha guru.

In 2014, working on commentaries to the "Hatha Yoga Pradipika," he noted the striking similarity between Babaji and Svatmarama, the author of this most enigmatic of all yogic texts. The more he delved into the interpretation of the shlokas, the more obvious the parallels became. The content linguistically echoed the explanation of the higher kriyas that Babaji had given him. Of Svatmarama little is known. More precisely, nothing at all. But Lahiri Mahasaya recorded in his diary: "…Babaji is the highest representative of Hatha Yoga." That is how Mahasaya called the Master, and it was not a coincidence.

As the theories multiplied, he decided not to add to them yet another hypothesis of the secret origin of the Mahavatar. Babaji's unwillingness to reveal his identity was to be respected. There must have been serious reasons for the secrecy maintained for hundreds of years. If Babaji decides to tell his story, He will do so Himself. Besides, what would the disclosure of the secret of His origin change?

Obscurity remains the greatest luxury for immortals; it allows them to be unnoticed among people, to observe changes, moving easily through the layers of history and time. All the interwoven episodes of human history were but ornaments on the stained glass of the Temple of Time. And to the mass only gods and immortals were invited.

## Siddhis, Small Miracles and Magic Tricks

Babaji possessed something else, and this something was not connected with the mystery of his origin. Fully controlling the basic elements of the body, the Mahavatar cast no shadow. Brought to equal proportion, water, air, ether, fire and earth made his body translucent or completely invisible. But this was only a side effect. Control of the elements granted eternal life. The Nine Siddhas of antiquity, the nine immortal Nathas, could decrease or increase the mass of the body, fly, enchant, obtain what they wished, control life and death. But what is the meaning of all this control? The body can be left at will or remain in it forever.

In his "Autobiography," Yogananda described an interesting episode. When Yukteshwar visited Lahiri Mahasaya after the Kumbha Mela, the latter asked: "Did you not see Babaji when you entered here?" No, Yukteshwar had not seen the Guru, but when his vision unfocused, behind a veil of the brightest light, blinding as the sun, he recognized Babaji. Seeing Yukteshwar's astonishment, Babaji said: "Son, you must meditate more. Your sight is still clouded…"

However, it was not Yukteshwar's lack of perseverance in meditation that made him unable to see the immortal Guru. The material world itself is a combination of numerous illusions. Or rather, the world is real, and the illusions are mostly optical in nature. Because of the limited spectrum of human sight, objects are visible only because they reflect light. At the same time, the eyes can simultaneously distinguish only three surfaces of an object, at best. Balancing of elements at the cellular and atomic level allows the body of a highly developed yogi to become invisible, and sometimes to be transported into other planes, assembling and disassembling the particles of Matter.

The physical world consists of the same five elements as the body. Through the practice of yoga, the basic elements can be balanced, decreasing the percentage of earth and water elements in the body, and increasing the content of air and ether. This can lead to the translucency of any gross substance, including the tissues of the human body. An equal proportion of the elements can reduce the density of the atomic structure, and then the shadow will not be noticeable; the body will begin to absorb the rays of light rather than reflect them. Gradually the shadow will become less dense, and in the end will "disappear" completely.

Sometimes, standing next to someone, he observed his own shadow, and compared to the shadow of his companion it was light and blurred. The brightness of his yogic aura diluted the density of the shadow; it disappeared almost entirely, like the luminous "nimbus" over his head, almost invisible in daylight. In medieval Europe the Inquisition would surely have burned him as a sorcerer or a vampire, but this was India, and any improbability could become probable.

Scientists recognized the proofs of Newton's discoveries comparatively recently, and Pythagoras's theorem was used by Einstein in the Special Theory of Relativity only in 1905. The metric equation was actually a discovery of Pythagoras, applied to three spatial coordinates. All these postulates were known long before their proofs were recognized, and therefore he studied the treatises of the ancient yogis with respect and trust. Some texts spoke of a vajra body, the diamond (or, rather, the electric) body, and this was not just an entertaining hypothesis but a confirmation of someone's actual achievement. The cellular structure of muscle tissue and bones could be transformed into the crystalline structure of a diamond; a body not subject to destruction became eternal. The next step — the miracle of the electric body.

The human organism works on electrical impulses generated by the brain, but only 2% of the voltage is used. Perhaps for safety, since a brain "switched on" at full capacity produces such strong currents that muscle tissue can burn out and turn to ash. In the case of the balanced elements of the yogic body, such electrical currents can transform the body itself into pure consciousness, a network of neurons.

In the nucleus of every atom there is contained an enormous quantity of light. Anyone can see external light, but yoga strives to discern the inner. It is difficult for the logical mind to recognize the existence of the inner light and accept it as a physical parameter, but for him the outer and inner light were one. Yogic consciousness is open to the perception of the broader picture of creation, and yet not to confuse physics with metaphysics is no easy task even for advanced practitioners.

What until recently sounded like science fiction was now being "rediscovered" by modern science. Gradually the attention of researchers came to be drawn to the cases of "zombie cells" of the brain continuing to grow after physical death, the transfer of consciousness into other dimensions, and other mysteries of Life.

Miracles formerly regarded as fairy tales were now finding reflection in cinema, computer games, and comics; they stimulated the imagination of scientists, and the boldest suppositions and theories became reality. Once proved and embodied by science, they were already taken as a given and no longer subject to doubt. Youthful Imagination ran ahead of the limping cripple — accepted Logic.

When Babaji showed Lahiri Mahasaya a golden palace, He was by no means creating an optical illusion. As a Siddha of the highest level, He could with a single effort of will easily assemble the elements of matter, as if they were Lego bricks. Such abilities are a side effect of advanced sadhana, and in all times yogis have possessed varying degrees of this art. Discovered in the solitude of thousands of sadhanas, yogic discoveries were kept secret, but every secret has a shelf life.

Somewhere around 1991 he came across an old book, "Puran Indrajaal," written in ancient Hindi. It was a manual on the art of mantras, tantric rituals and the use of medicinal herbs; the text contained astrological recommendations and fragments of the tantras of Dattatreya and Uddhava. The full text was called "Ravan Sanhitr," since it had been written by the lord of the rakshasas, Ravana. Out of curiosity, for entertainment, he followed the instructions and obtained impressive, if not surprising, results.

Some of the "miracles" described in the book were simple tricks, but even they required precise adherence to the instructions. Time, herbs, the particular arrangement of the necessary constellations, the correct pronunciation of the mantra, and the sankalpa — the willed impetus for the realization of the intention — were equally important.

A cotton seed planted under a certain arrangement of the stars in the skull of a wolf had to be watered daily with milk and water while repeating a certain mantra. When the plant gave a cotton boll, a wick was made from this cotton and placed in an oil lamp. In the light of such a lamp any face and body underwent incredible transformation. The invited witnesses of the experiment saw him in the guise of a furious wolf, but only within the radius of the lamp's light. This was not hypnosis at all, but an optical illusion reinforced by the power of mantra. Performing a similar experiment with the skull of a cobra, he obtained the same result. In the uneven light of the lamp a large snake stared at the petrified spectators, flicking its forked tongue. For another experiment a seed of a particular herb was required; the process was almost the same, only this time the plant grew in the skull of a panther. When it ripened, seeds were collected and covered with silver, gold, and copper. Now, in order to appear in the image of a panther, it was enough to place these seeds in the mouth — and those nearby would see a mighty predator.

A paste made from a certain herb could create an unusual and frightening effect — a person could appear dismembered, his limbs scattered within the radius of the lamp's light. Sadhus used these tricks to frighten off uninvited guests and possible marauders.

Having mastered one trick, he moved on to the next, and so discovered, or rather guessed, another amazing phenomenon, called "antara dhyan." One could stage a complete disappearance by stopping the projection of mental waves, drawing in all impressions of the external world, and blocking any thoughts. The mental radar of other people would immediately switch off, they would lose focus and not trust their own eyes. For this, not physical silence was important but the stillness of the mind.

The effect of antara dhyan he decided to test on his neighbor, Lavli Puri, who usually came onto their rooftop terrace to train with dumbbells and barbells. The young man was intrigued by the strange lifestyle of the young yogin — he did not work, hardly went outside, and received endless visitors. Finally Lavli Puri dared to ask: "What exactly are you doing — what is it that you do?"

Smiling, he answered: "This…" — and vanished from sight. Lavli Puri, in astonishment, stared at the concrete wall behind the place where his neighbor had just been standing, and saw only the scratches and irregularities of the plaster. Frightened, Lavli Puri fled and did not appear again for quite a long time, more than twenty years.

There was plenty of time for experiments. While his peers were achieving entirely predictable career results, he was studying the forgotten art of fakirs and sadhus. The practicality of such skills did not concern him.

Once in Sarnath he entertained a few teenagers and his niece with the "disappearance act." Obeying his command, they all closed their eyes. "Now look" — and they stared at the wall by which he had been sitting a second ago. The sofa was empty. Before they began to panic, his voice rang out: "Close your eyes again, count to five, and open them." They obeyed, and he was sitting before them again, smiling.

"Again." This time he did not vanish completely; only his smile hovered in the air, displaying only teeth. For some time he played the Cheshire Cat, amused by the reaction of the teenagers. But it was not advisable, in his view, to show such things to an adult audience.

Another fascinating experiment consisted in bringing dead flies back to life, and he treated the practice with simultaneous seriousness and humor. Large creatures did not suit the purpose, since warm-blooded animals or birds possessed individual karmas and one should not interfere with them. The insects were lowered into water for 15–20 minutes, then placed on the ashes of the dhuni, and he concentrated on the tiny bodies of the drowned females, mentally repeating a mantra. After two or three minutes the insects began to move their legs,

and then would fly away. The revival was magic, and the key to this miracle was Prana, the life-force animating everything around.

Ordinary people were impressed by these tricks; people had the impression that he was a great miracle-worker. For a while he enjoyed his "accomplishments," until he was visited by a revelation: all this could easily bring him money and respect, but what were the true motives of the experiments? The demonstration of magic tricks could easily turn seekers into silent followers. Was he sincerely interested in the secrets of Tantra and Yoga, or was he gradually turning into yet another spiritual showman? The masses willingly worshipped "messengers of the Divine"; such impostors had to live with their conscience, knowing that they deceived people in the name of God or yoga.

These thoughts made him stop the demonstrations. People who came to him to study yoga were not seeking thrills; they had to be ready to perform miracles themselves, combining will and intention with the power of Prana, in order to become witnesses of the indescribable.

Puja to Hanuman, 2008
Conversations with disciples, 2018

Shivaratri, 2008

## Avadhut and Devi of Braj

### Shivalingam

For several years in a row the celebration of the great night of Shiva at the smashan passed without particular pomp: in the evening the disciples gathered for puja, and at night he practiced as usual. But in 1998 it was decided to celebrate properly, with a night vigil and puja.

On 25 February five pandits arrived from Gwalior, and the ritual was held right in the house. When the sacrificial offering of milk was completed, everyone felt peace and serenity. Exactly at midnight he heard a low, rumbling voice: "Go to the Red Samadhi and perform there a Bhumi puja, the ritual of purification of the earth." He asked two pandits to begin the rite without explaining the reason for the request, well aware of the possible reaction to an explanation that began "a voice told me to do this." The small pavilion built on the cremation site of the raja resembled a set for a horror film; the smashan did have a grim reputation. The ritual of purification continued until dawn. When it ended, the same low rolling voice spoke: "I have waited for you a long time. Send someone for me." Suddenly he saw a certain locality, a crossroads of paths somewhere close by, on the Hill. Turning to those present, he tried to give his words a casual tone: "Begin to search not far from here, at an intersection of paths; you should find a natural Shivalingam." As it turned out later, this day was not particularly auspicious for new beginnings, and perhaps that is why the Shivalingam was found only the next day, after Shivaratri, around 11 a.m. Half-covered with earth, sprinkled with dry bougainvillea petals, it lay near the intersection of three paths on the Govardhan Hill. One of the disciples, Ravindra Dwivedi, noticed it first. Together with another member of the search party they dug out the stone and triumphantly carried it to the smashan. Standing on the veranda, he saw the disciples approaching; they were almost running, exclaiming: "We found it!"

The dark oval stone was washed, dried, and measured. It weighed exactly 39 kg; the surface was smooth, as if polished. Devahuti hoisted the stone onto her shoulder and carried it upstairs, to his bedroom, where it was kept for several months. The significance of the find gradually began to dawn on him: what had happened was not a hallucination; the instructions had been correct; the voice in his head had been real.

The Maharaja of Bharatpur had a secretary who occasionally came to the smashan to light oil lamps in memory of the raja's deceased ancestors, drink tea, and chat with the new owner. During one such visit he informed the secretary of the plans to install a Shivalingam at the cremation site of the late Maharaja Daswant Singh, and asked him to inquire whether the Maharaja would object. Although legally he was the owner of this place, formalities had to be observed. The smashan permanently bound the living descendants of the royal dynasty with their deceased ancestors, and any innovations were a delicate matter.

The next morning, around 6 o'clock, Maharaja Vrijendra Singh arrived unexpectedly with his son — upon receiving the message he had decided to come in person. His Majesty considered that the installation of a Shivalingam at the cremation site of his great-great-grandfather would be a great honor for the entire royal family. According to Hindu traditions, there is nothing comparable in importance to the ritual of Shiva puja at the cremation site of someone.

After some time the pavilion once erected on the raja's cremation site became a temple. On 26 June 1998 the Shivalingam was installed right on the samadhi of the ruler.

Usually toy flutes, the symbol of Krishna, were sold by the hundreds in souvenir shops along the road, but on the day when the Shivalingam was installed at the cremation site of the Bharatpur ruler, Govardhan was suddenly flooded with toy damaru drums. The sound of Shiva's favorite musical instruments came from everywhere, as if the drum-rhythms echoed the insistent beat of millions of hearts.

The five priests began the ritual; mantras were not recited. He simply placed the heavy oval stone, the body of Shiva, on the metal base, feeling the cool surface under his palms. It seemed to him alive, pulsating with an invisible current.

By tradition, worship in a new temple must be performed in all four corners, and the pandits began the veneration of the nagas, the sacred serpents, from the southwest corner. As soon as the recitation of mantras began, a real snake suddenly slid into the pavilion. About 25–30 cm long, golden-colored, with red eyes, the snake appeared from nowhere, its undulating movements were swift. Recoiling, one of the pandits tried to flee, but he stopped him, ordering him to finish the ritual. Finally, the priest pronounced the last words: "Now Shiva is present here" — and the ceremony was over. By that time the golden snake had disappeared, and no one noticed precisely when.

During the puja, several children with dogs remained in the house. Suddenly a young male German shepherd named Robin, tied up in a room upstairs, began desperately to twist out of his collar, trying to free himself. The dog spun wildly around his own axis, choking, wheezing, and panicking more and more… When Devahuti ran upstairs, the dog was already dead, and the inconsolable children were sobbing in hysterics. The dog's body was buried in the garden without much pomp; the incident was unfortunate and sad, but he did not take it as a bad omen. There was a feeling that this death was no accident, and his intuition turned out to be right. Among the Nathas there is a belief: anyone who dares to install a Shivalingam at a cremation site will die within twenty-four hours… Was this superstition, or did the death of the dog compensate for his own? Being unversed in such matters, he firmly believed that with the passage of time all secrets would become known. His innocence in these matters had nothing to do with ignorance. He learned quickly.

For the first time in the entire history of Braj a natural Shivalingam had been found on Govardhan Hill, and this became a truly extraordinary event. Of course, geologically oval stones could form anywhere, but on the land of Krishna, where the vibrations, beliefs and mentality of the inhabitants attracted entirely different energies, other deities — and during the last millennia Shaiva shrines had not manifested here. All of this was no coincidence, and he hoped that the reasons for Shiva's appearance would eventually become known.

The puja became for him a kind of addiction; the uniqueness of the ritual gave him remarkable energy. Visitors who had seen many other rituals of Shiva-worship throughout India said that this puja was the most unusual.

The first service began at 5:00 a.m., together with the awakening of nature, to the timid accompaniment of birds. To the sounds of drums, from the apex of the black conical stone there proceeded a beam of blue light, and he felt it penetrating his heart. At first he took this for the play of reflections from the flickering flame of the oil lamps, and only when several people who had attended the puja at the early hour swore that they had seen this phenomenon, did he believe in the reality of the beam.

With time, the Shivalingam changed color, becoming darker from the milk poured upon it, the honey rubbed in, the flour, yogurt, and flower petals that adorned it every night. At the top of the stone a small but clearly distinguishable "Om" sign was discovered, formed by almost invisible cracks. During the ritual the tiny hollows would fill with the whiteness of milk, briefly making the not-made-by-hands hieroglyph visible.

Mantras during the ritual he did not recite; most are intended for services outside cremation sites. But the damaru drums were beaten loudly, because every word of every language originates from the sound of His drum — and Mahadeva could choose any mantra from those sounds.

The story of the find was significant, but unclear. What exactly was required of him personally? He waited for signs. Yoga taught patience and contemplation; truth revealed itself to those who could make its essence part of their inner "I."

Were all these events simply the entertainment of card-shuffling higher powers? In Hindi "entertainment" or "manoranjan" translates as "coloring of the mind in colors." Reality teemed with juicy colors; the palette of events was too vast for a summary assessment or a single conclusion. However, everything that was happening confirmed him in devotion to this land and its gods.

## Nepal. Ritual

No one could give him advice on how to properly perform Shiva puja, and he had no idea what to do next. Together with Devahuti they performed aarti in the temple, the fire ceremony, lit incense, and that was the end of it. Until people who came by tradition to touch the Shivalingam began to fall ill. After a few hours visitors developed a high fever and the most violent vomiting — this was rather alarming.

Hoping for guidance, he felt relief when he heard a low voice during night meditation. It came from everywhere at once: "I came only to you. Go to Nepal, and the old priest in Pashupatinath will teach you to perform the ritual of worship properly." Before leaving he established a strict rule: no one but himself and the pujari was to touch the Shivalingam.

In October 1998, accompanied by one of his disciples, he boarded a train to Gorakhpur. Relatively small by Indian standards, Gorakhpur is the birthplace of the Buddha and of Paramahansa Yogananda, the home of the famous mystic poet Kabir Das, but first and foremost it is the city of the great Gorakhnath. Before the next leg of the journey they decided to perform aarti, the ritual of offering fire to deities.

…to visit the famous temple of Gorakshanatha.

Dozens of Natha-yogins in saffron robes, with the great kundala-earrings, filled the courtyard of the temple complex. Looking at them, he thought that the very Yoga which had raised the Nathas to spiritual heights ought to have remained their priority — not political games, not the management of a vast network of ashrams, not the printing and distribution of sacred texts for profit. And certainly not cunning deals in land and real estate. All these practical aspects were understandable enough, but the essence of the teaching had nearly vanished, had been forgotten, pushed into the background. Like many other religious organizations in modern India, the Nathas had to survive, and what mattered most was fundraising, not Yoga. Now it had become a relic, a by-product of past greatness.

And yet one could still trace the roots of Hatha-Yoga, which reached down into the Natha Sampradaya. Although Kriya Yoga is not directly tied to the Natha tradition, strict discipline and devotion to the goal of realization are branches of the same great tree of Knowledge. The practice of the Nathas is rather complex and demands a discipline of which renunciation of society is a part. Few modern seekers can follow such vows. As a rule, interaction with the world is curtailed; ascetics are expected to keep themselves apart and to follow the rules prescribed by the tradition.

The Natha maths-monasteries still preserve old traditions, but this does not always mean that yogic practices are maintained within them. Only a few yogins in the Natha sampradaya still possess the true knowledge. And although the Nathas are distinct from other sadhus, finding a practicing yogin among them is not easy. In his view, the sole criterion of realization was an unbroken yogic practice.

For even respected spiritual communities with well-established hierarchies cannot carry the essence of the teaching into the future, and the authority and reputation of a saint are not based on membership in any organization. The immense effort required for realization comes from personal attainment. Practice unveils aspects of knowledge that cannot be taught in words; they can only be earned in person, in the course of one's own tapas.

…They walked out of the temple, and the Nathas seated in the courtyard fell silent, staring openly at the strings of rudraksha around his neck and at his long hair. He did not look back; the energies of the place were somber, half-questioning, half-rejecting.

Gorakhpur is located not far from the Nepalese border, and they took a rickshaw to the checkpoint. The border guards let them through without any delay, simply waving them on. He had no documents, not even a driver's license, but at that time travel into Nepal was not hedged in with formalities. In the nearest town they boarded a bus to Kathmandu and dozed for the next ten hours, though they were periodically jolted on the hard seats. After a while the rhythm of the road shifted, softened by the breakwaters of the mountains and the narrowness of the green valleys. The highway grew smoother, and he fell asleep…

The next day they entered the main building of the temple complex at Pashupatinath, where he introduced himself to the chief pandit, handing him a copy of his commentary on the Gita. He explained that he needed to see the old Bhatt-ji (such was the title of the religious head of Pashupatinath). Taking the book carefully, the priest asked him to wait, and returned after almost an hour. He was invited into the living quarters behind the temple.

…Bhatt-ji was already 99 years old, and very weak. Owing to his health the chief pandit no longer received visitors, but the Gita brought by the yogin from Govardhan moved the old priest to meet the author.

All rituals at Pashupatinath are performed by two groups of priests: the Bhattas and the Rajbhandari. The Bhattas — highly educated Vedic Dravidian Brahmins from Karnataka — are carefully selected and dispatched to Nepal to perform the daily rituals. [footnote] The temple of Pashupatinath is the oldest and one of the most sacred Hindu temples. The complex stands on both banks of the Bagmati river on the eastern edge of Kathmandu and is dedicated to Lord Shiva. In 1979 it was inscribed as a World Heritage site. The place attracts hundreds of elderly Hindus who come here to spend the last weeks of their lives and to meet death, in order to be cremated on the bank of the river. It is believed that those who die at Pashupatinath are reborn as human beings regardless of the misdeeds that may have worsened their karma. The exact day of death is foretold by the temple astrologers. [/footnote] Initiated into Pashupata Yoga and the Shiva Agamas, they must satisfy many requirements before they may touch the idol. The Bhandari caste is not allowed to perform puja; they are mainly assistants and caretakers.

…The old man lay on the bed; beside him sat two rather ancient servants. In a cracked but still authoritative voice the priest asked in Hindi: "What do you want?" And he told everything: of the finding of the Shivalingam and Its installation at the cremation site of the Raja, of the illness that afflicted all who touched It, even of the death of the dog. But, most of all, of the voice that had sent him to Pashupatinath.

The words came clearly and distinctly, yet he had the strange impression that the story was being told by someone else. He finished; the old priest was silent for almost ten minutes. No one moved. A solitary fly was studying an unlit lamp, confidently sinking its proboscis into the viscous surface of the dark oil. Then Bhatt-ji began to dictate, and for a start ordered him to wear red clothes during the ritual. Bewildered by this unexpected recommendation, he asked: "Why red?" Hindus associate this color with tantric ceremonies, but Bhatt-ji gave an altogether different explanation. During a Hindu wedding the husband is regarded as Parameshvara Himself, a form of Lord Shiva. [footnote] Parameshvara — the supreme God or supreme Being. In Hinduism it most often refers to Lord Shiva. [/footnote] Grateful for the opportunity to be married to Divinity Itself in the image of her husband, the bride puts on a red sari.

Bhatt-ji tried to raise himself on the bed, looking intently at him: "To find a natural Shivalingam, Parameshvara, is a great fortune and an honor for a mortal. When you worship Him on a smashana, wear red as a sign of fortune, of gratitude, and of devotion." The long, detailed explanation of every aspect of the ritual took almost an hour, and he wrote it down diligently, trying not to miss anything. When the dictation ended, he bowed and walked out of the dark room, carrying the precious notebook. The old priest refused to accept money, but he nevertheless dropped 500 rupees into the donation box at the entrance.

The next day they flew to Benares, and instead of a passport he showed the border guard the spread of the Bhagavad Gita with his own photograph: "In this photograph it is I, Shailendra Sharma…". The small plane smelled of cardamom and old newspapers.

In Benares, after visiting the Vishwanath temple, he stopped at the house of Lahiri Mahasaya to see Banamali Lahiri, the great-great-grandson of the Master. As usual, the conversation was warm and engaging. Even so, he decided not to mention either the Shivalingam or the purpose of his trip to Nepal. Religious and esoteric subjects interested the professor little; he was inclined toward philosophy rather than metaphysics…

…Worship of Shiva began on the first day of the Dussehra festival. In accordance with the instructions he had received, the puja was performed twice a day, and for the first two weeks he was a little nervous, trying not to confuse the sequence. To hold simultaneously a pitcher of milk and the horn through which the offering was made was rather awkward, but Devahuti and Mahatma-ji helped him until the ritual became second nature.

…That day, around noon, they were sitting on the terrace when a child's voice came from the temple: "Har, Har, Mahadev!" Then followed the dry rattle of a drum. Going to investigate, they saw a boy dressed as Shiva standing in front of the Shivalingam. The child apparently belonged to a troupe of wandering performers, who usually beg for alms at places of pilgrimage in costumes of Indian gods.

While his companions rested in the dharamshala in front of the gates of the smashana, the boy had made friends with the unsmiling guard and asked permission to enter the temple. His thin body was painted blue; around his slender neck hung a necklace of rudraksha. Bathed in sunlight, with a lit stick of incense somehow fixed in his hair, he held a slim iron trident in his hand, and the incense smoke filled the air with a thick fragrance. Not trying to pose or to act, the child-performer simply soaked up that day — the birdsong, the light, everything around him. They gave him a few coins, and accepting them with dignity and gratitude, the boy bowed reservedly to the Shivalingam. And left. Sweet and unremarkable as this small incident was, something had shifted, as though the temple had been consecrated once again.

…In every temple dedicated to Shiva there is an oval stone that is worshipped as Mahadeva Himself. Of varying size, natural or hand-carved, the Shivalingam is usually installed in the center of a disc-shaped platform — the yoni. This installation symbolizes the merging of the micro- and macrocosm, the union of feminine and masculine, the eternal process of creation and rebirth. For the Shaivites, the lingam-yoni is a metaphor of spiritual truths and faith.

From the nineteenth century onward, various sources (mostly in Western literature) described the Shivalingam as a reproductive organ, the phallus of Shiva. This crude interpretation contradicts the higher meaning of Shaivism, in which the lingam-yoni stands for the dual principles of creation — different yet united elements and principles. The Skanda Purana states that all beings possess the attributes of Shiva and Shakti; all creatures display the duality of Nature.

For some, the Shivalingam may mean a part of Shiva's body; for the more discerning seeker, it is a symbol of the presence of Shiva-Time. Everything depends on the level of development. In South India the Tamil Shaivites call the Shivalingam kuri, "sign" or "marker" — and perceive Him exclusively as a spiritual symbol, without any sexual implication. For devotees of the Destroyer, the Shivalingam symbolizes the axis of creation, the passage from one reality to another.

Each night, touching the smooth oval surface, he felt that the stone radiated something that could only be described as the energy of eternity. Without trying to decode all these intricate metaphors and words, he simply felt the presence and the breath of Shiva. And he became one with Him. In his sadhana he felt a power ripening within him; the will, bound together by yoga and worship, became a silent witness to the mystery. The unconscious expanded until no mind remained at all, and only the soundless voice of Shiva-Time spoke with him through the lips of Prana.

The Heart of the Earth

The very first story of true love was the story of Shiva and Sati. [footnote] Sati — one of the aspects of Shakti. The first wife of Shiva, whom she married against the will of her father Daksha. [/footnote] When She performed self-immolation, grieving Shiva began to wander the world, carrying Her corpse. Mahadeva vowed not to stop until His beloved's body had disintegrated entirely — such was the depth of His love, the magnitude of the loss… The gods asked Vishnu to halt this terrifying march of mourning, and the Preserver of the Universe severed Shakti's body with the sharp disc of the Sudarshana-chakra into 51 parts. One after another, they fell to the earth, and so the sacred places were born — the Shakti Pithas. [footnote] Shakti Pitha — when Sati ended her life, grief overwhelmed Shiva; He carried His wife's body, refusing to part with Her. This might have led to the destruction of the world, and so Lord Vishnu severed Sati's body into 51 parts, which fell upon the earth in various places. These sacred places became known as the Shakti Pithas. [/footnote] The feminine force of the Goddess passed into the Earth, becoming a part of it, and these ancient sites of eternal creative power are still revered by millions of pilgrims, symbolizing the force of love, pride, loss, and eternal union.

Across the Indian subcontinent 51 Shakti Pithas are counted, by other reckonings 108. The four most important and sacred are situated in Odisha, Assam, and Bengal, and Braj is not on this list. Nevertheless, in the ancient…

…tantric text, the Yogini Tantra, there is a curious mention. Apart from religious and philosophical themes, this voluminous work — devoted to the worship of Kali and Kamakhya — contains some startling historical material. In it Govardhan is named as Kamakhya, the important tantric goddess of creative force, desire, and fertility.

Both physical and metaphysical signs of the Goddess's presence on the smashana appeared too often to be ignored. Yet to weave all the events into a single fabric was almost impossible. The mixture of myths, anthropological evidence, religious convictions, intuition, and mysticism had been fermenting for years; one needed to digest and assimilate all this data so that it could become wisdom. That is precisely what he was doing, but, whatever his conclusions, he kept them to himself.

Such revelations could not lead either lay people or academic researchers to constructive conclusions. People do not like to see widely accepted facts being challenged. Once approved and documented, history must become an undisputable postulate, signed and catalogued like a fossil. It is generally considered that disputed facts ought to remain veiled or to take the modest guise of alternative hypotheses.

The very name of the locality was a key to the riddle of the Shakti Pithas. A portion of the Skanda Purana, the text "Bhagavad Mahatmya," gave hints as to the origin of the name "Moksh Bhumi" in the legend of the last journey of the queens — the widows of Krishna. When, having completed His mission, Krishna left the body, His grandson Vajranabh inherited the kingdom of Mathura. Inconsolable, their hearts broken by grief, 16,107 widowed queens mourned Krishna. But Queen Yamuna did not look despondent, and the other widows wondered: "Why are you not grieving — do you not feel the pain of separation?"

Yamuna could not hide her surprise: "Do you not see Him? He is here!" — and she advised them to seek guidance from Shandilya Rishi, who could help the queens to be reunited with Krishna. No one in Braj ever says that Krishna died (what a base thought!). It is believed that He simply interrupted His divine Lila — and so there was nothing unusual in the queens' hope of seeing Him again.

The Rishi told them to hold festivities at the foot of the hill Govardhan, on the shore of lake Kusum Sarovar — there, where Krishna's immortal friend Uddhava lived. As the Rishi explained to the widows, Uddhava could endow them with the hidden knowledge of the Maha Bhagavad Gita, which would help them to find peace. Following the advice, the queens pitched a luxurious camp between lakes Manasi Ganga and Kusum Sarovar, and Uddhava — dark-skinned, radiant, dressed in golden robes, strikingly resembling Krishna — read them the Maha Bhagavad Gita for a whole month. And they all began to see Krishna everywhere. As it is said, they attained "Krishna consciousness," which in fact means that they left their earthly bodies. There the chapter ended.

All 16,107 queens were cremated on the plot of land between the royal smashana and the lake Kusum Sarovar. In the local dialect this area is known as Shaktitara, which means "the place where wives obtained liberation." Most likely, the origin of the name "Moksh Bhumi" is connected with this story.

Aware of the powerful energies of the place, an unknown sadhu marked the boundaries of the future dynastic cremation complex and bestowed on thirteen generations of the rulers of Bharatpur a priceless gift — release from rebirth. Powerful ancient feminine energies guarded and nourished this land, and in combination with the energy of the holy hill produced an indescribable effect — a sense of pure, inexplicable, but unmistakable grace. And of peace.

…The numerous Shakti Pithas across India are dedicated to different parts of Sati's body, and each locality has its own meaning. In the state of Jharkhand, the temple of Jayadurga in Baidyanath is held to be the "official" place where the heart of Sati is believed to have fallen. The site is widely known as the land of the sacred heart (Baidyanath Dham, or Hridaya Pitha). Yet the pulse of the metaphysical, mystical Heart of the Earth is felt everywhere.

As is known, the planet pulses with a particular kind of resonant waves called Schumann resonance — this is the "heartbeat of the Earth." These waves are regulated by electricity. Lightning striking the ground creates electromagnetic waves in the atmosphere. Held between the earth and the upper layers of the atmosphere, these waves do not race across the surface of the planet but produce a kind of standing waves, pulsating in their troughs and crests, and this resonant "heartbeat" pumps prana into the body of the Earth.

The heartbeat of the planet vibrates everywhere; in Braj it is felt especially strongly. Coming from all directions, it passes through trees, animals, birds, people, and inanimate objects. Invisible, the great Heart continues to work in harmony with the rhythm of Creation, and the tiny motors within all living beings beat in unison with the heart of the Mother.

…Sacred places are territories where religions and beliefs interact with nature. Only human cultures can confer such statuses upon natural objects, for only faith rooted in legends that were once someone's reality has enduring meaning. All these spatial markers — from the significant sites of the cradle of Christian and Jewish faith and the holy places of Islam, down to the hills and rivers honored by aboriginal and primal tribes — reflect human sensitivity to events of the past, filtered by time and charged with the power of the earth.

Of course, the value of any shrine and the multitude of its meanings exists exclusively in the eyes of the believers. Human hearts always thirst for miracles; people are born to believe — and they are given temples of valleys and mountains beneath the domes of the open skies, where any heart may connect with the primal Source, the heart of the Mother. The whole Earth is an enormous temple, consecrated by the Void in which She floats. Perhaps the heart of the Earth shifts its position within the body of the planet too, depending on the course of events, its trajectory and destination?

Wherever it may be, the exact location of the planet's heart is unknown, and people are not destined to learn of it. Here, in Braj, the Govardhan Hill has been worshipped for many centuries, and there was no need for new hypotheses and legends. The Shakti Pitha of Braj remained invisible, continuing to be charged with the energy of millions of pilgrims. The grace of the chief Shakti, the Earth, was meant for everyone, and She continued to carry all Her children.

Devi of Braj

The dilapidated wooden pavilion above the cremation site of Raja Jaswant Singh was erected in 1893. Half-ruined, covered in flaking red paint, the structure stood in the midst of the deserted, scorched earth, guarded by a single palm tree. When he saw the pagoda-like structure for the first time, a sudden revelation flashed through him: "The Shivalingam will stand right here." There was no clarity about the origin of this idea, but in conversation with Devahuti he shared the thought that the pavilion would be turned into a temple. She listened in silence, asking nothing. She had her hands full with endless practical matters of setting up the new household.

The pujari, who had moved with them onto the smashana, had previously worked in metal but also understood building. They often sat on the upper level of the palace, discussing the reconstruction of the structure, and in this way considered many variants. He had initially planned to buy and install a Shivalingam (more precisely a Banalingam) from the sacred river Narmada. When the symbol of Shiva was found in their own "backyard," they took it as a sign that the time had come to refit the building.

The repairs were discussed and refined so many times that the work was done quickly and without trouble. But no one could then have foreseen the significance of the events to which the consecration of a Shiva temple at the place of the Raja's samadhi would give rise.

Setting aside his reflections on the nature of this thoroughly tantric ritual, he began performing puja to Shiva, fully immersed in the new daily routine. Perhaps his unawareness was a blessing. To outsiders all these events had no particular meaning, but he was sure: Shiva had come to the Smashana because of Shakti. The discovery of the Shivalingam pointed to His intent to remain here, and Her presence had always been felt; She was the true mistress of this land. From time immemorial various ancient feminine spirits (besides the souls of Krishna's wives cremated here) had inhabited Braj. Saturated with their mighty energies, the locality was rich in mysteries.

When Shakti came to Braj, Her presence filled the surroundings with intense vibrations, which made newcomers giddy and gave them a state akin to intoxication. The sensation of intoxication without the aid of any liquor confused and disoriented many, but those who often visited the smashana were well acquainted with the strange energies and looked forward to meeting them.

That this place was a Shakti Pitha he understood when proofs began to surface — literally. The signs of Shakti's presence were discovered on December 1, 1994, during routine earthworks, when the workmen began to come upon strange stone fragments of a peculiar configuration — exact copies of female reproductive organs, breasts and the birthing organ, yoni. On the stone breasts nipples appeared with reddish areolae, as though grown into the stone, and all these fragments were patently not made by human hand. They continued to be unearthed, and within a few weeks more than thirty pieces of different sizes and shapes had been collected. The stones were washed, cleaned of clay, and gathered in the upper room. Trying to understand the meaning of these finds, he consulted with archaeologists and reviewed a great deal of specialized literature, but could not decipher the hidden meanings. The answers were oblique, evasive; possible theories and explanations varied.

The workers themselves, however, showed no surprise: it is well known that in Braj the sacred stones of the Govardhan hill behave in the most unpredictable manner. Some stones are even considered to be holy hermits and sages performing thousand-year tapas in this disguised form, moving along and around the Hill. The legend-like phenomenon was well known here — stones did indeed move, some could be found meters (or kilometers) from the places where they had been marked. According to age-old tradition, people draw eyes upon them, apply orange sindur paste, and worship them… But the stone fragments found on the smashana had a quite different, undeniably "feminine" nature.

…Two months later (on January 27, 1995), the guard brought a strange, half-buried object found at the gate. An oblong oval stone about 30 centimeters long, it was surprisingly light. Yielding to an impulse, he dropped it on the stone floor, and the upper part broke off. A small bronze face peered out from the broken surface.

Convinced that this was not stone but fossilized wax mixed with sindur, Devahuti placed the rest of the object in a large pot and set it on a low flame. Soon the wax melted, revealing a small bronze statuette of a four-armed goddess seated upon a great cat, distantly resembling a tiger. The Devi held a sword, a cup, and a strange round object resembling a doughnut. The statuette was placed on a shelf in the upper room, and from that day the Goddess silently, though attentively, watched his nightly sadhana.

The statuette and the strange stone fragments were tokens of Her arrival, but the Goddess Herself did not intend to prove the fact of Her presence to anyone. She simply filled the whole locality with the intoxicating sweetness of a sharp sense of being.

So far as he could tell, all these deceptively chance events caused great upheavals in the astral spheres.

In October 1995 Hanuman spoke with him personally for the first time, and this happened because of Shakti's arrival. But he understood that only later. Testing and guiding him, the Deity explained the reasons for some of the astonishing events.

And then, two months later, Babaji himself appeared at the smashana. Of course the Mahavatar was well aware of past, present, and future events and, most likely, influenced many of them. That the companion of Babaji brought here the symbol of the Goddess, the Shri Yantra, was no accident at all.

…On January 4, 1996, while the pond was being drained, yet another stone fragment resembling a female reproductive organ was found. Imagination had no part in the discovery; there were too many fragments simply to wave aside, to dismiss as random natural formations. The Earth was no longer shyly presenting "evidence" of the Divine — She was proclaiming it with all the passion of feminine energy.

Soon the forked trunks of trees in the garden of the smashana began to "bleed"; from them oozed a dark, viscous, resin-like substance. The unusual phenomenon occurred in particular months, as if coinciding with the lunar cycles. Devahuti noticed it first and pointed it out to him. First stone breasts, then yonis, and now menstruating trees? That would be enough to set the locals making ambiguous jokes at his expense.

…Six months passed, and in June Mahadeva Himself stood in the darshan hall. The unimaginable event was piercingly real; he could still feel the burning touch of His sweat on his own skin. Sadhana had taught him that reality is divided into many channels, and he had never tried to confine himself to the limits of the mind, since precisely the breaking of limits meant going beyond the body.

The events that followed gave some answers, though the divine play unfolded slowly. Less than two years (a fraction of a second by the time of the gods) after that, the Shivalingam was found, which expanded his duties beyond mere teaching and personal practice. The daily worship of Shiva became the occasion for beginning communication with the subtle spheres; a new stage of his training began. It was then that it became clear: Adi Shakti Herself had called Her beloved here.

In 2016 a well-known sculptor made a large copy of the statuette found at the gate. The Devi still had no name, but it did not particularly matter: She personified the Sacred Feminine and required no credentials or documents.
