There was no doubt that the smashan was in the hands of Yamaraj, whose giant buffalo often came down to the cowshed to drink. Probably, the animal had nowhere to quench its thirst in the realm of the God of Death. Puzzled cowherds complained that the cistern of water was once again mysteriously emptied — to tell them the real reason would have been unwise. It was already hard enough to find people willing to work at a cremation site, and stories like that would have scared off the most desperate.

The Trial of the Spirits and
the Tamers of Phantoms

It was Hanuman who first called him "Yogiraj." Apparently it was thus that the Deity had introduced him to the world of spirits, since all phantoms knew him by that name. Soon after his first communion with Hanuman various astral beings began appearing at the smashan, and all addressed him as "Lord of Yoga," Yogiraj. Usually this honorific is given to an advanced yogi, but it is also the title of the first guru of yoga, Lord Shiva. Some of the spirits called him "lord," "master," or "malik" if they were of Muslim origin — these beings were older than Islam itself. In any case, the way one is introduced matters in any society.

In northern India Hanuman is worshipped in the form of a child (Bala), called Balaji. According to belief, He tames evil spirits and possesses enormous strength (bal), which no demon can match. Full of compassion, Balaji-Hanuman also relieves pain, suffering, and misfortune. It is said that people possessed by benevolent spirits (dutas) are servants of Balaji, his messengers. They (possessed or "normal") are known as bhagats.

…The medium sat on the floor with closed eyes. Then, rhythmically lifting his hands above his head, he spoke quietly: "Balaji has ordered me to lead a darbar,[footnote: Darbar — a term, in Urdu (from Persian), meaning "court"; it can be the council of a feudal state for the conduct of affairs, or a ceremonial assembly.] at which people will be freed from evil spirits…" Appointed bhagat, the medium was to diagnose the nature and cause of each case of possession, exorcise the evil spirits, and heal the victims. And there was a particular condition stipulated by Balaji himself.

"Yogiraj, Balaji has commanded that your photograph stand in the darbar hall. And your name must be chanted aloud by all present."

Having thought it over, he agreed, and soon a large photograph of a young, broad-shouldered yogi with a red bandana on his head, looking straight into the camera without a smile, became the main accessory of the exorcism sessions. He did not remember who had taken this snapshot and when, but it seemed to work. After a successful darbar people chanted his name: Yogiraj Maharaj, ki jai!!! praising him and the grounding, liberating power of healing. There was no need to attend the darbars in person. The photographs and the jakara — the chanting of his name at the end of the session — were quite enough. The power of the yogic lineage, manifesting through him, helped everyone without exception. He did everything in his power to fulfill the new duties, and the "Spirit Rehabilitation Program" was operating successfully from December 1996

onward. By summer, the number of those possessed by spirits and healed had approached a thousand, but no one bothered to count the astral "clients."

He recalled the words of the great yogi Gorakhnath: "Meri bhakti, Guru ki Shakti" — "My devotion is reinforced by the power of the Guru," and made these words his personal slogan for difficult decisions and swift action. The world was severe and realistic, human bodies were fragile, and all souls needed support at various levels. Personal devotion and the power of the higher Guru helped maintain a balance between people and spirits. The Prem Darbar took place regularly three times a year, giving help to all without any discrimination: those suffering from possession and the spirits who had been wronged by people.

Any soul, regardless of whether it dwelt in a physical or airy form, could receive protection and ask for justice. To catch a phantom was not difficult; there were many exorcists who specialized in apprehending astral offenders. However, the task of the darbars was not only to detain a malevolent spirit and extract it from the body of the possessed, but also to "rectify" it, to help it find peace. That was the most important part of the work. People were of course helped too, but the "rehabilitation" of spirits was no less important.

For him personally this period proved rather difficult, not because of phantoms but because of the living. Some relatives were still critical of his way of life, and their dismissive attitude hurt. But when dozens of people who had received relief from suffering through his yogic abilities chanted his name, the stream of human gratitude and devotion stood in sharp contrast to philistine gossip and the condemnation of relatives.

As for the medium, things did not work out for him. Suppressed desires often wait for an opportunity to break out, to slake the thirst for money, power, sex. And the astrologer's son had no immunity against temptations, which in the end corrupted and devoured him. Balaji withdrew His grace. Let us say the medium was dismissed. He suddenly left town, and the darbars stopped. Several years passed, and in March 2009 a new visitor appeared at the smashan. This medium had been holding small trials over spirits on his own for many years. Long ago he had been given a book with yogic commentaries on the Gita, and he had long wanted to meet the author, but had kept putting off the visit. Until one day, during a ritual, a most powerful gust of wind burst into the courtyard where the darbar was being held and threw open the Bhagavad Gita (which the bhagat always brought with him to the proceedings), as if the book were being leafed through by impatient hands. When the wind died down, the participants of the ritual said that they had seen the astral form of a yogi whom, as it turned out, they all knew as "Guruji." The next day the medium went to Govardhan.

Bowing respectfully, the bhagat asked his permission to use his name and photograph during the "court sessions." He warned him of the dangers of abuse of authority and of the traps of the ego. Anyone dealing with astral law and order had to be filled with humility and modesty; this was the first rule of survival. Phantoms sniffed out human weaknesses splendidly and turned them into erupting volcanoes of desires and temptations that incinerated the weak in spirit.

…European disciples of the master, Rajkumar Sharma, were often introduced as a ghost-hunter, and everyone laughed willingly. But the medium's duties were more than serious. Over the next ten years, using the name of "Yogiraj," Rajkumar successfully summoned offending inhabitants of the astral world and decided their fates. There were other gatherings that conducted exorcism, but it was this darbar that became known as the most powerful and effective, thanks to its link with the yogi from the royal smashan.

Young-looking and trim, always dapperly dressed, with an orange tilak on his forehead matching his henna-dyed hair, Rajkumar did not lose his sense of humor even in the most difficult séances. Screaming women with bulging eyes and protruding tongues, the curses and threats spewed by the possessed, were for him only familiar sounds of the lower worlds. People shook, writhed, and uttered gibberish, but for Rajkumar these were not signs of schizophrenia or psychic deviation but a side effect of possession. Negotiations would begin with the inhabiting spirit, and a polite and calm discussion of the reasons for the "occupation" would in the end incline most "criminal" phantoms to cooperation. Some were "arrested" and punished until they agreed to abide by the rules.

Society regards exorcism as an archaic form of psychotherapy, since possession is a well-known archetypal phenomenon. The term itself is rarely mentioned in medical literature. Western medicine calls possession "dissociative identity disorder" or "multiple personality disorder." Such cases are usually placed in the category of medical anthropology, but for people in need of healing, the precision of terminology does not matter. In remote villages, doctors cannot diagnose the most seriously ill patients; proper psychoanalysis and psychopharmacology are rarely available there. Modern psychotherapy is the secular treatment of metaphorical "demons": psychological traumas, painful memories. Exorcism takes the existence of demons literally. Manifesting themselves as mental deviations, "evil spirits" are for bhagats and shamans real entities greedy for bodily experience.

As Rajkumar's ability to control spirits grew, his awe before the power of the Guru grew with it, since he repeatedly witnessed phantoms who, having beheld the astral image of the yogi, submitted, instantly recognizing the penetrating power of the dweller of the smashan. In the name of Yogiraj any spirit or phantom could be summoned. The cases varied. Sometimes they were the inventions of housewives disillusioned with family life or brides unwilling to marry, while at other times intervention required extreme caution.

Together with two other helpers and his wife, Rajkumar came to Govardhan regularly. There was no need to report to the Guru about current affairs in person, but the bhagat wanted to see the man whose power was again and again confirmed by the testimony of the penitent spirits. And this gave the ghost-hunter strength, since his occupation was, after all, rather dangerous.

From time to time the darbar organizers sent "patients" to the famous temple of Mehandipur, where in one of the halls a stone image of Balaji-Hanuman had been installed, from whose chest sacred water flowed whose source was unknown. It was believed that water mixed with the ash from the never-ending fire rite cured possession. No one could remain near the altar of Balaji — neither in human nor in astral form — but people with sufficient sensitivity sometimes asked Rajkumar about someone's airy body that they had noticed near Balaji. Those who had attended the darbars before instantly recognized the familiar astral form of Yogiraj. He himself never commented on these stories; he simply listened attentively to Rajkumar, sometimes instructing the bhagat in private.

Life went on as usual; seasonal harvest festivals alternated with religious holidays, gossip about political and other scandals gave way to rumors of new cases of possession. The twentieth year of the twenty-first century was approaching, and many spirits reported that soon the problems of the astral world would worsen — and would affect the world of men. The interrogated phantoms did not specify what kind of problems were to be expected. Some muttered something incoherent about a new world order, but once or twice the spirits whispered a long-forgotten name — "Mahamari."[footnote: Mahamari — Sanskrit/Hindi for a great pestilence or pandemic.]

Mehandipur

Some foreign disciples simply ignored discussions of phantoms and spirits; the pragmatic mind brushed off everything that had not been confirmed by Google and Wikipedia, or had no connection with yoga. It was hard for them to digest snatches and fragments of stories about black magic and the implantation of entities. Yet after several personal experiences, skepticism gave way to bewilderment and confusion, for the powers they encountered had no analog. The mental database of the foreigners consisted of well-grounded logical conclusions and incontrovertible scientific facts, and only after a personal collision with the paranormal did some accept the existence of a parallel reality. The other "agnostics" simply tried to ignore the topic. It was part of the Guru's duties to direct students toward new discoveries; more than once he repeated: "If you walk the spiritual path, you need to know the world of spirits as well."

The phantom of a seven-year-old child who had died in a car accident in a faraway country many years before, invisible but still suffering and lost, quietly roamed the apartment until he was "locked" into a coconut and brought to India. After a particular ritual the boy's soul received liberation, comfort, and the necessary guidance, which neither the prayers of the long-departed grandmother nor the apartment's renovation had been able to provide. There were enough such cases, but they were never discussed publicly. As a Guru of yoga, he did not officially specialize in taming phantoms, although he was well acquainted with all the details of the process.

In February 2008 two Russian disciples asked his permission to visit the famous "temple of phantoms" in Mehandipur. Their perception of India rested on a thick mixture of yogic texts and legends about mystical sadhus and avatars; now they wanted to see the rites of exorcism, and he did not object. Every serious seeker needs to come into contact with another reality in order to get an idea of the infinity of life. Astral forces are just as real as illness, madness, and inexplicable life tragedies.

The famous psychoanalyst Carl Jung once remarked: "What you resist persists." Beliefs in demons and evil spirits, who once populated the landscapes of human cultures, are written off as "primitive" traditions. Modernity labels possession by spirits as mental deviation. And yet, dwelling on the periphery of the human unconscious, some spirits still gladly use the world of people as their hunting grounds.

In exchange for the loss of medicine men and sorcerers, the psyches of Western inhabitants are populated by archetypes recognizable only linguistically. And yet the suppressed energies of the unconscious have remained. Wild, indescribable in words. In India, the denial of inner darkness manifested itself in ways long forgotten in the Western hemisphere. And in certain particular places these energies could be localized, tamed, and even "healed." And Mehandipur was the fortress that guarded the world of the living from the invasion of spirits.

…Setting out at dawn, from the car window they watched the countryside awakening. Heavily laden camel carts floated past; women defying gravity passed by in bright saris with bundles of brushwood and pitchers on their heads. Children in clean school uniforms or picturesque rags marched independently along the dusty roadside desert; thin dogs trotted by busily.

The bare and rather grim landscape was broken by occasional sun-scorched trees, whose thorny trunks darkened against the background of the Aravalli hills. The road into the outskirts of Mehandipur was clogged with cars, rattling buses, and ox carts. A thick veil of dust on the narrow main street draped the mishmash of people, cars, and mopeds. Their taxi slowly approached the massive temple, set between two hills, built of smoke-blackened stone with streaks of rain damage and copiously sprinkled with bird droppings. Some windows had been bricked up, and it was unclear whether this had been done to keep spirits from getting in, or to prevent them from getting out.

They got out of the car and were immediately surrounded by ragged children who began plucking at their sleeves: "Give ten rupees, sir, hello, hello!"

Entering the temple, they squinted, adjusting to the dim light. The echo of distant voices bounced off the walls; a torn cacophony of sounds poured in from all sides at once. In a passageway lay a woman on her side; her eyes were bulging, her hips moved in a furious rhythm, her loosened hair slid along the stone floor like living serpents. A man kneeling beside her was comforting her, smoothing the tangled hair; when she tried to bite his arm, her white teeth flashed. Stunned by what they saw, they pressed themselves against the wall, giving way to an entering procession — several men were carrying a large plywood board to which a young man was tied, his head rolling and bouncing on its surface, while deafening cries echoed down the corridors.

…The woman cleaved the crowd like a knife, as though her surroundings did not matter; her movements were measured and precise. Climbing to the top of a high staircase, she lay down on her stomach and slid down the stone steps. Lightly and elastically gliding, she flowed downward as if boneless, slipping between people like an eel, and no

one tried to catch her. Standing up, the woman climbed the steps again, as though catching the next wave. As she passed by, a stray strand of long hair brushed the shoulder of one of them, sending an impulse of something dark and unknown. Whispering, "Mujhe chhor do!" ("Leave me alone!"), unaware of anything around her, she was caught in a looped, endless cycle of movement.

A bronze bell hanging from the low ceiling, blackened by ash, rang ceaselessly; pandits muttered mantras. In the dim, reddish light they stood before the altar of Balaji, and inside the enclosure attendants threw offerings into the fire. People's mouths were half-open as if they were trying to eat the sticky sweet smoke rising from the white-hot coals. A stone obelisk painted gold, with a monkey mouth beneath two black eyes, was the shrine — here Hanuman-Balaji appeared as an untamed, raw, unpredictable force.

The driver who accompanied them pointed at another hall: "Pret Raj Sarkar, Lord Hanuman's assistant, who punishes phantoms." They saw a large, irregular stone with piercing black eyes, whose flat surface was covered with silvery paint or foil and decorated with strange patterns of gold squares. Before the journey they had tried to read about the temple, but had been unable to find information about this deity. Pret Raj, the king of phantoms, was not mentioned in Wikipedia. The stone radiated a heavy, oppressive energy, and they quickly left the hall, trying not to step on the men and women sitting on the floor. Some were in trance, others in visible agitation and obviously not themselves — the place was definitely not a tourist spot. Rather, it was meant to awaken the sense of the supernatural. Here the healing from madness and despair took place, and this noble undertaking was at once dreadful and pitiful.

A thin man in a yellow-stained kurta squatted in a corner of the hall, surrounded by a group of several people. His head and shoulders shuddered, as though he were trying to shake off the clouds of surrounding smoke. One of the companions stroked his arm and spoke in a pleading tone, negotiating with the phantom that had taken hold of the emaciated body of the man.

Another stone peeped out from a heap of flower garlands, and black eyes were painted on it too. A small trident, smeared with sindur paste — the trishul of Shiva — had been set up beside it. The painted eyes stared unwaveringly at a round hole in the floor. Mahakal Bhairav, the "god of punishment," whose court was held here, had yet another strange title — Captain Kotwal. In a hissing whisper the driver tried to explain to them the origin of the deity's name, but he lacked the English words. All they could understand and surmise was that this aspect of Shiva is also the Kotwal, the general of Hanuman's army.

Meanwhile a group of men carried in a woman bound with chains, who was quickly suspended head down over the opening in the floor, and her loosened hair became a continuation of the darkness disappearing into the depths. The men began to pull and release the rope, as though dipping the woman's head into the abyss; she struck her forehead against the edge of the opening again and again, and her cries of anguish carried through the hall.

"That is her preta[footnote: Preta — a supernatural being, a hungry phantom. It is believed that pretas in their past lives were greedy, depraved, and cruel creatures.] speaking, not her — and he refuses to leave!" said the driver. The men seemed to be gently exhorting the spirit. At last, they lifted the woman high above the floor, and one of them took her into his arms, carefully rocking her. Her eyes had rolled back, she looked exhausted but peaceful.

In the courtyard they came upon several people chained to the temple wall. The space was enclosed by the rear façade on one side and an iron lattice fence on the other, and bore little difference from a prison. Finding themselves on an open terrace, they tried to catch their breath and recover from what they had seen. The floor was strewn with flower petals, dust, and brown lumps of crushed sweets. The respite was short. Nearby a middle-aged woman with a drawn-out howl collapsed to the ground, and two pandits laid her face down, pinning her ankles and shoulders to the floor. Another carefully placed a stone slab on her lower back and another stone on her buttocks, and began chanting softly. The woman's groans died down; her lips parted, her breathing grew hoarse but even.

They felt drained, exhausted; clothes clung to sweaty backs, their faces were damp with sweat. Clouds floated by, covering the sun, and the noon was full of shadows that shifted their outlines and meanings. From the terrace they could see vertical stone slabs on the hillside.

"Are those gravestones?"

"No, those are markers for abandoned spirits. Some buy such a stone, and then the preta must remain beneath it; it will no longer trouble these people," replied the driver, who looked notably tired and seemed irritated. The hillside was studded with these slabs, and ravens perched above the dwellings of the spirits, like phantoms in dark cloaks still looking out for elusive prey.

…They had been told to eat nothing and drink nothing in Mehandipur, and not to look back, and they followed all instructions. They returned to the car with a sense of relief; the whole long way back no one spoke a word. On returning, they went to the darshan hall to bow before the Guru. He did not seem impressed by their accounts; recounting the eerie atmosphere of the temple, the complete absence of any blessedness or peace so characteristic of Indian places of worship, both disciples agreed that the temple of Mehandipur resembled a huge transit point, a station where arriving spirits waited for the next carrier…

Suddenly the Guru suggested that one of them check the water level in the pond, right now. Surprised, he got up and went out, feeling broken and tired. Sitting on the stone steps by the pond, still warm from the sun, he felt himself losing consciousness, and at that very moment he felt a push below his left shoulder blade. Fighting nausea, he turned and saw a small dusty whirlwind from which a half-transparent figure of a boy emerged. The figure quickly scrambled up the steps and dashed for the gate. The disciple was too exhausted to chase him or to ask the guard why a child had been let onto the smashan. Returning to the darshan hall, he reported the incident to the Guru, who said: "Yes, you brought a visitor. Some spirits want to see me personally, and sometimes they are allowed to." He advised the disciple to return to the hotel and rest properly. Unable to think of anything in particular, with a buzzing head, the disciple lay sleepless for a long time, filled with an inexplicable sadness. In the end he fell into oblivion, but slept little and badly. In the morning he felt better, as though the unexpected melancholy that had briefly nested in his heart had flown back to Mehandipur, gliding above the misty winter fields of Braj.

Gaya Kshetra

The smashan offered unlimited opportunities to observe spirits, to learn about the stages of the "otherworldly" journey of the soul, and to grasp the laws of subsequent incarnations. Dangerous astral beings now bowed before a mortal yogi, recognizing the power that stood behind him. Hanuman had shared a particle of his power with him, and by 1998 they were on good terms. Contacts had become rather frequent; the tasks He gave were carried out successfully. For the most part these tasks were connected with the affairs of the world of phantoms; in some cases only a human being could resolve certain problems.

One day Hanuman appeared in the company of a silent spirit needing a liberation ritual. The phantom of a thirty-year-old beardless man was nothing remarkable in itself, but the case proved unusual. Once, this person had been the messiah of one of the largest world religions and had carried out a colossal mission of rethinking religious and cultural landscapes. His followers founded a whole institution of worship and then, with the help of a powerful ritual, bound all the sins committed by the flock to the name of the messiah. It came to be believed that sinners were automatically purified, and now only the prophet himself bore responsibility for their deeds. There was no end in sight to the suffering of this spirit; burdened by old and new crimes of mankind, he was stuck in the lower worlds.

The blurred facts of this messiah's life and the legends of his death continued to grease the wheels of various religious corporations, and that rotation was not supposed to stop. Liberation was out of the question; the spirit was a trademark feeding a ramified network of organizations, and his name was used for the consolidation of funds, property, power, and souls.

The body of the former messiah was not cremated but buried, with certain parts kept as relics. Though almost decayed, these tokens of his earthly existence prevented the spirit from going on to higher spheres. As long as the remains were preserved, he was doomed to remain bound to the earth. Moreover, he was "immobilized" by the heavy karmas of millions of the faithful, the majority of whom believed in Him not out of purity of heart but out of inertia, by inherited tradition. During his short life this person had done only good, and yet he suffered even after death, unable to shake off the bonds of "obligations." The love and devotion of the masses can be wearying, especially if your name is constantly used as a cure-all. It is a heavy burden and an excruciating existence.

The pale spirit gazed at him impassively and yet expectantly, and out of astonishment he could not speak for some time. But in the company of gods and spirits no conversation is required, since any thought is expressed mentally.

Who would believe that Hanuman is engaged in "post-mortem affairs" of spirits of other faiths? And then he understood — there, in the Light, there are no distinctions of rank or creed. He agreed to fulfill the request; could one refuse such beings? Suppressing his astonishment, he merely inquired about the technical details and received instructions, together with the exact time and place for the rituals, and warnings about the danger of such intervention. Akin to walking a tightrope between life and death, these rites required enormous concentration of attention and physical strength; lifting the spells laid upon him could turn out to be lethal, and the observance of certain precautions was mandatory. In August 1999, together with the disciple and friend Mr. Javalekar, who organized all the logistics, they set out for Bihar.

…The city of Gaya is surrounded by three well-known hills: Brahmayoni, Pret-shila, and Ram-shila, and some of them had to be visited. According to numerous legends, the Pancha Koshi Gaya Kshetra is considered the most holy place on earth — here the body of the demon Gayasura turned into the rocky hills that formed the landscape, which was believed to possess the power of purification. For millennia people had been coming here to perform rituals for the deceased, since the performance of the rite of shraddha was meant to dispel

the sins of the departed and grant their souls liberation and peace. In Gaya there live hereditary Brahmins belonging to the Gayawal clan, supposedly created by Brahma to assist in the fire sacrifice after the destruction of the demon Gayasura. The Creator made fourteen clans, or gotras, specifically to carry out the rites, and about a hundred families of these Brahmins still live in southern Gaya, on the banks of the Phalgu[footnote: Phalgu (Falgu), the river — the river that deceived none other than Rama's wife, Sita. During her exile, Rama and Sita decided to rest on the sandy banks of the river. When the time came for the offering of food to the ancestors, Rama went for provisions, and Sita remained to wait. Suddenly the spirit of Rama's father appeared and asked that the ceremony be performed without delay. Instead of waiting for the offerings, Sita offered him sand. Fearing that her husband would not believe her, she asked the sacrificial fire, a cow, the river, a passing Brahmin, and a tree to be witnesses to the ritual. When Rama returned and questioned the witnesses, they all lied, hoping for repeated offerings. All except the tree said that Sita was lying. The angered goddess cursed them all, except the tree. Thus the river was doomed to run underground, hiding in shame. To this day, for most of the year, the river shrinks to the size of a brook wandering across a wide sandy bed.] river and around the Vishnu temple. A branch of the Gayawal clan, known as the Dhami, is still responsible for the rituals performed in the five sacred sites of Gaya.

They contacted one of the Dhami pandits, and on 19 August, from early morning until late evening, performed the rituals. That day he had to fast and allowed himself only occasional sips of water.

About 8 kilometers northwest of Gaya, on the hill of Pret-shila, lies the place for the rite of repose of the souls of the departed, pind dana. The temple of the Lord of Spirits, Preta Raj, is on top of a steep hill; six hundred steps lead up to it. He insisted on the ascent, and the pandit accompanying them, who had never been to the top of the hill, agreed. The place turned out to be deserted, as the temple of Preta Raj is visited only for rituals for the deceased. The "Garuda Purana" says that the souls of ancestors come to Gaya Dhama to receive offerings from the clefts of the hill Pret-shila. Many people believe that spirits pass through the rocks, moving from one world to another, and their movements create strange whispering sounds. Shadows appear where they should not be, and evidence of the presence of spirits is best not sought at all, especially after sunset.

A large boulder, named in honor of the daughter of the Lord of Death, marked the place for the offerings. They walked around it, scattering a little flour made of various grains. The place was eerie. At noon there were only four people at the temple; a dark-skinned middle-aged man squatting against the wall stared at them intently and unwaveringly. As soon as the ritual began, a whistling sound of a strong wind rang out, resembling distant singing, as though a chorus of spirits were reciting some indistinct mantras. The air did not move, but the sound continued until the rite was over.

On a boulder nearby was another famous landmark — the footprint of Lord Brahma — but they only briefly glanced at the hollow in the stone; they had to hurry down the slope, to be in time for several more rites.

One of the rituals turned out to be quite peaceful, another extremely exhausting. At the well of the dead, Pret Bhut Baori, he had to use iron and fire while remaining in continuous concentration — a tiring and strange process. At the end, the coconut used as a vessel for invoking the spirit had to be thrown into the well, and the pandit had told him in advance not to look back, since the ritual would have to be started again. The well had something like a lid, opening from one side, and he simply pushed the coconut through the slit — and a desperate weeping, reflected by the walls of the well, pierced his ears with a dull, furious force. Hurrying away, he struggled against the overwhelming desire to look back, and the sound followed him for a long time.

By now they were all rather tired; the last stop awaited them on the other bank of the river Phalgu, at Sita Kund, where Sita Devi had once performed a memorial rite for her father-in-law. There they had to make an offering, bringing to the Goddess the fruit sitaphal, named after Sita herself. When it was all over, he stood on the steps leading from a small mandir to the dry riverbed, contemplating the irony of the divine play. To become truly free, astral beings had to come down to Earth and entrust themselves to mortals. In places such as Gaya Kshetra, the ancient forces were mighty and palpable everywhere, but truly knew about them only a few.

In the pandit's house an abundant supper was laid out for them: mountains of rice, towers of flatbread, bowls of dal, curry, various sweets. He stared in astonishment at the preparations for the feast.

"Who is going to eat all this?"

The pandit smiled: "If you can't, you'll leave it…" They began to eat and could not stop. He finished every crumb of forty chapatis, as if someone else were swallowing along with him… or through him.

After supper he had to fill out a special certificate in which the name of the deceased had to be entered, as each puja of this kind was registered in the archives. Reading the name, the priest was visibly shaken, but then he shared with them an unusual story. One Indian businessman from Germany had once come to Gaya to perform a similar ritual — none other than Hitler had begun insistently appearing in his dreams. The Führer demanded that a ritual of liberation be performed for him. Worn out by sleeplessness, the businessman set off for Bihar, where he commissioned a similar puja for the former leader of the Third Reich. The story sounded wild, but had they not just performed a rite for the spirit of one even more famous than Hitler?

Powerful phantoms could communicate their wishes directly, but they entered into contact with sorcerers or mediums of the Western hemisphere rather rarely. They preferred to carry out what was needed in India, where the efficacy of the ancient rites was still effective. That is precisely why spirits of all ranks kept coming to the smashan, to the yogi who had passed through many death-samadhis, who possessed mind, breath, heart, and impulses. Familiar with the transition from the world of the living into the abyss of nothingness, he could withstand the furious onslaught of the astral entities and forces that opposed him there.

…At the beginning of the trip, on arrival at the railway station of Gaya, he had stepped on the scales out of sheer curiosity. Before leaving, he decided to do the same. It turned out that in a single day he had lost 7.5 kilograms. It took quite a long time not only to regain the weight but also to recover from the mental aftereffects of the intense rites. Even years later he felt that the sacraments performed still exerted a certain effect on him, though he did not regret what had been done. He was, rather, glad that he had at least tried to help the soul of a person who had come to change people's hearts and along the way had changed the history of mankind. Now this spirit was liberated from all obligations.

To foresee the outcome of the ritual performed, or to explain its denouement logically, was impossible, and no one informed him of the results. He had simply solved the problem, that was all. To analyze this extraordinary case is in order, but he could not arrive at a conclusion about the long-term consequences of the ritual, although certain guesses he had.

As the world beyond the gates of the smashan continued to seethe, so did the fermentation of collective and individual karmas accelerate, for there were no longer prophets onto whom collective and personal sins could be offloaded. Or they kept silent. He himself preferred not to call the coming changes "Judgment Day," but to view them as a gradual transition to the "Era of Earthlings." As the awakening of the human mind accelerated, compassion was to gain the upper hand over cruelty, and personal responsibility was to grow in geometrical progression to the ambitions of people. This was by no means an idealistic utopia, but rather the law of future survival.

Brahmarakshasa

In 2006 the smashan was visited by a modest schoolteacher named Umashankar Sharma, a reserved Brahmin from Kanpur, who had somehow begun to commune with a particularly powerful astral being — and had become his medium. From that time the favor of the teacher was sought by many businessmen and politicians who looked for luck in investments or guarantees of victory in elections. Becoming famous, Umashankar remained inconspicuous and modest, and channeled generous donations toward the construction of a temple of Hanuman. He came to the smashan at the bidding of his astral "friend," to introduce himself.

The spirit spoke up as soon as Umashankar entered the darshan hall. The astral being turned out to be a Brahmarakshasa, the powerful phantom of a Brahmin who in his previous life had been called Chote Maharaj (young master). "Brahma" means a Brahmin, and Rakshasa is a demon. The phantoms of Brahmins are extraordinarily powerful; to fight them and to drive them out is hard, since they have knowledge of their previous lives and remember all the Vedic texts and the complex rituals. Some who were initiated into powerful mantras during life retain subtle ties with deities after death, and there is nothing more dangerous than an educated demon.

The story of the phantom was extraordinary. A Brahmin priest, Chote Maharaj lived in a remote village, looking after an ancient temple of Hanuman. Being devoted to the deity, he renounced family life and devoted all his time to the upkeep of the temple. An educated and very devout pandit, he did not take sannyasa but simply remained an unmarried brahmachari priest, following the strict discipline handed down by his guru. He was a very pious man.

The temple adjoined a vast tract of agricultural land, and it was precisely that land that became the cause of the priest's premature death. Several times influential landowners — the thakurs — tried to take possession of the land, but the pandit was unyielding. One day after the festival of Hanuman, he was returning alone from the temple home, to the edge of the village. Several bandits hired by the thakurs ambushed and killed him. The pandit had only twenty rupees in his pockets, but the killers did not take that money, since they had been generously rewarded by their employers for "services." After the death of Chote Maharaj the thakurs seized the temple land, but they did not enjoy the spoils for long.

The murdered priest became a powerful and vengeful spirit. Standing before the Lord of Death, Yamaraj, every Brahmin becomes a brahmapreta, a phantom; this is the usual result of the post-mortem "verdict." But if a Brahmin who had observed the strictest religious and spiritual discipline (as Chote Maharaj had) dies a violent death, as a result of premeditated murder,

He was able to make out figures on the embankment. Ghosts did not particularly impress him—he was even and polite. The initial excitement experienced during his investigations of the "other world" had worn off. As the Doms¹ used to say: "One should fear the living, not the dead."

The ancient brahmin ghosts introduced themselves as Bhola Shankar Tewari and Jata Shankar Pandey; both were devotees of Shiva. The contact was mental, yet the visitors, woven from condensed air, radiated the power of intention and resolve. Although their forms were vague, the ghosts proved quite straightforward and declared that he would lead them to a place of liberation. This was rather strange, as spirits of this category had no need for "carriers" and could move about on their own, of their own volition. But he did not object. If they wished to accompany him, they must have sufficiently serious reasons for doing so.

…Six years passed. Gradually, he understood that both brahmapreta had been delegated from above—to follow him, observe, and help. Silent, invisible, they helped resolve both astral troubles and quite earthly problems, one only had to ask. Commanding the ghosts directly was unwise, but like obedient djinns, they read mental signals. He even introduced them to Devahuti, and she availed herself of their help several times, to the horror of those who wronged her.

In February 1993, in Sarnath, Bhola Shankar Tewari and Jata Shankar Pandey suddenly and categorically declared that he must return to Braj: "You must return, we will follow you. Our liberation is there and with you, and you must be in Braj." This stunned him. He had just moved to Sarnath from Vrindavan and had no plans to go back. However, after two months, Devahuti brought news of an incredible discovery: the palaces and the royal shamshan were for sale, the place was waiting for them. The brahmarakshasas, like the other 16,000 spirits that had followed him all these years, were happy. They were all returning home; a new stage was beginning.

…These two ghosts clearly knew the future, for they had decided to link their paths with his destiny. Somehow they had foreseen that he was destined to find the Shivalingam—and were present at the installation of the shrine. Perhaps their duty was to ensure his safety… Soon after the pujas became regular, the brahmarakshasas disappeared. Possibly, they were released from the obligations placed upon them and became servants of Shiva, Shiva Ganas, having moved up the career ladder. In any case, in memory of the "collaboration," the large brown bull rescued by Devahuti was named Bhola Shankar Tewari. Neither the bull nor its astral namesake objected.

**The Saddest of Spirits**

In Gwalior, he was friends with a photographer, even before the latter became his disciple. Whenever he was nearby, he would drop in on his friend for tea, and they would have interesting conversations in the tiny studio cluttered with portraits of newlyweds, smiling babies, and elderly couples whose faces showed no enthusiasm but were still full of dignity. The friend's business was not particularly thriving; there was plenty of time. A teenager, a sad and withdrawn boy, came to the studio owner to learn photography. Occasionally, he would speak with the lad, and gradually they became acquainted. It was a casual, fleeting crossing on the numerous paths of fate.

…Several years passed after he left Gwalior; in 1984, his father passed away, and the Guru instructed him to perform the proper rituals for the deceased according to Hindu tradition. During the tarpan² rite, he suddenly saw a ghost. The appearance of another soul during the posthumous ritual for his father was a complete surprise. He could barely recall the youth from his friend's photo studio, struggling to recognize him in the ghost of a young man of about twenty. He looked heartbreakingly sad and begged for help. The young man (now forever young, but no longer a man) told his story, and it was both depressing and frightening.

Despite being a desired son in an Indian family with four sisters, he was not loved. Moreover, for his mother, he became an object of smoldering hatred. From early childhood, his mother mercilessly found fault with him; he constantly and unsuccessfully tried to please her. The mother was a battle-axe, a stinging wasp, a biting dog; pursuing him day after day, she tormented him with the inventiveness of a professional torturer. When the boy grew up, money became the subject of her attacks and displeasure. A couple of times, he had to sell his blood for money to temporarily quench the flame of his mother's endless reproaches. He was never good enough for her, and she managed to convince him that the rest of the world also considered him useless and unworthy. The youth tried to somehow endure all this, and at some point decided to become a photographer. He refused to believe that such an amazingly beautiful world could reject its children. Yes, some mothers could do that, but there must still be a place for him in the world. He believed in the harmony of light and shadow, in the magic of colors and forms, in landscapes and textures, the hidden logic of intricate things, and the complexity of beings that only pretended to be simple. Enjoying photographing children, the youth tried not to photograph mothers. Smiling, dressed in bright saris, glowing with love and tenderly kissing their offspring, they were too incomprehensible to him; it seemed to him they had another, dark and unfathomable side. The roots of the supposed invisible darkness were unclear to him, yet he suspected they grew in all mothers.

One day, he accidentally uncovered the secret of the woman he called mother, and this secret was so shocking that the youth could not survive it. He learned that he was not his father's son: his mother had fallen in love with some rich man who refused to marry her, and she conceived a plan to take revenge on her lover in a perverted and disgusting way. She deliberately became pregnant, bore the child of the man who had rejected her, and poured all her rage onto him, tormenting her son in every possible way. The human mind is incredibly complex, but the dark despair of wounded female vanity is one of the most dangerous poisons, whose strength only intensifies while remaining in the vessel of a broken heart. To deliberately bear a child from a man you hate and then torment this "child of hate" requires a special, almost demonic resolve. It is known that victims of rape sometimes direct their hatred towards children born as a result of violence, but the birth of this boy was planned. It initiated a long vendetta against the lineage, soul, and well-being of his father. All this sounded wild, yet no one was supposed to know about it. The victim of this cruel and senseless revenge was a small, helpless copy of the offender; in her bitterness and hatred, the mother refused to listen to the whisper of the heart reminding her: "He is also your flesh and blood." Alas, "hell has no fury like a woman scorned³." This mother made her son pay for the neglect of a father who did not even know he had a son. Upon learning the truth, the young man committed suicide to silence the pain in his heart. Only it did not disappear even after the physical body ceased to exist. Samskaras, or mental impressions, continue to live in any consciousness. Stuck between worlds, the sad ghost could not forget the pain and could not move on towards the light.

Having heard the story, he did everything possible for the ghost who had never known a mother's love, who had been unable to trust the light of his own soul to break free from the nightmare of the past. He ensured that the spirit of the young man finally found peace.

**Nirmala Devi**

One night, he had a strange dream—an elderly woman in a white Bengali sari with a red border and reading glasses was flying towards him. Landing on the low canopy over the entrance to a cottage, she jumped to the ground, deftly catching her balance. "My name is Nirmala Devi, and I want to tell you something important. Please visit me in Mitani." As if through a tunnel, he saw the street and a very old house where she lived. These words—Nirmala Devi and Mitani—stuck in his memory. He had no intention of going in search of the woman from the dream, but at some point, this story surfaced again. In 1994, a disciple from Bengal, Madan Mohan Babu, casually mentioned that he knew the village of Mitani, located near Asansol, and he asked him to inquire there about Nirmala Devi. He carried out the errand.

However, upon hearing her name, the locals would change their expressions and quickly fall silent. Still, Madan Mohan Babu managed to obtain Nirmala's address and went to her. The house was guarded, but from a distance, he saw a woman in a white sari with a red border in the courtyard. Then two extremely unfriendly men approached him, warning him that by order of her husband, no one was allowed to speak with this woman. It turned out she was the wife of a local mafia boss. Upon learning that someone was asking about his wife, the wealthy landowner sent a couple of tough guys after the stranger. Fortunately, the disciple managed to explain that his guru had asked him to find Nirmala, and the situation became less tense. Then these people told him something curious.

…At the age of fourteen, Nirmala Devi was married off to a man twice her age. When they first found themselves in the bedroom, the husband asked: "Whom do you love and respect more than me?" She was still innocent and pure-hearted as a child, and therefore answered sincerely: "My Guru."

"Very well. Continue practicing what your Guru taught you; I will provide you with a house and everything necessary, but we will never live together as husband and wife. I will marry another woman, and you will continue to perform the sadhana given to you."

Was this a curse or a blessing? To remain alone, childless, and isolated from the world, to continue reciting the mantra and watch days turn into months and years… Cruelty can be long and elaborate; some customs are merely justifications for cruelty, and wounded pride can degenerate into sophisticated mockery of purity and innocence. Nirmala Devi spent her entire life in isolation, reciting the mantra of Chinnamasta⁴.

¹ Doms – a low-caste community exclusively engaged in the cremation of the dead. They were once high-caste Brahmins but lost the favor of the gods. As legend has it, while Sati's body was engulfed in flames, her earring fell to the ground, and one of the Brahmins picked up the jewel to keep it for himself. An enraged Shiva cursed his entire lineage, making the Doms the lowest of the untouchables. They begged for mercy, and Shiva assigned them to watch over the eternal fire used for cremation. The Doms are the only ones who have the right to watch over the eternal fire, touch corpses, or adjust them during cremation. Members of this caste are fairly well-off, but being untouchables, they can very rarely find alternative means of livelihood.

² Tarpan – a ritual performed for ancestors.

³ "Hell has no fury like a woman scorned" – the phrase first appeared as "Heaven has no rage like love to hatred turned, Nor hell a fury like a woman scorned" in the 1697 play *The Mourning Bride* by William Congreve. The play is written in the grammar of early modern English.

⁴ Chinnamasta – one of the ten Tantric goddesses, or Mahavidyas; having beheaded herself, the Mother Goddess usually stands or sits upon a divine couple.

which her Guru had given her. Who this teacher was, no one ever learned; it is only known that he was a sannyasi. Why did he give the girl the mantra of a wrathful Goddess? Perhaps Chinnamasta was Nirmala's family kuladevata, the deity of their clan. In Shaktism, the Goddess is worshipped in the Kali Kula, but temples and public worship of Her are rare. Chinnamasta herself is both the sacrificer and the sacrifice, and the one who receives the sacrifice; Her severed head is an offering to Herself. This paradox symbolizes the cycle of creation, destruction, and recreation. Ahead of Nirmala Devi lay years for comprehending the deepest symbolism and metaphor of the image of her Deity—through her own austerity.

Her husband appointed guards over her; she was forbidden to leave the house, and no one could visit her. She remained a virgin. For nearly sixty years, this woman lived in total isolation, obeying the will of her husband, who, even as he aged and grew frail, remained relentless. She never asked for her freedom back and never regretted the words she had spoken on that fateful wedding night. A year after the student's visit to Mitani, Nirmala Devi died.

holding her severed head in one hand and a sword in the other. Three streams of blood flow from her neck, and her two attendants, as well as her own severed head, drink it. A Goddess of contradictions, Chinnamasta grants life and takes it away; She embodies death, the transient essence of all things, and destruction, as well as life, immortality, and recreation. The Goddess, herself both the eater and the food, symbolizes the entire world through this act of devouring and the devourer; considered cruel, she can be merciful to the devotee.

In the autumn of 1991, around the same time that Nirmala visited him in the astral body, he began to experience repeated series of samadhi, which soon became ordered and regular. It seems that the power of Nirmala's tapas, amplified by the harsh circumstances of her destiny, helped her develop supernatural abilities—and she sensed his presence on the astral plane. And she was able to find him, because she recognized in him her next Guru. Perhaps the fact that Nirmala was able to contact him while still alive was the blessing of the goddess Chinnamasta. Although he himself did not reach Mitani, his student found the ascetic, and her request was deciphered: knowing that the end was near, Nirmala Devi asked him to take care of her soul. And also for initiation into Kriya Yoga. It is quite possible that she learned of the lineage of Lahiri Mahasaya and the masters of the parampara in her meditations. In any case, he found her and took care of her.

Several years passed. One day, a childless couple from Bengal was visiting him, and he suddenly said: "You must adopt a child. When you visit the orphanage, the first girl who smiles at you and tries to catch your hands will become your daughter." He could not say why he gave them these very specific details—apparently, it was time for Nirmala Devi to return to this world and live a new life, full of warmth, love, and opportunities. At the orphanage, the future parents were shown a seven-month-old baby girl. Bestowing upon them a toothless smile, the girl grasped their fingers with such determination that the couple immediately understood—this was their daughter.

…The beginning was excellent, and to this day everything goes well: the girl grew up and graduated from the Calcutta Academy, where she studied cinematography and photography. Basking in the love and adoration of her parents, she is extraordinarily talented, and her creative work is supported by the spiritual strength so painstakingly earned in her past incarnation. Having received initiation into Kriya Yoga, she practices sincerely and regularly. Her adoptive parents know the story of her past life and are glad that their daughter was and remains a highly developed soul. Once or twice a year, the family visits Govardhan, and the Guru observes the girl's progress. Even if she does remember her past life, she keeps the details to herself. The influence of past karmas is unknown, yet the girl must surely possess special abilities. The grace of the goddess Chinnamasta abides with her, and for her new journey, she has a reliable Guide.

**Mother**

On January 2, 1999, at around 4:30 PM, at the age of 82, Shrimati Gyani Devi passed away suddenly, but peacefully. She had no known illnesses; mother simply left her body, thereby precluding all possible objections and entreaties. Her time had simply run out; nevertheless, the news shocked him. When he reached Gwalior the next day, January 3, hundreds of students, relatives, and friends gathered for the cremation. Mother's body, wrapped in white cloth, was placed on bamboo funeral bier, an *arthi*, and carried to the *smashan*. Perhaps for the first time in Gwalior's history, instead of the traditional *Ram Nam Satya Hai*, people praised Shiva and the Lord of Govardhan Hill: "*Har Har, Mahadev, Shambo Jai Giriraj Ji Manasi Gange*," in honor of the Guru's mother.

People continued to place white shawls on the bier as a mark of respect, and by the end of the journey, more than 200 ceremonial scarves weighed more than his mother's body. Without a doubt, Gyani Devi's final departure was impressive. The cremation was the final *samskara*, the closing of the life cycle, and the "last sacrifice" or funeral rite was organized beautifully. And yet he could not shake the feeling that she would have been irritated by all this fuss and yet simultaneously satisfied with the attention shown. She was accustomed to always being the center of attention.

Deep sorrow slowly settled within him, seeping through the thickness of the initial numbness. His mother's death meant the end of the connection with the woman who had brought him into this world. This connection could not be broken even by death, for the elixir of love poured by a mother into her child's bloodstream flows forever, sealed by the earliest memories. First reactions and impressions, body language, and even food preferences reflect tiny bits and pieces of mothers. We are ourselves, but we belong to them just as they belong to us, as long as we or they are alive. This primal, ancient possessiveness makes the loss as sharp as a sting, regardless of the thickness of our skin.

…Gyani Devi Sharma was the eldest child, followed by five younger brothers, and she commanded her small troop with unyielding confidence. As a mother, she knew how to manage both children and adults. All her maternal uncles were successful: one became a colonel, another an IES officer, a third was a railway station master. Another became a renowned Ayurvedic doctor; there was also an uncle who was an official in the archaeological department of the state of Madhya Pradesh. They were all good people, and relations with the maternal side of the clan remained cordial and warm for many years. The daughter of a colonel in the Gwalior army, the daughter-in-law of a colonel in the Jodhpur army, and the wife of a respectable judge, she never demanded respect for herself; respect came naturally, for she was one of the strongest women he knew.

Married at the age of 16, she lived for some time with her young husband in the ancestral village of Shivram-ki-Garhi on the banks of the Yamuna, until her spouse obtained work in Gwalior. In those days, covering the face with a veil for married women was a mandatory tradition, even in Brahmin households, even in the absence of strangers. But she cast aside the veil and never covered her face, and the other young wives of the clan followed her example. Even today, this sounds like a feat, but back then it was open rebellion, symbolizing the impending changes, emancipation, and all that which outraged the patriarchy of the time.

Gyani Devi's horoscope showed an exalted ascendant Mars in the first house, which influenced her character, driving her towards bold actions and decisions. The red planet was so strong in her chart that dominating Gyani Devi was impossible, and no one ever tried. Always self-confident, she feared nothing and no one and possessed a keen sense of justice. If someone complained of injustice, she could do everything possible for the triumph of truth, leaving it no choice but to prevail. Protective of everyone and everything, invariably victorious in her rightness, she was the chief authority among the cousins, aunts, and sisters-in-law of their clan. No one contradicted her; she led, allowing others to follow. That was her style. No one could ever predict the onset of a storm or a sudden and generous flow of her sympathy; these processes were always unpredictable. But these were not mood swings, but a deeply intuitive wisdom of the heart.

A skilled horsewoman, she also drove a car; in those days, a lady behind the wheel was exotic, a rarity. Already the mother of four children, Gyani Devi entered college and earned a degree in Ayurveda—and all of the above would have been enough to call her an extraordinary woman. In addition to all this, she possessed a refined sense of humor and was an excellent mimic, capable of imitating anyone. Oh, how she could entertain!

When her sixth and youngest son became a Guru and initiated his first students into Kriya, Gyani Devi insisted: "You must teach me too, for I am your mother." Well-read, she reminded him of the story of Maria Letizia Bonaparte, the mother of Napoleon. At his coronation, Madame Bonaparte arrived at court but did not curtsy to the entering emperor. Napoleon said to her: "I am the Emperor, Madame!" Letizia replied: "And I am still your mother!"

…He was always proud of her. His mother's practice was exceptionally regular; she was the mistress of her given word and kept her vows. Kriya perfected her explosive character, sharpening some traits and softening others. Her remarkable memory allowed her to recite the Gita flawlessly: having learned all the chapters, she could quote any *shloka*. After lunch, during siesta, he would watch as for several hours his mother recited *shlokas*, counting them on the folds of her finger segments…

By tradition, she spent her last years living in the family of her eldest son, and it was a comfortable and dignified life. She never visited Govardhan, and she never came to the *smashan*: they preferred not to discuss their relationship, valuing confidentiality, and the opinions of others did not trouble them. In many ways, his cousin Devahuti resembled her, possessing the same ardent and loving heart. Although they belonged to different branches of the clan, these women had more in common than either would have cared to admit. Strong feminine energy always played a decisive, supportive role in many of his endeavors.

In India, it is believed that the mother is the first guru, and Gyani Devi used her authority in many matters, which often caused contradictions and conflicts. Yes, they argued a lot. Such was her character, fueled by Mars and forged by generations of ancestors, warriors and Brahmins. But even when their opinions diverged, he treated his mother's position with respect. He inherited from her not only remarkable physical strength but also an iron will. The inflexibility of his mother's character was imprinted upon his own, in anything but a softened version. He was grateful to her for everything.

…The rite was over. After several long hours, the students led him to another house, where an endless stream of people wishing to pay homage to him again began to flow. He longed for the solitude and silence of the *smashan*, where his mother's spirit might finally manifest. Perhaps now she would not mind paying him a visit.

**The Guru's Power and a Curious Case of Sex Change**

Every time disciples found themselves in dangerous situations, something incredible, almost supernatural, would occur. Some claimed they had seen the Guru or communicated with him while in a coma, in visions, in a state of extreme stress, or in the face of grave danger. In these visions, he was often clad in red robes, silent and serious, but at times he could also be in a humorous mood. One person recounted that during clinical death, Guruji scolded him with the choicest of words, literally kicking and shoving him back into his body. After such an event, the rescued individuals would develop positive tendencies, a turn towards recovery would begin; in short, help arrived in a timely manner. Many considered the positive outcome a miracle, often contradicting the laws of physics, medical facts, or general logic. The survivors would tell their stories, marveling at the second chance given to them, feeling purified by their brush with death.

As a rule, he knew when he was being asked for help: the mind would suddenly shift, and as if in a trance, he would see fragmented frames of what was happening, the situation itself, and the person calling out to him. And then it would happen – a mental concentration, created by an unyielding will, generated a certain ray of force, directed towards the one in need. It was impossible to classify this force, but its nature was rooted in personal resolve, in the Guru's duty. But most of all, this force was unconditional love. Love for the spirit that had a chance to continue learning, evolving, achieving its goal in this particular life, in this body. Love for everything that had a chance and a right to life.

Meeting someone for the first time, he would see the essence of the soul, its unique nature and, at times, its mission in this life. Although not everyone had a mission. Some took incarnation to rest from previous lives; such souls would live out their new birth in a state of hibernation, unwilling to wake up, and no one disturbed them. Enlightenment was a hard-won privilege, not a mandatory course.

The Guru's help and blessing were a tribute to ancient beings – souls inhabiting temporary bodies. To support the existence of the spirit, to help it prosper and fulfill the tasks set before it, was an acknowledgment of the difficult and long path the soul had already traversed, a reward for the efforts and pain of lives lived. With each story of miraculous rescue, his conviction grew that the mind seemed to receive signals sent by external forces. The Naths called this ability the power of the Guru, and it seemed the parampara sustained this most potent energy. The merits accumulated by previous Teachers created a powerful field, a sphere of probability for changing destinies, and the successor was a transmitter of their abilities. The cases of help varied: lives saved, heartbeats restored, sight regained, miraculous escapes from fatal incidents. But there was one occurrence that seemed to stand out; he felt that Shakti herself had winked at him playfully, Nature had whispered a mantra, and he had heard its echo.

In Gwalior, there lived a modest family; the husband was a refrigeration mechanic, his wife had just given birth to their first child, and the young parents were happy. For only a few minutes, until the doctor said that the infant was a hermaphrodite, whose genitals were neither male nor female. The news was sad; in India, a third-gender child is considered a disgrace to the family. If the Hijras, the community of transgender individuals and eunuchs, learn of such a birth, they try to take the child. Through persuasion or force, they persistently get their way, and the family eventually gives in. Life in the closed community of the Hijras severs all family ties, and such a child has no hope of a normal life.

Officially recognized as a third gender, Hijras are neither men nor women. They are often called Aruvani, Jogappa, or even Chhakka (which is a rather derogatory term); they themselves prefer to be called 'Kinnaras', magical, mythological beings skilled in song and dance. Since ancient times, the history of the Hijras has been described in various sacred texts, including the Kama Sutra, but this fact did not ease their daily life in any way. Despite the slow conquest of some freedoms and recognition, their communities still face severe stigmatization. Most Hijras perform at weddings and work in the sex industry. Speaking a secret language, Hijra-Farsi, derived from Persian and Hindustani, they follow their own Gurus and perform special rituals, worshipping the goddess Bahuchara, or Bala Tripura, usually depicted sitting on a rooster, which symbolizes innocence. When Devaki gave birth to Krishna, and he was swapped with Yashoda's daughter, that daughter was Bahuchara herself…

There is a version of the goddess's origin that she is one of the Shaktis in the Shri Chakra, and her true vahana is a snake with two mouths. The goddess sits on one end of the snake (or in Muladhara), and the other head of the snake rises to Sahasrara²¹⁸. Being a goddess of innocence, arising from mixed, inert sexuality, or rather, inert energy, Bahuchara symbolizes the awakening of kundalini. But all these semi-folkloric stories have little to do with the reality of life in the closed communities of the Hijras. Being completely ordinary is the greatest blessing, and given the socio-cultural circumstances, parents of such children usually try to conceal the child's indeterminate gender for as long as possible.

…The baby was given a female name, and the parents continued to hope for a miracle. And it happened. That day he was in Gwalior, where he was performing the final cremation rites for his mother; disciples invited him to their homes, and huge crowds gathered at every place. Finally, almost by force, he was led to a Darbar, one of the many so-called 'spiritual courts' held in his name. These events were never particularly pleasant, yet he could not avoid this duty. The child's parents were also there, having come to pray for a cure. The Darbar was held in a large private house, but at one point the crowd became so dense that even the homeowners were pushed outside the gates; people kept arriving to touch his feet, put a garland of flowers around his neck, receive a blessing.

When he finally walked down the narrow lane to the car, hundreds of people followed him, exclaiming "Yogiraj Maharaj!" Women held out their children so they could touch his feet – the dust on the feet of saints in India is considered a blessing, a divine prasad. Intolerant of crowds, he usually tried to stay away from large gatherings, as the depressive, overwhelming emotions affected his own consciousness and mood. The cocktail of suffering, desperate desires, and unfulfilled hopes produced a rather toxic than intoxicating effect, and all this influenced his nightly sadhana. As a rule, he avoided touching children; sometimes their past karmas would be revealed to him, and they were not always happy and serene. At times, a glimpse of a child's future could be so bleak that a dense, grey sadness would envelop his heart.

…The street he was walking on was so narrow that it was impossible to avoid the outstretched hands and the expectant, pleading eyes of the mothers. Some little one was brought to his feet, and suddenly he felt a sharp pain in his heart, as if he had been stabbed with a knife. So intense that he took it for a heart attack; holding his breath, he still managed to reach the car. The old 'Ambassador' started moving, the pain vanished, and he sighed with relief.

A week after this, the hermaphrodite child began to manifest undeniable female characteristics. The doctor who had been observing the child for the past five years was astonished to see that all external and internal organs appeared fully formed and indisputably female. He knew nothing of all that was happening until the happy parents visited him together with their daughter to tell this story. He could only recall the stabbing pain in his heart, unconnected to anything else. A wave of involuntary, uncontrollable compassion had passed through him then, as if Shakti herself had decided to manifest in this child.

The family visited him periodically, and a few years later, the girl began menstruating. Some time after that, she got married. Being a Hijra is not an easy fate, and he was glad the child had managed to avoid this destiny.

There existed a secret, well known to yogis: in this creation, there were no one hundred percent men or women. Every person has a certain percentage of male and female within them. Ardhanarishwara, the famous form of Shiva, represents the synthesis of the male and female energies of the universe; however, human sexual identity is largely shaped by society, and social stigmas are cruel to those who differ from accepted norms.

It was difficult to say what exactly had caused the changes that day, for he had not even known of the child's problem. One thing was certain – this transformation was a smile of Shakti, and he was merely an expression of Her will.

**Additional Responsibilities**

Upon departing, the Teacher left behind not only grieving relatives and devoted disciples, but also passed on to his successor the responsibility for over 16,000 souls of discarnate disciples. In fact, the invisible entourage of these souls was one of the factors spurring his search for a suitable place to live. The home of the late Guru in Benares was a refuge and a center of attraction for all these spirits who had not taken new births, and with the passing of Satyacharan Lahiri, they followed the successor. He keenly felt their presence. The Guru's responsibility for the well-being of his disciples continues even after they have crossed the threshold of death, but no one had warned him about this peculiar aspect of his duties. Subsequently, weighing the responsibility of such obligations, he was in no hurry to initiate new disciples.

From Benares, the spirits followed him to Gwalior, where for a couple of years they nested around a cottage on a farm, and just as imperceptibly moved with him to Vrindavan and Sarnath. They caused no disturbance and even sometimes offered help. But the temporariness of all these places and the frequent moves clearly made them nervous. The intensity of city vibrations and the exhausting chaos of human energies drained not only him, but also the ghostly retinue. Both he and the spirits needed a

In a quiet refuge. The air body, ideally suited for yoga, is capable of withstanding any weather and needs neither clothing nor food, but the yogi's soul requires a place where sadhana can be continued in a measured way. When the coveted palaces turned out to be memorials of the smashan, he rejoiced. For himself, and for the souls of his Guru's disciples, this place was excellent, and 16,000 spirits followed him to Moksha Bhumi. The process of the soul's liberation was long, and even bodiless spirits needed a secluded environment to prepare for the next stage of the journey.

After the installation of the Shivalingam, the spirits began to attend the puja together with other astral entities. Hundreds of large and small luminous, translucent spheres—sharply defined or amorphous, blurred at the edges but with visible complex patterns—hovered over the heads of the living congregants, and their outlines trembled, responding to the rhythms of the drums.

Not every spirit could enter the smashan; besides the disciples who had left their bodies, other astral visitors were present at the puja—ancient ghosts, bhutas, and even two giant jinns thickly covered in ash. The elder one always tried to get close to the people beating the drums; his enormous torso and bald head towered above the heads of the congregants. The younger one cowered timidly near a tree, not daring to come closer. Those who saw them kept their observations to themselves.

Once a disciple—always a disciple, there are no former yogis. And the spirits continued their sadhana. Now that their new bodies consisted entirely of the element of air, animated by consciousness, they could perform true pranayamas. In a human body, they were only capable of doing "breath-yamas" with the help of a pair of lungs, and these clumsy devices were not intended for the proper distribution of Prana. Nevertheless, yogis who had honed their pranayama skills during life were far better prepared for the journey than non-yogis, because their energy channels—nadis—were purified. Their air bodies were clearly defined and complete, and their mental faculties were preserved. Now, abiding in Vayu Sharira, they could know the essence of Prana in its fullness.

The human body is both a gift and an obstacle. For the soul, bodily incarnation is a chance for development and education, and therefore the body must be tuned through constant discipline. The yogic body needs to exist for as long as possible, for within it dwelt a spirit that could become a Teacher. After death, any spiritual progress achieved in the past life ceased. In air bodies, acquired knowledge was polished and perfected, but, paradoxically, further spiritual development in a disembodied state was unlikely.

Nevertheless, in the presence of the new Guru, the spirits of Kriya yogis could continue their tapas. Prana, no longer limited by Matter, easily passed through their air bodies, nurturing concentrated consciousness, preparing it for the next round of learning. The chance for another incarnation always depends on a combination of karmas, disentangled kleshas, and unresolved samskaras. And on the Guru, who might ask a freshly disembodied soul whether it wishes to accept a new birth. If so, the Guru could facilitate it.

If a prolonged illness or a sudden, agonizing death left an indelible imprint on the psyche of the departed, the soul might choose to remain in the air body. For a time. While the process of transformation was inexorable for all, certain exceptions and special cases existed. The Guru provided invaluable support in the post-mortem journey, and for the dead, his help was even more important than for the living. The astral world is cruel, and newly arrived, defenseless souls could become easy prey.

Among the spirits—the "long-term residents" of the astral worlds—were also those who refused a new birth; such souls simply wanted peace and quiet, and their wish was granted. The Guru cared for them, and they supported his sadhana. The complex symbiosis of the existence of the dead and living members of the parampara did not escape either him or the attention of the forces inhabiting the smashan. He observed these forces, and they continued to watch him closely. The gods knew the future, people thought they understood the present, and the wise could, with a certain degree of confidence, surmise what lay ahead.

Mahamari

One day, a visitor mentioned a strange procession he had witnessed on the way to Govardhan. First, he saw small bonfires burning right on the road, and then a group of men carrying torches passed by; they were shouting something. He explained to the guest that this was an exorcism ritual, quite common in Braj. When livestock fell ill, farmers would drive out the disease, luring the contagion out of the village and leading it to the borders of another settlement; then all participants in the ritual would run back. If the residents of the other village learned of the ritual, clashes between the peasants were inevitable. The procedure always proved effective; livestock mortality almost always began in the other village and moved on. Superstition or not, in bygone times this method was considered effective for getting rid of all epidemics. In connection with this incident, he recalled another story told by Dwarka Prasad Shastri, who was already over ninety. "Shastri" is a title denoting the rank of a priest, and Dwarka Prasad's grandfather had once been a pandit in a Kali temple in a remote village near Lalitpur.

One early morning, upon opening the temple, the pandit saw a naked, dark-skinned woman squatting in a corner. Her eyes were red, and long, matted hair covered the upper part of her naked, withered body; she wore no ornaments or any other visible markings. She looked insane and dangerous.

Being a learned and quick-witted man, the priest first completed all the temple rites, and only then respectfully addressed the woman: "What can I do for you, Mother?"

"I am Mahamari, the great epidemic. You must take me to the village. Invite me!" An epidemic always needs a first carrier, someone who will "introduce" it to others… Overcome with terror, the pandit implored: "I cannot become the instrument of such a terrible deed…" The woman replied: "I will spare you and your family. But if you do not take me to the village, you will die right here on the spot." Did he have a choice?

The priest said quietly: "Mother, come with me"—and she followed him. In the center of the village, at a crossroads, under a large banyan tree, there was a platform for sitting; there the dark, naked Mahamari sat down, observing the passersby. No one reacted, as it was not the custom of the villagers to approach strangers, especially those who looked so frightening. Or perhaps she was invisible. Nevertheless, she "rewarded" with cholera anyone who fell within her field of vision, and people began to die like flies. Within a day, more than half the village's inhabitants were dead. The pandit's family, having survived the plague, left the village to settle in a new place.
