All this happened around 1910, when the sixth cholera pandemic claimed the lives of more than 800,000 people in India and went on to ravage the Middle East, North Africa, Eastern Europe, and Russia. In those times, epidemics continued to easily take lives, and each disease had a supreme deity whose duty it was to cleanse the earth of human refuse. These tasks were both local and global, but in all cases, they were directed by the ruling goddess, the Earth. It was She who determined the time and place of the cleansing. Most often, epidemics began in ancient places of worship, in crowded temples, in close proximity to the deity through whom the order to begin the "clean-up" came. In Dwarka Prasad's grandfather's story, Mahamari was found in a Kali temple, meaning she acted with the consent of the Dark Goddess.

…In the summer of 2012, he was still performing the morning rituals in addition to the evening worship. That day, having finished the puja, he returned to the house and began his morning routine when he suddenly saw himself standing in the corner of a Shiva temple, dressed in red robes. Judging by the position of the sun, it was about 9 o'clock in the morning. A tall, big-boned but not stout woman was approaching him, dressed in a white sari without a blouse. Her gleaming skin was the color of dark chocolate, large teeth protruded, parting her cracked lips, and the whites of her large eyes were reddish. Thick, black, matted strands of her jata were gathered into a tail. The woman was beautiful with a frightening, mesmerizing beauty. She bowed in prostration, and he asked: "Who are you?"

"I am the great disease. Allow me to begin my work."

"Very well, go and do what you must…"

What else could he say? Arguing with such a dangerous being is pointless; moreover, she did not need to be invited anywhere, and she demanded nothing.

Turning around, Mahamari walked through the garden, and it was obvious she knew the way well. After this vision, he waited for some time for developments, yet there was no hint of a major epidemic. However, it would be wrong to claim that the following eight years were calm. The world was shaken by various ailments—from the rise of oncological diseases and psychological trauma to political and social crises in society and ecological disasters… The state of health of human civilization was clearly in question.

In 2020, Mahamari presented the world with its bill, announcing to all the impending disconnection from the cable television of illusions, false hopes, and passivity. The new virus was dangerous, but not everyone died. Illness and death became the best teachers, and people learned quickly. The traditional Indian greeting "Namaste" became more popular than the handshake; silent contemplation replaced loud parties. The prolonged solitude mandatory for all gave people a chance to awaken to reality. For a short time, at least, the art of enjoying one's own company became popular. People's self-awareness grew, awakening intuition, the ghostly component of intellect, and all this stimulated self-inquiry.

He had never been overly optimistic about the ways humanity interacted with Nature or with each other. The drive for suppression and domination was part of the human genetic code; however, the ability to analyze, compare, create, improve, and sustain was also part of human nature.

Listening to the news about political elections, military conflicts, and predicted economic chaos, he knew: Nature will always find a way to survive and continue its creation. Changes in the world of spirits were about to touch the Earth, and Mahamari was merely one of the first messengers of the coming changes.

Holi Festival, 2020

Alchemy

* * *

"Both Yoga and Alchemy aim at overcoming duality, reuniting what has been divided. This is the union of opposites—male and female, gross and subtle—into the indestructible. Both lead to the discovery of the inner 'Self,' which shows us that the goal and the source are one and the same."
Shailendra Sharma, "The Upanishad of Immortality"

The eggs of the golden bird

Upon entering the path of yoga, he did not think about such things as the transformation of the mind or eternal life. At that time, nothing of the sort was on his list of interests. The mind awakened gradually, evaluating and marveling at the new perspectives, the depth, complexity, and diversity of yoga. Observing his own bodily and mental changes, he gradually understood that the path ahead was truly infinite.

Everything changed with the first experience of samadhi; astonishment turned into the delight of achievement, but it was short-lived. Signs of rapid aging and mental inertia began to manifest with relentless inevitability, but, being in a state of excited semi-trance from the recent experience, he initially paid no attention to them. Then any desire to do anything, even to practice, was replaced by a steady apathy. The indifference and inertia that overcame him seemed to cover everything around with an equally even layer of invisible pigment, absorbing all colors, tastes, and smells. Noticing gray hairs on his chest, he understood that the body had taken the state he had experienced as a signal to prepare for departure, for death was near. But the path of yoga was long, and it was necessary to keep the body and mind in a "working condition." Otherwise, there would be no one to practice yoga.

Vigorous strength training helped halt the rapid decline. It took him about three months to overcome mental sluggishness. Having established a training regimen, he followed it zealously, as the exercises were an effective remedy against aging. At the same time, he began to wonder that there must have been other auxiliary means. After all, the ascetics of antiquity did not visit sports clubs, but surely used some methods of maintaining physical fitness. After a year and a half, the aging process stopped, and he decided to support the body with Ayurveda and alchemy, beginning to experiment. Mostly by trial and error, but some results were impressive.

His indirect acquaintance with alchemy began early. When you grow up surrounded by Ayurvedic doctors, it is impossible to miss conversations about medicines made from bhasma, the ash of burned metals, about the new qualities of various ingredients that acquire healing properties after complex transformational processes. Of course, in youth, people rarely pay attention to indirect, implicit, but recurring signals of fate. However, the accumulated memories destined to lead them through life will inevitably surface. A series of seemingly random meetings and events awakened his interest in alchemy, and he began to study and experiment. The information was so vast that extracting the correct essence of formulas from a sea of texts was a practically insurmountable task. At the same time, he learned to discern the seeds of truth in everything: in people, situations, yogic techniques, and substances. The entire world presented itself as a giant and fascinating laboratory, and its doors were wide open to the curious and the brave.

Being an insatiable reader, he compared Eastern and Western texts and understood that alchemical truths and allusions were described in the Old and New Testaments. Other sacred books also had numerous analogies with the process of the Great Work: the Bhagavad Gita, the Vedas, the Upanishads, and even the Quran. The awakening of the spirit, the transformation of the body, redemption, death, resurrection, and incarnation—all of this found resonance in alchemical, soteriological, or eschatological contexts. Alchemy encompassed a vast diversity of ideas, practices, and participants, but over time, the cultural spaces it occupied changed greatly, making talk of "alchemy" as a timeless, coherent science almost meaningless. It continued to exist, but it could only be discussed with like-minded people, so as not to evoke uncomprehending and bewildered looks.

The Vedic texts were interspersed with references to alchemical secrets. Vague descriptions indicated a connection between gold and longevity. As early as the 4th–3rd centuries BCE, the Arthashastra, the "Science of Material Gain," spoke of a vital alchemical ingredient, mercury. Buddhist manuscripts from roughly the same period also reported the transmutation of base metals into gold. There were numerous hypotheses that during the invasion of Alexander the Great into India in 325 BCE, the Greeks brought with them some alchemical ideas that were integrated into Ayurveda. This theory did not trouble him. Quite the opposite: the art of alchemy and the concept of immortality were born in India; the golden bird—Bharata—hatched the eggs of supreme knowledge in the nest of the subcontinent. No matter how many tried to hatch magical chicks from these eggs in other incubators, the secrets of the immortality of body and spirit and the transformation of matter remained well protected, guarded by the earth itself.

The ambitious goal of creating artificial gold required resources. For most alchemists and Ayurveda experts, preparing medicines was a more attainable and practical goal, and the process itself less complex and labor-intensive. Nevertheless, for a select few, the idea of an alchemical elixir granting immortality was of far greater interest.

The yogic quest for eternal life raised an extremely important question: why? The answer was simple: for the attainment of self-realization, to discern the play of the Creator. Such a unique desire could only visit a mature mind, driven by intense meditation. The consciousness-altering means, the elixir of yoga, must be taken constantly, for an immature mind in an eternal body could lead to simple madness and decay.

Around the 11th century, Tantrism adopted the radical ideas of alchemical transformation of mind and body and, most importantly, the possibility of changing human destiny. Alchemical principles were painstakingly woven into the fabric of Tantric philosophy. Around 1100–1300 CE, several significant works appeared indicating the influence of the principles of alchemical transmutation, such as the 12th-century *Rasarnava*, or the *Treatise on Metallic Preparations*.

On the walls of the old Birla Temple in Varanasi, there is a memorial plaque with the inscription: "In the month of Chaitra, Samvat 1999 (according to the Indian calendar, 1943), a pandit from Punjab, Krishnapal Rasavaidya, conducted an experiment in Rishikesh in the presence of P.A. Mahatma Gandhi, Mahadeva Desai, Goswami Ganesha Datta, and G. K. Birla. Mercury, presented by Desai, was transmuted into 18 kg of gold in the presence of all the aforementioned people." The gold was donated to the Samadhan Dharma Pratiniti Sabha in Punjab, and it brought the said fund 72,000 rupees in proceeds. Shri Rasavaidya repeated the experiment in the presence of Pratap Singh of the Benares Hindu University, and this was documented, and the gold was tested and found to be the purest. A similar alchemical experiment with a smaller amount of mercury (only one tola) was conducted by Krishnapal Rasavaidya in New Delhi earlier, in May 1942. The memorial plaque on the temple wall reminded of the vast alchemical knowledge passed down from the ancient rishis to contemporaries. The scientific explanation was a low-energy nuclear reaction, the famous "cold fusion," proving that the alchemists were not mistaken, and that ordinary metal could be turned into gold. Simply, besides the ingredients, the process required extreme patience.

In the late 1960s, physicist Judith Temperley of the Edgewood Arsenal in Maryland studied the properties of various metals by bombarding them with high-energy neutrons. In one experiment, the target metal was mercury, an element with 80 protons: a neutron struck the nucleus, causing the destruction of a proton in a process of "electron capture." The result was an atom with 79 protons, making it an atom of gold. Well, to obtain gold from mercury in this way would take about 1,000,000,000,000,000,000,000,000,000,000 years. And yet, many centuries ago in India, they knew how to transform metals, and these were not just legends and fairy tales. Modern science agrees with the correctness of the ancients, who asserted that one element could be transformed into another, as is done in our time, in the conversion of uranium-238 into plutonium-239. It is a matter of technological breakthrough. And of providence.

The precious eggs of the golden bird had no expiration date. Dormant in time, the knowledge was kept safe in the nest of the subcontinent, within the shell of secrecy. The metaphysics of alchemical and yogic concepts, processes, and goals, the layers of meaning and the essence of knowledge, were accessible to those who set the development of consciousness as their goal. Whatever the inquiry, samadhi opened access to the vast database of Universal Knowledge. The dazzling darkness of the Void contained all answers, and he learned to ask the right questions.

**The Formula**

The first true alchemist he met could not possibly be suspected of his occupation. Hardly anyone would have dared to call the ragged and overgrown man he encountered on the beach in Puri by that name. The sadhu (in India, any homeless person can be a sadhu) came to the same spot every day, as did he. For five or seven days, the sadhu sat motionless nearby, and probably studied him unnoticed. Eventually, they began to talk, and these conversations lasted for hours. Days merged into weeks, waves crashed against the shore, and the gusty wind caught and carried away the words, as if trying to hide their meaning. The chance interlocutor looked like a vagabond, but spoke on topics that ordinary tramps were unlikely to understand. This man's speech was correct, seasoned with technical terms. Besides many interesting things, the sadhu spoke of alchemy and even made him write down some formulas. In this subject, the sadhu was clearly knowledgeable, unlike him, who had not the slightest interest in alchemy. But there was nothing else to do on the beach anyway, and he obediently wrote down the components and methods. One such formula was a step-by-step instruction for solidifying mercury. All this was interesting, but completely useless. And one day, the sadhu simply disappeared, and the daily conversations ended as abruptly as they had begun.

The only experiment he could perform at that time without any proper ingredients or equipment was not entirely alchemical. The Marana mantra he had mastered brought brief satisfaction but made him irritable and sullen, until the Guru forced him to abandon the dangerous knowledge. Several eventful years passed, during which he diligently practiced Kriya, marveling at the daily (or rather, nightly) changes in consciousness and the surrounding world.

When one of his aunts died, he attended the family memorial rites, where several relatives, quite well-known Ayurvedic doctors, were present. During a casual conversation, the topic of alchemical mercury came up. He had no deep knowledge of the subject, although the small notebook with the notes made on the beach in Puri was still with him—and he volunteered to make solidified mercury. The relatives laughed sarcastically, and he took it as a challenge. The sadhu's formula was worth testing. Having purchased the necessary ingredients at the local market, he treated the mercury, without even bothering to purify it, and it solidified. The next day, he presented the sample to his relatives; the short presentation ended in complete silence. Although the respected doctors had practiced Ayurveda for over 30 years, they had seen solid mercury for the first time in their lives.

Who was this sadhu, and why did he decide to pass on a unique alchemical formula to him? One thing could be said

To meet a person who would willingly and voluntarily impart the secret alchemical mystery to a stranger was a blessing and an extraordinary stroke of luck…

One way or another, after the experiment with mercury, he began to read everything he could find on the subject. One of his deceased relatives, a renowned Ayurvedic physician, had left behind a vast collection of specialized literature. After the doctor’s death, no one in the family knew what to do with the mountains of books. Several years later, during a visit, he saw that his uncle’s precious books were stored in the open air under a tin awning. The bindings of the folios were covered with mold, the pages marred by streaks of rain—his cousins were planning to sell all this rubbish as scrap paper. He offered to take everything at once, and the relatives gladly agreed. The unique collection contained nearly every book on Ayurveda ever published, and he read them from cover to cover. Sometimes, in search of a rare copy, he would interrogate a bewildered bookstore owner in detail, and the owner would think that this remarkably well-informed customer was writing a doctoral dissertation on alchemy.

His family regarded his experiments with considerable favor. Since his mother held a degree in Ayurveda and was well acquainted with the rejuvenating properties of solid mercury, she even ordered that dull silver pieces of mercury be placed in the kettle in the kitchen. From then on, tea in the house was prepared with mercury, and for several years the family patiently waited for alchemical gold, until their expectations were supplanted by more pressing concerns.

Over many years, he encountered sadhus who knew well the secrets of processing mercury, sulfur, and rare aspects of the alchemical craft; each had their own approach to the process. The procedure itself was the same, but the methods always differed.

At times, while reading Ayurvedic texts, he could grasp the very essence of a formula—the “insignificant” details that were not revealed to others. When the formula was applied in accordance with these nearly invisible and seemingly minor technical specifications, it worked with astonishing precision.

Driven by simple curiosity, he began to uncover the profound meaning of the transformation of substances; alchemy extracted the essence of Matter. The parallels and similarities between yoga-sādhana and the process of the Great Work, as alchemists called their eternal quest, became increasingly evident. Like the idea of a perpetual motion machine, the concept of transmuting base metals into gold seemed utopian to many. To many, but not to yogis. They pursued the goal of transforming the mortal body into an immortal one, strengthening the abode of the spirit with the sole purpose: to persuade the developed consciousness, that magical treasure obtained through the alchemy of sādhana, to remain in the body. Both yoga and alchemy sought to grant the body eternal life, so that it might continue to hold the mind. The alchemy of the spirit, discovered by yogis, uses Prāṇa and Apāna just as Alchemy itself uses sulfur and mercury. Mercury is like Prāṇa-Śiva, and sulfur acts as Apāna-Śakti. The discoveries came as concrete instructions, not merely metaphysical hints, and he meticulously followed them.

Initially, he used mercury as a rasāyana²²¹, a rejuvenating agent. Small, silvery balls of processed mercury were fed with gold, and the mercury devoured it greedily, in vast quantities, as if intoxicated by the preciousness of the metal. Thus the human body greedily absorbs Prāṇa, without assimilating it. Burned by excess oxygen, oxidized by the very source of Life, it ages.

The Sanskrit word “rasāyana” literally means “the path of essence.” This common Ayurvedic term for techniques to increase lifespan and rejuvenate the body also denotes the striving of the very essence of Matter toward perfection. The path of any essence to perfection lies through the boundless fields of process and method.

The soul is the concentrated essence of the living body, and sādhana embodied the alchemical process of extracting the precious substance of the immortal spirit. The body must be processed by prāṇāyāma, and its fire burned away the slag of karmas and the sheaths of ignorance, revealing purified consciousness.

Closely intertwined, the physics and metaphysics of yoga and alchemy led to tangible results. Slowly, drop by drop, the Matter of the body produced the nectar of the Spirit, and during this process the organism needed support.

The thick Indian tea he had drunk for years was prepared with an addition of solidified mercury: several small lumps were always at the bottom of the pot. When preparing his food, the women in the kitchen kept mercury under their tongues, adding spices to the dāl or kneading dough for flatbreads. The solidified mercury imparted certain properties to the cooks and the food they prepared, yet no one dared to ask him about the specifics of this method.

Considered the semen of Śiva, mercury is practically the only metal capable of being in a liquid state at room temperature. Having experimented with it for over 37 years, he knew that the alchemical process and its product change both the psychological and physical states of the experimenter, but not all transformations could be described or translated into scientific terms.

Disciples who received small silvery pills of mercury from him looked younger and more energetic. Their progress in sādhana was determined by a combination of factors: ambition, motive, perseverance, karmic predisposition, and the Guru’s blessing. Those who practiced continuously began to perceive reality in all its vastness, and the web of worldly illusions gradually faded from the stained amalgam mirror of Māyā, no longer distorting the true nature of things. Steady and gradual for some, uneven and difficult for others, the progress of accepting and acknowledging a new worldview was achieved with difficulty, but was inevitable.

He patiently observed their transformations. Seekers had to free themselves from various limitations, confront their own saṃskāras, but, one by one, the students awakened from the slumber of ordinariness to fulfill the most arduous task: to see their true nature, to understand the design and course of their own destinies, and to perfect them.

**The Naked Alchemists**

The winter of 1985 was unusually warm, and he was sitting in the courtyard of the house, enjoying the weather. It was around four o’clock in the afternoon; the sharpness of the daytime shadows was beginning to soften, and the “hour of cow dust”²²² was approaching. Two naked sādhus walked by; the shadows of their moving silhouettes were, for some reason, sharply outlined only down to their chests, ending around the area of their beards, which were clearly visible on the cracked clay of the road. The remnants of the shadows were indistinct and blurred, and he thought it was a play of light and shade. Neither young nor particularly old, completely naked except for garlands of rudrākṣa around their necks and wooden bracelets, with long, matted jaṭās gathered into buns, the sādhus walked slowly. Their dark, sun-withered bodies were proportionate, without any fatty deposits, yet they did not look thin. The nudity of the sādhus looked very natural; they showed not a trace of awkwardness, as if nakedness were a kind of uniform that gave them confidence.

Stopping and leaning on their staffs, the ascetics spoke with him, asking about the locality. Then the conversation took on a more interesting character. It turned out they were planning to stop in a neighboring village to build a temple and feed the needy. Such charity is widespread in India, but how were these sādhus going to accomplish all this? He was intrigued. These two radiated serenity and peace; their speech was gentle; when they invited him to walk with them for company to the neighboring village, he agreed. What they were about to do was too interesting to miss—the sādhus planned to turn tin into silver and gold. Having first thought it was some kind of trick, he became, for the first time, a witness to alchemical work.

…On the outskirts of the neighboring village, the sādhus quickly set up a temporary camp and arranged for the delivery of the necessary ingredients. One of them placed a pot of tin on an improvised stove; the other lit a chillum and, after a while, shook the ash into the pot. The tin melted, turning into a silvery lava; undoubtedly, it was a metal, but how could it become silver? Insisting that this was alchemical silver of the highest purity, the sādhus asked him to arrange its sale. He had several jeweler acquaintances and set off for the city market, wondering what would happen next. In the shop, the substance obtained was tested and bought as purest silver. All this was quite intriguing, and after some time he visited the sādhus’ camp again, where he witnessed another experiment.

The sādhus communicated in some secret jargon, and one of them addressed his companion: “Swamiji, what shall we make this time? Dāl soup or roṭī flatbreads? How about some hot dāl?” In their slang, gold was called dāl—a traditional Indian soup made from split beans—and roṭī (unleavened flatbreads) denoted silver. One of the sādhus lit a chillum, adding something that looked like crushed dry herb. When the chillum was half smoked, the melting metal had already turned into a golden liquid lava. The sādhus did nothing more; they added no ash to the pot—at least, it all looked as if they were simply smoking while the tin melted.

Becoming a regular intermediary, he continued to visit his new acquaintances. Someone was needed to help with the sale of the alchemical metal, and he faithfully carried the resulting product to the shops of his jeweler acquaintances. Each time, the substance he brought was tested—and it turned out to be purest silver or gold. In those days, gold was not so expensive; it seems it was about 600 rupees for 10 grams, yet the local buyers thought he had found a treasure. He preferred not to elaborate on the origin of the precious metals, only occasionally mentioning relatives who supposedly wished to sell their savings.

Usually, at the sādhus’ camp, he was offered tea; sometimes they discussed details of the alchemical process in his presence using short, coded phrases, but he had already read enough about alchemy to understand the hints and grasp the essence. However, most of the time the ascetics spent in silence, and he enjoyed this powerful and meaningful stillness. With the proceeds from the “magical cooking,” the sādhus began the construction of a temple to Hanumān, and the builders worked quite quickly. The sādhus also fed thousands of people from the surrounding villages, organizing free food distribution. From time to time, local residents or contractors came to them to discuss the progress of the construction. After brief reflection, the sādhus would usually calculate the necessary sum to continue the work and set about preparing the next batch of precious metal.

The sādhus, of course, did not discuss the sources of funding for the construction with outsiders, but they deliberately allowed him to observe the alchemical process. Why did they trust him, a chance stranger?

The practical magic of alchemy not only brought wealth; it could prolong the life of the body, in which the Spirit could remain longer… He never asked them where they had come from or where they planned to go next. These naked alchemists were not inclined to idle chatter; moreover, they were older, and so such questions would have been a breach of unspoken etiquette.

When the construction of the temple was finished, the sādhus left.

...simply vanished, dissolving into the veil of the vernal haze that covered the plains of Madhya Pradesh. They had given people food, hope, and spiritual support, and were preparing to continue their mission wherever they were headed…

The encounter inspired him to continue his own experiments with mercury, especially since the sadhus had shared some formulas with him. He also understood something important. These ascetics, capable of turning tin and copper into silver and gold, voluntarily renounced domestic comforts, eating only chapati with rice and dal, and sleeping on the bare ground. They could have become the wealthiest people in the world, but cared only about serving the needy. Possessing incredible siddhis, they fed the poor and built temples. Devoid of all possessions and attachments, they continued their mystical journey, and the freedom to leave everything behind was their greatest luxury.

Yet the yogic concept of freedom was even broader. The greatest of all known privileges was the ability to leave the body at will, or to choose a new home for the soul. Or never to be born in a body again. The greatest wealth was the freedom of choice.

**The Ever-Young Sadhu**

In 1985, Swami Shivananda Saraswati of Jhansi was 88 years old – and it was then that he first saw a photograph of a young, broad-shouldered yogi with heavy black kundala earrings. Swami asked an acquaintance to introduce him to Yogiraj. Shivananda Saraswati was very intelligent and sincere, and therefore received initiation into Kriya. Some time later, he visited Gwalior again, this time in the company of a man of about thirty, also dressed in the ochre robes of a sadhu. Swami Shivananda asked that his companion be initiated into Kriya Yoga: “Guruji, please give him diksha.”

When the newcomer was asked his age, he replied: “I am 96 years old…”. It was impossible to believe, but Shivananda Saraswati confirmed: “Yes, it is so. He is a friend of my elder brother, there is no mistake, he is 96 years old…”

The “young man” received initiation without further delay and mastered the initial techniques excellently. On the last day of the visit, the newly initiated yogi addressed him: “Guruji, being a wandering sadhu, I cannot offer you money as a token of gratitude. Is there anything by which I can express my appreciation?”

He studied the new disciple for some time: “You are beginning the practice of yoga at a very mature age, at ninety-six years, and you probably plan to progress in your sadhana. Tell me, how did you achieve longevity and why do you look so young? Perhaps you have undergone some secret rejuvenation procedure, Kaya Kalpa?”

The sadhu was silent for a long time, almost a minute, and then began to speak. He was born in a village lost among the endless fields of Uttar Pradesh, and until he turned 36, he lived completely carefree. Village life suited him well; for pleasure he practiced freestyle wrestling and worried about nothing much. His elder brother was a farmer, working tirelessly, while his brother’s wife constantly nagged her brother-in-law for his idleness: “You are good for nothing, a useless bum…”

One day, after another family quarrel, he was so offended that he left the village. In those days, everyone who left home and family headed for the Himalayas: most often such people became wandering sadhus due to a lack of choice, rather than because of a spiritual calling. And he became one of those wanderers. Hungry and destitute, he kept walking and gradually grew weaker. He was ashamed to beg for alms. Somewhere in the jungles of Uttarkashi, he lost consciousness from hunger and exhaustion. Upon waking, he saw an elderly sadhu bent over him, trying to give him warm milk to drink. This man nursed the hapless pilgrim back to health, and he joined his savior, becoming his self-proclaimed disciple. It turned out that his benefactor was actively experimenting with various Ayurvedic substances, trying to decipher the code of eternal life, but he did not explain the specifics of the formulas to the novice. Soon the reason why the sadhu had nursed him back to health became clear – he needed an assistant.

After many weeks, they finally reached the floodplain of the Narmada River and spent entire days searching for the necessary seeds and herbs, waiting for the correct alignment of the constellations to cut and process the plants. The sadhu meticulously extracted oil from the collected herbs and filtered it. This painstaking work seemed endless, but the novice did not object – he had no other business anyway. Extracting and processing the herbal oil took several months. Then the sadhu began testing the resulting substance on the disciple. The pleasantly scented liquid was dark, thick, and bitter-sour to the taste. After each dose, the disciple would usually fall asleep; he saw no dreams and would wake up with a heavy head.

For forty days, the teacher kept him on a special diet, allowing him to eat only unsalted rice and drink milk. The sadhu said that if the elixir were prepared properly, with its help one could live up to 360 years without any signs of aging. The day came when he took the last dose. The next morning, he woke up with difficulty. As usual, a pitcher of milk and a plate of rice awaited him by the makeshift hearth under a large banyan tree, but his companion had disappeared. He had also taken the elixir, every last drop. The painstakingly collected ingredients were gone too. The goal had been achieved, and the sadhu no longer needed an assistant.

What was the former disciple to do? Continuing the journey alone, he eventually settled in one of the many ashrams of Rishikesh on the banks of the Ganga. He was familiar with the lifestyle of a sadhu and lived as an ascetic, a simple and pure life. However, after a few years, people began to notice that he was not aging, while they themselves were becoming covered in wrinkles and stooping under the burden of time… Someone spread rumors that he was a siddha, a miracle worker, and this caused inevitable complications: people began asking him for blessings, begging for healing from illnesses and sufferings, but he could give none of this. In the end, he had to leave the ashram and go far away from Rishikesh. He took a vow of non-attachment: those who have taken such a vow cannot stay in one place for longer than three or four days and must continue wandering. In such a short time, attachment to people and places cannot form; moreover, no one could notice that the wandering ascetic was not changing with the years.

…The story was incredible, but true; the 96-year-old swami showed no signs of aging, having taken the last dose of the elixir about 60 years earlier. In farewell, the swami handed him a folded piece of paper with the formula for the medicine, but to find the necessary plants, one would have to live for months in the jungles by the Narmada River, waiting for the right moment to collect and process the herb…

After the swami had mastered Khechari Mudra, he came to the smashan to receive the next level (it seems this was in November 1992). He still looked young and fit; apparently, the combination of Kriya Yoga and the secret herbs had done him good. The swami’s last visit was in 1994, when he turned one hundred years old, but he had not aged a single iota.

Since then, there has been no news of him. Most likely, both he and his teacher continued to wander across India. These unknown people possessed the priceless treasures of ageless bodies and longevity, and could contemplate their life experience without falling into the depression that often accompanies physical decline. The lives of ordinary people must have seemed to them like nothing more than the flutter of a butterfly’s wings.

**The Search for a Rare Herb**

The right plant, the right time, the right constellation, and the right proportions… The formula for immortality existed – texts, legends, stories, and signs spoke of it. One just had to be attentive. In 1989, a rare plant became the subject of lively discussions among Ayurvedic specialists, many of whom praised its properties. When his uncle, an experienced Ayurvedic physician, confirmed the plant’s outstanding qualities, he became truly interested. The problem was that it could only be found in a rather remote place, near the Narmada River in Central India. Judging by the description, this area resembled the one the “ageless swami” had spoken of.

Despite being in his seventies, one of his disciples, Dwaraka Prakash Shastri, was known for his love of adventure and readiness for any escapade. Enthusiastically volunteering to accompany the Guru on the expedition, he immediately booked their tickets on the Malviya Express, and one fine evening they set off for Ujjain. During a stop at Jhansi station, they stepped onto the platform to stretch their legs. While walking, he heard a heated argument between the ticket collectors and some youth of about 18 or 19 years; he looked like a mere boy, unspoiled and simple, clearly in a difficult situation. He intervened: “What happened?”

“I lost my ticket…”

“Don’t worry, I will pay for your ticket from this stop onwards.”

The youth was invited into their carriage. In the morning, the sleepy protégé asked about their route, and they told him about the expedition for the rare herb, naming their destination. “I will guide you there, for your safety. My tribe lives there, and my father is the village elder. My presence will protect you from possible problems…”

The offer proved invaluable, as the Kanjars do not allow outsiders to pass freely through their territories. During British rule, they were branded a “criminal tribe,” called “despicable” – and to this day, the Kanjars are subject to significant social stigma. This is linked to the illegal production of alcoholic beverages, prostitution, and petty theft. In India, everyone knows of their strange practices: from training wall-climbing lizards to grim wedding rituals of worshipping the Goddess. Ritual defecation from rooftops was, so to speak, the cherry on the cake. There are a great many such stories.

The language of the Kanjars, Narsi-Parsi, consists of animal and bird sounds, coded words, and gestures, and this closed community keeps its secrets to this day. During the celebrations of Holi, Diwali, Rakhi, and Amavasya, some Kanjar tribes gather at the smashans with bottles of alcohol and treat the deceased. Reciting prayers, they raise a toast in memory of the dead, and then distribute the remaining alcohol among those present as prasad. After cremation, the Kanjars usually bury the ashes under a special mound, and on top of the tomb they leave a small door – for the soul, if it wishes to visit this world. Inside the mound, the Kanjars leave jewelry, a mirror and comb, towels and clothes, hair oil, soap, and a few rupees. A couple of bottles of homemade liquor, tobacco, and hand-rolled cigarettes usually complete the “posthumous kit,” providing the souls of the Kanjars with excellent equipment and pastimes…

…Arriving in Ujjain early in the morning, at the youth’s insistence they purchased the necessary equipment: small shovels and a sack; they had somehow not thought of this beforehand. Then they boarded a bus to Baranagar, from there they walked through the jungle all night until they reached the desired place. It was worth it: the required herb was abundant. The constellations were in a favorable position for harvesting, the plants were at their peak of maturity, and they began digging without delay, getting to the valuable roots.

Despite the tiring journey, Shastriji was full of energy and enthusiasm, and the large sack quickly filled. Suddenly, several local residents emerged from the forest. Out of breath from running, the Kanjars told them that…

In the village, news of the strangers spread, and the entire tribe, armed with swords and spears, headed toward them. However, the young man accompanying them did not look worried at all: “You have already gathered what you wanted. Leave. I will hold them back and speak with them. I am the chief’s son; nothing will happen to me, but you must hurry.”

Danger gave them strength. Like a wild deer, Shastriji ran ahead, weaving through the dense jungle. Having penetrated about four hundred meters into the thick of the forest, which provided an ideal, though temporary, shelter, they looked back. Nearly a hundred clearly battle-ready men with swords and spears had surrounded their guide. But the conversation seemed quite friendly, and the smiling young man spoke with the agitated villagers with calm confidence. They had no desire to verify whether the chief’s son’s charm had fully worked, and they plunged deeper into the thicket. A few hours later, they emerged onto a road where luck smiled upon them—a local bus arrived in just ten minutes. In Ujjain, they safely boarded a train to Gwalior.

Was all of this merely luck, a series of coincidences? An unfamiliar young man had lost his ticket; he paid for his fare, and in gratitude, the boy led them straight to the right place. And on top of that, he literally saved their lives. Nothing was a coincidence; all events had a hidden meaning. The pendulum of Fate is always suspended from a chain of links of trials and their results.

**The Encoded Mantra**

The collected herb proved truly magical: it eliminated liver problems within four days, and acute jaundice in one student passed in just three days. Treating serious heart diseases took longer, but the medicine made from this herb proved effective there as well.

…Before the “expedition,” he had been interested in little-known natural remedies and encoded healing formulas. One need only listen and discern the essence of the transmitted information. Clues were generously scattered everywhere, and an attentive mind could decipher them.

In the autumn of 1997, loudspeakers at a neighboring temple played a recording of the Mahamrityunjaya Mantra from morning until late at night. This no longer irritated him; by that time, both he and Devahuti had grown accustomed to the incessant din of religious festivals and were almost immune to the cacophony of sounds tearing apart the silence of Smashan.

But on the tenth or eleventh day, something clicked, and he suddenly understood: this mantra was an alchemical formula, a recipe for healing. As a rule, the Mahamrityunjaya Mantra is recited in cases of serious illness. It is believed to cure disease, and he himself had witnessed this. And yet, if he had not been reading yet another thick Ayurvedic manuscript at the time, such a realization would hardly have occurred to him.

*Om tryambakam yajaimahe*  
*Sugandhim Pushtivardhanam*  
*Urvarukamiva Bandhanam*  
*Mrityur mukshiya mamrtitat*

“Tryambaka” is one of the names of Shiva (pronounced “tryambakam” in the mantra), literally meaning “the three-eyed one.”

“Tryambakam yajaimahe” (translated as “We worship the three forms of Shiva”) refers to the three incarnations of Shiva, as well as to the three substances called “Shiva” in Sanskrit: mercury, shilajit, and black datura.

Thus, the first line indicated the necessity of understanding the essence of these substances, studying and processing them. The second line spoke of combining all three “kinds” of Shiva (mercury, shilajit, and datura) with processed sulfur. In Ayurveda, “sugandhim” was the name for sulfur; it was believed to increase vitality.

The next step was the processing of the mixture “urvarukamiva,” a fruit resembling a small watermelon with pinkish flesh.

“Mrityur mukshiya mamrtitat” meant that even one who has found himself “in the jaws of death” could escape it. That is, the remedy is capable of curing anyone, even a person on the verge of death.

He loved to get to the literal meaning; the direct significance of the words of the Mahamrityunjaya Mantra revealed the essence of a very practical ancient recipe, and he began experimenting with the mentioned substances. The proportions were unknown, although several trial samples produced cured two women with tuberculomas of the brain acquired as a complication of tuberculosis. Among other things, the medicine was prepared at the smashan, in the “house of Shiva,” which increased the effectiveness of the preparation. Such claims contradicted all pharmacological facts, but the results outweighed any arguments.

…For pragmatists, the very idea of gathering magical herbs in distant jungles borders on madness. Deciphering ancient mantras seems like a whim, an impractical hobby. The overwhelming majority of people consider the concept of eternal life extravagant simply because its very possibility contradicts the “generally accepted” average lifespan. “Average Joe” was never programmed to achieve immortality; people perceive the idea of an elixir of eternal youth as a fairy tale.

In general, society expects ordinary people to rely on the achievements of modern pharmacology and medicine, which support the average lifespan.

As a rule, limited needs and beliefs significantly narrow one’s perception of life and its goals. No one plans to live forever, since the meaning of such an existence lies beyond initially programmed expectations. For him personally, a limitation of imagination was like Death.

**The Philosopher’s Stone**

Western alchemy sought the Philosopher’s Stone for its supposed ability to transmute base metals into precious ones. Even if the desire for enrichment was banally materialistic, wealth was not the only goal. Alchemists believed that wondrous substances could grant eternal life, and with this transformation, the immortal soul would attain true perfection.

For Western philosophers such as Carl Jung, alchemy was a precursor to modern psychology, and it was alchemy that provided him with the foundation for studying the integration of conscious and unconscious aspects of the Self. By investigating alchemical science, Jung sought to decipher its rich allegorical symbolism, believing it would help understand the human psyche. Yogis had discovered the connection between alchemy and yogic metamorphoses of consciousness several centuries before Jung, yet the Western scholar was the first to systematize these principles.

An instrument for the transformation of Matter, the Philosopher’s Stone was also the result of inner evolution: the transmutation of the essence of the soul into the jewel of the higher Self. Medieval mystics considered the Stone to be a rather ordinary and widespread, though unrecognized, substance. Almost until the end of the 17th century, its quest drove medieval alchemists to study countless variations of ingredient interactions. In India, the Philosopher’s Stone is called Paras Pathar—presumably an analogue of the Western “lapis philosophorum.” Sometimes it was found raw, in its natural state.

Some historical records speak of instances where an unknown phenomenon transmuted base metal into gold. Somewhere around the end of the 18th century, when Maharaja Scindia’s army was crossing the Morar River near Gwalior, the iron chains on one elephant turned into gold. Perhaps something was in the soil of the riverbed, in the water, or in the sweat running down the elephant’s back. In any case, the combination of unknown components caused a natural transmutation of iron into gold, altering its properties and atoms within 40 minutes. That was exactly the time it took for the cavalry and baggage trains to reach the opposite bank. This incident was widely known, and the chain was even displayed in the palace for public viewing—until the gold dissolved in the numerous whirlpools of history and time, which took the form of the pockets of heirs. His colonel grandfather often visited the palace and saw this unusual souvenir in person.

Alchemical gold itself could not transmute other substances into precious metal; a magical catalyst was required—the Philosopher’s Stone. If the coveted ingredient turned any base metal into gold, then knowledge worked even greater wonders. In yoga, the Stone was the Guru himself. Working with the disciple’s consciousness, the spiritual teacher could transform ordinary human “material,” enhancing his qualities, revealing virtues, purifying the optics of the seeker’s inner vision without any tactile influence. If the disciple was able to “touch” the Guru with sincerity, zeal, or devotion, this could arouse the Teacher’s interest and initiate transformation. And yet, the seeker had to initially possess certain qualities.

The tradition of yogic parampara implies the transmission of knowledge from Guru to successor, ensuring the immortality of Truth. In Hinduism, the concept of Shaktipat means the transmission to a seeker of spiritual knowledge and divine energy from a Guru, sage, ascetic, or deity. Sometimes Shaktipat is bestowed upon a person who has asked for nothing of the sort—and yet he becomes a conduit of divine energy by virtue of destiny.

The term “Shaktipat” itself derives from the word “shakti”—energy, and “pata”—“to descend, to bestow.” Many methods of conducting such a transmission are known: through a secret word, a mantra, a simple glance, and even through a mental impulse. Or a touch. Among Western esotericists and spiritual seekers, it is widely believed that by touching a person’s eyebrow center (ajna, agya chakra, or third eye), it is possible to transmit knowledge, divine vision, and grace. A spiritual master (or a higher being) can activate someone’s third eye, and it becomes an entrance into the inner cosmos, opening a portal of higher consciousness. Spiritual vision through the third eye is an ideal New Age paradigm, easy to sell and consume. In any case, interest in spiritual vision is worthy of all praise.

Theosophists suggested that the third eye is actually a partially dormant pineal gland, which is quite possible. The “Eye of Consciousness” represents a concept in which a specific part of human anatomy is linked to the possibility of attaining enlightenment. The opening of the spiritual eye has also gained popularity in mass culture, but this is more a convenient symbolism, an allegory. What actually happens is rarely described, only hinted at. Shiva opened his third eye only once or twice, and the causal world ceased to exist; Mahadev intended to destroy the Universe and begin anew.

One thing is absolutely certain: Shaktipat was a manifestation of supreme grace, and whoever transmitted this energy was

**a messenger of divinity.** This grace cannot be begged for or seized by force, since superconsciousness must penetrate the soul of the recipient and become a part of it. Sometimes a flower, a fruit, or a sip of water could bestow Shaktipat from a distance. He had never personally encountered such cases, although legends abounded.

…After prolonged austerity, the future founder of the Aghori sect, Kinaram, was making a pilgrimage to holy places until he came to Girnar, the abode of the mystical Guru of the Aghoris and Nathas, Dattatreya. Climbing the famous 9,999 steps to the summit of the hill, to the temple of Guru Datta, Kinaram noticed a disgustingly filthy beggar gnawing on a dog's leg. Kinaram sat down nearby to catch his breath; the beggar held out a bone with scraps of meat, offering him refreshment. An experienced Aghori and a realized yogi, Kinaram knew the nature of aversion and attraction, beauty and ugliness, defilement and purification – and, without hesitation, taking the offered "treat," he bit into it. And immediately he attained a divine vision: Dattatreya himself had taken the form of a beggar to test Kinaram.

The beggar began: "The Emperor in Delhi…" And Kinaram continued: "…rides on a black horse, and a beautiful shawl falls from his shoulders, touching the grass." This was a test of clairvoyance, attained along with yogic power. It allowed one to see events occurring a thousand miles away.

Dattatreya said: "Your pilgrimage is over. Return to Varanasi, stay there, and do good." This was an obvious case of Shaktipat, the grace of the Guru, but before this Kinaram had performed intense austerity for many years. Guru Datta merely activated the abilities Kinaram already possessed.

In India, there is a great multitude of spiritual and religious groups that give initiation-Shaktipat to those who desire it. But he never saw any significant progress or expansion of consciousness in the initiated. Satisfaction with the mere fact of initiation deprived them of further incentive for seeking and self-perfection. The sadhus he encountered during his travels possessed certain powers and mystical abilities, yet none of these ascetics could transform another person into a jnani – one who knows. These holy men could curse or bless, but they could not transmit their abilities to others through a stream of direct knowledge.

Certainly, abstract grace of the Gods and the Guru existed; this precious gift could change a person's inner life. Shaktipat was an internal, assimilated divine power that remained with the fortunate recipient for life.

…The mediums through whom Hanuman communicated with him were called Shakti-Dhari in India, "spirit-possessed" – and the name described the meaning of what was happening quite accurately. Sometimes before death they would transmit to another person the spirit by which they were "possessed" and with which they cooperated. The new "bearer" would receive the astral lodger, and this too was a certain type of Shaktipat. But in such cases, no knowledge was transmitted; the spirit simply began to use the medium's body for communication. The person was merely a vehicle, a container for an astral entity pursuing its own goals.

The meeting with Babaji, of course, was Shaktipat. Listening as the Mahavatar explained the subsequent levels of Kriya, he felt a wave of inspiration: "Yes, I can achieve this!"

Babaji did not touch his brow, although he placed his hand on his head in a gesture of blessing. However great the grace of the Guru's physical touch, it did not guarantee instantaneous spiritual realization, only awakened the desire to attain it. It was this incomparable inspiration and confidence in his own strength that influenced his subsequent practice. Sadhana became even more intense and demanding; he had to work even harder. Miraculous changes did not occur overnight.

Observing his disciples and recalling his own experience, he could not help but admire the grandeur of Indian philosophy, which ensured the continuity of knowledge – through reflection upon the received teaching combined with self-inquiry and purification. The Master's wisdom could sound simple, but never banal, and if the disciple practiced sincerely, the deep meaning of the Guru's words would begin to unfold. This was the descent of Shakti through the touch of the supreme mind or the mind of an enlightened Master. It could become the catalyst for irreversible change. The Alchemist and the Stone became one.

**Alchemical Parallels**

As one popular contemporary author writes, attempts to prolong life beyond the body's expiration date are like repairing an old vacuum cleaner: it will break down anyway. Even if you constantly change parts, it is impossible to make the body eternal. A biological being must die and free up living space for others – this unbreakable script is established by Nature herself. The world needs the death of each one just as much as its birth, perhaps even more. In other words, the hope of living forever in an immortal body can be considered hopeless; Creation itself is against it. Individuality dies with the death of the brain and the destruction of the nervous system – how could it be otherwise? This is what everyone was taught, until science learned that brain neurons differ from other cells in the body and have no limit to their lifespan. For now, this hypothesis more or less reconciles the general public with the fantastic theory of consciousness transfer. Today it seems quite possible, as does the preservation of the organism by alchemical means. The question is only – why?

Rooted in yoga and sealed by the sacredness of alchemical processes, immortality is a feature of the Indian land, the precious egg of the golden bird of Bharata. Nowhere else in the world are there ancient texts speaking of the immortality that yoga bestows. Stories of yogis, sages, and pious kings who performed tapas for thousands or millions of years – an exclusively Indian theme. The events described in the Puranas were measured in various time scales; in the past, the average lifespan significantly exceeded the modern statistic of 72-plus years. History is nothing but pieces and fragments of retold and rewritten legends, and these legends are not just a collection of fairy tales, but testimonies of Time itself.

Driven by a thirst for higher knowledge, the pioneers of immortality crossed His threshold, realized His essence and non-linear nature – and merged with Time. For these ascetics and sages, human life is insultingly short; they wanted to know more and refused to succumb to the blackmail of Time's limitation. These seekers had no mortgages or student loans, did not burn themselves in the flames of urgent projects and business plans, and did not retire at the age of 65. Their ambitions and goals were far grander. Truly egocentric, these people sought to attain liberation in one lifetime, and their approach is quite practical: the lifespan must increase, and if the body serves longer, the Spirit will have no reason to leave its sheath.

Of course, all this sounded like fantasy, an unattainable dream – but such an attitude suited those who wished to remain unknown. For the immortals, complete anonymity is merely one aspect of the grand play. Non-believers and skeptics lived out the meager years allotted to them by Nature and karma, and returned back into the non-existence of the Void. Those who cast an unprecedented challenge to death, ascetics and magicians, became immortal. There were few such beings, yet they appeared in all ages. Merging with Time, they came to know it through the prism of yogic samadhi, and this attainment is the result of individual karmas and austerities, the result of life's efforts. For some fortunate practitioners, the first fruits of yoga could come quite quickly. The pure nectar of Shambhavi Mudra225 or Omkar Kriya was received by those who dedicated their lives to it.

What would a worldly person do upon becoming immortal, how would they spend their time? An unprepared mind could not cope with such a trial. In India, the goal of longevity is well known: it is intended exclusively for the knowledge of God. Eternal life is meant for the continuation of knowledge through the path of spiritual practice.

225 Shambhavi mudra is one of the highest yogic attainments. Also, Shambhavi is one of the names of Shakti, striving towards Shambhu (Shiva). When performing Shambhavi-mudra, the yogi remains inwardly focused, holding consciousness and pranas unwavering, and the gaze steady, as if seeing everything and seeing nothing. In this state, a state of non-duality arises. The technical execution of this mudra is described in many texts as holding an unblinking gaze directed into emptiness, but with simultaneous inner awareness. There is another level of Shambhavi – the level of siddhas, when the yogi abides in unity on the subtle level of the outer and inner, the level of jivanmukti, the liberated soul.

…An interesting case was once told by Harish Sharma, a banker and his longtime disciple: one of the bank's clients submitted a loan application and provided the necessary documents of ownership along with other papers. All certificates were in perfect order, but they turned out to have been issued 150 years ago. The bank sent an inquiry, and it was discovered that the applicant's great-great-grandfather and the owner of the property was still alive and well. The 180-year-old raja of a small principality in Rajasthan had long since withdrawn from royal duties, yet was still mentally and physically active. And quite alive. After confirming the identity of the long-liver, the bank granted the loan, and the family asked the bank management to keep the information confidential. They did not want their patriarch to become an object of attention and idle sensation. It is known that the Natha yogis were spiritual mentors to the rulers of Rajasthan – perhaps this was one of those cases where a siddha granted a long life to a person who had sufficient means for a comfortable life – for the continuation of spiritual development. Other goals are unworthy of such a gift.

Pastime is a problem exclusively for mortals, whereas the immortals know perfectly well how to spend unlimited time. The very process of attaining immortality forever changed the consciousness of the siddhas, no longer bound to a narrow list of earthly affairs. The processes of perfection and assimilation of acquired knowledge are equally applicable both to techniques of knowledge and to the transformation of matter. The yogic goal of transforming consciousness and the alchemical goal of transmuting metals into gold are similar and go hand in hand; they grew out of the desire to overcome the limitation of form, to test the boundaries set by Nature, and to see how substance overcomes the need for form.

Such a metamorphosis was both the goal and the means. Ichchha, Kriya, and Shakti are the most important tools of yogi-alchemists. Will, the ability to act, inner and outer power – without them one cannot succeed in either yoga or alchemy. As in any art, mastery is honed over time. The seed of Time-Shiva – mercury – embodied the magic of the transformation of Matter. It was not without reason called the main ingredient of alchemy: although it could be "killed," it never completely "died."

The hunger of all-devouring Time is insatiable. Mercury, if allowed, can swallow a huge amount of precious metals and stones, like a binge drinker – such is the nature of this liquid metal.

Nature herself undergoes transformation through yoga and alchemy; both disciplines work with the predispositions and patterns of the mind and the essence of substances. Alchemists say that mercury acquires the essence and spirit of gold. In the process of treating it with various herbs and minerals, the essence

The "emotions" of these substances are transferred into the mercury.

But what does Time feed upon? As far as he could discern, human emotions are sacrificial offerings upon the altar of Time, which towers above all other altars and temples. Time nourished itself on the life force of spirits, "snacking" on myriad emotions supplied by countless souls.

All living beings were that very prima Materia, the building material of Life. The immortal essence of each soul contained the seeds of memories and feelings, and it was these that quenched the insatiable hunger of Time.

Alchemical mercury, fed with gold and precious stones, was "sweated," "steamed," "killed," and finally "revived"—only then could it transform a mortal body into an immortal one. In Indian alchemy, such mercury is called "siddha." If it turned copper into gold, it could be ingested—and it would transform the mortal shell into the eternal body of a siddha.

In the Bengali language (as in ancient Sanskrit), a fully baked potato or well-cooked rice is also called "siddha"—that which has been brought to the proper condition and is completely ready.

Ultimately, the entire alchemical process is a test of willpower and intention. Properly processed, mercury can transform a body conditioned by time and karmic factors into an imperishable chariot of the Spirit. Yoga subjects the Spirit itself to trials and perfections. The rider and the chariot must become worthy of each other.

**The Alchemical Gita**

Years of reading the Bhagavad Gita convinced him that the book, to which he had once written yogic commentaries, was a manual for immortals. This understanding was based on personal realization and required no proof. He simply knew it. Conjecture is the lot of the worldly; the privilege of the practitioner—the tapasvin—is knowledge. In principle, yoga itself is an attempt not so much to change the nature of the mind as to crystallize the essence of the mind by removing impurities from it.

This rather ambitious goal consisted in the transformation of the mind using the properties of human nature: perseverance, curiosity, and desire. Striving for development, in the process of yogic sadhana, the mind must burn samskaras (mental impressions, memories, or psychological imprints). The basic building material of karma is samskaras, which should not be confused with samsara, although they are part of it. Samsara is that which "flows around." Samskaras, however, are the fragments of a shipwreck that the soul clings to as it is carried to the shore of the next life. According to the conclusions of Indian philosophy, the spirit seeks to find liberation from the bonds of past actions.

Samsara is the central concept of metempsychosis—the transmigration into a new body after death. Past, present, and destined karmic actions and the memories of them (samskaras) constitute the main part of the giant web of Samsara. To escape the endless cycle of rebirth, all the slag of the past and present must be purified by the fire of yoga. Nevertheless, the concepts of purity and impurity are themselves limiting. The seeker must overcome even these, going beyond the boundaries of these states.

Along with detailed descriptions of yogic practices, the adepts of antiquity left descriptions of alchemical processes as a guide for overcoming duality. Not to conquer, but to go beyond the very concepts of purity and impurity, good and evil, black and white. All fixed polarities are enslaving and binding limitations—and this was recognized by both yogis and alchemists.

…Unfortunately, among modern Ayurvedic physicians, he had not met any true yogis. There were truly great scholars, diligent religious adherents, experts in rare medicines and complex rituals, but no one pursued the goal of liberation through yoga. Such a combination was possible only in antiquity, when those who knew were also practitioners. Most likely, they lived much longer, could conduct the most complex alchemical and Ayurvedic experiments, and had time to enjoy their results. The cousin of alchemy and yoga—Ayurveda—describes truly fantastic formulas, but their realization requires a whole lifetime, literally absorbing vast knowledge. This ancient science demanded effort, selflessness, an understanding of the goal, pure luck, and longevity.

…In 2019, while answering students' questions, he began to interpret the Bhagavad Gita as an alchemical manual, explaining the parallels of the text with the various stages of the alchemical process. All eighteen chapters of the Gita correlated with the eighteen stages of the transformation of mercury. Incidentally, the milestones of the alchemical journey, the various stages of "taming" and "purifying" the metal before it acquired magical power, are also called "Samskaras."

Before the main alchemical process begins, mercury must undergo complex purification procedures. Similarly, yoga insisted on the preliminary purification of the body for the purification of the mind; like mercury, the mind is uncontrollable and fickle. Mercury is the main ingredient of alchemy, and consciousness is the key element of yoga. It is the mind that sets the goal and makes the decision to achieve it. "If a person is able to stabilize mercury and stabilize the mind, nothing remains that cannot be achieved," the *Hatha Yoga Pradipika* succinctly summarized.

One after another, the chapters of the Bhagavad Gita revealed a hidden connection with the alchemical process of the soul's transformation. In the first chapter, Arjuna is plagued by moral dilemmas, which are nothing other than impurities of the mind. Toxic and uncontrollable mercury must be "purified" before it can absorb the essences of each substance used for its purification. "Bombarded" from all sides, exposed to diverse ingredients, mercury is tamed by the wisdom of Nature. To become a super-element, it must surpass its own nature, die and be reborn again, purified and freed from old impurities. As does the mind.

The second chapter of the "Gita" speaks of the creation of a kshetra, or "field"—and Arjuna is prepared to receive the highest teaching. In yoga, before the body can receive the seed of supreme wisdom, it is purified by various methods. Without proper preparation, the field-body will not be able to "cultivate" the seed of developed consciousness. The alchemical stages of mercury processing echo this process: various washings, meltings, and roastings, called Shodhanas. "Creating the field" in alchemy is like preparing the soil for sowing. Before mercury is "fed" with mica and other substances, it is prepared to digest and assimilate them, and this preliminary preparation is called Kshetrikarana. The 13th chapter explains two categories: the "field" and the "knower of the field." The human body is called the field, and the self, the "I," which knows this field, is that very immortal substance dwelling in every body.

The dualism of the concept of "soul-body" seems endless, but the Great work of the yogi-alchemist can bring it to an end. If the body became immortal, the eternal spirit did not leave it. In the purification process, mercury is made to "sweat," and then the stage of Mardana begins. The steamed mercury is made into a paste-like substance, grinding and mixing it with various crushed herbs and acids; the vessels with all these ingredients are repeatedly heated. This causes the mercury to absorb the properties of the various herbal and mineral additives, imbibing their essence and "emotions."

In the course of his sadhana, a yogi observing all kinds of human emotions develops a very special type of empathy. He absorbs the essence of each emotion in all beings and becomes aware of them: the feelings of all animals, trees, insects, and people—on whomever the mind is focused. This stage is difficult, yet it prepares the practitioner for further spiritual evolution. Now that the mercury is filled with various kinds of essences of herbs and substances (their "emotions"), it can be stabilized. Solidification, or bandhana, stabilizes the mercury; it no longer evaporates. When Arjuna "strengthens" his emotional state, taming his doubts, he is ready for battle.

The fifth and sixth chapters of the Gita speak of samadhi. The "unconscious" phase of mercury processing resembles this highest yogic state. Now mercury experiences death without dying. Siddha-alchemists knew how to resurrect and revive it again and again.

The eighth chapter speaks of the end of one state and the beginning of another form of life. All this directly relates to the next phase of mercury transformation. Life and Death are merely states of transition, transformation. Just as the soul leaves the dead body, the essence of mercury leaves its previous form, leaving behind a residue. After the "swooning" and "killing" of mercury comes the most difficult part: its incineration to the state of bhasma. The result is "dead ash." Mercury loses its density, fluidity, and luster. It is "stunned," but no longer "overexcited." Some alchemical sources assert the opposite: it is impossible to "kill" mercury; it merely passes into an unconscious state, Mahamurchchhana or Mahasamadhi 226.

He himself was a witness to the most incredible experiments, where burned iron, gold, silver (any metal) were revived, "brought back from the dead." The point is that nothing actually dies; the essence of all Matter continues to live.

In the 10th chapter, Time shows the stunned Arjuna the myriad of Its faces and facets. In Alchemy, this stage corresponds to the "opening of the mouth" of mercury. The next, 11th chapter, vividly conveys the insatiable hunger of Time, devouring everything: universes, elements, and essences. Creating and destroying everything around, It remains unchanged. Thus, processed mercury "swallows" offerings without the slightest increase in its own weight. From gold to precious stones, from beans to mica, greedy mercury devours everything. The uncontrolled and insatiable hunger of the liquid metal fills the alchemist's heart with fear. Thus Arjuna was horrified by the universal form of Time, watching It swallow Universes.

At the next stage of processing, the "digestive fire" of mercury is intensified. Dipana, or "kindling," enhances the power of mercury, igniting in the substance a desire to "devour" other metals. The previous eight processes-samskaras purified the mercury. Now it is ready for the next transformation. At this stage, the intention with which all the work was carried out will determine the outcome of the experiment. Processed mercury can cure diseases—or help conquer the main ailment, Death.

The 16th chapter of the "Gita" describes the qualities of human nature (rajasic, tamasic, and sattvic), formed by the Gunas. According to this worldview, the Gunas have always been present and continue to be present in all beings. Goodness, calmness, harmonious balance, passion and activity, as well as ignorance and inertia, exist in everyone and everything in various proportions. The "dosage" and interaction of the Gunas determine a person's character and destiny. For each of the three psychotypes, its own path of spiritual development is prescribed.

Alchemical metal is also divided into varnas, in

In accordance with the class-based structure of society accepted in Hinduism. This may be a "low" or a "noble" caste. The purest mercury, called "Brahmanical mercury," is considered the most potent substance for attaining an immortal body.

A common misconception is that the entire Bhagavad Gita is supposedly retold in the final chapter. Without reading all the chapters, some proceed directly to the 18th, which is fundamentally wrong. It is impossible to reach supreme knowledge by a shortcut.

…One day, a distant relative visited him. Still unwilling to believe that his nephew had become a Guru of yoga, he was blunt: "And what have you achieved in yoga?" Without hesitation, he replied: "Not much…" The uncle exclaimed with satisfaction: "Ha! I knew it!" – and that was the end of the conversation. What more could be said? It is impossible to express the experience and realizations of an entire lifetime in a couple of sentences. Impatient readers who have not mastered the preceding chapters of the Bhagavad Gita will never understand the concluding chapter that summarizes them. Without the painstaking processing of mercury, without admiration and reverence for the mysteries of Nature, a person cannot withstand the properties of the final product of Alchemy – pure Brahmanical mercury.

The refinement of nuances in acquired knowledge and the process of its "digestion" are equally applicable to both mind and matter. To approach the divine state, the sadhaka must pass through all stages of Kriya Yoga with the same perseverance with which siddha-yogis subjected mercury to various stages of transformation. Having absorbed all that is necessary and discarded the superfluous, the processed mercury acts as a catalyst for eternal life. A life that can be dedicated to the ultimate knowledge of Creation and the Creator, His (or Her) roadmaps and blueprints. Knowledge is infinite, and in the process of learning, one can create one's own worlds and realities.

**Khechari, the Key to the Inner Heavens**

Of course, to outsiders this mudra seemed more than strange. The tongue had to be "curled back" and inserted into the throat. Guided behind the upper soft palate, it had to remain in this position throughout the entire practice, for several hours. For the uninitiated, it is a bizarre and strange trick, yet Khechari is a key condition for progress in higher yoga.

…Having first read about it, he persistently sought those who knew and practiced Khechari and could teach him. It was the criterion of steadfast sadhana and the key to yoga. All subsequent stages of Kriya Yoga were given to students only after mastering Khechari; it had to be mastered before the more complex phases. Considered mystical even among practicing yogis, this mudra was almost forbidden, and little was known about its properties, and few practiced it. Initially disheartened by its difficulty, he mastered this mudra after a year and a half.

He usually demonstrated it to new students during initiation. Some had heard of Khechari before; the most ambitious would begin intensive practice and usually master it after some time. It was also believed that karmic predisposition played a major role in the successful performance of Khechari, but how is this strange position of the tongue connected to karma? Why did this particular organ play a decisive role in continuing the yogic path? Certain hints were contained in old books, and some answers he discovered during his personal sadhana.

Interestingly, the name for the tongue, "Jihva," in Sanskrit also means one of the names of Shakti. It is believed that Shiva cannot exist without Shakti, and Her help is necessary to attain the state of Shiva. Shakti is power, desire, hunger, the thirst to attain, absorb, and assimilate. The satisfaction of desires has many forms, and Khechari Shakti is the most refined of all existing pleasures. In Kriya Yoga, Khechari mudra is the "bridge" between the lower centers and the "entrance door" of the soul, the Brahmarandhra, or fontanel at the crown of the head. Perfected, this mudra connects the lower and upper centers, physically and metaphysically. "Bram" means "mind," but this word can also be interpreted as a connection with Brahma the Creator.

The tongue acts as a receiving device, delivering amrita to the heart of the yogi. During the practice of kriyas, the brain secretes certain hormones, and Khechari prevents the precious drops of nectar from being burned in the digestive fire.

The tongue is important: it governs speech and communication, expresses thoughts, and perceives taste. What would happen if this organ did not exist? In yoga, the tongue is a magical instrument, literally leading to the transformation of the soul. The encoded meaning of Khechari was secretly transmitted by ancient masters to those who wished to practice true yoga.

Those who could not perform Khechari would eventually abandon the practice, finding it strange and difficult, and the mudra something unattainable. "Karmic predisposition" was mentioned above. True, there existed rather extravagant methods of achieving the mudra by cutting the thin connective membrane under the tongue. It is believed that the desired result can be achieved this way, but in the tradition of Lahiri Mahasaya, it is not encouraged.

"Cutting" the lingual frenulum and forcibly stretching the tongue can irreversibly damage the most delicate nerves in the tissues of the organ; the tongue must remain untouched. The factor of slowing down aging is an indicator of positive progress in this yoga. Conversely, rapid physical decline indicates that something is going wrong, a possible serious damage to the nervous system. He knew two people who underwent "circumcision" to achieve Khechari, and both aged with frightening speed, despite their practice of Kriya Yoga. Furthermore, the procedure is technically complex and was used only as a last resort.

In 37 years, he himself cut students' tongues perhaps seven or eight times, in cases where they could not progress in practice after many years of steadfast sadhana. Sometimes certain anatomical features prevented the tongue from stretching, and only cutting gave people a chance to move to the next stage of kriya… All procedures were successful, although no one had ever taught him this. Somehow he instinctively knew where to make the incision and how deep. Two or three microscopic drops of blood would appear, and a slight swelling might develop the next day. A tiny incision only 2 millimeters long, but this was usually enough to perform the mudra.
