However, all this was useless without special tongue-stretching exercises, practiced for a long time before the procedure. Without them, the tongue could not "stay" in place.

All yogic texts agreed on the uniqueness of Khechari; it is Shiva's favorite mudra, bestowing protection and the power of clairvoyance. Everything written about Khechari in the *Hatha Yoga Pradipika* is true. Although there was a subtlety: results came if all ten mudras mentioned in the text were successfully mastered. Then the yogi, drinking amrita with the help of this mudra, could avoid aging and death. The tongue "milked" the brain, which yielded the precious nectar, and only by becoming a magician could one obtain the magical elixir of eternal life. Performing the seemingly impossible condition, Khechari, seemed quite fair…

One secret yogic text said: "He who attains perfection in Khechari attains the state of 'Khecharatva'," – "moving in the sky." He never met a person capable of flying with the help of Khechari, and assumed this statement was more of a metaphorical comparison than a description of a process. The *Hatha Yoga Pradipika* described Khechari as a state of mind that has entered the Void. Everyone who attained Khechari moved in the heavens of their own mind.

Another treatise, the *Gorakshasataka*, summarized: "The mind moves in the ether (khe), because the tongue moves in the void (khe); this is the Khechari mudra, worshipped by all siddhas."

As a result of persistent practice, the yogi could achieve union with the consciousness of the Void. The expanded consciousness of the yogi absorbed higher knowledge and brought him into a completely new reality. Physical limitations lost their significance; the yogi transcended the body. From a practical standpoint, the mudra provided powerful protection from any possible physical harm, which was confirmed by a number of cases, including those he personally experienced.

Alchemical texts noted Khechari as an important condition for processing mercury. The liquid metal had to be processed exclusively while holding the Khechari mudra and a specific mantra, and the presence of people with physical deformities during this process was forbidden. Why? The texts gave no explanation, but emphasized this condition as mandatory. Alchemical manuscripts also recommended wearing necklaces made from human bones or the teeth of a black donkey, combined with reciting certain incantations during the "work" on mercury. However, in his experiments, he used nothing but Khechari. The slightest error in reciting the mantras could spoil everything, and any mistake could be fatal.

In those days, he lived in a cottage; there was plenty of space for alchemical experiments, and there was no shortage of volunteers. For nine months, he and his students continuously processed mercury with various herbs, preparing it for the final steam bath. The mercury had to be boiled in 40 kg of mustard oil until the liquid completely evaporated, and the substance was already at the 6th stage of processing. A fire was lit under a huge cauldron; all four participants in the experiment stood around: himself, his friend Ram Babu, and two other students. Each held their tongue in the Khechari mudra. The liquid was half evaporated when they noticed an elderly man running towards them. Convinced they were working on obtaining alchemical gold, Vyasa-ji, who had arrived unannounced, wanted to join the ritual.

He was missing one finger and, in addition, had some kind of growth on his head. According to the texts, physical imperfections made him absolutely unsuitable for participation in the process of alchemical mercury processing. At that time, none of them knew anything about the specific requirements of the alchemical process, and besides, there was no time to stop Vyasa-ji. An explosion occurred: the cauldron, the cylinder, and all the ingredients were destroyed; the remains of the mustard oil intensified the fire. The pale flame of the explosion was visible from afar; bushes and trees within a radius of 7 meters were burned to the ground, but the participants miraculously survived, as if the explosion passed through them without causing harm. The Khechari mudra had saved them.

The Guru's duty also included warning those on the path of higher yoga about the necessity of strictly following all instructions. The Teacher's words about the meticulousness of performing techniques are by no means general recommendations; they are a guarantee of survival. The same rules applied to Ayurveda and Tantra, where success depended on the precise dosage, correct preparation and administration of the remedy, combined with the right intentions. Yes, the main focus of his interests was yoga, but Alchemy and Ayurveda aided in sadhana. With the help of correct alchemical ingredients and effective tantric principles, yoga-sadhana yielded tremendous results.

All these disciplines carried a vast amount of Vidya, Knowledge, gleaned by him from the volumes of the Universal Encyclopedia. Spirit was the main element of Life, and its distillation and refinement is the most intense process. The secret alchemical element of yoga, Khechari, could transform a seeker into a siddha, one who reaps the fruits of achievement, exploring the heavens of the Self.

**Sister**

* * *

“…True relationships are not of blood, but of the heart. Like lovers or spouses: people not bound by blood ties can become examples of the strongest bond.”
— Devahuti Pathak

Devahuti

Apart from other factors, the presence of a brother and sister at the *smashan* ensured the success of summoning the Avadhut to the cremation ground (or to any other place) – this is precisely what Ganganath communicated during their memorable meeting. Nath said literally the following: "For the Avadhut to come, a brother and sister must live on the *smashan*." This was as sudden as if someone had unexpectedly shone a flashlight in his face. There was ample food for thought, and he did not then dare to interrupt his interlocutor to clarify this statement. Attempts to find confirmation in legends and myths yielded no results. It seemed this secret was known only within narrow yogic circles, and knowledge of the specific aspects of summoning the Avadhut was a privilege of the chosen few. The sacred energy of Shiva and Shakti in its pure, sattvic form – the secret ingredient, the key to the success of invoking the Deity.

And yet, in the fact that the "little sister," as he called Devahuti, was one of the key participants in this phantasmagoria, there was nothing surprising.

The ninth daughter and twelfth child in the family, she possessed hidden abilities that manifested themselves under the most unexpected circumstances. It seemed her presence was the key to many inexplicable phenomena, a condition for many events. Her prophetic dreams foretold large-scale changes, mystical premonitions were reinforced by the intuition of the heart, and it was she who found their future home, the *smashan*. Various Gods and astral beings came into her dreams, sending messages and blessings. She was destined to be by his side to maintain the fragile balance of hidden forces, to ground them; through her, Devi Braj herself watched, breathed, softened sorrows and illnesses. Perhaps all these assumptions are speculative, and yet, intertwining with subtle magic, life itself compelled the acceptance of this theory as a given. Under the lens of the *smashan*, their destinies were seen quite clearly; both were confident in the rightness of their chosen path. Yoga was his destiny, and her lot was service to the Teacher, the Guru, and the brother.

Short and laughter-loving, she was the strongest woman he had ever met. Like his mother, but softer, more "domestic." The women of their clan belonged to the warrior caste, were persistent, often stubborn, with a heightened sense of justice. Devahuti's character was cut from the same cloth as his own; yet he always knew: beneath the thin steel armor she donned in the world, she was soft-hearted and compassionate.

From early childhood, she was impossible to ignore; without any pressure, she displayed persistence and inquisitiveness, taking an interest in life and people. Wherever she found herself, her presence was felt by everyone and in everything. He was grateful to her for many things: for the palace she found; for the food prepared without any outside help, with which she managed to feed endless crowds of guests; for her sharp mind and jokes, which always lifted his spirits; for periodically shielding him from intrusive visitors. Having taken upon herself the task of maintaining his *sadhana* at any cost, she selflessly fulfilled this unspoken obligation – so that he could remain on the path of yoga, without burdening himself with worldly concerns.

Devahuti was responsible for his immaculate clothing: snow-white in summer, black in winter; the *kurtas* were ironed, folded, washed, and they continued to materialize daily. The vast household required attention and constant upkeep: a large house, kitchen, cow sheds, farm chores, sowing, harvesting, and fodder supplies. She was a born manager, skillfully managing all of this, including him, as he liked to joke.

…The first meeting with his future companion, friend, and confidante was unremarkable. He was nine, and they were living in Shivpuri when an aunt visited them with her daughter, seven or eight months old. He was the youngest among his brothers and sisters, and the appearance of a tiny cousin gave him a chance to become an older brother, protector, and mentor. For the first time, he felt responsibility and cared for a small, vulnerable being completely dependent on his guardianship. She followed him everywhere she could crawl on her own. A strong bond formed between them, and when his family moved to Gwalior, the aunt and Devahuti visited them quite often. Her name meant "call of the Gods" – and she could summon anything she wished, even as a child.

She was in middle school when she mistakenly drank poison from her father's home laboratory – and died a completely real death. Like many Indian doctors, her father experimented with various remedies, and their little pharmacy was cluttered with jars of ointments, bottles of syrups, and various boxes of pills. Doctors' children, as is known, are savvy about health matters, and Devahuti was well-versed in symptoms and medicines. That day, suffering from a severe cough, she swallowed a syrup from a familiar bottle, only fleetingly surprised by the strange taste. It turned out to be chloroform. She lost consciousness right in the living room, during a family dinner. At the hospital, her stomach was pumped urgently, but despite all measures taken, her pulse weakened and soon disappeared entirely, followed by her breathing… The weary doctor who came out of the operating room told the relatives that she was gone. However, about an hour later, she suddenly began to breathe, to the astonishment of the medical staff and the relief of the shocked family. Having fully recovered, she told an astonishing story.

…Sitting in that same parental home, she was looking out the window: a spaceship resembling a giant marigold flower was landing on their lawn, and she ran out of the house. The flying vehicle had seven seats, and six ancient sages, *rishis*, were already seated there. The seventh seat was intended for her. One of the *rishis*, with a white beard and kind eyes, asked her very gently: "Child, do you wish to come with us?" At that moment, she heard her mother crying and replied that she would like to stay with her mother for a while longer, to comfort her. The sage said: "Stay with her. When the time comes, we will return for you." She woke up disoriented, with a bitter taste in her mouth. The astral adventure lasted only a few minutes, but more than an hour had passed since her breathing stopped.

…As they grew older, they remained friends, still feeling at ease in each other's company. The path he had chosen, the stories he told, and the plans he shared, she considered righteous, truthful, and perfect.

The shock caused by the death of his elder brother Mahendra, and the almost demonstrative disregard of some relatives for their family's grief, made him abandon any career plans. The naked, blatant meaninglessness of life filled him with heavy sadness, and at times cold anger – and Devahuti, even as a child, sensed his state of mind, although the age difference prevented her from understanding the nuances of the changes taking place within him. And yet she tried to encourage him, to help him confront grief, difficult situations, and painful conflicts.

In 1990, she asked for initiation into Kriya, and after initiation began calling him Guruji, like the other students and visitors. In the evenings, when everyone had left, she would sit down at a low coffee table and talk about the day's events. Her voice was high and clear, like a bell, and her mood was most often quite cheerful. Sitting in the huge white house on the *smashan*, in inhospitable and unfamiliar lands, they laughed and joked, although the situation was not conducive to merriment. The chatter and anecdotes they exchanged, the songs from Bollywood films that they both loved and sang, cemented their friendship, which had begun long ago, on the day a chubby little girl began persistently following a unsmiling boy with deep, dark eyes the color of *jamun* berries.

Having moved to the *smashan*, she poured all her energy and love into that land, into the people who became more her family than hired workers. But her most sincere friends and admirers were the animals, and she was always surrounded by a devoted company of dogs, bulls, antelopes, and monkeys.

At the foot of the sacred Hill, she founded a just and prosperous kingdom. Those *rishis* from the spaceship could wait; she had too much unfinished business here. She arranged marriages and prepared lavish festive meals, dealt with financial troubles and quarrels among the workers, sang songs, and healed everyone with the firm hand of a hereditary medic. And she nursed him back to health when he fell seriously ill.

**The *Sadhana* of Life**

She was born into a most humble family, and her unremarkable youth suggested no deviation from the expected course of life. Like most girls of Braj, she was supposed to marry and start a family; the society of the North Indian province demanded nothing more from young women. But the strength of her will and devotion to her brother-Guru led them both to the most unexpected place.

In 1989, when Devahuti's elder sisters were married off, talk began of her proposed betrothal and a search for a suitable groom. But she flatly refused any further matchmaking proposals. Initiation into Kriya Yoga changed her attitude towards life, and she declared that she would follow the yogic path. The same one her cousin followed.

He warned her: "Think carefully. This path is not strewn with stardust and rose petals. In the countryside, even on the holy land of Krishna, most people are by no means filled with lofty thoughts and have poisonous tongues. A young man living under the same roof as an unmarried sister, even if he is a yogi, is an excellent target for the worst gossip and criticism." Devahuti replied that gossip did not trouble her in the least; she only wanted to follow him and support him in every way. Thus, the unspoken pact was sealed with mutual readiness to go through life on their own terms, regardless of society's expectations or censure.

When it became clear that Devahuti's choice was final, he said: "If you marry, you will have a husband and perhaps several children; but, setting out with me, you will become a spiritual mother to many. People will honor you for entirely different achievements, and you will live an unusual life, rarely granted to householders." And she chose that very life. Her mother believed in them both and supported their plan, while the other family members simply did not dare to object.

The beginning was not easy; overcoming numerous difficulties, Devahuti always took the initiative into her own hands. A new, unfamiliar place, an initially hostile attitude towards newcomers… Life in enchanted palaces was not at all like a fairy tale: there was an abundance of hard labor and sweat, funds were perpetually insufficient, and prospects for quick improvement or stability were absent. And yet, she showed not a shadow of regret or doubt, always finding ways to bring healthy, practical common sense into this new, strange life. And she did not at all mourn the predictability of the modest but secure future she had renounced for a life on the ancient *smashan*, amidst magnificent but crumbling palaces.

Her practice was unwavering, and her faith in the Guru was unshakable.  
He was her work, her responsibility, her family, and her Teacher.  
Once, she spoke of this once and for all in a casual conversation: "Perhaps I will not succeed in yoga, but I will do everything in my power to support Guruji's sadhana."  
And so it was.  

The hardships of the early years in Govardhan revealed her hidden abilities: it turned out she could command snakes, and they did not harm her. The area was teeming with enormous cobras; several times, while overseeing the work, she accidentally stepped on them. The snake would merely spread its hood, staring intently at Devahuti, and then slither away. Cobras, as is known, do not tolerate such treatment and usually bite their offenders, yet Devahuti remained unharmed. Then it became clear that she could command them, simply saying: "Begone!" – and the reptile would glide away. Sometimes, for amusement, she would order the cobra to return: "Come here!" The long, undulating gray-brown body would freeze, and after some hesitation, the snake would turn back.  

In one old photograph, Devahuti stands before the palace in a semi-trance, her eyes slightly upturned, her face serious and serene. A large snake is coiled around her neck, while several snake charmers in the background observe her with genuine respect… For her, all living beings were, above all… alive.  

Hospitable, open, fond of a sharp joke, Devahuti loved people, and they returned the feeling. Her unerring intuition usually allowed her to diagnose illnesses, and the knowledge she had gained from her father, a doctor, helped her heal them. But when he fell ill, her clairvoyance was as powerless as the doctors they consulted. He was losing weight rapidly, and she could not stop the inexorable decline.  

**The Mantra of Change**  

In the summer of 2007, he turned fifty, and a grand celebration was organized in Gwalior: loud, rather exhausting, but obligatory. So many people sought to express their respect, love, and admiration for him that it would have been impolite to deprive them of that chance. Even spiritual leaders (and they especially) cannot avoid certain social obligations. After the celebration, his body began to change, and these changes were so dramatic that friends and visitors became convinced he was seriously ill. From the sharp weight loss, his face became gaunt, his cheeks sunken, though he moved with his former confidence. With each day, the signs of physical ailment became more and more evident.  

The corset of muscles weakened; his lower ribs seemed to have collapsed inward into his ribcage. The changes were strange, inexplicable. All manner of specialists could not determine the cause of the malaise, as all tests came back normal. Some doctors suspected prolonged arsenic poisoning, as other hypotheses were refuted by test results. In general, there were grounds for such conjectures.  

The cradle of enlightenment, India, was in many ways no different from any other land. Nurtured by the worst qualities of human nature, ill will, intrigue, conspiracy, and envy flourish everywhere. In villages and cities, black magic is still widely practiced, and even blood ties are not a restraining factor. For the worldly person, there is no greater pleasure than to witness the suffering and death of a more successful, prosperous, or popular neighbor. Hypothetically, he did not fall into any of these categories, but he preferred not to share his thoughts regarding his own illness. Although he had theories about the nature of the malaise.  

After some time, he became so weak that Devahuti had to practically carry him on her back, helping him climb the stairs. The most difficult trial for him was not even the loss of muscle and increasing weakness, but a heavy, debilitating depression. And yet, certain yogic postures and mahamudras helped him keep from sliding into the abyss of despair and darkness. These exercises were known to be effective in cases of poisoning, and he struggled painfully and persistently with his body, forcing himself to complete the series.  

He was also tormented by a constant thirst, which suddenly passed a year later, in September 2008, when several Russian disciples made a pilgrimage to Mount Kailash. Having completed the ritual circumambulation of the holy mountain, they left his photograph near a small chorten, at the highest altitude accessible for climbing – as a sign of gratitude to the Teacher, who had opened new depths for them in the knowledge of the eternal, primordial Dharma. To the Teacher, who was their friend and who might now need any help at all.  

More than anything, this strange illness resembled a planned transformation, rather than the result of black magic, and he associated it with the mysterious mantra he had received on January 10, 2005.  

…After the Department of Forestry transferred the land adjacent to the shamshan to the management of the Siddha Siddhanta Academy, he began expanding the plantation and earthworks. Bulldozers were leveling a kilometer-long stretch of the future road; the days were full and long. There was little time, or rather, no time at all for anything but work. They slept little, in snatches. On the night after the roadwork was completed, Devahuti had a dream. Around three in the morning, when he had just begun to fall asleep after his night practice, she knocked on the door of his room, and then began shaking him by the shoulders: "Wake up, wake up! It was Parashurama!"  

In her dream, the great immortal was emerging from the shamshan pond after bathing. Standing on the steps, he was wringing water from the long locks of his jata; the falling drops were heavy and viscous, like honey. Devahuti saw herself handing him a folded cloth, something like a towel. The Avatar's gaze was fixed upon her; his eyes were black and devoid of any expression, yet his voice sounded soft. As is usual in dreams, mental perception grasped both the meaning of what was said and the subtext. "Tell Shailendra that the time for his initiation has come." The dream was significant, as Parashurama was the spiritual Teacher of their clan.  

Of course, he remembered the story of Parashurama's summons, told by the old Aghori many years ago, and since then he had learned much about the great warrior-ascetic, but he had never imagined that he would become part of his own story. Whenever this initiation was meant to happen, he was ready.  

Several months passed. The trees planted along the road laid out beside the Hill had taken root, and he was satisfied with the results. One day, after the practice was over, when the house had grown quiet and the night cicadas had finally put away their bows, he had a vision. In it, Devahuti was persistently leading him along the new road towards the plantation. The approaching dawn was slowly adding halftones to the palette of night; a gentle veil of mist enveloped the hill, and the melting darkness clung to its folds. Along the road on the slope stood several enormous gorilla-like ghosts, about 10 meters high, with huge, clawed hands folded across their chests. Silent, dark-skinned giants stared directly at them, but somehow it was understood: they posed no immediate threat. Following Devahuti, he approached a large stone in the shape of a cobra with its hood spread. A fire was burning before the stone.  

Straight out of the hillside, a young ascetic came towards them, wearing a white loincloth, no older than sixteen, carrying a staff and a kamandalu – a ritual water vessel made of coconut. Slender and tall, with very long, curly hair reaching his thighs, the youth radiated beauty and peace. The ascetic stepped towards them directly through the fire, and the bright flames tinged his skin a golden color. Then he felt Devahuti's hand on his shoulder; pushing him forward towards the fire, she stepped back. The youth gestured for him to sit, and he obeyed. Then the yogi (whoever he was) repeated an unfamiliar mantra three times, and each syllable was imprinted on his memory. The ascetic said: "Esco japa karo" — "continue repeating this japa." The words were in Sanskrit and had a deep meaning, yet the mantra was not connected with any tradition or deity known to him.  

The vision dissipated; coming out of the trance, he repeated the words until they became a part of him. Attempts to discover the origin of the mantra yielded no results. One thing was clear: this was a Guru-mantra, since anyone who transmits a mantra is a Guru – and this yogi with the golden skin was his astral Teacher. What he had received was called a "Swapna mantra" – a formula received in the state between sleep and waking. It became part of his sadhana.  

Years later, in his commentary on the ancient text "Gorakh Both" (The Enlightenment of Gorakh), he wrote: "Most teachings are given on the physical plane, but some advanced seekers can receive an astral form of initiation. A yogi who has reached a certain level in his sadhana can receive initiation from an astral teacher: he gives him a special mantra without revealing himself. The teacher who initiated the seeker into sadhana remains his guru until the appearance of the astral master. He comes to help the worthy practitioner advance further. Sometimes the physical and astral guru are one and the same. Ideally, a teacher present on the earthly plane should be able to lead the disciple into the astral and guide him there, as well as provide the disciple with protection in the astral world. After many years, the true identity of the astral teacher is revealed, and this signifies the realization of the Guru."  

He was describing his own experience. The identity of the astral Guru remained undisclosed, but he believed: to those who know the value of waiting, everything necessary eventually comes.  

…He and Devahuti belonged to the Bhargava gotra, to the lineage of Parashurama – perhaps that is why the Guru of their clan (kula) came to bless his initiation in person. In any case, the physical and mental changes began from the very day he started reciting the new mantra.  

By January 2008, his weight had dropped from 100 to 62 kg. Devahuti, who was far more worried about him than he was himself, insisted on consulting ever more specialists in search of a diagnosis and treatment. The doctors performed complex tests, but were unable to make a definitive diagnosis. Only his blood sugar level was above normal; otherwise, there were no abnormalities. Apart from the pain in his legs and the weight loss, he himself could not identify any other anomalies. He perceived the pain as a sign of the transformation that accompanies any serious change, but he did not cease his sadhana, no matter how ill or depressed he felt. At times he understood: if he had known in advance that the mantra would bring about such dramatic shifts on the physical plane, he would have feared such an effect.  

During these events, which could be called nothing other than a trial, he did not attempt to establish mental contact with the teachers, knowing that Shri Satyacharan Lahiri, Babaji, and the parampara were watching over him. And in a difficult moment, they would take care of him, removing obstacles, sending relief, as had happened before. He had already undergone serious trials several times, and was now confident that his life was not in danger.  

The very fact that Babaji had told him to begin a new practice on the day of his 57th birthday gave him courage. The Mahavatar had foreseen everything, and would hardly have taught him if he were not meant to survive. In moments of extreme weakness and depression, when hope for improvement was fading, he perceived this as a promise for the future.  

The mantra received on the astral plane affected the physical body in a certain way – and the body simply had to cope. Such was his assumption, and his intuition usually did not fail him.  

**Preparations**

Even when his communion with Hanuman became regular, he did not trouble the Deity with questions about his health. If a mortal is destined to know certain things, the gods will reveal them themselves. In one such conversation, Hanuman mentioned that Devahuti had little time left to live. "Little time" could have meant "decades," as the gods' concept of time is entirely different, and he did not dare ask for exact dates. She and Devahuti had long known that she would not live to see fifty, yet they never spoke of it. There was no need to discuss the future, for the present was ripe, tangible, and demanded constant attention.

When he told her of his conversation with Balaji, she did not lose her composure and appeared to everyone else as before: cheerful, witty, and smiling. She was advised to begin preparations for leaving the body, and although no precise deadline was set, they felt there was still time. Preparing for her departure, she was to visit several holy places, and they organized a series of short trips. Some whispered behind her back: "How can she travel while Guruji is so ill?!" — but the journeys were part of the impending transition. Moreover, she intended to pray for him as well.

Devahuti, who had never been particularly religious, began to "see" and "hear" deities in meditation after Shiva snatched her Guru from Vishnu's hands. It was then that the visions began. When she first claimed to be conversing with Krishna, he chuckled. Then he thought that they had both experienced something incredible, and that it was wrong to judge or reject it. Such things can only be lived through.

She felt a need for some hope or promise that no one but the ancestors or the Deities could give. Of course, she knew well that a new birth awaits everyone, but the transition from one life to another held great significance. Would her dying be painful and humiliatingly prolonged? Would she depart with dignity?

In the autumn of 2007, she journeyed to the source of the Narmada River, in the state of Madhya Pradesh. There, the river originates in a small reservoir known as Narmada Kund, located in the pilgrimage town of Amarkantak. This is a powerful place of Shiva, from which two other rivers, the Johila and the Sone, also begin. There is a belief that immortals, including their clan's ancestor Ashwatthama, still dwell in the vast jungles surrounding Amarkantak. Devahuti resolved to ask Ashwatthama's blessing to meet death without fear. She shared the details of the trip with no one, but certain signs after her return indicated that her prayers had been heard.

She also went to Chuchura, a place near Kolkata, where an ancient Kali temple built by her mother's clan stands. Shiva was the Kul Devata of his lineage, and the Fierce Goddess was the Kul Devi of her family. Kali and Shiva, deities dancing in the darkness, awakening the Light… Devahuti hoped to receive blessings and final instructions.

In August 2008, after visiting Bhubaneswar and Puri, she also reached Tarapith, a famous pilgrimage site in West Bengal, where the Goddess is worshipped as a mother nursing Shiva at her breast. Arriving in the afternoon, she immediately went to the temple. To the left of the entrance, by a red brick wall, in a designated sand pit, several sacrificial black goats were tethered to stakes, their glossy coats still wet from ritual bathing. A rite was in progress. Devahuti turned away, and compassion pierced her as the animal was killed with a single blow of a curved knife.

In the darkness of the temple, the flickering light of oil lamps reflected off the silver mask of Raj Bechwa, concealing the Goddess's face, yet this is not the main sacred image. Invisible to all, the true idol is completely covered by garlands of yellow flowers, and the large dark stone, the Devi Shila, could be seen only by priests during secret rituals. Devahuti asked Mother Tara for blessings and for certain other things she deemed appropriate to request. The temple was empty; no one disturbed her concentration.

She was already heading for the exit when a middle-aged woman, dressed in the black robes of an Aghori, suddenly turned to her: "Look, sister, everyone thinks that the third eye of Devi fell here from the sky. Idiots! The Goddess is present here in Her whole, undivided form — Akhanda Mata Ji!" And the sadhvi lightly touched Devahuti's forehead…

Just a few hundred meters from the temple, beyond ancient trees, the funeral pyres of the famous Maha Smashan, one of the most mystical in Bengal, were visible. Surrounded by forest, the ancient cremation ground had long been favored by ash-smeared tantrics and sadhus, who built huts and shelters right among the cremation platforms, adorning them with human and animal skulls. There was no need to inspect this landmark; Devahuti's own smashan awaited her.

Crossing a bridge over the sleepy Dwaraka, she headed back to the hotel with her companions… In India, it is believed that rivers flowing north possess supernatural powers, and Devahuti suddenly felt the Goddess's presence: the gentle movement of the water and the sounds of the city, inseparable from each other, as if the very inevitability of life's flow was woven from all of this at once. A pang of nostalgia stirred a desire to return home immediately, to the comfort of familiar daily life. But one more stop was necessary, in Kolkata.

The Kali Temple in Kalighat is one of the most important and powerful Shakti Peethas. Here, the right big toe of Sati fell from the heavens, though some Puranas claim it was the Goddess's face. Theories are of little significance, as most true Shakti Peethas are hidden, unknown, forgotten. Yet it is they that fill these places with the powerful force of creation, giving people the will to live and celebrate life by the very fact of their existence.

Unlike other depictions of this Goddess in Bengal, the image of Kali in the Dakshina Kalika temple is unique. Three enormous eyes gaze intently at pilgrims, Ma's long tongue is extended, and four hands made of pure gold hold a scimitar and the severed head of the asura king. The scimitar symbolizes Divine Knowledge, and the severed head represents the ego, which must be destroyed to attain liberation. With the fear-dispelling gesture of Abhaya Mudra, the All-Beneficent Mother of the World blesses everyone without exception…

Breathing in the fragrance of flowers and incense, absorbing the heavy yet grace-filled energy, Devahuti bowed, and having said to the Goddess all that she could and deemed necessary, felt a sense of peace; the fatigue from the long journey almost dissipated. At the temple exit, a small bottle of red ritual alta dye, usually applied to the hands and feet of a bride or dancer, suddenly rolled from somewhere, shattering right before her. The crimson puddle gleamed on the dark stone floor — it was an auspicious sign, a manifestation of Ma's grace and confirmation that the Goddess had heard her words. Those who accompanied Devahuti that day said her aura shone like a flame.

…A few months later, she undertook another journey to a place near Mirzapur, on the banks of the Ganga, in the state of Uttar Pradesh. Guidebooks claimed the temple there stood on the spot where Sati's big toe had landed, but in truth, another goddess dwelt there, Yogamaya, Krishna's sister, also known as Mother Vindeshwari. Vindhyachal was the place where the Goddess chose to remain after Her birth in the prison of Mathura. That day, Devahuti and her small group hurried up the hill, into the protective shade of the temple complex. The sun blazed mercilessly, and they felt relief only inside the temple. The entrance to the small chamber before the altar was narrow and cramped, forcing those entering to stoop. Although the inner space was tiny, the power of the presiding Deity was so great that Devahuti felt waves of energy passing through her body. This was a place for the removal of suffering and sorrow — Dukh Nashini. Whatever awaited her ahead, she was filled with resolve to accept the coming trials.

…That same year, she went to Gujarat, to the Dattatreya temple, located on the highest peak of Mount Girnar. Older than the Himalayas themselves, Girnar was an object of worship long before the first temples were built on the Indian subcontinent.

She began the ascent at 4 a.m., climbing 9,999 steps, and reached the mountain's summit by noon. For some time, Devahuti sat before the temple, contemplating the breathtaking landscapes. A Nath yogin tending the temple gestured for her to approach. A clay water pot for pilgrims stood in a niche by the entrance, and after taking a few sips, she turned to the Nath: "My brother is a yoga Guru in Govardhan. He is very ill…" "Sister, he will be healed. Guru Datta Himself watches over him."

Devahuti returned from the trip restrained, composed, filled with new energies, and joyfully immersed herself in her usual tasks. The daily efforts were part of her continuous service to the Guru, brother, and friend, and she intended to continue as long as she had the strength.

In the summer of 2008, he began to gradually gain weight; in October, he started exercising again; muscle tone slowly returned. Overall, the situation improved, and the next few years were almost serene, less heavy, more joyful. At least, that is how it seemed to visitors.

New students came, and discussions touched on topics never before addressed: the consciousness of the Earth, the nature of particles, the origin of the Spirit. Sometimes in the evenings, she and Devahuti would sing old Bollywood songs, and her high voice was as clear as the sound of a silver bell. The dogs were walked, the cows and guests fed, supplies stored. Her new passion was photography, and she enthusiastically mastered a brand-new digital camera. Devahuti did not appear worried, and both hoped there was still time.

**Departure**

Devahuti learned of her illness in the spring of 2013. Instead of following the doctors' insistent advice and agreeing to chemotherapy in a hospital, she decided to stay home and meet her fate. This type of cancer was almost untreatable. Devahuti hated any manifestation of self-pity, whether sincere or feigned sympathy. She did not want anyone to pity her and was determined to continue serving the Guru until the end. In the last months of her life, she ate little, and at times could not swallow food at all. Her melodious, cheerful voice disappeared, becoming a whisper. They tried various remedies, and from time to time, glimmers of hope arose.

Witnessing her departure was agonizing; he tried to shake off the chilling feeling of impending loss, but it tenaciously held his heart in its tentacles, gnawing into it. In the final days before her death, the house filled with people who had only recently learned of her illness. Visitors lay on the floor, sat in the rooms, wandered through the garden, bringing oxygen cylinders and useless medicines. Students and friends tried to support him, to preserve their own crumbling world, but very real death was already busily entering the house, making its way to Devahuti's room.

The night when her Spirit departed, leaving the motionless body on

crumpled sheets, was endless and terrible. When Devahuti breathed her last, he collapsed with fever and weakness and slept for several hours, rising only to attend the cremation, which was conducted right there in the garden of the house.

The pain was both sharp and numbing; his entire being pulsed like an open wound. The fog of loss was tangible and thick, and moving through it was difficult; nothing moved either, except for the endless stream of visitors. Burdened with grief, they shuffled as if underwater, dragging their feet, stooping. Only the chipmunks and birds, usually fed on the patch of ground before the kitchen, scurried about with their former energy, but even their chirping and twittering seemed muffled.

For thirteen days, leaning his back against the wall, he sat motionless in the empty room from which all the furniture had been removed. People came and went, offering condolences, bowing to him and to the large black-and-white photographic portrait of Devahuti, where she looked like a Bollywood film star. Hundreds of flower garlands formed mountains before the oil lamps, and flower petals were everywhere: on the steps of the house, on the carpet, in the teacups. Perhaps the roses and marigolds were trying to mark the path her soul had left behind. But where had she vanished to? Spirits occupied with their own affairs could not help with this. Moreover, the unspoken code of astral etiquette forbade inquiring about newcomers, who needed time for “debriefing” and rest. There was no central information service in the astral realm, and finding Devahuti seemed impossible.

A few days after the cremation, a well-known astrologer sent him a message: Devahuti would be reborn here, in Braj. Quite soon, and most likely, in her own family. By the time of this prediction, all her brothers and sisters already had children and grandchildren; the clan was steadily growing, and her soul had the opportunity to choose new parents… The only correct reaction to such news was to wait. And he continued to wait.

In the evenings, after the visitors had left, the house filled with a frozen silence. Even the scandalous monkey, who considered herself a princess, grew quiet in her cage; when he gave her the traditional water bottle for the night, she looked dejected. He realized for the first time that he had never turned to anyone for help. People and events had always arranged themselves in such a way that obstacles dissolved on their own, and he attributed this almost occult magic to the power of the yogic lineage to which he belonged. The Teachers had always provided support, but what could he ask of them now? *Sadhana* is a bridge to Knowledge, not a painkiller. And yet, practice was the only connection to that other side where his sister now was.

One night, while he was still in *samadhi*, Hanuman appeared in his now-familiar guise as an old man. The deity was in excellent spirits; before they “left” the room, his divine companion nodded at the old bicycle in the corner: “You won’t need that there…”

…He found himself climbing a steep hill; the dry, yellowish earth crumbled under his feet. The landscape was sparse and deserted, not a tree or blade of grass in sight. Reaching the sun-scorched plateau, they saw a gigantic complex of magnificent buildings, whose architectural style was neither Indo-Saracenic nor Victorian; the structures were clearly very ancient, built of yellowish basalt finished with a thick layer of plaster.

The image shifted, and he saw an intricate system of inner courtyards and enormous halls, some covering 600, 1500 square meters, with high ceilings of 15–18 meters. Somehow he could see the buildings both from the outside and from within. These palaces were built by giants and for giants, and now stood empty, inhabited only by the muffled echo of the wind.

The room they entered was vast, perhaps 50 meters across and 100 meters long, and everything in it was disproportionate. The interior seemed copied from *Alice in Wonderland*, revealing a sudden change in scale. There were no windows, only a huge door slightly ajar leading to an inner courtyard. Spanning the entire width of the room was a giant wooden bed over 2 meters high; wooden steps were placed against it.

Devahuti was sitting on this enormous bed; beside her lay several open comic books. Seeing him, she gasped with joy, and he could no longer contain himself: “When will you return?” – and in her melodious, clear voice she answered: “In a little while…”

In the room was a thin, dark-skinned ghost dressed in white garments, assigned to Devahuti as a servant. Seeing him, the ghost was overjoyed, respectfully saying: “Oh, I have heard your name so many times, and today I am fortunate to receive your *darshan*!”

It seemed only moments had passed when Hanuman said to him: “You should not stay here too long. It is time to return.” And he immediately found himself in the bedroom, in the house at the *smashan*; the sounds of the night were soft and soothing, his heart no longer bound by numbness. The emptiness gave way to a fragile but growing certainty that he would see Devahuti again.

This brief astral meeting gave him hope for a new beginning, whatever it might be. Of course, returning to the past was impossible, yet he prayed for the opportunity to guide and protect Devahuti in her next earthly incarnation. She deserved joy and a life without adversity and sorrow, for their magical island, the old *smashan*, awaited her. The possible circumstances of her birth and the exact date of her forthcoming incarnation were unknown, but already planned.

**The Return of Shakti**

The days were still cold, and two small heaters emitting weak streams of warm air were unable to warm the entire hall. The woolen shawl that Devahuti usually took out of the cupboard in winter was draped over his winter jacket. He did not remember who had wrapped his shoulders in the fine *pashmina* this time, who had served lunch. The taste of food was indistinguishable, and he ate automatically, feeling neither hunger nor satiety.

All who visited the *smashan* in those days and months felt an almost physical heaviness of the hours and minutes; the silence of the house was oppressive. Some visitors timidly began a conversation, which most often ended in a pause that dissolved into the same deafening silence that had greeted them at the gate. Absorbed in thought, he was absent, dwelling somewhere in the subtle worlds.

The routine once established by Devahuti was meticulously followed by the workers and servants, and the first months after her departure required no special intervention. They tried not to disturb him with trifles.

…It was around 11 o’clock in the morning; he was sitting alone in the *darshan* hall. The bored monkey in her cage was shrieking upstairs, demanding company and attention. Immersed in contemplation, he relied on the infallibility of the intuitive unconscious. As a Guru of the *parampara*, he had to meet the souls of all disciples who had left their bodies. By the decision of the Teachers, and sometimes considering the wishes of the departed, he could help them attain a new, “suitable” birth. Being merely an intermediary, a conduit of the higher will, he was nevertheless the first whom these souls met on the astral plane. Being not only his sister but also his disciple, Devahuti had come to her Guru immediately after death, literally throwing herself into his arms. And then he had ceased to “see” her.

The *Naths* knew of her, she had seen Babaji, and Hanuman himself watched over her in the astral world. Whoever her Spirit was, it was special…

His reflections were interrupted by a low voice, belonging to Babaji. The Mahavatar commanded him to go immediately to Govardhan Hill, to the very place where he had received the mysterious mantra. Then, in the vision, Devahuti had led him there; now he went alone. The late morning was still foggy, yet the sleepy monkeys were already listlessly settling along the edges of the path, and the voices of the birds grew more confident.

…Devahuti stood before a large stone resembling the head of a cobra with its hood spread. Beside her, slightly stooped, stood the young ascetic, the very one who had given him the Guru mantra. Their bodies were neither transparent nor blurred, and looked quite material. Devahuti in her favorite attire: a cream-colored *kurta* with maroon *salwar* and scarf; he could make out the stitches on the fabric, saw tiny pigment spots on her cheekbones. A strand of black hair was tousled by the wind. She was neither a hologram nor a ghost.

Two frightened peahens darted past and disappeared behind the bushes, pursued by the rustle of dry leaves.

Numerous encounters with spirits had taught him never to initiate communication first; astral beings knew better when to make contact. The nameless Guru spoke, and the words reached his consciousness: “She is returning to this world again… contact Neha, your niece.” The expression on Devahuti’s face was serious and somewhat expectant, but she remained silent. And then, adjusting her hair with a familiar gesture and smiling slightly, she vanished together with the ascetic, as if someone had abruptly turned off a projector. An uncontrollable tremor shook his body in a wave of energy that passed through him, and the emptiness that had made his heart bleed with pain for all these months disappeared.

That same day he called his niece, saying they needed to talk urgently. In private. Neha rushed to Govardhan from Mathura with her husband Gaurav, thinking something extraordinary had happened. They wondered if they had done something wrong, for he had never called them. He never called anyone. He did not even have a mobile phone, and used the one that had belonged to Devahuti. In the first weeks after her death, the phone lying on the coffee table in its pink, sparkling, rhinestone-studded case rang incessantly. Many refused to believe she had died, and wanted to hear from her herself that it was someone’s cruel and crude joke, a mistake. But there was no mistake; she had indeed left her body, but somehow managed to arrange a swift return. A few months before her departure, Devahuti had been urging the newlywed Neha and Gaurav not to delay having a child – and her urging had had its effect. Neha was in her seventh month of pregnancy. He had not known about this.

…He informed Neha and Gaurav that they would have a daughter, and that he would personally oversee the child – in every sense. This girl belonged to him. The young couple was astonished: actually, they had dreamed of a son. And they wanted to discuss this with their relatives. But after a while, they agreed: Guruji would have the decisive voice in all matters concerning the girl’s interests, and would become her legal guardian.

The child was born on April 17, 2014. Delegated to the hospital, a close friend and disciple of the Guru, Deepak Kumar, took the newborn in his arms an hour after her arrival – and she smiled at him warmly and knowingly. She was named Yashashwini, the name meaning “Victorious,” “Glorious.” It was both magical and yogic – so was named one of the energy channels of the body, control over which awakens leadership qualities in a person.

Frequent visits to the child gradually dispelled the depression. Life triumphantly rang with the bells of children’s laughter, and all who knew the story of the magical return

consecration, they speculated about the life plan destined for Yashashwini.

Some, however, who dismissively regarded this entire story as mere wishful thinking, were fully entitled to their opinion. Yet certain marks on the child’s body confirmed the return of Devahuti’s soul, along with other signs pointing to the strong potential of her destiny, together with an unknown life mission. Her reincarnation was extraordinarily swift, and there had to be a reason for this. He believed that the girl’s purpose would be revealed in due time. The echo of the future already wandered through the rooms of the old house.

…Yashashwini was about eighteen months old and walked more or less confidently when her parents placed her on the terrace steps. Without hesitation, the child chose Devahuti’s daily route, which she had followed for many years. The girl headed to the kitchen, then to the cattle yard, and, having walked past the cowsheds to the fountain in the garden, paused briefly before the new memorial pavilion, built just a couple of months before her birth. Then, looking at the flame flickering in its depths, she hurried back to the house.

…The white pavilion-mausoleum stands in the midst of a lush garden. The small chapel, erected directly on the site of Devahuti’s cremation, is surrounded by several amla trees, whose small, green, sour fruits delight the birds and chipmunks. Inside, it is serene and quiet. By tradition, a lamp fueled by oil burns there around the clock, and a dish of *prasad*, special food for the spirit, is renewed daily. Such veneration sustains the strength of the departed one’s spirit, and the food appeases their hunger. In India, the ritual is considered extremely practical, as the departed one’s other desires may be fulfilled after their most basic needs are satisfied. There is not a trace of contradiction in this tradition. Souls already embodied in new bodies continue to receive support and benefit from the ancient ritual.

Every evening, having finished the night *puja*, he stops before the pavilion and communicates mentally with his sister. Sometimes he offers a prayer, sometimes he shares a silent joke. From time to time, Yashashwini plays before the chapel, in which the flame lit in honor of her soul’s strength flickers. The spirit, now dwelling in a restless child’s body, is peaceful and never bored, for a new story is being told to it, a story continuing here, on the land of liberation, on the lap of the Dark Lord of Govardhan Hill, in the loving embrace of Devi Braj.

I waited for death,
I wept so much
that my tears dried up,
abandoning me.
With empty and hard eyes
I sat on a stone
surrounded by sufferings —
only they
did not leave me.
Suddenly someone called: “Are you tired?
Weary one, will you come with me?
It is not time to stop,
And this
is not the end of your life and the world!”
I did not believe my ears,
that even now someone could
support me,
when stumbling itself
had become a habit,
a destiny…
That gentle voice
frightened me.
It was strange,
for I considered
this wounding and thorny
path, this stony path,
my
sole companion…
The voice
calmed me:
“Come with me, and share
your longing —
I will share
your sorrow with you.
I will remove the suffocation,
free you
from suffering.
Think of the companion and of destiny,
Follow me along the path,
It simply must be walked.
The path is not just a trail,
It will lead you
through exhalation and inhalation
Into the depths of the soul…”
Devahuti Pathak, 1993

Devahuti, Diwali, 2010
with Yashashwini, 2015

with Neha, Gaurav, and Yashashwini, 2023

Gurukula

* * *

“It is said that the children of the *gurukula* (the teacher’s house) are of five kinds: the children of *ayi*, the children of *vileshwara*, the children of *vibhuti*, the children of the *nathas*, and the children of *yogishwara*, and all of them possess different qualities.”
Fifth Instruction; *shloka* 43 of the *Siddha Siddhanta Paddhati*
Shri Gorakhnath

Children of the Mothers

None of the Teachers in the lineage of Babaji belonged to the Nath tradition. Kriya did not require the initiations prescribed for the Nathas, but traces of this teaching were evident. Those who could practice this yoga were called “children of the mothers,” “born of women.”

The ancient text *Siddha Siddhanta Paddhati* enumerates several types of students who were welcomed into the Guru’s house. They formed a family, a *gurukula*, where students received knowledge and sometimes performed household chores. This is part of the tradition, and he regarded it with respect. Visiting the Teacher’s house in Benares, he, on a par with other students, sometimes helped with the housework, considering it an honor. The Teacher lived alone, and such help was necessary; moreover, the students were part of the *gurukula*, his family. But years later, when his own household began to require unceasing support and daily maintenance, he preferred to hire workers. Students should only practice yoga.

All who came to him for knowledge were children of *Ayi*, children of the mothers. Ordinary people who had chosen an extraordinary path. The others mentioned in the ancient text were *siddhas*, created by the power of the Guru. *Vileshwara* were naked ascetics, *digambaras* or “sky-clad” Naga Babas, and the children of *vibhuti*, the Aghoris, Pashupatas, Nathas, were so called because they smeared themselves with ash.

He regarded those who came to him for guidance primarily as children of Mother Earth. Nature herself encouraged all human achievements, providing the components and equipment for any experiment and investigation, including yoga. Observing people, he rejoiced in their progress, the awakening of dormant abilities, their becoming what they were meant to be — knowers. The students of the Siddha Siddhanta Academy represented all strata of society: among them were doctors and teachers, farmers and merchants, jewelers and politicians, military personnel and engineers, scientists and police officers. They were Hindus, Chinese, Jews, Ukrainians, Italians, Finns, Brazilians, Russians, French, and Germans. What mattered was that they all aspired to yoga, and this desire stimulated a rethinking of their own identity.

The experiment of Kriya yoga, conducted by the immortals over centuries, is but one of many experiments on human society. The initiation of Lahiri Mahasaya lifted the veil of exclusivity and secrecy that had so long concealed the teaching. Of course, the practice itself was not revealed to the uninitiated; the transmission of knowledge was still carried out individually. Before the beginning of the spiritual journey, the Guru weighed and assessed each one’s potential, for not all were ready. The *parampara* is selective. But one thing remained certain: the *kriya* given by the immortal Teacher bore within it the seeds of immortality, and this definitely helped to prevent old age and even death. To a certain extent. If Babaji himself had attained the highest of yogic states, then the keys to immortality must be hidden in the techniques of His teaching.

Why did all these people want to become his spiritual children? Why, regardless of age, gender, and nationality, did a close, trusting friendship with the Guru eventually arise in each one? After all, he taught only breathing techniques; the rest of the time passed between endless cups of tea and varied discussions, exercises in the gym, and short but powerful nightly rituals.

The answer is simple: trust grew out of friendship, and thus a unique bond was established between Guru and disciple. It is impossible to feign a sincere interest in people’s destiny and spiritual development; the teacher must be genuinely interested in the person he is teaching. The student must possess potential, a spark of a certain adventurousness — and the mutual trust that develops over time is the most important ingredient in the alchemy of teaching. And then this currency inevitably turned into the gold of faith. Faith in the Teaching, in the Teacher, in the rightness of the path once chosen.

Probabilities and Predictions,
Motives and Expectations

What would have happened had he continued his military career? The result of such a choice remained in the gray zone of probabilities. Most likely, having followed the trajectory of an army career, he would have turned into a grumpy brigadier, or perhaps attained a higher rank. Probably, he would have spent his evenings in the officers’ club, cursing stupid civilians, sipping whiskey surrounded by shabby plastic chairs, unrealized opportunities, and similarly disillusioned colleagues.

According to the horoscope, even a military career would have been unsuccessful. Of course, Vedic astrology cannot predict the future with absolute accuracy; this ancient science contains unique information, but correct interpretation is crucial. Only a few can “transmit” information so that the inquirer can hear and understand. Not everyone pays attention to the warnings and recommendations of the stars; people want to hear only positive predictions. Since the planets indicate possibilities and tendencies, it is necessary to heed their hints. By the time he had become a Guru, many astrologers spoke of numerous signs pointing to the spiritual path destined for him, but all this had already happened. The past does not tolerate subjunctive moods — that is the prerogative of destiny. He had already begun the journey, and the “forecasts” turned out to be yesterday’s news. Only one royal astrologer from Gwalior, a man nearly a hundred years old who still retained a passion for his profession, cast his horoscope with high accuracy, and most of the predictions came true.

The horoscope was cast when he, as a homeless Guru, lived in an ashram in Vrindavan, when the palaces from visions were still dreams, a whisper of promise, fragments of a misty future. The old astrologer then said: “This young man will live in a huge ashram near Mathura” — and the prediction came true. The period of Rahu’s transit in his chart indicated an influx of visitors from distant lands: “Numerous foreigners, *angreze*, will study under him…” And this proved accurate, but in 1992 no one could have imagined such a development. The students who had asked the astrologer to read their Guru’s natal chart tried to clarify something, but he snorted: “Don’t get your hopes up, he won’t waste time on ignoramuses like you! He will be surrounded by millionaires!” But those days were still ahead.

Honestly, he never thought he would become a Guru; he simply wanted to practice to the best of his ability and attain realization, whatever that might imply. At that time, he did not particularly understand the role of a non-religious Guru, thinking that a Guru of Kriya yoga was someone who transmitted various techniques, like an instructor. Only later did he realize: this role is by no means exhausted by teaching methods and techniques, and the practice itself is but one of many steps on the path of spiritual development.

Like the late Shri Satyacharan Lahiri, he received visitors daily. The questions varied, and for correct guidance, concentration and clarity of consciousness were required. Realized consciousness. He was aware of the degree of responsibility for each person’s destiny. To guide and console those who sought advice or peace was akin to balancing on a tightrope stretched over a karmic abyss. The consequences of wrong advice could be extremely serious for both the teacher and the student.

At first, when the Teacher asked him to answer visitors’ questions during *darshans*, he felt constrained and tense. That he was proclaimed Guru of the *parampara* after two years of practice was a bewildering surprise, almost shocking. However, the uncertainty of the state soon gave way to confidence.

He learned to see the deep causes of each question; while the words were still balancing in the air, they were already forming into an answer. People dressed the skeleton of doubt in words, veiling vague, unformed desires with long phrases. Sometimes syllables hid deep remorse, and intentions glimmered within the phonemes. The more answers to questions he gave, the easier it became to see the nature of each question.

Shaniya and the sought solution. The invisible door opened with each answer, and through it poured a stream of truth. An answer intended for a specific person could resonate with the unspoken thoughts of others present, and standing in the midst of this field of knowledge, he gathered an astonishing harvest and shared it with everyone. This knowledge was a property of Prana; it was diffused in the very air that everyone breathed.

The collective portrait of the seekers was incredibly diverse and included both those who had never performed any asanas and experienced yoga instructors. He had nothing against the latter. By introducing people to a healthier lifestyle, familiarizing them with various ways of feeling bodily comfort, yoga teachers were doing a good deed. They helped people find peace of mind, to feel the forgotten joy of physical wholeness. Having achieved the highest results in the yoga of the body, some began to realize that beyond the asanas there was something more, and they began the search for a higher yoga.

Having heard the silent call, learning of it from friends or through word of mouth, they found the path to the smashan, to him. Experienced in the art of asanas, the teachers were highly disciplined and worked diligently. Their advantage lay not in superior physical fitness, but in a firm faith in discipline and perseverance, in the determination to develop new astral muscles and crack the code of enlightenment.

Problems appeared after a couple of years (and sometimes earlier), when some realized that the practice of Kriya is for life. And it was rather “practicing” them, and not the other way around. For some yoga instructors, the bonuses began to seem dubious – this Kriya could not be passed on to others and, consequently, it did not contribute to commercial success, was not a commodity, and took up too much time. Thinking and social connections, habits, aspirations, and priorities changed, and goals were reset to zero. Life seemed to be getting out of control, rather than being harmonized and balanced. Some were troubled by the lack of visible signs of progress, and they insisted on seeing the “promised” results, believing they had proven their loyalty to the practice long enough and were ready to enjoy its fruits.

Observing people over many years, he clearly saw: success in this yoga depended on motivation. There were people who resembled warm champagne: as soon as the bottle was opened, there was abundant foam, but what remained of the bubbling liquid barely covered the bottom. Overly enthusiastic and exalted representatives at the beginning would “deflate” after some time. Those who were truly passionate about yoga did not flaunt their feelings or make plans, but moved slowly towards their intended goal.

The motives that brought people varied. Some had not the slightest idea about the aspects of higher yoga and came along with friends. Others were intrigued by the personality of the Teacher, the mysticism of the practice and the place itself. And some needed an anesthetic, a painkiller for the torment of reality.

Some of the Indian disciples belonged to the third or fourth generation of Kriya Yoga practitioners. For them, it was a family tradition, not a personal choice. Initiated by Shri Satyacharan Lahiri himself, their grandparents were already advanced in years when he himself was declared the successor.

Only a few realized the ultimate goal from the very beginning. Remembering the years of his own search, doubts and fears of never finding a teacher, the indifference, misunderstanding, and skepticism of so-called well-wishers, he supported all practitioners. Not only in their striving for yoga, since he himself never considered that the sole purpose of life. As a rule, no one knew what the achievements would be. Beginners hoped to experience Samadhi, Nirvana, bliss, whatever that state was called, and in most cases had not the slightest idea what could happen to their bodies and psyche. As a rule, their expectations were radically different from the realities of the sought-after result. Again and again he described the side effects and aspects of states after samadhi, and the picture was far from glamorous.

Students spoke of intriguing things read in books: about the rise of Kundalini energy through the Sushumna channel, about piercing the chakras… He listened patiently and with interest, and at the end gently informed them that no one had chakras yet, they must first develop them. He taught them, as an artist teaches an apprentice: to stretch the canvas on the stretcher, to prime it, to carefully mix paints and make several sketches before beginning to paint an epic canvas.

But overall, he was proud of his disciples, their steadfastness, will, and unwavering self-knowledge. Persistent practice changed the contours of the future. Each had to discover the essence of their own Self, which wore such different masks. Like a coat rack completely hidden by clothes, the core of the personality was concealed under layers of masquerade costumes, and he taught people to get rid of unnecessary coverings and false ideas about their own nature.

**The Artist**

Name withheld, Bali, Indonesia

…Having first heard of him from his disciples, I simply listened, without trying to inquire about details. These people were serious practitioners, not schizoterics preaching homemade esoteric dogmas, and I treated them with respect. What they described sounded intriguing even to me – and in 20 years of living in India, I had seen many amazing things. In short, my acquaintances strongly advised me to visit this Teacher.

By that time, I had already left the ISKCON community, received tantric initiation, and was zealously performing tantric sadhana, yet a feeling of strange hunger was growing. No, I was not a yogi, I had always been a devotee, a bhakta, and that is what defined my spiritual essence. However, bhakti did not quench my hunger. Remember the four types of believers described in the Bhagavad Gita? Some are the afflicted, others seek benefit, others knowledge, and finally, there are the wise. Being a mixture of all four types, I sought above all true knowledge. In any case, I did not crave any exotic spiritual experiences, as I had already seen much, and yet understood too little. It was necessary to change the record. I craved clarity, not new pieces and glimpses of Truth; I needed to assemble Her puzzle. I know it sounds too general and idealistic – but many begin the next stage of knowledge in this way. Each person can digest exactly as much knowledge as they are allotted to assimilate, and I wanted to test my limitations and the strength of the material I was made of.

This Guru came into my dreams twice before I finally made it to Govardhan. In one of the dreams, he stood in a garden surrounded by disciples examining the palms of his hands and marveling at the absence of life lines on them. Trying to see his hands, I craned my neck, and the Guru suddenly addressed me: “You should learn to read the lines of your own destiny.” Stunned, I jumped up in bed, realizing that the time for a visit had come.

…The chaos of dusty streets and the noisy bustle of the city were left behind; the greenery of the trees behind the rusty gates was calming and almost refreshing, the air smelled of datura flowers, peacocks flashed among the trees. The place looked exactly as it did in my dream: majestic mausoleums, a pond with steps, and a dense garden with a huge white house surrounded by hibiscus bushes and fig trees. I even stopped being nervous. But only until I entered the house and saw him.

Later I learned that he always wears dark glasses so that people, especially newcomers, can feel more comfortable. And also because one old farmer died after looking at him for too long. Although that was probably local folklore. In any case, I felt relief not seeing his eyes behind those black mirrored sunglasses.

Describing him is rather difficult. Not because of a lack of distinguishing features (oh, his features are more than exceptional!), but because of the power he radiated. Tangible, heavy, almost frightening, it cut through the silence. I still did not soil my dhoti (although it almost happened later), but bowed in prostration and spoke in broken but quite understandable Hindi, explaining who I was and how I found him. He listened.

Tea was served; for me, the faces of the Indians and Europeans present were blurred, forming only a background. He asked a couple of questions in English, and I answered, desperately trying to explain everything at once: my path, my search, my motives, myself. But it all seemed unimportant, and he echoed this thought, saying somewhat absently in response to my incoherent tirades: “Dvaita, Advaita, Tantra – none of this is truly important. Only your personal experience at this moment in time matters. You are an artist, gifted with a special view of reality, and you must be intuitive. Tell me, what do you see and feel here and now…”. And he continued his conversation with another visitor, not waiting for my answer.

And then I saw… I can only give rough, fragmentary sketches, describing what I saw in general terms. No watercolor of words can convey the battle scene of Life, painted with broad strokes of Light. I found myself surrounded by clots of this condensed Light, although no one else present in the room seemed to notice it. Eight or nine fluid, constantly changing beings hovered behind the sofa on which the Guru sat. With many arms, woven from iridescent light, translucent, luminous, with white, almost neon outlines, these beings (or entities) watched those present. They knew everything and saw everyone, they gave some kind of energy or impulses that filled the breaches in the torn causal bodies, karana sharira, of people – and took something in return…

Listen, I am no novice to miracles, and I know about different points of assembly in our perception. And I understand perfectly that when entering altered states, our attention passes through different levels of perception. First, people see familiar images, things they can identify according to their level of consciousness… Being an artist and a practicing devotee of Lord Krishna, I assumed these beings were yoginis or dakinis, goddesses, shaktis – and then my mind began to deceive me. The subconscious whispered – these are not yoginis, but ancient beings of light, terrifying and wrathful.

Then the mind fell silent. And without the aid of any logic, I understood: these Shaktis were forms of Prana, the sacred forces of Life, guarding and guiding travelers. Of course, only those who knew the destination and had maps of the terrain. Walking these paths, one could enter different dimensions, if it was permitted. These were channels opened (or laid?) by ancient siddhas and sages, leading to different worlds. Using the channels of Prana and Light, mortals (certain mortals) could move to different floors of the Universe.

I tried to remain calm, knowing this was not a hallucination, I had been given a darshan, a gift of revelation, I had been allowed to see a portal to other worlds. There I saw pure Power and Knowledge that had fully known Themselves and had no need for a knower. In their presence, the witness disappears, itself becoming Knowledge. But, disappearing, you still remain. Nothing can take you away from your Self…

My interpretations of the vision ended, replaced by fear. We humans are always bound to form. Even our Gods are mostly depicted in human, or rather superhuman, but still recognizable forms. Form is called Nara, and it limits the human mind, since our perception presupposes form. I was horrified because I could no longer recognize forms, nor associate my concepts with them. I perceive reality in volumes – it is the artist’s curse… And I prayed, prayed to see the dear and recognizable form of the Lord, but

not this naked and merciless, annihilating truth  
of my own limitations, microscopic in infinite  
Time and Space. Who can bear  
the truth of Time?

Somehow pulled out of the body, I was shoved back  
into it. The entire trip took a few minutes, and the same force  
that squeezed me out like toothpaste from the tube of the body  
landed me again on the red carpet before the Guru. His form  
was certainly human, but I was no longer deluded  
about who he truly was. Oh no, he was not the Master  
of these powerful beings, these luminescent  
forms; they were here voluntarily. He was a conductor,  
catalyzing various forces – and these energies came to  
him because he is the rarest of mortals, able to see  
them, play with them, confront them…

Returned to the world of familiar concepts and sensations, I  
rejoiced in the cozy, habitual surroundings – just a room,  
I am just one of the visitors. Perhaps I sobbed and  
trembled – and even if so, no one reacted. The Guru did not  
ask whether I still wished to receive initiation into this  
yoga; he told me to go home, try to eat and get some  
sleep. And return the following week, if I wished.

I sat for a long time by the pond, unable to think or move.  
Several dogs, who had furiously barked at me at the gate upon my  
arrival, now surrounded me, licking my tear-wet face.  
I had not even realized I was crying.

It was a mistake to show me that radiant eternity; I would like to  
forget that terrifying sensation of separation from the body. Yes, I was  
shown absolute Freedom – from everything. But I myself was not  
free, and still wanted to remain in the body. Beyond absolute  
freedom lay absolute power, which must be controlled,  
having learned to interact with it. Despite  
what had happened, I decided to return.

Life beyond the walls of the *smashan* continued, loud and  
exultant, self-assured. I dragged myself to an auto-rickshaw,  
returned to Vrindavan, and slept for three days.

A week later, I came again to the *smashan*, and was told  
that the Guru would initiate me in a year. Giving me  
a set of exercises, he was straightforward: my body must  
be prepared for practice. Lord, how I sweated, doing push-ups…

You ask what I hoped to learn? Well, first  
of all, to know reality, to rid myself of bookish notions  
of Truth. And yes, I received what I asked for, at least  
a glimpse of understanding. This yoga is the art of surviving in the  
bright, merciless light of absolute reality, in which  
all shadows of personified thoughts vanish along with  
the logical mind. Absolute Knowledge contains the residual  
aspects of your personality. If you lack the strength to endure  
this light, it will burn everything in you, practically annihilating the  
sense of “I am.” Many think this is bliss,  
yet have no idea how terrifying it can  
be. This yoga is for the bravest. And, as in Tantra, purity  
is mandatory. *Sadachara*, or piety, is necessary  
more than anything else – so that the darkness that may crawl out  
of you does not build a nest in the heart.

I began to understand why the Guru speaks of immortal  
*siddhas*, bearers of the light of eternal Life, living in this  
merciless radiance of absolute Knowledge – they can  
play with their subtle and causal bodies, manifesting themselves at  
will, without leaving their forms. No, I do not think this is  
a fairy tale. India holds astonishing secrets, and  
knowledgeable people still live among us.

…You ask what exactly he teaches? Here is what I  
think: this Guru explains not only how to enter the light of  
*Samadhi*, but also how to survive that light. And to continue  
to exist in that state during life and after death.  
He possesses ancient knowledge of the light of Time.

A true devotee can say to God: “I do not care  
where I am, in hell or in heaven, but I will be with You for  
eternity, wherever You are…” This is *bhakti*, devotion – and  
this is what is my essence. I carry these words in my heart  
and intend to develop this resolve. I would like to remain in  
the Light of Time forever. Do I think of any benefits  
from this yoga? Yes, I would like to continue existing as  
a ray of pure consciousness. And in that state, I would like to continue  
creating.

The Astrologer
