Even if such a ritual is carried out sincerely or if the performer of the rite does not know of his origin, the spirits of foreign ancestors derive no benefit from it. Whether superstition or not, in India it is firmly believed that the participation of those not by blood belonging to the lineage destroys it. The intentions and influence of the spirits of a different clan can also affect the structure of society as a whole. Yet even one born outside marriage can attain spiritual heights; the circumstances of birth are not a punishment, they cannot stop personal growth and development; the child simply had no other choice but to be born.

Put more simply, practitioners of Kriya yoga promised to respect social discipline and not to increase chaos and suffering. That is the meaning of the vow, and he considered it logical. And he repeated it both to Western and Indian disciples: as part of society, yogis must respect the rules existing within it.

…And what is the meaning of the daily reading of a chapter of the Gita? What good did it bring? Like the daily practice, the reading of the Gita built discipline, and then quantity became quality. The body, tempered by pranayama, in the end engaged the mind, and the mantric rhythms of the Bhagavad Gita revealed their deepest meaning to the heart, since any true cognition is always inner, not intellectual. The meaning of the text opened simultaneously with the practice, and consciousness expanded while the body came to know the nature of Prana.

In his book The Bhagavad Gita from the Standpoint of Kriya Yoga, Lahiri Mahasaya wrote: 'If you repeat the Gita with love and devotion, then the vibrations of its mantras will penetrate you, and, thanks to your sadhana, all the energy will be directed inward, rather than continuing to dissipate into the external world. This is the greatness of the mantras of the Gita, and this can be considered the essence of all religious texts, allowing the attainment of liberation from births and deaths.'

Knowledge

From time to time some of the newcomers spoke of their desire to know God, and he related to this aspiration sympathetically. The wish to touch the Beyond was expressed in different ways, and the word 'God' was only one of the terms. Every being aspires to merger with the source of its own origin, experiencing a sense of loss and separation from 'It.' The inexpressible subconscious pain of duality gives the impulse for the search of consolation, for the longing to find protection in the cradle in which the given individual life was born. Having walked this path, he knew this feeling well.

The question of the existence of God and of the methods of connecting with Him had come up in conversations many times, and his answer remained unchanged. Most mathematical reasoning is based on the supposition: '…suppose that…,' and one then had to apply the right formula to arrive at a conclusion. For him personally, faith was like mathematical induction [footnote: Mathematical induction — a mathematical method used to prove the truth of certain statements for all natural numbers; a way of proving statements depending on a natural argument.]: it is supposed that God exists, along with spirit and soul. All that is needed at this stage is the correct formula, and the conclusion will inevitably follow.

Though the opinion that Kriya yoga helps one come closer to God is widespread, he formulated the aim differently. The aspiration of yoga is not the cognition of the Creator, but the recognition of one's own essence — it was precisely this that guaranteed an entry ticket to the Colosseum of Creation. He did not want to know God; it was enough for him that God knew about him. For those lacking a sense of humour, this might have sounded a little haughty, but he was not concerned with ideological conformity to the norms of society. He and God were on good terms, and each was busy with his own affairs.

Knowing the Creator is only part of self-realization; the real self-awareness is far deeper than dubious religious revelations. Why? Because of the shortage of time, the fleetingness of life, and the availability of laboratory material, one ought to work with what is always to hand — with oneself. According to the Bible, every person is created in the image and likeness of God. To argue with this proposition is pointless, and he suggested to those thirsting to know God that they investigate themselves as the available sample of the Divine. Love of God, devotion and service to God — all these notions and aspirations belonged to the unclear sphere of religion and had nothing to do with the aim of yoga that he followed and that he taught.

It is useless to try to come up with a new definition of Kriya yoga, since the timeless Darshanas of Yoga, written by Maharishi [footnote: Maharishi — a Sanskrit term (formed from the combination of the words 'maha' — 'great' and 'rishi' — 'sage'). It refers to ancient Indian sages, known as 'seers.' This title is intended for those who attain the highest state of consciousness on the path of evolution. It is believed that maharishis are able to bestow holiness and transmit divine knowledge to those who are worthy of it.] Patanjali, did this beautifully. The 'Sadhana-pada' section says: 'Great efforts applied to know the Lord through selfless tapas and self-knowledge are Kriya yoga.'

Many years ago in his commentary on this shloka he wrote that the practice of Kriya means awakening the dormant, boundless consciousness by means of meditation. Then, having concentrated the awakened consciousness on the Lord, one had to apply enormous efforts to know Him. That is why he insisted on the necessity of self-knowledge before the alleged meeting with the Creator. Before making an appointment, one had to awaken the dormant mind by means of the practice of breath.

The Creator's visiting card, Prana, was issued to every being born with a pair of lungs, an inquiring mind, and a heart yearning for knowledge. And the Lord waited patiently, since He was Time itself and the Void, and this hypostasis of the Creator had room enough for all. Kriya explained the essence of God as Prana.

And then there was Vasudeva [footnote: Vasudeva is both a name and a term. The one who dwells in every atom, every living being. In Hinduism Vasudeva is worshipped as the supreme Deity. The term itself means that all beings dwell in this supreme being and that He Himself is present in all beings. In the Bhagavad Gita, Krishna is also called by the name Vasudeva (because he is the son of Vasudeva and Devaki).], the immortal spirit of the entire universe,

[footnote: Vasudeva — see footnote above]
and he taught people to see Him in their own hearts. Foreign disciples often asked who and what Vasudeva was. The answers varied, since they had to correspond to the developmental level of the questioner. Yet the essence of the answers was always the same: he uttered the same words that the Teachers of the parampara had uttered before him. Vasudeva existed everywhere. He was called Param Atma, the highest universal Soul, pervading and supporting all Creation. The fundamental principle of all things and phenomena, Vasudeva was the essence of Life itself in all Her manifestations. To 'see' Him in the heart during practice was the highest attainment. It meant to recognise Prana as the source of life — since Vasudeva is the very essence of Life.

When he saw Him for the first time, the stopped heart allowed the bud of an immeasurable rapture to unfold. From then on Vasudeva dwelt in the garden of his heart every night, and that 'presence' had certain prerequisites and conditions.

…The tradition of Babaji and ancient texts such as the Yoga Darshanas describe two specific stages of samadhi, characterized by different physical states. In the initial stage the yogi sinks into suspended animation, which is a 'safety technique' for the organism. In the state of suspended animation the subconscious awakens, but the nervous system can

be damaged by the enormous charge of energy. The body has to be 'switched off' to prevent the burnout of the nervous 'network.' The 'switched-off' body falls into hibernation, the pulse and heartbeat disappear, and this state of complete suspended animation is the first stage of samadhi.

As constant practice strengthens the nervous system, methodical repetition develops the capacity to withstand the accumulating energy. While the mind experiences the state of samadhi, the body functions as usual, without falling into suspended animation. At this stage the nervous system can endure the current of energy without switching off the body. Even while in samadhi the person remains aware of what is happening around him and is able to communicate and react. This second stage of samadhi is known as Nirvikalpa-samadhi; another term for it is 'Sa-kriya samadhi,' which means 'to be active in samadhi.'

Sa-kriya is not the exclusive feature of Babaji's lineage; the Natha yogis also speak of it. Several times he had personally met people of whom it was said that they had attained the state of Nirvikalpa [footnote: Nirvikalpa samadhi — the highest state of awareness, in which the human ego and samskaras dissolve, and Consciousness remains. Then the material world becomes like a shadow, and the seeker is liberated from it. In Nirvikalpa Samadhi there is no mind in the form we know it. There is only the state of infinite peace and bliss.] or Sa-kriya samadhi. But they never entered the state of suspended animation, their hearts continued to beat, their pulse continued to be present. Yet only the absence of both these signs is the sole reliable confirmation of real samadhi, and so he had well-founded doubts about the truthfulness of the claims regarding the state of these people.

…The experiences of Sa-kriya samadhi began for him after a series of fantastic events of 1996: the encounter with Shiva, the flood, and the first appearance of Hanuman. The list of incredible happenings was so packed that to determine the exact cause of the onset of Sa-kriya samadhi was difficult. It happened in December 1996. One fine day he realized that the mental state in which he usually was during the night practice now continued through the day as well. He was in it during conversations and walks and, as always, reacted instantly to questions during darshan, until he suddenly understood that the answers were coming from a sphere of unconscious, larger Knowledge. At first the Sa-kriya samadhis came in waves; after a while the state became stable and unbroken. There was also an additional factor: now the sound 'Om' was sounding evenly and continuously, drawing him into such elevated states that visitors sitting beside him felt drugged, as though lulled by invisible waves. Regular physical exercises helped him maintain balance; the body needed a daily reminder that it was being counted on, that it still belonged to the material world. Like any equipment, it required regular maintenance.

…Even after the state of Sa-kriya samadhi had stabilized, his sadhana was not interrupted. Regular practice was part of the vows he had taken, and no signals about its cessation were forthcoming. Earlier, while in samadhi, he would usually 'switch off' the body for an hour or two. After Sa-kriya samadhi became stable, the time interval of 'total immersion' shortened to ten or fifteen minutes. Now he could open the door to the Void at any moment, using only Shvasahita pranayama, and that was enough to slake his thirst.

What and Why

Being a Guru for more than 39 years, he still felt himself a beginner; an experienced and in some sense wise beginner, who had glimpsed the horizon in the boundless ocean of yoga, who understood the navigational charts, but who was still learning. Distant lands existed, since other ships had set sail and reached 'those shores' long before his own journey began. In the past other ascetics, undertaking the same austerities, had attained incredible results, demonstrating the triumph of the eternal Spirit over death. Centuries later the guiding star of Kutastha still shone, and he followed it.

First and foremost he was a yogi, and only then a Guru, since he had taken the vows prescribing him to practice before he undertook to teach others. This yoga could be transmitted to seekers only as part of the Guru's power, and that power could come only from the Guru's personal sadhana. The austerity that lasted a lifetime charged his body with currents of almost supernatural energy, and he knew how to transmit part of it to those who had been initiated. Remembering his own difficulties and uncertainty at the beginning of the spiritual search, he taught with understanding and patience. There was no conflict between the duties of the Guru and his personal sadhana; the rest of his affairs revolved around the night practice. Sometimes he would say: had it not been for the obligations imposed on him by the parampara, he would have chosen solitude and obscurity.

…The Spirit unhesitatingly cast off the worn shell of the body, which required constant care and had an expiry date. People died, while their immortal essence went on living. To yogis this dubious bargain seemed unjust: life is too short, it is not enough for cognition of all the depths of yoga, and only a few managed in one lifetime to achieve liberation from subsequent births. And it was no banal stroke of luck (although that, undoubtedly, played its part), but a combination of merits accumulated in past lives, karmic preconditions for realization, and the magic of yoga itself.

Year after year he was asked the same questions: what is Kriya yoga and what is its goal? What is the criterion of 'correct Kriya yoga,' is it measured by the number of samadhis, by the duration of breath retention? Was Kriya an anaesthesia, numbing the torments of existence, was this yoga an antidote to the poison of the everyday? And if so, was it suitable for those who were already relatively happy, at peace with themselves, and simply wished to maintain balance?

Sometimes going into detail, he patiently explained: Kriya is an advanced form of yoga, revealing the practitioner's potential and helping to recognize and realize one's own immortal substance, the Spirit dwelling in the body. This yoga develops consciousness to the level at which the 'possessor' of consciousness grasps the ancient contract between mind and body. If this explanation sounded too complicated, he added: 'What you are practicing is the art that takes you beyond karma. Beyond the conception of happiness and sorrow, into a state of peace.' This was perhaps the rarest of states, since in it was attained the balance of the three principal qualities of nature, the Gunas.

It is believed that Kriya is done in order to know God, but he held that the main aim is a meeting with one's own 'I.' And the Spirit awaited that meeting, guarding the dormant capacities of the subconscious, anticipating its awakening and transformation into a conscious mind. Kriya yoga was intended for explorers attempting to interpret what they already knew intuitively. It was the key to understanding the workings of Creation, the path to knowing the Creator Himself.

Depending on the personalities and motives of the teachers, the versions of the yoga varied. For decades the debates about the 'real,' 'correct,' 'original' Kriya yoga had gone on, techniques being compared and assessed. Comparative analysis did not interest him personally. From his point of view, repeated controlled samadhi was the only criterion indicating the truth of the teaching — and he taught beginners the same yoga that Sri Satyacharan Lahiri and Babaji had transmitted to him. It was precisely this method of self-realization that allowed astonishing results to be achieved, and he needed no other 'proofs' of efficacy. Before leaving the body, Sri Satyacharan Lahiri transmitted to him the six levels and techniques of Kriya in the form in which all the successors of this tradition had practiced them.

When in 1861 Lahiri Mahasaya was charged with popularizing Kriya yoga, the intentions of Babaji left no doubt: this yoga must be returned to laypeople. Whatever techniques Lahiri received then, they were intended for the first generations of practitioners.

127 years later, the Mahavatar instructed him himself, the Fifth Guru, in the advanced kriyas, individually choosing the difficulty level of practice. The recipient had already experienced samadhi, his body was prepared for the serious test of the next levels. What is interesting is that Babaji did not say whether these higher kriyas should be taught to others. The Mahavatar did not give him instructions on dividing the practice into sequential levels and the proportions of inhalations and breath retentions. The advanced kriyas were explained as a single, integral system, and figuring out the stages came down to him. As a Guru he was obliged to teach, and so he divided the techniques into 11 levels for better assimilation. This systematization is based on personal experience and understanding of the limitations and possibilities of the body, and therefore it came naturally. It was then that he established the corresponding proportions for the pranayamas, which only seemed simple but required gradual mastery.

To help students understand the essence of the Kriya method, he compared it to an abundant Indian dinner, the thali, where many dishes are served on one tray. The food is served in abundance for the enjoyment and satisfaction of any appetite. But not everyone can manage the whole portion at once. Yet it was presumed that all eleven levels must be passed in one lifetime, and only the mind could compel the body to do this. It is all a matter of will and intention: one can attempt to swim across the ocean of Samsara or keep drifting in it. He held that even the shortest journey is more useful than sitting in one place.

The first, introductory level of Kriya included seven preparatory techniques. Two exercises for stretching the tongue helped one perform Khechari mudra, then mental pranayama was carried out, helping to improve concentration on the spine at the points of the potential chakras. This was followed by Shvasahita pranayama and Nabhi kriya. After this the practitioner performed Yoni mudra, during which the yogi could for the first time meet his Spirit. And finally the practice was finished with Mahamudras, preparing the nervous system for the higher kriyas. Each exercise had clearly established proportions.

This practice could be transmitted only by a Guru. Why is this so important? The teacher transmits to the disciple both the energy of knowledge and the impulse of intention, the so-called 'sankalpa,' for continuing the practice. During initiation the Guru touches the disciple's spine at the points of the formation of the potential chakras, uttering 'Om' — thus 'connection' is made to the invisible network of Masters. Of course, this part of the ritual can be received skeptically, but the achievements of those who have undergone the traditional initiation speak for themselves.

Regardless of the number of developmental seminars or time spent visualizing the chakras, most people's centres remained inert, unactivated. The ancient techniques of switching on the chakras differed greatly from modern methods. These centres were traffic-lights at the crossroads of energetic super-highways. To activate the embryos of potential and possibility, invisible and dormant in the cerebrospinal fluid, took hard work.

…The Queen of Kriya yoga was considered to be Khechari mudra — the key condition of practice and the blessing for success. Some managed to perform it at once, some mastered the mudra over several months, with others it took years. This technique was the magic pass that opened the way to the higher kriyas. After performing Khechari, rearrangements followed in the techniques, and the student received a new pranayama. Specific mudras were added, and all this was called the 'first level with Khechari.'

A person ready for the second level of practice received it in three stages. First an introductory part, then a specific mantra, and finally the technique of certain movements of the head with an additional pranayama. The second level was aimed at

the opening of the heart knot, Hridaya Granthi Bheda [footnote: Hridaya Granthi Bheda — the 'opening' of the heart knot. Granthis are the names of energetic knots in the human body; on the psychophysical level they are responsible for what limits, hinders flexibility, deprives of alternatives. The granthis are located at the base of the body, in the heart region, and at the brow centre (and also at the throat). It is believed that, rising up the spine, Kundalini must pass ('pierce') through the main knots. The granthis indicate three stages through which human consciousness must pass for full release from all bonds and merging with the true 'I.']. The body must grow accustomed to the new practice, and this took some time. Having successfully mastered pranayama in Yoni mudra and in Nabhi kriya, the disciple could move on to the third level. Now the set of techniques stimulated the development of consciousness, and the experiences in Yoni mudra could become deeper and more mystical.

The fourth level was transmitted when the body and the nervous system were ready. The mastery of this powerful pranayama took from one to two years. This breathing technique is complex; it is hard to perfect. And yet the most ambitious students tried to master each successive stage in order to go further. Some managed.

The fifth level required time. Dhyana kriya is the technique of absolute concentration; the truly deep meditation begins precisely here. The mind became ever more refined, capable of concentrating on anything and travelling anywhere. This technique allowed one consciously to leave the physical body and return to it. When the fifth kriya was mastered, the Guru taught the pranayama of the sixth level, and from this stage things became still more complex. The new technique allowed one to untie the knot of 'Vajra nadi' [footnote: Vajra-nadi — in Sanskrit means 'thunderbolt'; the awakening of this channel acts like a thunderbolt. Whenever the physical body needs additional strength, this nadi accumulates the body's energy and raises adrenaline. Vajra-nadi begins at the starting point of Sushumna and goes up inside Sushumna itself. This channel helps maintain the flow of energy in all parts of the body at a proportionate level. Vajra-nadi controls Sushumna and keeps it in proper form for the awakening process.] within the Sushumna channel and removed obstacles for the rising of Kundalini energy. Having studied all known practices, he had found no effective way of activating 'Vajra Nadi' other than the kriya technique.

The seventh level was devoted to the study of the third-level pranayama in combination with a perfected practice of Nabhi kriya. This is the art of consciously leaving the body at the moment of death while preserving full clarity of consciousness. All this time, up to the seventh level, practitioners continued to master the 'piercing' of the heart knot, Hridaya Granthi Bheda.

At the eighth and ninth levels the yogi must perform passage through the knot of Rudra, perfect Shambhavi mudra and a special mantra. The unique, beautiful and very complex technique of Omkar kriya was followed by the final stage of Yoni mudra. After this the yogi could remain in Samadhi continuously, retaining the ability to speak, walk and perform daily duties, while simultaneously experiencing unity with the whole world. These are the Tenth and Eleventh levels.

…Along with detailed explanations of the higher kriyas, Babaji taught him to leave the body at will. Combined with the final technique of Yoni mudra, the technique of Omkar kriya allowed one to open the door between the worlds of the living and the dead. The sadhaka could contemplate the astral world and then return again to the body. This technique had nothing in common with the state of Samadhi; it was directed solely at the transference of consciousness. At the moment of death Omkar kriya transfers the spirit to higher spheres, and the yogi leaves the body consciously, without experiencing regret or fear.

Explaining the stages of this yoga, he often quoted the famous American poet Robert Frost:

'The woods are lovely, dark and deep.
But I have promises to keep,
And miles to go before I sleep,
And miles to go before I sleep.'

For all the apparent simplicity of the vows, keeping these promises was not easy. Although the Spirit never sleeps, He can become inert if the yogi abandons the practice. Time-frames present an even greater problem: some practitioners never advance beyond the initial stages.

…The Spirit lives endlessly, in many bodies, and may attain realization in one lifetime in any of them. Everything comes down to will, discipline, intention and motivation. And, of course, there is the condition of karmic predisposition to realization — yoga must become the meaning of life. Before self-realization (self-recognition) and god-realization (recognition of the Creator) the very essence of the practitioner must be 're-cut.' Initiation into this yoga includes something incredible: immediately after initiation the person begins to see his own immortal substance, although a deeper understanding of one's own soul takes much longer. To approach knowledge of one's own essence, the practitioner must arrive at the 'meeting place.' The Spirit never comes when summoned, but the time the yogi spends waiting for Him or alone with Him expands the channels of communication. As the yogi and his Spirit come to know one another, mutual ease grows. This process is sorcery, alchemy, and it is taught by no other religion or practice.

Evaluating the dormant potential of each disciple, he preferred to ignore human imperfections. Ideal people simply did not exist, but every one deserved the chance of awakening.

Greeting those entering the darshan hall for the first time, he paid no particular attention to age, race or sex, but saw only the uniqueness and irreproducibility of each Spirit. And he taught people the art of acquaintance with Him and the necessary etiquette.

What then is needed to begin such a journey? To arouse the Guru's interest. The teacher must become interested in teaching the particular person. That, properly speaking, was the only condition for the beginning of awakening.

The Magic of Reality

Sometimes the legends of the 'Yoga Vasishtha' [footnote: Yoga Vasishtha — a monumental philosophical work on Vedanta. The text, attributed to the sage Valmiki and dated roughly between the 6th and 15th centuries CE, contains more than 29,000 verses and is written in the form of a dialogue between Sri Rama and his guru Vasishtha.] and the 'Mahabharata' sounded like stories of his own adventures. Somehow personal events of the recent past wove naturally into historical fact, while the geography of his travels could easily be traced on the maps of events laid out in the Puranic texts. Perhaps because individual universes are formed from traumas and victories, images and thoughts, places and emotions, mixed by the Pranic force, the breath of Time and the Void. Prana feeds equally suppositions and denials, faith and unbelief. The very imagination is conceived in the amniotic waters of Prana and grows in the womb of a world founded on carbon. The fresco of being consists of a mosaic of many lives, and he advised disciples to become the brightest of these pigments — by means of imagination.

Attempts to suppress the perception of reality through the prism of imagination flow from the modern separation of people from the mythology of places. Modern man often longs to tear himself from his own roots in order to become a projected image of himself. And this tears him away from understanding the foundations of existence. Today magic is questioned, analysed and at once rejected; sorcery is welcomed mainly in the digital sphere, where technology is worshipped with awe and hope, at least for now.

Modernity demands practicality and constantly updates the rules of survival together with software updates. Gods, spirits and immortal beings live somewhere on the periphery, while most people are quite comfortable in the cocoons of supposed truths. Only seekers continue to play matryoshka, where the magical and the ordinary are inserted into one another.

Secrets are revealed to those who live in immediate proximity to nature, to those who tread the earth in the literal sense — peasants, blacksmiths, potters, shepherds, hunters… Living together with nature and its primary elements, they are familiar with the original force and theurgy [footnote: Theurgy — in Greek 'divine action,' 'miracle.' A kind of magic with which one believed it possible to change the course of events by subordinating to one's will the actions of gods and spirits.] of the earth. He always listened to them attentively, even with anticipation, since in their stories he heard the whisper of the Supreme Priestess. Most 'educated' people contemptuously called such beliefs 'rural superstitions,' but true yoga took root only in unprejudiced minds and hearts. The yogic method of cognition requires impartiality and naturalness of perception; the legends were instructions and maps pointing to hidden treasures.

Greeting adepts, magic allowed them to enter the world of dreams and at the same time remain in the state of waking. The continuity of practice ensures the change of consciousness, and such consciousness can create new worlds. Of course, the future cannot be programmed by intention alone, for any transformation requires action. Kriya was a method of relentlessly transforming the 'normal' into the paranormal.

Like enchanted farmers, regardless of the season, yogis persistently plough the fields of their bodies with complex pranayamas in order to reap the fruit of austerities. Duality does not trouble those who without particular effort squeeze through the narrow cracks of the physical world and are carried into another dimension. For such surfers of reality such transitions are perfectly natural. Sometimes people who entered into immediate contact with such yogis themselves felt the temptation to begin the search for hidden portals and came to the smashan, to him, since he was known as the Guru who helps to find the secret paths into the kingdom of the Spirit and was considered one of the Guides.

Those striving to believe but not yet daring to trust the heart, beginners, would speak of the desire to solve the puzzle of existence. And he taught those who were sincere in seeking, but did not trouble himself to explain aspects of the yogic path to sceptics. The entrance to the higher reality is open to people who genuinely wish to understand Life in her astonishing wholeness, and not to dissect Her into degrees and categories. This path is thorny and full of traps and ambushes, and was intended for persistent pilgrims, not package tourists.

…Everyday life dismisses the subtle, inexplicable phenomena that fall outside the laws of Matter, yet the unconditioned consciousness switched on by yoga is no longer able to maintain linear perception or to squeeze itself into the framework of established norms. Like a flower needing soil, the transformed mind needs rooting in a body capable of supporting the developed consciousness. In the course of such a transformation the logical mind becomes secondary, and its sleeping twin, the subconscious, slowly awakens. Sensing the changes, the body responds. After many lives of separation, the Gross and the Subtle can truly embrace each other.

His own principle of magical realism was simple: daily investigation of life reveals its constantly changing essence, the astonishing and omnipresent Prana, nourishing all intentions, events and thoughts; past, present and future flow in the current of Prana-Time. Disciples were invited to accept the strangeness and irrationality of the subconscious; yoga fed their imagination with pranic energy on the physical and mental planes

Together with applied will and controlled breath, Kriya was the pass into the kingdom of the Void, where everything is possible.

Imagination was the windsurfing board through Samsara, the turns of its rainbow-coloured sail erasing the boundary between the 'imaginary' and the 'real.' The subconscious not-mind knew everything, and was never shocked by the inexplicability of emotions or events, which normally throw the limited logical mind into confusion. The paranormal and the supernatural fitted organically into the yogic routine without distorting its course.

Interaction with the 'paranormal' is unexplainable from the standpoint of human science, which demands explanation of miracles and phenomena. The perception of existential aspects is excluded from the matrix that runs a limited number of algorithms in conditioned minds.

As an alternative, he proposed to students that they accept the world as it was, with all its forgotten histories, signs, mystical parallels and metaphors. And yet the card catalogue with the traditional chronology of events and documentary evidence should not be thrown out; from time to time it had to be consulted. Practitioners should assemble new databases not from offered concepts but from empirical experience. And this process was characterized by the problematic, painful and ambiguous invasion of the irrational into their minds. The overall picture of the disciple's world was never distorted, since reality itself had not originally been 'rational.'

Once a student from Canada asked, slightly embarrassed: 'When I came here to study yoga, I fully accepted the Teacher and the teaching. But how should I treat the stories about talking snakes and appearances of Gods? Must I believe in fairy tales? Or treat all of it as metaphors and hints?' The problem was perfectly solvable: yoga whispers the answers to the subconscious through the awakened body and consciousness, and the heart never doubts what it already knows. The fine-tuning of inner vision removes the conflict between the 'real' and the 'imaginary.' The unification of the developed subconscious with the phenomena of reality is only a matter of time and intention.

…What happened on the smashan did not contradict events in the outer world. The strange and bizarre incidents at the ancient cremation site were caused by the 'churning' of higher pranic energies. Spreading like waves, these energies were perceived by the mental receptors of living beings. But who exactly was causing these processes? Only higher beings are able to manage energies of such magnitude — it was precisely they who were sorcering at the retort of the smashan, distilling their own Prana into the oxygen of intentions and changes.

The Venezuelan writer Arturo Uslar-Pietri, who was the first to apply the term 'magical realism,' described every person as 'an enigma surrounded by realistic facts.' Life on the smashan proved the opposite. Even having gone through the indescribable, he characterized himself as 'an ordinary man living an extraordinary life,' surrounded by the unknown and the unintelligible. Was there a touch of coquetry in this statement? Perhaps, but such self-definition slightly calmed people.

Realization required careful study of the alphabet of Life. To read the Book of Fates and the diary of the Spirit, a person must learn to assemble words and sentences from the letters of being. The exclamation marks of the past and the question hieroglyphs of the future deserved close attention, and an ordinary full stop sometimes conveyed meaning better than long sentences.

With traditional, even if limited, methods of cognition, all was in order; they offered a convenient and generally accepted coordinate-system. But those studying higher yoga in time adapt to the ancient system of road-markers, and he taught them to overlay inner vision and imagination on the map of known reality. And then invisible wires, charged by providence, between events and people connected the subconscious with the higher spheres, interrupting the linearity of perception. Such reboots revealed the watermark of higher Reality, existing parallel to the physical world.

Hiding its undertone of grey, the blackness of night dissolved in the gleams of dawn. Encased in the eggshell of individual karma, the yolk of death floated in the viscous albumen of life. Containing molecules of various intentions, Good seeped out of the vessel of Time and mixed with so-called Evil. And this potent elixir nourished the Gunas, taking shape in the placenta of unstoppable Life.

Sound. Void. The Elements. Prana

For the first time he heard the Sound at the beginning of September 1979, when he was practising bhastrikas, pushing them to their limit. Perhaps these exercises also contributed not only to the recognition of the sound 'Aum' (or Anahada Nada, Om, Pranava), but to the out-of-body experience that occurred several months later. That night, having finished the exercises, he sat for some time with closed eyes. An even, monotonously vibrating buzzing came from within, but it was impossible to classify. The sensation resembled the friction of countless invisible tiny glass beads against each other, and the descending current of this sounding drew him in. Not overlapping outer noises, this constant, inaudible hum was merely the background of other melodies of the night.

In the following days the intensity of the inner sound grew. Although he was not yet practising Kriya at that time, he was, however, aware of the sound and even discussed it with members of a yoga group. And besides, he was acquainted with people attending gatherings at the 'Adhyatma Niketan' ashram. This ashram had been founded by a well-known spiritual organization, and the doctrine consisted of immersion in the sound 'Om'; this kind of practice was called 'Surat Shabd [footnote: Shabd, or Shabda — sound, word, the subtle element of sound. This Sanskrit word denotes 'the sound of speech.' Classical Indian philosophy of language held that shabda ('speech') is eternal, as is meaning, and that they are indivisible.] Sadhana.' The adepts of this teaching believed that, by plugging the ears, fixing the gaze between the brows and listening to the Sound, one could attain enlightenment. But nobody told them that the powerful vibrations of the Sound negatively affected the body and mind, and only intense physical exercise could prevent the accelerated destruction of the nervous system.

…Despite his awareness, it took him about a week to understand the nature of the transmission. The Sound did not stop and did not go away, taking root and fastening itself in the body and consciousness.

…Several years later he shared this experience with his Teacher, and he expressed extraordinary satisfaction: 'Wonderful! You heard Pranava without even being initiated into yoga! Now, with practice, this state will deepen and bring you new experiences.'

Wholly absorbed in the Sound, he could not stop listening to it, but somehow he managed not to drown or dissolve in It. The mind merged with the constancy of the sound, and he no longer tried to 'switch it off' by an effort of will. The Sound did not interfere with daily activities; he could walk, speak and act in the constant presence of Nada, while remaining completely aware of and in control of himself and his surroundings. But the body, of course, required constant attunement, since the invisible frequencies were destructive to the fragile human shell. Over the years, having grasped the source of the Sound, he learned to concentrate on it and then release that focus. Led by vibration, the mind moved between atoms and particles of Matter toward their source, the Void, from which the Sound issued — and it was then that the nature of the elements began to open to him.

Both Eastern and Western sciences acknowledged the existence of basic elements. Countless combinations of the building-blocks of the Universe gave rise to all the forms of the material world. Earth, water, fire, air and ether manifested out of the primordial Sound of Creation. Their properties and density differed depending on the degree of intensity of the cosmic vibration that gave birth to all the primary elements, from the most subtle ether to the element of earth. Science had long explained the properties of the basic elements, but they nevertheless remained mystical, elusive, deceptive. The gross element of earth had a relatively constant form; water could take the shape of any vessel; the energy of fire gave a temporary configuration to the tongues of its flame. Air was even more subtle (although it could be felt), while the fifth element, ether, was inaccessible to tactile perception, although in its all-pervading field everything existed. Western alchemy did not even acknowledge ether as an element, yet the East knew and honoured Shunya, or 'Space,' as one of the bricks of Creation. The Void was present in all objects and beings, residing in Her; Matter emerged from the union of Time and Void, from the vibration of the eternal Sound.

The Void is the mother of the Universe, and the Great Time is its father. Having given birth to Matter, the Void carried galaxies as a mother carries a child. The particles of Matter contained the genes of both parents, Time-Void and Sound. The Void filled the space between atoms and within them; the very structure of Matter was based on the Void that gave birth to it. Molecules consisted of atoms, atoms were built of nuclei and electrons, and so on. With every new discovery there was less and less Matter in the world, since the empty space between its components never ceased to grow.

…Once a scientist who visited him offered an interesting example: if all the voidness were removed from the Himalayas, the majestic mountains would shrink to the size of a matchbox. In samadhi the grand magnificence of the Void, Time, Sound, and the elements opened up to him. Solving the greatest puzzle, he felt incredible satisfaction comparable only to the slaking of thirst. Prolonged immersion in the Sound explained the phenomenon of the world's emergence; It was the basis of all sciences and the cause of the existence of the very scientists and their discoveries.

At some point he understood that the true form and essence of objects are elusive and concealed, since the Matter of which they are made is constantly changing. Only the Void remained unchanged, and this constancy made possible the movement of Matter within Her. Matter is conscious, and Her consciousness could be born only by another mind.

All births and changes began in the consciousness of the Void and Time. Was this a joint, collective Universal Mind? Could duality have begun with the separation of these great essences, with the birth of Matter? His intellect boiled, demanding discussion of these questions, but suitable interlocutors could not be found. Once he turned to his cousin: 'Do you know what lies between us? The Void! It fills the space between us, within us, and between everything else in this world!' The surprise in the cousin's gaze gave way to visible alarm. Such reflections were impractical, strange and quite unconnected with the everyday tasks of survival, but for him they were precious treasures. In time the disciples began to comprehend these notions, gradually opening Universal science through their own practice.

…Western students were interested in the transference of consciousness after death and the principle of the next incarnations — and most of all in the technical side of the process. Indians rarely asked such questions; for them existence and non-being were in perfect harmony. The law of the continuity of Life was absorbed by them through countless incarnations of their ancestors, through the DNA of

Hinduism itself. He tried to explain the process as simply as possible: 'After death the body is freed from the grossest elements in the flame of cremation: three of the five gross elements burn up, while ether and air form an aerial body — and consciousness attaches itself to it. The element of earth, most essential for the existence of the physical body, returns to the earth, ashes turn to ashes, dust to dust.'

In India corpses are cremated as quickly as possible to prevent attempts by the soul to enter the dead body or settle near the place of burial. After the loss of shelter, the shocked soul has no other choice than to settle in the aerial body. The time of death is extremely important. Linked to the cycles of planets and seasons, it determines the next stages of the soul's evolution. The Bhagavad Gita gives hints, speaking of periods favourable for departure: during the dry season the souls in etheric bodies have the opportunity to rise to the higher layers of the atmosphere for rest and preparation for new incarnations. In the rainy season, in winter, fogs and rains beat the souls in aerial bodies down to the earth, and they cannot leave the lower atmosphere to reach the higher levels. Drawn down by the rains, the souls enter the soil and feed plants, becoming food for insects, birds, people, large and small creatures. Drawn into various wombs, such souls take new births in conditions different from those that rose higher, into the astral spheres. The lottery of reincarnations is never accidental, for the time of death and new birth is predetermined for every being.

The schemes of human chakras popular among adherents of modern yoga symbolize the primary elements. Each chakra is depicted with petals marked by the letters of the Sanskrit alphabet. The chakras symbolize a specific vibration of a separate element and therefore have different numbers of petals. Their number depends on the intensity of the element's vibration. The chakras are nothing other than the notes of the musical score of the symphony of the Universe; all together they personify the unity of the Source. The ancient practitioners recorded this cosmic music and determined the sound vibration of each element. In the body the ratio of basic elements is uneven, but yoga can balance them, and such an equilibrium of elements leads to the acquisition of the ideal form, and ultimately, to immortality.

For many beginners in Kriya the knowledge of the Sound begins from the first day of practice, and from this same moment starts the recognition of the basic elements. The secret art of balancing the elements is grasped not by the logical mind, but in the process of practice, through the inner assimilation of unspoken knowledge.

The human body is nothing but a capsule of Matter brought forth by Sound, animated by Prana. Equally balanced, the five primary elements give the body different qualities. Without a body consciousness could not exist; it would be homeless.

Observing spirits who continued to change bodies for the sake of preserving form, he considered their dependence on the shelter of flesh almost tragic. He recommended that disciples perceive their own bodies as Apana, and consciousness as Prana. This perfect union of pranas could give the 'carriers' the highest knowledge. In fact, all bodies are composed of five primary elements with a single purpose — that Prana and Apana might continue the eternal play in a temporary shell.

Incidentally, another name for the cosmic Sound, 'pranava,' derived from the word 'prana.' Both mind and pure consciousness, which is the catalyst of further transformation of the soul, are all Prana. She did not merely deliver oxygen, She was the elixir of Life, the most important ingredient in the cocktail of Creation. Prana Herself was the great Animator, Alchemist and Yogi.

Slightly overwhelmed by all these revelations, the disciples were silent. Someone uncertainly broke the silence: 'Where can one read about this? Could you share the names of the books?' He smiled: 'Try to give me at least a little credit. These discoveries I made myself, but now they can become yours. For friends nothing is too dear…'

The Origin of Karma

Questions that had sounded during the day continued to bounce off the walls of the darshan hall in the evenings, until at last their soundless rustle was absorbed by the fibres of the carpet, the whisper of trees, and the soothing breath of night. Usually his answers pinned the truth down by their conciseness. Piercing the shields of ordinary logic, the words fell into hearts and were absorbed by them. For those who heard, his answers were full of deep meaning, possessed of broad range, and exerted a prolonged effect.

Sometimes mushy, slippery, poorly formed or lumpy questions floated, stuck, or bounced about like ping-pong balls. And yet he unmistakably saw the essence of the question and the germ of the answer beneath the layer of words. Occasionally questions were sharp, stabbing, like a blade, and these he especially liked. But, in truth, all questions were interesting in their own way, and he always answered. His words pollinated the wings of an inquiring spirit, helping it rise higher.

When he was asked about karma, he tried to explain in a clear way, using the prism of metaphor. The source of all karmas was Thought, which gave rise to absolutely everything: inspiration, jealousy, love, pride, vengeance, desire or reactions. Therefore he never tired of repeating: 'If you are capable of understanding your own thoughts and controlling them, then you will rise above karma, you will move beyond it.'

The seeds of karmas dormant in consciousness await their hour to sprout or wither. If one manages to activate the subconscious, the creation of further karmas becomes more refined, and the yogi can rise to a higher level of karmic deeds and thoughts.

Having realized the roots of all the actions he had ever committed, he gathered the fruits they had brought and tasted them. The moment of going beyond most of his karmas was still significant, but even after samadhi every action or reflection had the potential to teach the soul something.

Karmas resemble educational courses, an individually tailored program. Playfully, but quite consciously, the spirit fills the body with life in the name of learning, of the experience of feelings, of victories and sufferings. Are these experiences good or bad? Such definitions are the prerogative of the limited mind and meagre human morality. Developed and discerning consciousness can choose the right path and perform right actions; the yogic mind can determine cause and effect, initiate appropriate action or choose inaction. Like dealers in the Casino of Life, the vasanas [footnote: Vasana — desire, aspiration, idea, inclination, mental tendency.] and samskaras of past lives constantly spin the roulette wheel of action. If they are pacified, the mental imprints may become only silent spectators contemplating the stage on which the Actor performs. The Stage of the inner 'I' is brilliantly and concisely described in the Shiva-sutras: 'Atman is the actor. For this actor the inner 'I' is the stage.'

The so-called 'instinctive' reactions are also considered karmic, since they are born of the mind (even if the subconscious). Becoming actions, these reactions can cause quite real consequences. He was often asked why the physical body is called the karmic body, and the explanation he offered was both accessible and at the same time profound. Any body is the result of thought. Initially thought has no form, but in the end it can 'express itself' in an individual body. Often listeners did not fully understand this assertion. How can a formless thought turn into form, become flesh and blood? And if the physical body is the expression of thought, then whose thought is it?

It belonged to the universal Mind. But since people are earthly creatures, this could also be the thought of the Earth, since She gave birth to the most varied progeny, and all living beings were neurons of Her consciousness.

Of course, humanity was quite comfortably settled in the bubble of so-called scientific discoveries about the material world and was not about to change its views. He, however, encouraged students to think on a large scale and to trace any idea or hypothesis — for the sake of destroying stereotypes. In contrast to the abstraction of universal mind [footnote: Universal mind (or universal consciousness) — a metaphysical concept. It supposes an essence underlying the existence of all created things, the substance of beings and being itself, with the becoming of being that took place in the Universe before the appearance of the concept of Mind. In general, the term 'mind' often refers to the organic, human aspect of universal consciousness. Universal Mind contains all knowledge. It is the potential limit of any thing. For It everything is possible. Universal Mind has unlimited resources at its disposal; it is omnipresent; people must be manifestations of this Mind.], the concept of Earth's consciousness was more digestible, at least it can be tied to something concrete. The planet is a living and breathing being, with thoughts, emotions, cycles, seasons, and shifts of mood. Of course, She could not know every soul, just as people themselves are unaware of the myriads of bacteria being born and dying within the body. Nevertheless, all beings were Her children — there are no orphans on Earth. Sometimes the Mother disinfected Herself with a contrast shower of downpours, hurricanes and tsunamis, bathed in melting glaciers, dusted Herself with hot sands, and was not at all worried about climate change. She had already seen many changes, and they had not particularly affected Her. For Her, 'human civilization' is only a small skin irritation. The Planet may dust Herself with the talc of volcanic ash or apply a lotion of hot lava to these blisters at any moment. She has already outlived many suns, and after the death of the next luminary She will choose another and draw it to Herself.

Of course, the planet's time differs from the human system of time-measurement. One second of Earth equals approximately 700 years on the human scale of time, and therefore the planet barely 'noticed' the presence of people; they were no different from other beings living on Her surface. Time itself spoke through Krishna: '…only a tiny particle of Me attracts the 5 elements, and that is precisely how the body is born.' Being the embodiment of Time, human flesh reflects the aspect of the past, since it is the product of the combined karmas of the parents.

Simplifying the philosophical allusions, he explained that the absence of a body means the absence of karma. The old 'merits' manifested at every new birth together with the potential for new karmas.

'Gheranda Samhita' connects the allegory of the circle of rebirths with an irrigation wheel: its continuous turning continues to pour water into the buckets under the weight of the water — this is karma. One of karma's purposes is the creation of new karmas. However, the irrigation wheel does not turn by itself; it is set in motion by the bull harnessed to it and its master.

Although the body is only a drop of Time, it serves as an incubator of the mind; from it consciousness hatches. Each drop of the ocean carries the properties of the ocean; so too in every body there is hidden a similarity with Time, together with the seeds of accumulated karmas. The Universal Mind continues to create the 'illusion

of the body' in myriads of other drops of Time. Passing through these temporary bodily forms, Time lays a path through the past, the present, the future.

Individual fate is almost always influenced by the totality of karmas of a specific locality, country, and nation, lending concreteness to the destiny of a particular person. Creating a body, Time provides for the soul a temporary means of travel. In addition to genetics and DNA, bodies are shaped by the soul's desire to acquire new shells. Every human body reflects the tendencies of the Spirit, future intentions, and even inevitable actions. The stitches of past karmas in the fabric of the body are interwoven with the airy, fluid threads of the future.

In his commentary on the 'Shiva-sutras' he explained in detail the cycle, origin, and cause of birth. In creating the physical body, the universal Mind invests in it His seed. Called consciousness, or soul, it grows to meet the Spirit. The body is the maternal womb, the incubator of consciousness, and the embryo of the soul ripens in the body and is nourished by it, so that consciousness may hatch from the shell of flesh. The ripened soul outlives the aging body, and after its death continues to wander, finding a new one.

Very rarely is a soul able to develop to the level of Spirit in one lifetime; this takes many cycles. Working out various karmas, it changes bodies until it has 'ripened.' Once it has become Spirit, the soul no longer needs a physical body and can return to the source, to the infinity of Time-Void, to dissolve in them. The filters of karma exist so that souls, passing through them, may return to the Source.

The body is the counter of Time and its sign, as well as the embodiment of the Void that dwells in each of its organs, cells, and atoms. The Void and Time are the Universal Mind, and this great Consciousness gives rise to all cosmic beings and planetary bodies. People are only one of countless variations of Matter created by Time and Space. Like many other forms of life, they are dreamed up by the planet, born so that She may dream Life.

…Students need not worry about their karmas; all that was supposed to happen to them had already happened — they had found their way here, to this yoga, to the Guru. Now the time had come to set in motion the alchemy of sadhana, which burns up all karmas.

The Kingdom of Death, Mritya Loka

The Earth is herself a Creator, and Her creative designs and patterns are organic, if one may put it that way. The blueprint of a complex, delicate and at the same time grand plan, without any exhaustive logic (from a limited human point of view), is superimposed on the endless cycle of creation. This process has no other special purpose than exploration. The Great Spirit comes to know Himself through the life and death of various organic forms.

In his daily dialogues with disciples he tried to inspire them to an awareness of the forces of creation that stand behind the eternal play driven by the duality of being. To an understanding of opposing energies, their resistance and pushing-away from each other, alongside the eternal striving for merger. In the final analysis, absolutely everything is only the play of consciousness in imagination.

He told them that the Earth is Death itself. It is held that Death always follows us; all beings on the planet walk on Her, and Earth extends everywhere — she is always close. After the death of living beings the remains are absorbed by the soil, returning into Her, or evaporate in the veil of the atmospheric envelope that wraps Her.

The Earth is called Mritya Loka [footnote: Mrityaloka — 'Mritya' means in Sanskrit 'death,' 'Loka' means 'world, space, dimension'; in Shailendra Sharma's opinion, Earth is the place where death must be experienced.], the kingdom of death, and souls come here to experience Death after the cognition of Life. Again and again they are sent to Earth for the continuation of their education. The planet creates myriads of bodies, 'space-suits of flesh,' and the Void and Time place in them the seeds of souls and animate them with Prana. The Earth never ceases to give birth to life in all its forms, yet souls, and the Prana that enlivens them, belong to the Void. Each soul is a form and imprint of Time, or Shiva, and because of this in Hinduism the soul-Jiva is also called 'Shiva.'

Only souls come from outside, from cosmos; everything else belongs to the Earth. All parthiva, all that is made of Earth and on Earth, returns to Her. The primary elements created by the Void are in the end also 'appropriated' by the planet. Dissolving in Her, they supplement the endless compositions of Her vast palette. After the death of the earthly body the formless, unmanifest and immortal Jiva-Shiva sets off back to its source, to the Void and Time.

…Moving from the nature of the human mind to the consciousness of the primary elements themselves, he explained to listeners the nature of consciousness in its totality. Starting with the concept of the consciousness of Matter (Chaitanya [footnote: Chaitanya — absolute consciousness.] Padartha), he discreetly led the disciples to an understanding of the consciousness of the Void (Chaitanya Shunyata). In time they had to glimpse a flash of consciousness of Time, and not by means of measuring instruments or academic explanations, but with their own body and mind. Such is the path of this Yoga.

…Hinduism held that the realm of souls is governed by Death and Her authorized agent, Yamaraja. All the lessons, experiences, achievements and failures of the earthly path of every soul are within the purview of Yamaraja; all souls return to the lord of Dharma.

Disciples were recommended to read the Puranas, the great encyclopaedia of original knowledge: in the ancient texts it was said that the chief dharma, or destiny, of a human being was maran dharma. The duty of accepting death. This is the real duty of all souls coming to Earth. Everything else is merely a moment between the important events of incarnation and the laying-down of the shell. From the moment of birth people were hostages of death, and the goal of any life is to live it consciously and, where possible, with joy.

The fear of the unknown and the instinct to remain in the refuge of flesh are often too strong, and therefore Kriya yoga is not meant for everyone. It teaches one to die so as to live, to pass through Death — and to glorify Life. Only at the moment of merger with death does the sharp sense of self-awareness arise, the sense of true being manifested in its fullness, and no self-knowledge can be considered complete without passage through death. For some the first experience of Samadhi may part the veil of Death, but it may also become the last; not very many people are capable of digesting such a revelation. The fright may forever take away the wish to repeat the 'journey.'

Kriya taught constant self-awareness which arises only at the moment of transition from one world to another. The yogis of Babaji's lineage practiced constantly to maintain a permanent state of self-awareness — and his disciples were steadily progressing. But he had not yet taught any of them Omkar kriya. The Omkar technique is intended for the transference of consciousness from one plane of being to other dimensions; this highest kriya was taught only after the disciple had passed through Samadhi. Only those who had experienced the state of complete suspended animation could recognize the precise moment of Death and discern the point of transition, the fragile edge between Life and Death.

Speaking allegorically, the seasoned practitioner had to come to the threshold and pull the handle of the door dividing the kingdoms of Life and Death. Carefully opening it, he peeked inside, without entering. After contemplating what lay beyond, the door was carefully closed, and the yogi returned to Life. This kriya was dangerous, and required preparation to bear the consequences of staggering experiences. Any mistake could lead to burnout and collapse of the nervous system. Samadhi as the experience of Death awakens the subconscious, and for the unprepared this uncorked energy is too harsh. Therefore students must first prepare their bodies before the step into the abyss; they must be able to come back, in order to see the divine play of Life through new lenses.

Without the blessing of the Mother such a journey is impossible. Of course, the blessing of the biological mother is important, but he was speaking of the Earth, capable of opening the deepest secrets. He believed that Earth could introduce the yogi to the Father. In the book 'Gorakh Bodh' Gorakhnath asks the question: 'Who is the husband of the Earth?' The husband from eternity was the Void, the Earth is wedded forever to Her, the Father of all elements, all matter, all bodies…

The Vedic scriptures assert that even the gods strive for human incarnation, since certain spiritual attainments are possible only on this planet, only in human form. All souls received the possibility of spiritual development here, because Earth provides the conditions for such progress. Bearing the living and the dead, She cares for them, nourishing their bodies and teaching them, providing everything necessary for the journey. The Earth ceaselessly gives people varied things that help them while away the time of earthly incarnations — from sports to politics, from various religions and forms of worship to atheism or any other beliefs. Among the toys with which She amuses humanity are the various sciences, discoveries, and inventions. Earth nourishes all and, in turn, all beings must nourish others. Living bodies provide for the existence of millions of bacteria and viruses, and that is just. The planet is a gigantic laboratory in which the great infinite cycle of Life is honed. The creative forces of the planet make Her higher than a multitude of invisible deities. She is mighty and more than tangible.

For him personally, Earth and Kali were inseparable. The dark goddess, Kali, was the embodiment of Earth, and both were called 'Shyama,' the Dark One. The naked four-armed Kali Ma defends with one hand, carrying in the others a sword and a severed head. The Goddess's neck is adorned with a garland of skulls, dead bodies hang from Her back, She stands on the body of Shiva. So too, supported by the eternity of the Void, Earth floats in Time-Shiva and likewise wears no clothes. Instead She adorns Herself with endless garlands of skulls, carrying with Her the bones of all dead children. Guarding and destroying, She holds the genetic archives: the fossils of Her 'family album' contain the DNA of all those She has nursed. The journey of the Earth is a riddle, since the destination is by no means limited to Her orbit. The orbit is only a temporary evolutionary trajectory.

In his understanding, the yogic union with death meant union with the Earth: through death-like samadhi, developed consciousness could unite with the consciousness of the planet. This union also meant discovering the final point of Her arrival, for She was carrying all Her inhabitants somewhere. Most listeners usually took these words as poetic allegories, but they reflected his personal experience and contemplation.

Indiscriminately, the Earth gave the possibility of exploring existential mysteries simply by allowing them to be here, on Her magnificent breast — and people continued bravely to pursue these abstract, unthinkable and at the same time entirely real goals, attempting to crack the code of life itself and grasp its meaning. Every living being strives to keep living, however greatly it has suffered in the very process of life. Even the salty taste of failure is the aftertaste of life, not death, for all failures carry within themselves a spark and a vibration of the Source.

…At dawn, emerging from the trance, he caught the scent of earth moistened by the first monsoon. This moist, tart aroma reminded souls that Earth was their common cradle.

Stages of Evolution

…“What do you see when you look at me?” The American looked at him expectantly. The question itself admitted a variety of answers, but each seeker was to receive what would aid him in his spiritual development.

“I see that you are the result of a combination of your father’s sperm and your mother’s egg.” Visibly taken aback, the guest had expected assurances that he was an expression of the Atman, a particle of the Universal Mind. After all, according to various spiritual theories, man is an embodiment of Divinity, directly connected to Her. And of course, the guest was all of the above. But… he sought to redirect people’s imagination toward themes they had not yet attempted to comprehend, as they were excessively fixated on themselves. They had to learn to contemplate different aspects of being, to shift focus, to cease concentrating on the conditioned “I.” To think outside established frameworks meant to step toward non-conformist knowledge.

Of course, disciples differed, but all were receptive to transformation provided constancy and perseverance in sadhana. In general, the properties inherent in people were more or less defined and established; any forward movement required enormous effort. Sometimes progress could be provoked by the shock of re-evaluating seemingly unshakable postulates, and at other times the Guru’s words stimulated change—and he could employ such methods.

…The properties of Nature-Prakriti described in many texts—the gunas—represent a coordinate system, a set of criteria, an existential matrix. It is said that the nature of all things is limited by the three Gunas. The concept of Sattva is interpreted as purity, goodness, truth; the Rajasic state is ascribed activity, passion, and desire; and Tamas, ignorance, darkness, inertia. However, he found this interpretation of the gunas too general; it created the illusion that a person could pass through all three stages in a single lifetime. Everything and everyone had a mixed nature.

In contrast to this widely held concept, he asserted that all human beings are in the second stage of evolution—the state of Rajas. In the initial stage of development, they abided in darkness and ignorance, defined as the tamasic state. Any being is merely a combination of sperm and egg; knowing neither its sex nor nature, unaware of its belonging to a particular class of beings. The spermatozoon and the egg simply cannot know the source and cause of their own origin.

Only the most fortunate spermatozoon could reach the egg and deliver the genetic material necessary for transformation. After completing its mission, the spermatozoon becomes inert, and the egg takes over the process of development, creating an embryo. Gathering cells and forming flesh and organs, the fertilized egg molds the body of the future human. For a time, the fetus resides in the womb, in a tamasic state of darkness.

Then the embryo develops a spinal cord, a heart, and a brain. In the first months, all embryos have distinctly (at least visually) female genitalia—and only at later stages of development do the sexual organs begin to differentiate. The feminine energy of creation grants them passage to the next stage of evolution. Being feminine, Nature creates male beings as couriers for delivering and spreading the necessary chromosomes and genes. Reaching the egg, the spermatozoon becomes still, while the other competitors (active sperm) perish. The egg accepts only one “courier,” and its tissue dissolves into it after the mission is complete.

The spermatozoon and the egg can never meet the parents unless they turn into a child. Only after such a transformation can the “creators” recognize the child, and it can acquire a new worldly identity. At this stage, exploration and cognition, seasoned with desire, included every earthling in active learning and action, in the state of Rajas.

The ancient Naths described all phases of embryonic development with striking accuracy—they perceived the human body as a reflection of the Macrocosm. The “Siddha Siddhanta Paddhati” speaks of the pinda, the body, as a model of the greater Universe. Literally, “pinda” means a sphere or egg, the cosmic egg of the world, as well as the human egg awaiting fertilization. Whence does the body come, and whither does the spirit go? And in what way (if at all) does this pinda unite with the Supreme Reality? The ancients spoke of five Gunas based on personal experience. They defined the bioenergetic channels (Nadis), along with the five forces, five Shaktis, the energies of manifestation and the qualities of the Antahkarana, the properties of the individual soul that persist from birth to birth. The pinda is an expression of Nature-Shakti, whose transforming energy is encoded in every body so that it may carry the spirit with all its baggage of previous karmas and inclinations.

…When he said that the human brain, attached to the spinal cord, resembles the body of a spermatozoon, listeners smiled. But such a comparison was not a metaphor; he quite sincerely believed that advanced yogis could merge with the Earth, transforming into a kind of “awakened sperm.”

At a certain stage of his personal practice, he came to the conclusion that the keys to eternal life are encoded in kriya itself. The Nabhi technique was a program that “switched on” longevity (and, in certain cases, immortality); Khechari mudra started the engine of the mind; and the spinal cord was the cable through which the voltage of the ascending energy of Kundalini-Shakti reached the brain. Speaking of the activation of chakras and the awakening of Kundalini energy, various yogic traditions described the same concept: emerging from the inertia of a “sperm-like” state, awakening the activity of the spinal cord, through which the brain is “charged.” Once awakened, consciousness is fully capable of uniting with the largest egg—the Earth. The mind, expanded as a result of samadhi, begins to transform the body for yet another metamorphosis, and the yogic body, charged with the voltage of developed consciousness, is able to connect with the consciousness of the planet.

In ancient times, highly developed yogis were buried, not cremated, because their bodies became inert—simply stilled, not dead. Figuratively speaking, the incorruptible bodies of ascetics in samadhi were precisely that transformed “sperm.” A body untouched by death, with preserved expanded consciousness, testified that the person was ready for union with the planet; its very presence was a signal for the “egg” of the Earth to take control of the process—this was the highest level of yogic attainment.

Passing from one state to another, all living forms are constantly changing; therefore, the stage of Sattva occurs only after the journey through the darkness of Tamas, through the activity and sensuality of Rajas. Then, in the state of Sattva, the imago transforms into a butterfly. Uniting with the Earth, the yogi acquires a new, as yet unknown form.

And, accepted by the Mother, by Her grace, he may be presented to the true Father. As a son (or daughter), he may be His exact copy. Therefore, it can be surmised that a yogi who has undergone transformation in the depths of the Earth could become a copy of the Creator Himself. The All-Father, who creates myriad souls to carry His genetic material into the egg of the Earth, fertilizing Her with Life. For a yogi, such an achievement would be the pinnacle of Sattva Guna. One born in this way would have transcended the Gunas, becoming trigunatita, “one who is beyond the three gunas.” Also known as Turiya, or the fourth state, this is the true secret of the Three Gunas.

The story seemed grand, incredible—due to the scale of events and participants; yet this information was obtained by him during samadhi. Proof awaited discovery by practitioners, not by scholarly bookworms. His views on the theory of such evolution developed gradually; it took considerable time to assess it critically. He also believed that the ancient legends of gods born of the Earth lent this theory of evolution a level of credibility.

What was the apocalypse for sperm? The survival of one spermatozoon and the death of the rest. Sperm could only realize the vastness of the world after transforming into a fully developed human form. So too does the soul come to know the continuum of life after passing through the stage of existence in an airy body.

At some point, he came to the conclusion that the human mentality in its mass was no different from the behavior of spermatozoa: people thought that salvation could be achieved by running as fast as possible—or by trying to hinder the running of others. At least the first method could lead somewhere. But politicians and priests had long tried to use the second method—to stop others from unauthorized movements, to limit choice. Everyone was running somewhere; some “chased after the presidency,” others conducted “races for peace”; the expression “life is a race” had become extremely popular. But what survived in this race was not the fittest, but the most intelligent individuals.

…The faces of the listeners reflected varying degrees of disbelief, astonishment, and doubt, yet no one was indifferent. Skeptics could consider this concept a fairy tale; it did not concern him. After a long silence, someone asked: “Is such a union with the planet even possible? And if so, can it be achieved in a single lifetime?”

“Why not try to verify it yourselves?”

The Final Transformation

…The discussions about the three gunas had only touched the tip of the iceberg, triggering an avalanche of new questions and theories. The topic required deeper explanation, as some students assumed that everyone leaving the body automatically transitions into the state of sattva, of goodness. This is not at all the case.

When consciousness transcends the body into the state of samadhi, the boundlessness of formless, supportless, pure consciousness could be “seen.” And in it was everything. Attachments, sorrows, regrets, the thick sticky brew of emotions and feelings—all the unprocessed refuse remained in the bulky chest of the body. Samadhi liberated one from everything, although individual memories were preserved, but colored in tones of absolute freedom from bodily limitations. “Exiting” the body, the mind was no longer dependent on the elements. This is not a hypothetical assumption, but knowledge from direct experience.

Being in Samadhi, having transcended the body, from Raja Guna, the yogi gains an inkling of the state of Sattva. In physical bodies, people are constantly in the state of Raja Guna. But what happens when the body is no more? Why does physical death not guarantee an automatic transition into a “sattvic” state?

Sometimes he encountered ghosts who continued to suffer as a consequence of their karmas even in their aerial bodies. Lacking physical bodies, these spirits were still far from a state of bliss, since kleshas and samskaras follow every soul into the astral world. The indelible, cemented history of past contacts with the world remains an integral part of the soul.

There was no guarantee that the soul (consciousness) would automatically transition into the blissful state of sattva in the astral realm. The jiva had to perform a certain work; after completing the transformation from a spermatozoon into human form, all beings are born from the darkness of Tamas into the world of sensory cognition, Rajas. And as long as blood flows in the body, the sattvic state is unattainable, for blood is part of the transitional stage of Rajo-guna, which determines embodiment. Incidentally, this is why intrauterine blood is called "Raj"; it embodies Rajas, or the second phase of the great general plan of the soul's development.

What occurs after discarnation has been described many times: the three gross elements of the body (earth, water, fire) disintegrate after cremation, burial, or any other method of disposing of the body; the remaining elements—air and ether—mix with Prana, and the soul attaches itself to the element of air, forming a Vayu Sharira, an aerial body. It will now exist in the astral world, most often in the atmosphere enveloping the planet.
