Earth is a true magnet for spirits in need of bodily incarnations, to continue their journey, to transition to the next level. Perhaps the greatest secret of Earth was that She preserved the past, the future, and the present. And, as the keeper of all archives, She provided Her children with a comprehensive education. To attain the highest, spirits had to be born on Earth, so that the element of earth would be present in their physical bodies. Then they would begin to travel through the world of the five elements, sensing, interacting, knowing, and learning to discriminate.

In the newly formed aerial body, the soul hung suspended "between worlds" before the next transformation. All its former memories, sorrows, and joys were now neatly packed into the continuing consciousness, but this state was not sattvic. If the Jiva had previous yogic experience, it could choose to practice in the aerial body, and the development of consciousness would not be interrupted. Such a soul would inevitably come to understand the meaning of the concepts "heaven and hell," for in the astral realm there exist diverse worlds, and all of them are created solely by collective mind. There, in the astral, an inquiring soul could discover the essence of life and death, know the nature of divinity and the origin of the gods. And it could decide to remain forever with Earth, undergoing no changes, renouncing the next birth. But this is possible only for highly evolved souls. Some Jivas returned back into human bodies, for gradual movement toward higher levels of development. Not so many souls are sufficiently evolved to perceive the subtle mechanisms of body creation, the nature and the very idea of bodily incarnation. Therefore, not all are ready for the next stage of spiritual evolution.

But even for evolved souls, the path to the state of Sattva is long: they must be born and pass through the stages of Tamas and Rajas anew, this time developing consciously. Only then, incarnated in a physical body, can they attain Samadhi and unite with the consciousness of Earth.

To attain the sattvic state in the body is possible in the state of Samadhi. The continuing living body of a yogin became like a frozen spermatozoon. Ascetics who had entered the state of mahasamadhi (having left their bodies) were surrendered to Earth, resting in Her giant ovum.

Although most practitioners reflected on the meaning of life and death long before initiation into yoga, such a scheme of evolution seemed complex to them. At the beginning of the path, students were overwhelmed by the incredible intricacy of the designs of Creation, but Kriya accelerated the thought process, and gradually students could follow the flow of the Guru's thoughts.

…The silence in the room became almost oppressive. "So, does a purely sattvic state not exist?"

"Not to my knowledge, no. Perhaps only trees and plants are fully sattvic; there is not a drop of karma in them, for there is no blood in beings of the plant world. They are directly connected to Mother Earth, the stages of their transformations are pure, and their consciousness is developed, and trees know everything that She knows. Here, in Braj, every tree is considered sacred. It is believed that for hundreds of years, holy ascetics perform their tapas here, having taken the form of trees, and I am inclined to believe this. 'Dying,' trees merely change form, like everything else in this world. A true devotee deserves liberation if only because his sole desire is never to be separated from the Mother."

Earth, Kundalini, Samadhi

When asked about Kundalini, he tried to explain as accessibly as possible.

"We are all earthlings, of that there is no doubt. But for some reason, few realize the direct connection of the earth element in the human body with the planet itself and its direct connection to Kundalini. The powerful energy dormant in the muladhara chakra is usually depicted as a golden square with four petals, the symbol of the earth element. The power of Earth is encoded in the earth element, a constituent part of the human body, and a yogin who has 'opened' the muladhara chakra can establish a connection with the planet. Awakening in Muladhara, the energy of earth rises in the body, activating all five primary elements of which it is composed. Thus occurs Prithvi Yoga, union with the planet; thus human consciousness connects with the consciousness of the planet, and the seeker receives universal Knowledge of Earth. Any wisdom we receive here, in physical bodies, is merely a part of the science of the nature and essence of the planet. What if this is insufficient, and the seeker wishes to comprehend extraterrestrial phenomena? The planet floats in space; She is perfectly aware of the infinity of the Void. Having once 'connected' to the consciousness and energy of Earth, the yogin will be able to understand this mystery as well. Such is the true meaning of the so-called 'awakening of Kundalini,' and this explanation is by no means a metaphysical allegory, but a description of a specific psychophysical process and method, described in numerous yogic texts.

The fourth chapter of the 'Hatha Yoga Pradipika' speaks of mudras and the awakening of Kundalini; without delving into anatomical details, the text nevertheless describes the awakening and ascent of the supreme energy, the sequence of processes occurring in each chakra during this.

There is a widespread misconception that the rise of Kundalini occurs simultaneously with Samadhi. Allow me to disagree; my personal experience proves that the awakening of this energy is a gradual, progressive process, and even after the state of Samadhi becomes routine, the full awakening of Kundalini is a long affair. For anyone striving to unravel this mystery, the very word 'Kundalini' should equal 'Consciousness,' for the activation of Kundalini means the expansion of consciousness.

When a snake is struck with a stick, it rises and straightens like a rod; similarly, consciousness rises and expands when it is provoked, 'challenged.' The practice of yoga is nothing other than a challenge to the inertia of the subconscious. But before it awakens, it is necessary to clear the Sushumna. The preparation of this channel consists of removing blockages and obstacles from the path of the ascending energy. Fused together and redirected into the Sushumna, Prana and Apana form Kundalini, and such an explanation will suit those interested in the physical aspects of the greatest yogic mystery.

This process is both simple and divine simultaneously: Khechari mudra acts as a fuse and ignition key, and the fused Prana and Apana pass through the 'heart knot,' then 'pierce' the navel, or Nabhi, and descend into muladhara, 'unlocking' it. The unified pranic energy enters the 'cleared' Sushumna, meeting no obstacles or blockages, and rises upward, into the skull, into Sahasrara, and beyond. It is thus that consciousness acquires its divine aspect.

The original sources of yogic texts mention that the awakening of Kundalini grants immortality; the yogin attains eternal youth; freed from the duality pervading existence, he is no longer obliged to be reborn and die. Is this not the eternal and unattainable dream of humanity: to stop the repeated journeys through life and death, in order to finally realize one's purpose?

I have met no one whose Kundalini was fully awakened. While some yogins attain Samadhi, the complete ascent and stabilization of the risen energy remains a most rare phenomenon. Of two yogins who have attained samadhi, only one may awaken Kundalini fully. I can assure you: the complete and constant 'activation' of this energy occurs once or twice in a thousand years.

The fact that the phenomenon of the rise of Kundalini is described in tantric texts does not mean it was widespread. It is simply that today knowledge has become more accessible, the term has become known and discussed, and Kundalini has turned into a 'spiritual commodity,' part of esoteric jargon. The seriousness and depth of this subject require adherence to the highest standard of techniques laid down by the ancient Masters. And yet the complexity of the task should not stop seekers; they must find a worthy guide and study the road maps. Information from primary sources, intense sadhana, and the guidance of a knowing Guru will help them reach the goal."

Hatha Yoga Pradipika and the True Mahavedha

…A little over a week had passed since the visit to the Aghori, and Shri Satyacharan Lahiri insisted that he also visit the Sanskrit scholars, the famous "mahamahopadhyayas." The rulers of ancient India gave such titles to experts in the Shastras, and the tradition was preserved under British rule. In Benares there were several scholars of this rank, and the Teacher knew them all.

"They are the last of the true experts; go to them while they are still alive, and perhaps you will hear something useful…" The required place was less than a 20-minute walk from Chausatti Ghat, and the accompanying boy led him confidently through the narrow lanes. Soon they knocked at one of the houses; he was led onto a terrace. Two elderly people, appearing around ninety years old, were completely absorbed in a lively discussion, surrounded by seven or eight attentive listeners. He quietly sat down on the floor. The atmosphere in the room was filled with extraordinarily pure vibrations. Effortlessly moving from shloka to shloka, quoting passages in the purest Sanskrit, turning to Vedic texts to extract necessary arguments, the scholars were clearly in an altered state of consciousness. It was there that he heard one of the strangest stories about the Hatha Yoga Pradipika. Of course, he knew and had read "The Light on Hatha Yoga," yet that day the mahamahopadhyayas were discussing the famous text from a completely unexpected angle. In Benares, the city of scholars and the cradle of Knowledge, those who wished to see the origins of Knowledge always dug deep. The history of the text itself was quite unusual.

Somewhere at the end of the 19th century, a man named Brahmananda decided to become a Nath yogi and set off for Haridwar, to the Sapta Sarovar ashram. There he asked for initiation, and the Mahant of the ashram agreed: "Good, you may stay, and if you meet the requirements, you will become a Nath." The novice washed dishes, cooked, and performed other menial work, and the Naths patiently observed him. After about five years, Brahmananda began to worry; enlightenment was still unattainable, the daily routine irritated him, and his inflated ego could not accept such a way of life. In the end, Brahmananda left the ashram, appropriating the original manuscript of the "Hatha Yoga Pradipika." Being a Sanskrit scholar, he published his own commentary on this text in 1893. From a yogic perspective, they were utterly useless; an academic who never became a yogi set forth only assumptions, false conclusions, and incorrect interpretations.

Both respected scholars agreed that non-practitioners cannot successfully translate yogic texts. This story, together with the extraordinarily deep analysis

and explanations of certain Sanskrit terms led him to a conclusion that later became a tool of his own teaching methods: one must always turn to the original source, whether it be the original text or the primordial idea. Even the shadow of Truth lay closer to Her than everything else.

In 1982, having joined a Kriya Yoga group, he read the *Hatha Yoga Pradipika* in Hindi, translated from Sanskrit. The classic fifteenth-century manual on hatha yoga was written by Svatmarama, and the text was directly connected to the teachings of the Natha Yogis, but at the time he did not pay attention to this. Several group members were trying to master asanas, and he also attempted to perform the postures indicated in the book. There were only sixteen of them. At first, the body was stiff and inflexible, but within three to four months he learned to perform most of the asanas and absorbed the basic rules: bandhas, breath retentions. Thus he practiced until initiation, and then, with the practice of Kriya, he gradually developed an understanding of the mudras described in the book. The *Hatha Yoga Pradipika* was the primer of Kriya Yoga.

After his first samadhi, he focused on strength training instead of asanas, and now only mudras, padmasana, and nauli were part of his yogic routine. The intensified weight training expanded his ribcage to such an extent that he could no longer perform baddha-padmasana. The lateral back muscles also increased, and the practice of asanas became a memory. Yet, alongside strength training, some asanas were an excellent compensation for the load on the nervous system. They worked on isometric principles, strengthening ligaments and joints, while training with kettlebells and barbells developed endurance. Both approaches were good. Flexibility of the body and steel of the muscles, observation of polarities and stability of emotions. Padmasana, the lotus posture, followed exercises with heavy Indian clubs—mudgars; winter mist gave way to summer heat. One simply had to find a point of support and exist in that interval between exhalation and inhalation.

Moderation in everything was the prerogative, except in the striving to reach the limits of the possible in sadhana. Here he went beyond the boundaries of the possible and urged others to do the same. For him, the Middle Way, the first teaching given by the Buddha after enlightenment, and the yogic journey into the central channel, into Sushumna, meant the same thing. For years he perfected a semi-reclining pose on a couch, with his legs stretched out on a coffee table, jokingly calling it "Guru-asana"—and was perfectly aware of what some visitors thought. Looking at him, they secretly wondered whether this Guru could sit in the lotus posture. His relaxed manner of behavior differed greatly from the style of other gurus and swamis, adorned with flower garlands, in ochre robes, ceremoniously seated at satsangs with crossed legs. Yet, being occupied with worldly affairs and the management of their organizations, these respected masters hardly spent long hours in yogic meditation. He remained in padmasana for six to eight hours every night. Sadhana was the only thing that mattered, and he did not care about the impression he made on others.

In the winter of 2013, Ukrainian students began asking questions related to the *Hatha Yoga Pradipika*. Young, full of energy and inquisitive interest, they were masters of body yoga, performing the most complex asanas and practicing kriya for several years. Now they were performing pranayamas with Khechari mudra, trying certain postures and mudras, following his explanations in the light of kriya. Everyone was charged with the magic of Knowledge, intoxicated by Its taste, perceived anew. He dictated progressively and evenly, explaining the hidden meaning of seemingly well-known asanas. It was then that the students first heard a new interpretation of the *Hatha Yoga Pradipika* as a guide to Kriya Yoga. Every asana and bandha described in the text was performed in the course of kriya practice. Twenty years ago, he had thought of publishing an exact translation of the shlokas and commenting on this text, but he received a signal from Babaji not to do so. The Guru of the parampara said that the truth is set forth in the original Sanskrit text, and the wise will understand the essence of the teaching. There was no need to go into details.

When, after a long time, permission to continue the work came, no one dared to ask in what form and exactly when. Moreover, there were other, no less interesting revelations: one day he casually mentioned that Svatmarama, the author of the *Hatha Yoga Pradipika*, was none other than Babaji himself. No one knew how to react to this statement. After a pause, he added: "If you have received initiation into the practice, there is no need to endlessly seek additional sources of revelation. Knowing where the teaching and techniques come from is good, but true discovery occurs only through practice, through your sadhana. Of course, it is better to know certain things to save time and effort, and for that, original sources exist." That day, the students first heard the story of the true Mahavedha. It is a perfect example of a reshaped truth and of how it can turn into a distorting mirror of ignorance and ambition.

Modern practicing yoga specialists believe that Mahavedha activates the Kundalini energy at the base of the spine, in the root chakra, and helps the united prana and apana move through Sushumna. Most yoga teachers instruct students to sit in padmasana, place their hands on the floor, lift themselves up, and begin to progressively slap their buttocks against the ground while holding the breath. The lifting and "throwing" of the body onto the floor in the lotus posture with the help of both hands creates a cushioning effect in the sacrum. Doing all this and concentrating on the Muladhara chakra, one is supposed to awaken Kundalini.

In 1986, having analyzed the true meaning of the Sanskrit shlokas and compared it with the widely known version of Mahavedha, he could not help but laugh. In the translation and commentary on the original manuscript, stolen from the Natha ashram, the meaning of Mahavedha was grotesquely distorted. But since this was the first known commentary on the ancient manuscript, everyone began to meticulously follow it, without questioning the meanings. Mahavedha (the second Nabhi kriya in the practice of kriya) had nothing to do with bouncing on the buttocks, as proposed by ignorant translators. The misinterpretation began with the description of two parts of the frontal lobes of the brain being translated as… buttocks. And so modern yogis began to lift their hips and slap them against the floor. In the original text, the brain was described in precise terms, but the translator understood it completely incorrectly. The seriousness and pomposity of the "yoga gurus" who performed this asana and taught others were grotesque and sad.

The original shloka of Mahavedha states: *samahastayugō* – "bring the hands together," not "place them on the ground." Then comes the word *bhumau*, literally translated as "earth." But the Sanskrit dictionary also gives another meaning – "tongue." "With *bhumau* strike the hip-like region." In any anatomical atlas and in every medical reference book, one can find an image of the frontal lobes of the brain – it resembles buttocks.

Mahavedha is to be performed with the hands folded in Namaste, holding the breath, and sitting in Padmasana, with the tongue in Khechari mudra, raised into the nasal passage. Then the tongue begins to "stroke" the brain (the hip-like region – the frontal lobes of the brain). Be that as it may, this amusing error crept into well-known yogic texts, and thousands of people mercilessly pounded their posteriors against the ground in hopes of revelation. When performed correctly, Mahavedha had an effect on consciousness. The brain waves, stimulated by Khechari mudra, calmed down, and the practitioner could experience a temporary state of "brain death." Absolute stillness of the mind, beautiful and infinite like a clear sky. Brain activity decreased, yet the body remained alive. After exhalation, the brain returned to a semi-active state, awakening theta waves. Then the yogi could enter a superconscious state, and the conscious mind became subconscious. And vice versa.

The commentaries on the *Hatha Yoga Pradipika* were published on November 3, 2014.

**The Law of Repulsion and Surfing in Time**

Newton's third law states that every action has an equal and opposite reaction. This also means that every reaction causes an equal and opposite action, that cause and effect are merely the edges of the rim of the Samsaric wheel. Indeed, what binds and holds this world together?

Once, while discussing the law of gravity with an American physicist, he inquired: if gravitational attraction is so great, why do all the stars and planets not eventually collide, crash into each other? The physicist gave an extremely complex answer, which turned into an almost two-hour lecture. Somewhere in the middle, he stopped following the meaning, lost among hypotheses and secondary topics. To the surprise of the esteemed professor, he suggested that gravitational "repulsion" might keep the stars and planets at a distance. Such a force could be the reason they do not collide with other celestial bodies, since they mutually "repel" each other.

Shuffling the cards of human history, this same force of "repulsion" set countless processes in motion. Perhaps the "Law of Repulsion" held together and united kingdoms and peoples, helping to maintain the fragile balance of races and cultures. They did not lose their identity because they resisted invasions, gravitating toward the unifying force of power, and by centralizing, they rejected everything foreign.

Today, the main rule of the new game became decentralization. The "Law of Repulsion" created new "centers of attraction" for various communities striving to survive and begin anew. The knowledge of the collective past preserved in archives was the greatest joke humanity played on itself. The ability to analyze and draw conclusions is undoubtedly a brilliant and unique feature of human intelligence, but most conclusions are tailored to fit the political and national needs of the moment. Any data is manipulated, and conclusions are adjusted by modern historians, since history is always written by the victors. Achieving objectivity is not so difficult, but no one can maintain it for too long. Human history was a writhing worm, bait on a fisherman's hook.

Undoubtedly, the Earth is the most ancient being, embodying its own past. Human bodies are also an expression of the past, measured by time frames and the duration of previous lives. The past karmas of ancestors are encapsulated in the living. All previous incarnations are watermarks, postmarks on the sealed envelopes of bodies. So, if everything (cities, states, traditions) originated in the past, history, to a certain extent,

To what degree is it predictable? Only under the condition of objectivity, if all the details of the past are known. This is an excellent task for a developed yogic mind, and that is precisely how he approached everything, getting to the roots, to the very essence.

The present can be lived in its fullness only under the condition of stepping out of the physical sheath, overcoming the limitations of form. The body always carries individual karma. Separation from it allows one to observe the present objectively, without becoming entangled in the personal history of experiences and memories, avoiding deeply rooted self-pity or hatred.

The imprints of past and present karmas do not allow the mind to experience the Present in its pure form. Everything, absolutely everything, is a representation of the past. The human ability to recognize, distinguish, evaluate, and draw conclusions is based on previous experience, and therefore total cognition is exclusive. The ordinary path of humanity is the acknowledgment of the past and its continuous analysis.

The law of cause and effect is merely an abstraction, pointing to how the world unfolds, and to grasp the truth, to catch a glimpse of tendencies, intuition is necessary.

This is a fairly broad and widespread phenomenon, but the yogic mind goes deeper, to the roots of any beginning, to the primordial instincts. At the base of any process lie the causal factors that trigger it, and all of them are in the past. The final effect also triggers other processes, building a chain of events leading to a possible future. Perhaps this is why some writers and scholars have asserted that causality metaphysically precedes all notions of time and space. And some philosophers have claimed: the probable events that are about to occur have already occurred; the world simply moves through the amplitude of the ripples of Time, where the past has already determined the future. Everything was possible.

For him, Samadhi was the key to understanding both the present and the future. It freed consciousness from the physical limitations of the body, which was an expression of the past. Perhaps everything always happened simultaneously: the past conceived the future, the future looked upon this conception in disbelief and sometimes with disgust, and the present was too selfish to be interested in its own origin.

And yet, a yogi honing the experience of navigating the subtle worlds could attract the attention of the Masters, and they could teach him the advanced practice of surfing in Time. Possessing such a skill, such a yogi could choose any period of time to visit. Having fully known the present, he could see the past, penetrating into the times of Jesus or Buddha, into the era of the Mahabharata, into the Jurassic period for the sake of understanding the laws of the movement of Time itself. As long as souls are confined in bodies, the present did not exist. Natural death is merely an effect of rejection: the Spirit no longer considered the body fit for further journey.

**Twilight Language**

More than 40 years had passed since, in one of the bookstores, he paid 2 rupees for a thin little book whose content proved invaluable. The text entitled “Gorakh Bodh” (“The Enlightenment of Gorakh”) contained about one hundred and thirty shlokas, written in archaic Hindi. It is a dialogue between Guru Matsyendranath and his disciple Goraksha, fascinating, simple, and yet complex to understand. The play of poetic metaphors and hidden meanings presented there constituted a seductive puzzle.

The twilight language, or “sandhya bhasha,” spoken by the immortal Nathas, was a complete enigma. Often used in Tantric and alchemical texts, the “inverted” language was invented by yogis. They were the first Tantrics and alchemists. Such texts were intentionally written in a format inaccessible to the uninitiated. The encoded slang spoke of “real” and “imaginary” worlds that were neither invented nor entirely real, and such texts could only be explained by a practicing Master. And although Shri Satyacharan Lahiri often discussed the Bhagavad Gita, the diaries of Lahiri Mahasaya, and various Puranic texts with his disciples, the hidden meanings of the “Gorakh Bodh” somehow never came up.

The secret language sounded deceptively simple, and many took it for primitive archaic folklore. For most academic researchers, the text represented merely a linguistic curiosity. But it was a treasure awaiting its discovery, left for future generations of yoga practitioners. For the uninitiated, the parallels and metaphors of the text remained a play on words, and even after translation into modern Hindi or English, the text continued to seem like flowery poetic gibberish.

Over the last couple of centuries, the world has developed thanks to discoveries in mathematics, astronomy, and physics, and these sciences did not recognize allegories. But when scientists tried to speak of higher forces and universal laws, they inevitably reached a dead end; they lacked knowledge of the fundamental component of being, the Universal Consciousness dwelling in every living being, the Spirit. However, yogis, alchemists of consciousness, knew that yoga-sadhana could condense, crystallize, and distill the Spirit that filled the crucible of the body. The raw material for the experiment is quite accessible – Prana and Apana, plus the body and mind of the practitioner himself. Together with other ingredients: life circumstances, karmic predispositions, and the grace of ancestors and teachers. Luck, perseverance, and will could transform the yogi himself into “alchemical gold.”

To explain the origin of the world from a single Source, the Nathas used multivalent metaphors. They spoke of the condensation of Sound and its vibrations, which created Matter, from which the tangible world originated. “Gorakh Bodh” spoke of physical reality and the consciousness perceiving this reality, of Time and Emptiness – the eternal parents of unborn Sound, of the play of Sound with the primordial elements it generated. While Prana and Apana dwelt in the human body, they were loving spouses, whose separation at the time of the physical body’s death also signified the departure of the Spirit.

For the ancient siddhas, the continuity of Creation was obvious – ongoing and unstoppable, it is like life, death, and breath. Life interrupted in one being continued in a multitude of others. “Gorakh Bodh” described the highest goals of yoga, the life of consciousness, and the essence of the eternal Spirit. Beneath the layer of the twilight language of the text, the yogic science of the eternal alchemy of the inquiring mind was expounded. Despite its allegorical nature, the text described the purely physical aspects of the body’s capabilities required for the awakening of the Spirit. Special attention was paid to the necessity of turning the body into an ally and the effort of establishing a friendly relationship with the immortal substance dwelling within the body. The Spirit could safely be called “holy,” for it dwelt in the temple of the body, awaiting the meeting with its worshiper. Often this waiting was in vain. Undetected, but by no means discouraged by the lack of recognition, the Spirit continued to move from body to body, settling in different “sanctuaries.”

His personal psychophysical transformations were the steps of alchemical sublimation, and some of the disciples passed through the same stages of regeneration. He gave them the necessary formulas, and now they were extracting the gold of knowledge through painstaking refinement. The twilight language of the text was such an artful code that only a developed consciousness could decipher its meanings. And then magic happened.

Matsyendranath advised Gorakhnath: “…make the Spirit your friend – and then it will become the Guru of your mind. Then the disciple and the Teacher will change places, and the Spirit, having become the disciple of the mind, will no longer leave you.” For practicing yogis, aware of the magic of such a union, this was a practical recommendation for achieving immortality, no more, no less. The meaning of the intricate metaphors was revealed exclusively through sadhana. While the Spirit listened to the words of the ancient Nathas, simultaneously with cognition, the transmutation of the material body into an immortal one occurred. And if someone decided to choose such a path, Life itself – the greatest alchemist and yogi – offered enough material for the experiment.

As with the commentaries on the Gita, his thoughts were only waiting for structure and format to turn into a continuous narrative. He was first asked about “Gorakh Bodh” in 2008, and he gave explanations of the properties of Prana, citing some passages from the text and revealing their meaning. Other questions followed, and in 2015, full-scale work on the commentaries to the book began. The text was no longer a mystery to him; years of Kriya practice allowed him to rely on personal experience. The answers poured forth as if he were reading an invisible book or listening to words inaudible to others. The disciples’ questions revealed different degrees of their own awareness; what had not yet been revealed to them in practice was patiently explained as accessibly as the context of the conversation allowed.

The yogic commentaries on the original text were published in 2017 under the title “The Twilight Language of Gorakh Bodh,” and he was convinced that this work would not have been accomplished without the blessing of Matsyendranath himself. He believed that it was the day of meeting the Immortal teacher in Mahurgad that sowed the seeds of new inquiry, stimulated new perception – and the metaphorical nature of the text acted as a catalyst for realization.

The collection of his literary and philosophical works grew: the yogic commentaries on the Bhagavad Gita, the Shiva Sutras, the Yoga Sutras, and the Hatha Yoga Pradipika were followed by two books – “At the Right Hand of God” and “The Upanishad of Immortality.” Every word and every idea presented in the books was filtered through many years of practice and consolidated by knowledge. Once someone inquired: “What do you think about the future of your books, will they ever attract a wider audience?” – “They will become shastras.”

**Tradition**

There is an excellent aphorism, often mistakenly attributed to the composer Gustav Mahler: “Tradition is not the worship of ashes, but the preservation of fire.” Whoever the author of these words may be, he captured the very essence. Any tradition is alive only thanks to those who fill their reality with its meaning. People tend to analyze postulates, but does this mean the freedom to interpret and modify the teachings?

…In 2013, a group of yoga enthusiasts released a documentary film entitled “Yoga: Traditions Through the Eyes of Modernity.” The filmmakers interviewed renowned yoga teachers from around the world: Indians, an Australian, a Briton, and an American. At the end of the long and informative film, the only one among the film’s participants, Guru of Yoga Shailendra Sharma, was asked about the essence of the yogic tradition, its indestructible component, and the place of spiritual traditions in the modern world. The answers were direct and uncompromising.

Question: How is yoga changing today as a cross-cultural tradition? How is this tradition assimilated by the minds of Westerners, and how does Western culture influence modern Indian yoga?

Answer: Yoga was never merely an ancillary,

**a complementary discipline.** It is one and only, integral and inviolable. Karma, Bhakti, and Raja Yoga have always been streams of one river and parts of one system. The Yoga of Patanjali is the foundation of all other directions of yoga. However, at present, people have no clear understanding of this ancient tradition.

**Question:** What then is traditional yoga, if all its directions known today in the West originated in the 20th century?

**Answer:** There is only one Yoga, the Yoga of Adinatha, the yoga of the Adi Guru, the First Nath and the First Teacher of Yoga – Shiva. Continued by Matsyendranath and Gorakhnath, it is transmitted through the line of the Naths. The Sampradaya of the Naths exists even today, but the depth of the true teaching is revealed only to those who are ready to go to the end, until they attain liberation. To those who are ready to observe the strictest discipline. According to the ancient tradition, in this Sampradaya the Guru-disciple system still lives.

Yoga has always been a system of transmitting knowledge from Guru to disciple. The Guru is the one who gives the teaching and the practice, and the disciple is the one who performs it. After the Guru has accepted the disciple, he must make yoga the goal of his life. The system of relationships and interaction between Guru and Disciple, the yogic tradition, has been preserved for centuries. In the past, a yogi had to leave home and family, sever all ties with society and dependence upon it. Interaction with laypeople was limited to collecting alms and food, and this was the only way to break all the threads that bound the yogi to society. Thus, the tentacles of thousands of obligations, attachments, and dependencies enveloping the consciousness of the practitioner were cut off.

Modern yoga in the form it is known today in the West was formed in the 20th century. It is well known that any teaching and invention is inevitably modified by society for commercial use. Society reshapes everything according to its own needs, as it is based on the principles of profitability. Today, yoga, accepted and adapted for modern consumption, has become a commodity. I consider this a direct violation of tradition. Of course, a modern person cannot completely leave society, abandon home and family. And yet, true seekers should try to follow yoga as a path of self-development. And yes, from the point of view of ancient, traditional yoga, those who live in society and are connected to it cannot be called true yogis. Nevertheless, the original tradition will live forever, as it continues even today. The general principles remain unchanged; religions change, new gods replace old ones, messiahs and new followers appear and disappear, but yoga still has its main goal: to overcome limitations through the body and mind. They must become limitless.

This teaching is intended for all, but not everyone can follow the strict discipline. Therefore, different degrees of immersion in Kriya Yoga yield different results. Sometimes I ask myself – are we not missing in this teaching the most important thing, its true purpose? Any honest yoga teacher must ask themselves: what is it for? People are born the same, live through more or less the same physiological cycles, and then die in a relatively similar manner. The spirit that dwelt in the body always moves on, leaving the old shell behind.

Yoga is the only thing that can give a person the opportunity to realize what Life is. Through this system, the yogi experiences death while still alive. Yoga helps to understand who a person was before birth, and what will happen to him after he leaves the body. Hindus, Muslims, Christians, and agnostics live and die by the same laws, this is indisputable. And if so, then yoga is not intended exclusively for representatives of only one faith.

First and foremost, we are human beings. From time immemorial, Yoga has recognized this simple fact, equalizing the rights of everyone and anyone to receive its grace. Upon those who are ready to achieve the ambitious goal of self-knowledge and knowledge of God (which are one and the same), yoga imposes the duty to maintain discipline. This integral spiritual system is more than banal self-gratification. It provides the opportunity to become acquainted with one's own Spirit, the ancient invisible being living within everyone. With the Spirit, shedding old garments and taking on new bodies in the course of endless rebirths. In each incarnation, through the physical body, the Spirit learns through the senses and the mind, trying to know the infinity of existence and itself. How can we call ourselves spiritual people if we have still not met our own Spirit?

Religions teach following different deities, observing certain rituals, while promising the attainment of nirvana, grace, and bliss at the end of the path. However, no religion teaches one to realize and approach one's own Spirit, which is that very essence capable of leading us to the knowledge of the Creator of this very spirit.

To know God, first and foremost by knowing one's own Spirit – this is the utterly unique task of yoga. The traditional role of the Guru remains unchanged: he must transmit this knowledge.

**Question:** In many branches of 20th-century yoga, there was always someone who took the authentic knowledge of yoga, transmitted by Shiva himself, and "improved," "added to," and modified fragments of the system. All the famous yogis of the 20th century had Gurus, but they developed and supplemented the teachings given to them with their own methods.

**Answer:** The more Indian masters came to know Western people, the more they adapted their methods to the psychology and physique of new clients. Traditional Hatha Yoga was intended for flexible Indian bodies and a different type of thinking; it worked with various mental imprints and algorithms, different goals and sets of moral codes. I believe that the saints who created the original system of yoga studied throughout their lives and came to the conclusion that only this specific method was effective for achieving the goal. They left knowledge tested by their own experience and proven by their own achievements. People who try to change the tradition to suit the needs of the modern "client" cannot be called true Gurus. Of course, the physical, mental, and social level of each disciple is individual. Therefore, the Guru must always decide what level of knowledge and in what volume he can transmit to a particular disciple. And this is an individual approach, not an attempt to feed everyone from the same spoon. Nevertheless, a commercial price tag is added to certain methods of enlightenment by those who calculate annual revenue regardless of the seeker's individuality.

**Question:** One hundred or two hundred years ago, only sadhus and hermits practiced yoga. Then Lahiri Mahasaya took on the mission of introducing yoga to laypeople. Is this not a change in tradition?

**Answer:** No. The technical side of the practice of Kriya remained unchanged. However, the process itself became accessible to laypeople, ordinary people striving to know the extraordinary. The return of this yoga depended on the current situation in society, on the mental state of practitioners. Why? According to the Bhagavad Gita, advanced yogis, hardened in the fire of yogic practices in previous lives, must be born into families of yogis. And if there were no yogis among laypeople, how could these new highly developed souls come into the world? How could they fulfill their subsequent missions in human bodies? The spirits of many yogis were awaiting birth to change the world. That is precisely why Kriya Yoga was reintroduced to society. Such was Babaji's plan. My personal opinion, or rather, feeling, is that when the task of the incarnation of these souls is fulfilled, Kriya Yoga will again leave society.

With a certain degree of pragmatic objectivity, this particular segment of human history can be viewed as a "yogic breeding program plan," aimed at higher goals, at the development of humanity. And it occurred on all levels: scientific, social, moral, ethical, and mental. Let us try to trace the evolution: the birth and spiritual quest of Yogananda, his inspiration and goals of knowing the Divine changed the lives of millions, including subsequent generations. Remember his book on Steve Jobs' desk?

The new world in which we now live is driven by the minds of many spiritual giants, for it was they who inspired scientists and humanists. Traces of human evolution inspired by yoga are everywhere. I think the teaching of yoga is eternal. As long as people continue to be born and breathe, to comprehend and consciously know the essence of Prana, yoga will not lose its significance, no matter how many changes it undergoes. It is the eternal connection between body and spirit.

Of course, few strive to know their spirit. Nevertheless, all those attempting to realize the body through yogic asanas also have a chance to awaken their Spirit. And to explore further. This process will always be the shared responsibility of Guru and disciple.

"Tradition is not the worship of ashes, but the preservation of fire" – some historians claim this is an abbreviated version of a statement by the French politician Jean Jaurès.

Shailendra Sharma, 2024

With Blackie, 2023

Waves of the Future

* * *

"We live in Puranic times. Why? We are experiencing events predicted by the Puranas a thousand years ago with absolute accuracy, as if they were clearly seen from the future. But it is the past that determined today's events."
from a dialogue of Shailendra Sharma with disciples

The Beginning of a New Era

The celebration of Yashashwini's seventh birthday coincided with the second wave of COVID; a curfew was again imposed, rumors of a complete lockdown again circulated. In terms of tension and anxiety, April 2021 in northern India equaled the spring of the previous year, however this time the number of sick and dying sharply increased. Sticky anxiety hung in the darshan hall; dense and suffocating waves of fear emanated from people.

While the children played hide-and-seek and tore open gift boxes, filling the rooms of the huge house with joyful cries, the gazes of all the adults over their respirator masks were fixed on the Guru. People needed a glimmer of hope, the certainty that everything would soon return to normal. This he could not promise, as it would be untrue. Nothing could remain the same.

Fear was mainly stoked by the mass media, but the sight of bodies being burned right on the sidewalks next to overflowing crematoriums did not add optimism. The incessantly ringing phone delivered alarming news about sick relatives and friends, messages about high fever and suffocation. Listening to all this calmly, he replied laconically, almost without emotion, but not at all from a lack of compassion. This unshakable equanimity reflected the deepest awareness of the transience of everything, absolutely everything. Life, death, illness, and health are fleeting. Again and again he repeated to his dejected listeners:

"This is just a stage, it too will end…"

"But what to do now, in the face of real danger?"

"Continue practicing, strengthen your immune system, stay away from cities."

…For many years he advised disciples to move away from densely populated places and stock up on supplies for at least a year. City dwellers, company employees, business owners smiled and nodded politely. No one was ready to live in a bunker atmosphere. The survival culture that became popular in the West in recent decades never took root here, in India. Besides, food itself was never a problem. Agrarian to the core, India could always survive on its three "C"s: Chai, Chawal, Chapati. Tea, rice, bread…

But now these advices resonated with the sudden events as never before. Isolation became a necessity, and it was safer to wait out the uncertainty away from densely populated cities, where people were dying not only from the virus, but also from a lack of empathy and hope, from corruption schemes eating away at the system, from weariness of life and fear. And yet, in villages, remote jungles, and distant mountains, people continued to live in unity with nature and

...its cycles. There, the news of the deadly virus did not provoke the paranoia that had paralyzed the city dwellers. The farmers, who carried the genetic memory of epidemics, famine, and drought, continued to gather their harvests and tend their herds. In the villages, problems were solved as they arose. In Braj, there were almost no cases of the new disease.

Deep in his heart, he was convinced: the current pandemic had begun preparing for its world tour eight years ago, on that fateful morning when Mahamari asked his permission to begin her work. Of course, gods and their messengers need no approval from mortals; such forces are too immense to be influenced in any way. And yet, why did Mahamari come specifically to Moksha Bhumi? It seemed he had been informed of the coming changes, though without specific dates. Since then, outbreaks of Ebola and epidemics of various strains of influenza had become more frequent, but were not excessively alarming, until the winter of 2019, when the Goddess of diseases ceased to hide behind minor seasonal colds. Standing to her full height, She began to reap a bountiful harvest.

There was nothing new under the heavens. It is well known that cataclysms bring about changes that mark new epochs, accelerate progress, and increase the resilience of humanity. Epidemics and wars remain the bushings on the wheels of the chariot of world order, jolting over the ruts of civilization. Hired by nature herself, Mahamari stimulated evolution by her own unique method. A dutiful assistant to the Great Reaper, the pandemic held up a mirror to reality to show humanity the intricately intertwined roots of the collective unconscious, with all its vices and virtues. The Great Epidemic carried out its mission methodically and thoroughly; whether it was a planned depopulation or a random mass eviction of souls mattered not. The pandemic reminded everyone that people are merely a part of Creation and play no special role, unless they make a substantial contribution to the entire ecosystem.

The current turn of events neither shocked nor surprised him. He had thought and spoken of possible cataclysms (natural and man-made) long before they occurred—of epidemics, wars, sudden reforms of financial systems… There was nothing apocalyptic in these reflections; he was merely trying to describe the stages and cycles of impending change. Never considering himself a futurologist, a perspectivist, or a predictor—one of those who scan the horizon of what is to come—he was open to any versions and assumptions about the future, about its emergence from the present, whether concerning human society or life on Earth as a whole.

Through yoga-sadhana, his knowledge of history and philosophy had deepened immensely. His assessment of Big History was based on yogic perception, in which the scale of an individual’s joys and sufferings was commensurate with the scale of society’s interests. And above all this stood Matter, Time, and Emptiness. Everything was relative, and this relativity itself was the motto of existence.

…However strange this hypothesis might seem, in his opinion, the current problems could be caused by the aggressive extraction of natural resources, disrupting the ecological balance and destroying the integrity of the epithelium and deeper tissues of the planet. The technological breakthroughs of the 20th century accelerated the industrial consumption of raw materials. No civilization of the past could compete with modernity in the scale of exploiting natural resources; the annual extraction of natural raw materials exceeded all conceivable volumes compared to the extraction of the same minerals (oil, gas, timber) in the previous century. The body of the Earth was constantly and unceremoniously cut, drilled, torn apart; Her blood was sucked out. Riding the wave of expansive globalization, accompanied by the exploitation, pollution, and destruction of nature, governments extremely rarely conducted internal audits to assess the balance between the good of society and immediate, instant profit.

There existed a recurring historical tendency: whenever natural resources were depleted beyond a certain limit, whenever people initiated something that could seriously affect the planet’s ecosystem, cataclysms followed, sweeping away entire civilizations. Of course, it is known that peoples have disappeared due to war, pestilence, and famine, but some died out because of aggressive resource extraction. Besides the mindless exploitation of the Earth, people poisoned the land and themselves in the name of banal, momentary gain. Polluted air, dying vegetation, the silently screaming soil… Each day, another bird, animal, insect, or reptile vanished. No civilization of the past polluted the planet as the countries and governments have managed to do over the last 60 years.

…The notion that humanity is a virus that needs to be contained is not new. It could (or even should) disappear for a time, as some eco-activists suggested. This would inevitably cause a shortage of new bodies for spirits, but even they were not immune to hardship and deprivation. In the end, modern civilization will be forced to undergo what usually follows the crash of a tsunami, when the current of Time will sweep humanity into the ebb before a new wave.

Collective discussion and attempts to “save the planet” are, of course, useful, although the participants in the debates failed to notice that human civilization is simply incapable of this due to its own insignificance. The planet has always managed to save itself. It was humanity that stood at a crossroads, contemplating the possibility of change. One path of development could be the refinement and improvement on all levels of human activity (though this sounded very general). However, the pandemic forced the development and implementation of a new ethic. Another possible scenario relied on the main impulse of human nature: the readiness to fight for new spheres of influence, to try out new pastures. Both approaches were not mutually exclusive. They could proceed in parallel, if the notorious factor of the human drive for self-destruction were eliminated.

…News feeds continued to scream of impending danger: “Stay home, wear masks, avoid contact, touch nothing with bare hands, fear breathing in death at any moment…” And yet, it is in human nature to find ways to profit from any situation. The reaction to the pandemic was no different. If human communities failed to benefit from expansion, they began to think about decentralization. Within local, small settlements, it is much easier to maintain (and contain) all necessary needs: services, infrastructure, social initiatives. The days of global governance were clearly receding into the past, while familiar social structures were being tested for strength and viability.

These thoughts might seem naive, outdated, or utopian, and yet, throughout the world, in most rural, tribal, or indigenous settlements, grassroots forms of governance have always existed. In India, from time immemorial, village affairs have been handled by panchayats, or councils of elders; they were headed by a sarpanch, an elected decision-making person. During the pandemic, it was they who were forced to make decisions to ensure the survival of the inhabitants. Perhaps this is one of the first signs of a turn towards rudimentary decentralization, in which communities will consist of neighbors, and the most vital life-sustaining businesses and structures will become predominantly local. Tested by harsh times, old ideas were putting down new roots.

In a couple of decades, people might prefer life in such communities—not post-apocalyptic at all, but thriving. Secluded, but not isolated; small, but self-sufficient. Given current trends, this would be the best possible option. Ideally, in a decentralized future, the spheres of government expertise could be reduced to maintaining infrastructure, law and order, and the protection of citizens. Improved democratic electoral mechanisms could cleanse the electoral processes so that decent and qualified people could truly contribute to governance. Although realists (invariably turning into skeptics) insisted that politics could never become transparent. Who knows, the history of humanity is still relatively short…

The coming years promised to be dramatic for everyone: for those who could not come to terms with global metamorphoses, and for those who understood the benefit of change and welcomed it. As far as he could tell, positive transformations should occur and become tangible in thirty to forty years. But for this to happen, everything outdated, superfluous, and toxic must be left behind, amputated, remade. The world was changing radically and irreversibly. The Present held the Future tightly in its embrace, and both looked expectantly at their brother and parent, the Past. Time was conceiving itself anew, to give birth to a new era.

…Meanwhile, the helplessness forcibly learned during the times of self-isolation could have become a real torture, were it not for the wonders of the internet. Those who had previously shown no interest in new technologies became experts in online interaction and remote work. Even in the midst of total isolation, people could remain connected to the world, be heard and informed. The marvels of technology, which had previously created a feeling of invulnerability and total control, overnight turned into necessary tools for survival. Indeed, the last 10 years have presented a staggering multitude of new digital applications, tools, and means. And, like a seasoned card sharp, modernity created new, artificial meanings by expanding virtual perspectives, making people look away from tangible reality. The material, tangible world again seized people’s attention during the pandemic, and they mastered all kinds of digitalization with lightning speed and incredible zeal.

It remained to be hoped that in thirty years, when everything that can be sold has yielded profit, the eternal passion for satiation and possession on all levels would be quenched. And, probably, it is then that remaining ignorant of the surrounding world will become impossible, and people will learn anew to relate to Nature, rather than plunder Her. At some point, all mammals cease to depend on mother’s milk and begin to forage for food on their own.

…Puzzled by such distant prospects of normalization, the listeners reflected in silence that the predicted changes were too far off. In their view, these forecasts were not directly connected to the urgent needs of the moment. In April 2021, people simply wanted to survive, to avoid a humiliating and senseless death in overcrowded hospitals. Ultimately, the future is unknown, as it is always perceived through the murky lenses of the present. Forecasting models have a large degree of error and outline only a multitude of possibilities and tendencies. And yet, change was a gift, a secret treasure sewn into the hem of Mahamari.

He believed that through trials, people would become more acutely aware of life as the most precious of all earthly possessions.

...ness. And this awareness will stimulate the development of mindfulness, giving people a chance to become truly conscious beings. The question is whether they are intelligent enough to evolve, to refine new (and revive old) methods of interacting with Nature. Are people intuitive enough to notice that the loss of direct connection with the environment also meant the loss of deep, primordial wisdom? Be that as it may, the new era was approaching swiftly. And it is unknown whether humanity will preserve its place among other species.

In those days and months, permeated with uncertainty and fear, many wondered whether there was any meaning in human life beyond the experience of life itself. His work was not to console, but to remind. To remind that objective perception of reality is possible only through contact with their own Spirit. Because the Spirit had known many births and passed through countless deaths, wars, and epidemics. It continued to shed worn-out bodies and move on, expanding experience and acquiring memories, so one of Its names was Consciousness.

**A Brave New World and the Rebirth of Spirituality**

In any era, the spiritual life of a person is permeated with a special intuitiveness that sets a course for further development. The divine, unattainable in this life, may draw nearer in the next incarnation. This conditional yet quite real horizon awaits approach, if not touch. In today's world, active spiritual seeking is largely conditioned by the loss of the religious context familiar to the society of the past. This is not even about the presence of meaningful faith, which requires enormous spiritual effort, but about the barrenness of the soil in which human faith must grow in our time. At the end of the last century, the very foundation upon which the need for spiritual development and growth rested began to change and shift. Perhaps because the soil had become too dry and acidic for the seeds of faith to take root.

In megacities, faith suffocated, trying to squeeze into the dense schedules of city dwellers, to occupy the remaining space between pragmatism and hope. Untroubled, it still flourished in villages, in harmony with primordial rhythms. Rooted in the traditional way of life, it sprouted from the soil, entered the streams of rain, and lived within the capsule of each grain of the harvests. Bound to the changing seasons and the cycles of death and rebirth, human faith is as ancient as the earth itself. The intellectual need for faith remains, but the niche for thoughtful reflection on this necessity is quickly filled with social myths and clichés. Modern man does not understand how "rationalism" (which has nearly destroyed the ability to perceive symbols and ideas of the divine) has exposed an abyss of uncertainty. Stimulated by the constantly renewing adaptability of the human mind, the new "hells" and "heavens" of modernity are recreated only for the convenience of orientation among the ruins of the "old morality" and the newest practicality.

By readily renouncing spiritual values, modern man frees himself from "prejudices." The interruption of traditions, general disorientation, and the disintegration of ethics pose a real threat to a rapidly developing world where former moral guidelines and boundaries are declared irrelevant and old-fashioned. It is quite logical that postmodernism gave rise to post-religious uncertainty, an obvious departure from the program of the Enlightenment, against which Modernity so consistently opposed. Post-religion, modified according to the needs of modernity, constantly turns to various forms of imitation and simulation of faith. Successfully claiming the role of spirituality in a globalized and secularized world, it offers surrogates instead of Tradition.

Postmodernist ideas, which dominated the second half of the 20th century, are becoming obsolete, and postmodern systems of thought view realities as multiple, subjective, and dependent on the individual's worldview. Today, religion consistently reduces true spirituality to a simplified imitation, not allowing the exploration of the verticality of Tradition leading to the Divine. Everything is smeared across a horizontally oriented perception, without delving into details. The very model of such a concept resembles shopping malls, where the consumer rarely rises above the second floor.

This approach makes post-religion the peak of the global process of desacralization. Actually, this is characteristic of many things, concepts, and modalities. Along with the expansion of access to information, people are not actually encouraged to lift their heads from their phone screens or attempt to reach distant horizons. They can only contemplate them. Post-religion assimilates a set of postulates with recognizable and convenient interfaces. Tunnel vision replaces panoramic perspectives.

A product of social development, religion has become the aged parent of modern spirituality. And this rebellious child stubbornly chooses its own path. Archaic by its nature, statehood believes it must defend itself against new ideas, new blood, new players. Therefore, it protects its old ally – religion. For this is a proven and convenient system of mind control. The establishment still views uncontrolled and eclectic spirituality with suspicion.

Being an active state of the soul, spirituality strives for extra-physical experiences and metaphysical revelations. Today, it educates itself through the deconstruction of religion. There is a growing tendency to revise and deconstruct everything – from image and cultural tradition to political programs and religious authorities. To question any information or doctrine before discarding it or, having ground it into pieces, making a collage out of it. Skepticism towards moral guidelines, a cynical perception of truths, has become popular. Everything is perceived as a play of the mind and may be subject to deconstruction, while an appeal to ethics looks like an attempt at repression, suppression, or manipulation of individual beliefs.

Deconstruction always has a purpose. An old building can be demolished with the ultimate goal of building something new, something that will last longer. A wide highway is laid over an old path to withstand the elements. Religion resists this process, defending itself in every possible way, appealing to morality and loyalty to long-established values. It operates through hidden persuasion, veiled suppression, and well-disguised and carefully thought-out prohibitions; it sells itself less aggressively, as fatigue and skepticism grow among consumers. Seizing the opportunity, agnostics and mainly the new generation of potential parishioners question and dissect the very necessity of faith.

Most organized religions arose in times when monarchies already existed. But even then, the periodic "renewal" of gods was a necessity that could ensure a better future for states and nations. In the first decades of the new 21st century, a certain spiritual impatience, a thirst for change in orthodox belief systems (on many levels, on all continents), began to grow rapidly, as if trying to catch up with new technologies, for they too require a new and modernized moral goal-setting, along with the clarification of existential meanings.

Spirituality has never been "organized" due to its abstractness, and more and more people believe that it is spirituality that should inherit the modern cultural landscape. God-seeking is slowly being replaced by the striving to know the Spirit, not the Holy Spirit, but the human spirit, and this knowledge is becoming a priority even from the perspective of the growing cult of individuality. If contemporaries are encouraged to know their bodies and sexual preferences, manage their personal financial portfolios, and form unique individual fashion styles, then they may well study the intention of their own spirit, its aspirations and desires. After all, few doubt that It is present within every living being.

The immortal spirit was born even before the gods appeared. The tenth chapter of the Rig Veda hints at this:

*But who can say,*
*From where this all came, and how creation happened?*
*For the gods themselves arose only after Creation,*
*So who truly knows from where It came?*

The Spirit no longer needs to force itself into the shackles of religion. The current religious foundations were invented and perfected by efficient managers of the past who were in the service of statehood. States provided protection for taxpayers who were "regulated" by religious establishments. There is nothing wrong with this, but today such protection is no longer required. Being in the bosom of the church no longer ensures a reliable connection with the Divine. Modern spirituality is a Wi-Fi connecting a person to their own spirit, and the server of this network is located beyond the carbon-based universe.

Being a Guru, he knew: all people carry the seeds of faith in their hearts, all are created to believe and dream, to imagine something greater than the crude and rather disappointing reality. In the Academy of Siddha Siddhanta, the taught discipline is considered the science of the connection of the individual spirit with the Supreme Soul. This is a continuation of the ancient path, maintaining an unbreakable bond between Guru and disciple, and for this journey, no intermediaries are needed except a qualified Mentor.

Rooted in the philosophy of Hinduism as one of the six *darshanas* (Sankhya, Nyaya, Vaisheshika, Mimamsa, and Vedanta), Yoga remains part of the Hindu tradition, no matter how skillfully it is repackaged for sale. It would be a mistake to view Hinduism as a religion – it is a way of life, a state of the soul. The multiplicity of Indian gods leaves people no choice but to profess tolerance towards all forms of the divine. An ancient form of spirituality, Hinduism does not limit the range of beliefs. In India, the difference between *dharma* and religion is never confusing. Religion is an organized system of beliefs, but the path of *dharma* is the eternal path, Sanatana Dharma. It is a continuous process of exploring truth through inquiry, not a rigid set of once-established rituals.

He considered Yoga to be the immortal soul of this subcontinent, where new bodies had the opportunity to fulfill the purpose of incarnation: to find the Spirit within oneself and to realize (or actualize)

...it.

And he firmly believed that a person must complete himself in every respect in order to be transformed through the process of transformation. Having united with the soil, the seed grows into a tree; to assume a new form, it must cease to be a seed. In the same way, the search and investigation of the inner “self” transforms the seeker. A person is forced to adapt to a rapidly changing world, and adaptation stimulates inner metamorphoses. The soil for spiritual growth is fertilized by the past grandeur of many religions; any revision of belief systems inevitably conditions the beginning of a new cycle.

…The strangest thing about people is that they cannot truly feel time. A person simply knows that history continues to change. This perception is purely intellectual, since life is too short to contain and understand all aspects of change. In the absence of comparable experience, one must rely on historical archives and one’s own imagination. For most young people, events of 60 years ago and 600 years ago are largely similar—they all happened long ago, and are therefore perceived as abstractly relevant and alike. The sensation of Time is blurred mainly due to the loss of traditions. Throughout history, religion has provided the most significant landmarks. Now, spirituality is becoming the marker of Time’s flow. What will it orient itself toward? The internal

timekeeper exists thanks to the body, which gathers data by observing the aging of others and follows established and expected processes, meekly allowing time to seep into bone and flesh. And could it be otherwise?

…Many years ago, he left home, abandoned his accustomed way of life, and chose the path of yoga. Deconstructing habitual patterns and predictable narratives, yoga led him toward a gradual understanding of the continuity and wholeness of Time. Studying the world through the lens of Kriya, he learned to observe the collisions of old and new, the struggle of different principles, the battles for resources and minds—all of which were the engine of humanity’s progress. These cycles are quite bloody, yet Time is least concerned with pain. As a true Scientist, It observes the birth of worlds and new species, continuing to assemble and disassemble the universal construction set.

…He did not try to guess whether humanity would develop a broader understanding of reality or whether new religious, mystical, and apocalyptic meanings would prevail. One thing is clear: spiritual awakening is destined to become the dominant trend. Along with the changing world, new codes of the sacred arose. Myriads of inner universes awaited an awakened mind capable of discovering them.

**The Lottery and the Prize**

Assumptions that enlightened masters hold a protective shroud of their prayers over the planet are exaggerated. As far as he could tell, prayers (collective or individual) exert a therapeutic effect exclusively on those who pray. The fact that during his time in samadhi he never encountered other realized mortal masters in the astral world proved nothing. Perhaps they worked in other “astral departments.” In general, there is no one to welcome and protect newly arrived souls, and over time they drifted toward whatever egregore they had been drawn to in their past life. Souls relied mainly on the help of living relatives and rituals performed for their pacification. The laws of the subtle worlds are complex and harsh, but even they are subject to radical changes.

Shifts in the astral hierarchy are akin to corporate appointments and resignations; each new ruler brings his own team. In the time of the Mahabharata, avatars and children of Deities born from earthly women (such as Arjuna, Karna, Krishna) carried out the will of the rulers of the subtle worlds. Some of these “double agents” knew of their tasks and represented the interests of the gods consciously (or against their will). Most obeyed the call, fulfilling their des-

tiny and initiating significant changes in the world. Besides them, in all eras the ark of civilization remained afloat thanks to murderers and scientists, inventors and poets, dictators and dreamers who influenced the course of events. The role of the individual in history was determined not only by karmas and intentions, but also by algorithms programmed by higher forces.

No, he had not watched too many science fiction films, but merely observed and from time to time communicated with various spirits, ghosts, and their masters. The life of astral inhabitants is as complex as their motives and goals. Over the last thirty years, he had spoken about the reshuffling in the hierarchy of astral rulers and how these shifts affected events on Earth. It was entirely unimportant if some interpreted these views as extravagant theories. Never trying to convince or impress anyone, he was content with his personal understanding of the complex system of world order, which continued to reveal itself to him.

The struggle of celestial entities for dominance exerted a strong influence on human affairs, since the planet is a transit hub where six different timelines and countless dimensions intersect and merge. Astral beings came here for various reasons. Some were fugitive criminals who, trying to avoid their sentences, were born as humans. These extra-

terrestrial offenders were meant to die during wars or as a result of natural disasters, so that their souls could be “deported” back to their worlds. However fantastic this may sound, mass deportations of such souls are not uncommon.

Earth is not merely part of a portfolio of celestial real estate. There was something about this planet that made it a center of unflagging interest and attention for immortals, gods, lower and higher spirits, and demons. For the gods, She is a deity, a conscious being whose power and energy allowed even the highest spirits to incarnate. Astral rulers cared for Earth and revered Her because She was everything at once—a giant womb and the Garden of Eden, a laboratory and a refuge for refugees, a prison and a university, a vast cinema and a torture chamber. For the inhabitants of the subtle worlds, humans are merely livestock carrying out a continuous program of reproduction, since spirits constantly need new bodies.

Recently, however, planned reproduction has spiraled out of control. Resources were sufficient, but the logistics of incarnations and timely departures were faltering. One thing is clear: whoever controls Earth inevitably rules the subtle worlds as well. The Indian epics provided many hints and allusions confirming this fact. The identities of the players are always hidden, but the prize is great.

…After the Second World War, the world changed; sig-

nificant shifts occurred, stimulated by people’s hopes for healing and recovery. And then, on September 11, 2001, another turning point in modern history took place. The vulnerability of Western society was exposed, its values challenged. And yet, capitalism as a system continued to prove its effectiveness and viability.

The world had not yet fully recovered from the shock of the pandemic when 2022 began rattling quite real, albeit rusty, weapons. A revision of civilizational frameworks began anew, with military conflicts in Eastern Europe and the Middle East, grotesquely reflecting events of the early twentieth century. Geopolitical transitions never occur without fertile ground, without hidden or overt causes. Dictatorships and autocracies fight for survival, attacking a weakened democracy that, having lost vigilance over years of prosperity, had nearly forgotten the science of war, confrontation, and self-defense.

It cannot be said that this confrontation was so inevitable. Like countless wars of the past, the current conflicts marked changes intended to reset and accelerate the development of society, to revise the spiritual and moral paradigm. The battle of Kurukshetra began anew; brothers and relatives killed each other, and the struggle between archaism and modernity could drag on indefinitely.

In the subtle worlds, this organized tragedy was perceived as one of countless combinations in a giant Nintendo. The betting pools of the future were spinning again for millions of people, and no winners were in sight. The birth pangs of the old world were becoming more frequent, and the birth of a new era promised to be difficult.

Having clearly delineated the foundations of morality, ethics, and spiritual and moral views, the transition of epochs sharpened dualism, allowing no multi-variant interpretations. And yet, he urged students to see a broader picture of the world, to realize the global nature of the changes taking place, and to live through them consciously. And, as a spiritual mentor, he supported all disciples in moments of pain, darkness, and struggle.

There was never absolute predetermination in history. Its course was determined by collective karma, composed of mosaics of intentions and actions of individuals, communities, and countries. As always, all of this was filtered through the divine will of Time itself.

**Remaining Desires**

One of the exquisite pleasures of life is to forget one’s “self,” floating in the ocean of thoughtlessness, where no memories of past personalities dwell. Kriya led to another kind of satisfaction: remembering one’s own essence, realizing the wholeness of one’s “self” as the sum of experiences from past incarnations. The magical kaleidoscope of this yoga revealed concentrated clusters and contours of elusive Time, allowing a glimpse into the astonishing diversity of worlds. Of course, this is not a panacea for the inevitable finitude of bodily incarnation. The relentless millstones of Time indiscriminately ground both the subtle and the coarse, seasoning the grind with the bittersweet pollen of emotions and unfulfilled desires.

Everything and everyone is created from the same substance, saturated with the same pranic force: the reaper and the grain, the miller and the millstones. All forms are transient, but prana, Time, and Emptiness live in every object and being, awaiting transformation, if not recognition. One life is insufficient for a full inventory of these concepts, simultaneously familiar and eternally alien to the mind. In the end, the mind would abandon attempts to grasp the meaning of the infinite transformation of substances, objects, and seasons; dropping all objectivity, thought would defocus, only to experience a blind-

ing explosion of Truth. This usually happens when a mature consciousness is ready to absorb the new.

…At times it seemed that *smashan* triumphed over Time, and His tangled nets were left to soak in a pond, where apathetic fish and indifferent ducks pecked at them. Then Time was hung to dry on stone balustrades, and ghosts sometimes fashioned hammocks or camouflage veils from His torn cloud-fibers. Years passed, and suddenly someone would appear with grown children, or even grandchildren. The blushing youth of yesterday was about to retire. The former chubby little girl who used to come with her parents now held her own baby in her arms. The old tree in front of the house had grown threefold, and the fourth generation of German Shepherds chased the same monkey through the overgrown garden. The photographic film of life developed slowly, in the old way. The emerging contours and forms were recognizable, yet the essence remained hidden.

…Straightening his legs from padmasana, he waited for the numbness to subside; the years spent in the lotus posture resonated in his muscles with a tingling sensation. His gait was steady; daily exercises with dumbbells, barbells, and mudgars kept his body in excellent shape, according to a pact once made. His hair was still coal-black, and only in the stubble on his chin did gray appear. Shaving made this marker of time insignificant. Apart from these nuances, he felt the fluid elusiveness of Time rather
